7.14: A fulfillment: 'Behold the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and they shall call his name Immanuel.' The historical sign (birth to a young woman in Ahaz's time) is the type; the antitype is Jesus's virginal conception by the Holy Spirit, which makes the 'God with us' name literal for the first time in history. Every previous 'Immanuel' moment (YHWH dwelling in the tabernacle, in the temple, with Israel) was mediated through materials and institutions; in Christ, God is with us in person, in flesh, without mediation. The Immanuel bookend that Matthew establishes (1:23 = Isa 7:14; 28:20 = 'I am with you always') frames the entire Gospel as the Immanuel narrative.
7.1: The Syro-Ephraimite crisis (735 BCE): Rezin of Syria and Pekah of Israel march against Jerusalem, and the Davidic dynasty trembles. The political threat to the Davidic line is the immediate context for the Immanuel sign. Matt 1:1 opens with "the book of the genealogy of Jesus Christ, the son of David" — the survival of the Davidic line through this and every subsequent crisis is the precondition for the incarnation. Every attack on the Davidic house is implicitly an attack on the future Messiah.
7.2: "The heart of Ahaz and the heart of his people shook as the trees of the forest shake before the wind." The fear of Ahaz before the political threat is the opposite of the faith called for in 7:9. Jesus's "why are you afraid, O you of little faith?" (Matt 8:26) parallels the shaking of Ahaz — both are called to trust YHWH in a crisis. The peace that passes understanding (Phil 4:6-7) — "do not be anxious about anything" — is the NT resolution of the political anxiety that Isaiah diagnoses as faithlessness.
7.3: Isaiah goes to meet Ahaz with his son Shear-jashub ("a remnant shall return") — prophetic naming as enacted oracle. Matt 1:21 continues this tradition: "you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins" — the child's name is the prophetic word in compact form. The highway to the Washer's Field connects to 40:3 ("prepare the way"), linking the Syro-Ephraimite crisis narrative to the long arc of salvation history that culminates in Christ.
7.4: "Be careful, be quiet, do not fear, and do not let your heart be faint because of these two smoldering stumps of firebrands." The prophetic "do not fear" (ʾal-tîrāʾ) is the most common divine command in both testaments. Jesus's "fear not" occurs throughout the gospels (Matt 10:31; Luke 12:7,32; John 14:27: "do not let your hearts be troubled, and do not let them be afraid"). The prophetic reframe of political reality from the divine perspective is Christ's own practice: "in the world you will have tribulation, but take heart; I have overcome the world" (John 16:33).
7.5: "Because Syria, with Ephraim and the son of Remaliah, has devised evil against you." The naming of the threat in order to diminish it is a feature of prophetic speech. In Rev 2:10, Christ names the specific threat to Smyrna: "the devil is about to throw some of you into prison... Be faithful unto death." Jesus's "fear not" is always specific — he names what his people fear and reframes it within the larger narrative of divine sovereignty over all human plots.
7.6: "Let us go up against Judah and terrify it, and let us conquer it for ourselves, and set up the son of Tabeel as king in it." The plan to overthrow the Davidic dynasty and install a puppet king is the recurring Serpent's strategy to destroy the messianic line. Herod's slaughter of the innocents (Matt 2:16-18), Pilate's offer of Barabbas (Matt 27:20-26) — all operate in the same pattern. The preservation of the Davidic line through every attack is the divine faithfulness that makes the incarnation possible.
7.7: "Thus says the Lord YHWH: It shall not stand, and it shall not come to pass." The divine veto of the political conspiracy is absolute. The same divine "no" underlies the resurrection: "you killed the Author of life, but God raised him from the dead" (Acts 3:15). Every human plan to neutralize Christ — Herod's massacre, Pilate's execution, the sealed tomb — meets this divine counter. Acts 5:38-39 generalizes: "if it is of God, you will not be able to overthrow it."
7.8: "For the head of Syria is Damascus, and the head of Damascus is Rezin. And within sixty-five years Ephraim will be shattered from being a people." The prophetic precision grounds the credibility of all prophetic speech. Peter's argument at Pentecost (Acts 2:30-31: "knowing that God had sworn with an oath to him that he would set one of his descendants on his throne, he foresaw and spoke about the resurrection of the Christ") rests on prophetic precision of this kind — earlier prophecy fulfilled in history validates the unprecedented promise of resurrection.
7.9: "And the head of Samaria is the son of Remaliah. If you are not firm in faith, you will not be firm at all." The Hebrew wordplay taʾămînû / tēʾāmēnû (believe / be established) is compressed Isaianic faith-theology. Paul's fundamental category of pistis (faith) in Romans is grounded in this Isaianic usage: "The righteous shall live by faith" (Rom 1:17). Heb 11:1-3 similarly grounds the hall-of-faith survey in the Isaianic understanding of faith as the stabilizing foundation that invisible things provide for visible reality.
7.10: "Again YHWH spoke to Ahaz." The renewed divine address to the faithless king sustains a patience that the narrative will show Ahaz rejects. The persistence of divine address despite faithlessness is the pattern of grace: Rom 5:8, "God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us." The second approach to Ahaz is mirrored in Peter's threefold restoration (John 21:15-17) after threefold denial — faithlessness does not exhaust the divine approach.
7.11: "Ask a sign of YHWH your God; let it be deep as Sheol or high as heaven." The generous offer — any sign, from death's depths to heaven's heights — corresponds to the depth and height of the sign ultimately given: the Immanuel born of a virgin who descends into Sheol and ascends to heaven. Eph 3:18-19 prays that believers would "comprehend... the breadth and length and height and depth, and to know the love of Christ" — the sign offered to Ahaz spans the same dimensions Paul uses to describe the inexhaustible reach of Christ's love.
7.12: "But Ahaz said, 'I will not ask, and I will not put YHWH to the test.'" Ahaz's refusal is pious in form but faithless in substance — he has already decided to ask Tiglath-pileser for help (2 Kings 16:7-8). The Jesus who refuses to test YHWH (Matt 4:7: "you shall not put the Lord your God to the test") does so from genuine faith, not political calculation. The contrast highlights the Servant's authentic trust: where Ahaz uses the language of faith to cover faithlessness, Jesus practices the faith Ahaz refused.
7.13: "And he said, 'Hear then, O house of David! Is it too little for you to weary men, that you weary my God also?'" The "house of David" address widens the sign beyond Ahaz to the entire Davidic dynasty. Matt 1:1 opens the NT with "Jesus Christ, the son of David" — the genealogy is precisely the "house of David" to whom the sign is given. The wearying of God by faithlessness is echoed in Heb 3:17-19: "with whom was he provoked for forty years? Was it not with those who sinned?"
7.15: "He shall eat curds and honey when he knows how to refuse the evil and choose the good." The one who "knows how to refuse the evil and choose the good" is the one who "was tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin" (Heb 4:15). Jesus's forty-day fast (Matt 4:1-11), where he refuses the devil's temptations, fulfills the moral capacity described here. The Immanuel child who chooses good and refuses evil is the Servant who "had done no violence, and there was no deceit in his mouth" (Isa 53:9).
7.16: "For before the boy knows how to refuse the evil and choose the good, the land whose two kings you dread will be deserted." The sign operates on two timescales — near-term (the Assyrian resolution within years) and far-term (the Immanuel who is literally "God with us"). This two-horizon structure of prophetic fulfillment is the key to understanding NT citation of OT prophecy: the near fulfillment is the type; the far fulfillment in Christ is the antitype. The historical specificity of the near sign grounds the credibility of the far promise.
7.17: "YHWH will bring upon you and upon your people and upon your father's house such days as have not come since the day that Ephraim departed from Judah — the king of Assyria." The judgment following Ahaz's faithless alliance with Assyria is the consequence of refusing the divine sign. Jesus warns in the same pattern: Matt 23:38, "your house is left to you desolate," and 24:21, "then there will be great tribulation, such as has not been from the beginning of the world." The structure is exact: refusing the sign brings the very disaster the sign was meant to prevent.
7.18: "In that day YHWH will whistle for the fly that is at the end of the streams of Egypt, and for the bee that is in the land of Assyria." The fly from Egypt and bee from Assyria are imperial powers summoned as instruments of judgment. In Rev 9:3-11, locusts from the abyss combine insect and military imagery as divine judgment agency. The nations as divine instruments of judgment is a consistent OT pattern — Hab 1:6-11 (Babylon as divine instrument) — fulfilled in Christ's Olivet Discourse where the nations are simultaneously agents of judgment and subjects of the same judgment.
7.19: "And they will all come and settle in the steep ravines, and in the clefts of the rocks, and on all the thornbushes, and on all the pastures." The swarming occupation of the land echoes Joel 2 (the locust army) and Rev 9. The "clefts of the rocks" where the invaders shelter echoes 2:19-21 where the proud hide in caves from the Day of YHWH — the same geography serves both the agents of judgment and those fleeing from it. Those who reject the Immanuel find no safe cleft on the day of the Lamb's wrath (Rev 6:16-17).
7.20: "In that day the Lord will shave with a razor that is hired beyond the River — with the king of Assyria." YHWH "hires" Assyria as his own tool; the cross reveals that what appears as the hiring of human violence against the Messiah is in fact the divine instrument of redemption: "this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God" (Acts 2:23). The shaving/degradation of the judgment-instrument becomes the degradation of the Servant himself who "gave his back to those who strike, and his cheeks to those who pluck out the beard" (Isa 50:6).
7.21: "In that day a man will keep alive a young cow and two sheep." The remnant's small holdings after Assyrian devastation is an image of radical simplification. In NT terms, the stripping-away that judgment accomplishes becomes the poverty that is called blessed (Matt 5:3). The early church's sharing of all goods (Acts 2:44-45) reproduces this radical simplification — the Assyrian stripping is voluntarily reproduced in the communal life of those who have found in Immanuel their true wealth.
7.22: "And because of the abundance of milk that they give, he will eat curds, for everyone left in the land will eat curds and honey." The same curds and honey that mark the Immanuel child (v.15) are the food of the remnant. John 6:31-35 transforms the manna-in-wilderness tradition: "the bread of God is he who comes down from heaven and gives life to the world... I am the bread of life." The remnant's abundance within the devastated landscape is the type of the supernatural nourishment Christ provides in the wilderness of this age.
7.23: "In that day every place where there used to be a thousand vines, worth a thousand shekels of silver, will become briers and thorns." The reversal of the vineyard — the thousand-vine estate reverting to thorns — echoes ch. 5 and anticipates the crown of thorns (Matt 27:29; John 19:2) placed on the one who is the true vine (John 15:1). The thorns of the curse (Gen 3:18) become the symbol of human mockery of the divine king — the judgment-land's thorns woven into a crown for the one who bears the curse.
7.24: "With bow and arrows a man will come there, for all the land will be briers and thorns." Rev 6:2 opens the seal judgments with a rider carrying a bow, who "came out conquering, and to conquer" — the bow-and-thorn imagery of the devastated land becomes the apocalyptic imagery of conquest. The desolated land awaiting the Immanuel's birth is the broken world into which he comes to begin the restoration; the thorns and briers are the sign of the curse he will eventually reverse.
7.25: "And as for all the hills that used to be hoed with a hoe, you will not come there for fear of briers and thorns, but they will become a place where cattle are let loose and where sheep tread." The cultivated hills becoming untended pasture is an inverted pastoral. The lost sheep who wander untended (Matt 9:36: "like sheep without a shepherd") are the fulfillment of the Immanuel-era's abandoned pastures. The Good Shepherd of John 10 comes to restore the tending that judgment disrupted — the overgrown hills become pasture again under his care.

42.1: A direct revelation: 'Behold my servant whom I uphold, my chosen, in whom my soul delights; I have put my Spirit upon him; he will bring forth justice to the nations.' The first Servant Song is the OT's portrait of the Messiah as servant rather than conqueror. The divine delight in the Servant echoes at the baptism (Matt 3:17: 'my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased') — the Father's Psalm 2 / Servant-declaration. The Spirit's endowment (v. 1) is the anointing that Luke 4:18 identifies with Jesus's public ministry. The justice-to-the-nations mission is the mandate for the Gentile mission of Acts. The first Servant Song defines Christ's method: gentle, persistent, non-violent, Spirit-empowered, globally-oriented justice.
42.2: "He will not cry aloud or lift up his voice, or make it heard in the street." Matt 12:19 quotes this verse directly as fulfilled in Jesus's withdrawal from controversy and his command to silence after healings (12:16: "he ordered them not to make him known"). Mark's Gospel is saturated with the "Messianic secret" motif — the repeated commands to silence (1:25,44; 3:12; 5:43; 7:36; 8:30). John 5:41: "I do not receive glory from people." The Servant's non-publicity is the opposite of the power-display expected of a messiah — his silence is the silence of the one who "did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped" (Phil 2:6).
42.3: "A bruised reed he will not break, and a faintly burning wick he will not quench." Matt 12:20 quotes this. The gentleness of the Servant toward the vulnerable is Christ's pastoral signature: the tax collectors and sinners welcomed at table (Luke 15:1-2), the woman caught in adultery (John 8:10-11: "neither do I condemn you"), the prodigal received by the running father (Luke 15:20), the disciples restored after failure (John 21:15-17). The "bruised reed" is the one who has been damaged by life; the "smoldering wick" is the one whose faith is nearly extinguished. Christ tends both rather than discarding them. Matt 12:20 continues: "until he brings justice to victory" — the gentleness serves the ultimate triumph.
42.4: "He will not grow faint or be discouraged till he has established justice in the earth; and the coastlands wait for his instruction (tôrâ)." The Servant's perseverance "till" — the completion of the mission is guaranteed. Heb 12:2: "who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame, and is seated at the right hand of the throne of God." The coastlands waiting for his Torah (tôrâtô yĕyaḥēlû) is the Gentile mission: Acts 13:47 (Paul and Barnabas quoting 49:6 but fulfilling 42:4's expectation); Matt 28:20: "teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you." The law that goes forth from Zion (2:3) is Christ's teaching that the Gentile coastlands now receive.
42.5: "Thus says God, YHWH, who created the heavens and stretched them out, who spread out the earth and what comes from it, who gives breath to the people on it and spirit to those who walk in it." The creation formula that introduces the Servant's commission establishes the commissioner's credentials. John 1:3: "all things were made through him, and without him was not anything made that was made." Col 1:16: "by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth." The creator-God who commissions the Servant is the Father who sends the Son — and the Son is himself the one through whom the commission's creator-God made the heavens. The creation-authority of the commissioning deity is shared by the one commissioned.
42.6: "I am YHWH; I have called you in righteousness; I will take you by the hand and keep you; I will give you as a covenant for the people, a light for the nations." Two of the most significant Servant titles. (1) Bĕrît ʿām (covenant for the people): Heb 8:6: "he is the mediator of a better covenant"; Heb 9:15: "he is the mediator of a new covenant." Christ's blood is "the blood of the covenant" (Matt 26:28; Heb 13:20). (2) ʾÔr gôyîm (light for the nations): John 8:12: "I am the light of the world"; Luke 2:32 (Simeon): "a light for revelation to the Gentiles"; Acts 13:47 (Paul and Barnabas): "I have made you a light for the Gentiles." The divine hand-holding and keeping is the "I am with you always" of the Great Commission (Matt 28:20).
42.7: "To open the eyes that are blind, to bring out the prisoners from the dungeon, from the prison those who sit in darkness." Luke 4:18 quotes Isaiah 61:1 but fulfills 42:7 also: "to proclaim liberty to the captives and recovering of sight to the blind." John 9 is the enacted fulfillment: the man born blind receives sight, and the chapter explores the deeper blindness of those who claim to see (9:39-41). Acts 26:18: Paul's commission from the risen Christ is "to open their eyes, so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God." Rev 3:17-18: the church at Laodicea is "blind" and needs eye salve from Christ — the prison-opening and eye-opening continue as the Servant's ongoing work through the Spirit.
42.8: "I am YHWH; that is my name; my glory I give to no other, nor my praise to carved idols." The exclusive divine glory — and yet Christ shares it. John 17:5: "Father, glorify me in your own presence with the glory that I had with you before the world existed." Phil 2:9-11: "God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name." The glory YHWH refuses to give to any other (v.8) is the glory the pre-existent Son possessed before the incarnation and received fully at the resurrection. The apparent tension is resolved in Trinitarian theology: the Son is not an "other" to whom glory is given, but the one who is YHWH and shares the divine glory eternally.
42.9: "Behold, the former things have come to pass, and new things I now declare; before they spring forth I tell you of them." The divine foreknowledge that authenticates prophetic speech. Jesus's predictions of his death and resurrection — given before they occurred (Matt 16:21; 17:22-23; 20:18-19) — fulfill this claim: "I am telling you before it happens, so that when it does happen, you may believe that I am he" (John 13:19). Rev 1:19 commissions John to "write therefore the things that you have seen, those that are and those that are to take place after this" — the prophetic announcement of new things before they spring forth continues through the risen Christ.
42.10: "Sing to YHWH a new song, his praise from the end of the earth." Rev 5:9: "they sang a new song, saying, 'Worthy are you to take the scroll and to open its seals, for you were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation.'" Rev 14:3: "they were singing a new song before the throne." The new song of Isaiah 42:10 is explicitly identified as the song of the Lamb in Revelation — the new thing that YHWH declared (v.9) generates a new worship that was impossible before the Servant accomplished his mission. Ps 98:1 also calls for "a new song" for the marvelous things YHWH has done — Christ's resurrection is the marvelous act that fulfills both Psalms and Servant Song.
42.11: "Let the desert and its cities lift up their voice, the villages that Kedar inhabits; let the habitants of Sela sing for joy, let them shout from the top of the mountains." The gathering of worship from the desert margins — Kedar (nomadic Arab tribes) and Sela (Petra/Edom) — is the beginning of the Gentile mission's reach. Acts 8:26-40: Philip baptizes the Ethiopian eunuch on the desert road to Gaza — the desert lifts its voice. The geographic universalism of the new song anticipates Paul's missionary arc from Jerusalem to Illyricum to Spain (Rom 15:19-24): the mountaintop shout reaches to the ends of the earth.
42.12: "Let them give glory to YHWH, and declare his praise in the coastlands." The coastlands that "wait for his Torah" (v.4) now give glory rather than merely waiting. The progression — from waiting (v.4) to worshipping (v.12) — is the trajectory of the Gentile mission in Acts: the people who sat in darkness (42:7) and waited for instruction (42:4) now respond with worship. Rev 15:4: "who will not fear, O Lord, and glorify your name? For you alone are holy. All nations will come and worship you." The coastlands' final act is the worship that Christ's mission made possible.
42.13: "YHWH goes out like a mighty man; like a man of war he stirs up his zeal; he cries out, he shouts aloud, he shows himself mighty against his foes." The divine warrior who fights for his people. Rev 19:11-16: the rider on the white horse whose "eyes are like a flame of fire," wearing "a robe dipped in blood," who "wages war in righteousness" and strikes down the nations. John 2:17: "zeal for your house will consume me" — Jesus's temple-cleansing is the earthly enactment of the divine warrior's zeal. The Servant who was gentle with bruised reeds (v.3) is also the warrior who defeats the Serpent (3:15). Both gentleness and warfare belong to the Servant's full identity.
42.14: "For a long time I have held my peace; I have kept still and restrained myself; now I will cry out like a woman in labor; I will gasp and pant." The divine silence broken at last. Heb 1:1-2: "Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son." The incarnation is the end of the long divine restraint — the birth-cry of the new creation (the "woman in labor" yôlēdâ). John 1:14: "the Word became flesh" is the moment when the long-held peace ends and YHWH cries out in the person of his Son. The gasping and panting of the woman in labor becomes Gethsemane (Heb 5:7: "loud cries and tears") — the divine birth-cry voiced through the incarnate Son.
42.15: "I will lay waste mountains and hills, and dry up all their vegetation; I will turn the rivers into islands, and dry up the pools." The divine warrior's landscape-transformation as preparation for the march of the blinded people (v.16). The leveling of obstacles for the gospel's advance — Isa 40:4 (every mountain made low); Luke 3:5 (the Baptist's proclamation of leveling). The drying of waters and transformation of terrain is the new Exodus: as YHWH dried the Red Sea for Israel's deliverance, so the gospel's advance encounters YHWH's clearing of all obstacles that would prevent the blind from reaching him.
42.16: "And I will lead the blind in a way that they do not know, in paths that they have not known I will guide them. I will turn the darkness before them into light, the rough places into level ground. These are the things I do, and I do not forsake them." The divine guidance of the blind through unknown paths is the pastoral promise for the new community. John 10:3-4: "the sheep hear his voice, and he calls his own sheep by name and leads them out... he goes before them, and the sheep follow him, for they know his voice." 2 Cor 4:6: "God... has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ" — the turning of darkness to light before the blind (42:16) is the same act as the shining of divine light in Paul's heart on the Damascus road.
42.17: "They are turned back and utterly put to shame, who trust in carved idols, who say to metal images, 'You are our gods.'" The shame of idolaters — those whose confidence is in things made by human hands. 1 Cor 8:4-6: "we know that 'an idol has no real existence' and that 'there is no God but one'... yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things... and one Lord, Jesus Christ." Acts 17:29-30: "we ought not to think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone, an image formed by the art and imagination of man. The times of ignorance God overlooked, but now he commands all people everywhere to repent." Christ's resurrection is the demonstration that the living God who raised him is not the product of human art.
42.18: "Hear, you deaf, and look, you blind, that you may see!" The sudden address to the blind and deaf servant-people is the reversal of the healing commission (v.7: opening blind eyes) directed inward. Mark 8:18 (Jesus to the disciples): "Having eyes do you not see, and having ears do you not hear?" Rev 2:7,11,17,29: "He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches" — the repeated call to spiritual hearing across the seven letters. The deaf who need to hear are not only the nations but also the community of faith that has become spiritually imperceptive. The Servant who opens blind eyes finds that his own people have closed theirs.
42.19: "Who is blind but my servant, or deaf as my messenger whom I send? Who is blind as my dedicated one, or blind as the servant of YHWH?" The shocking paradox: the servant who was commissioned to open blind eyes is himself blind. In the NT, this becomes the diagnosis of the religious establishment that rejected Christ. John 9:40-41: the Pharisees ask "are we also blind?" and Jesus answers, "if you were blind, you would have no guilt; but now that you say, 'We see,' your guilt remains." Rom 11:7-8: "the elect obtained it, but the rest were hardened... 'God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes that would not see and ears that would not hear.'" The servant-nation called to witness is the first example of those who need the Servant's eye-opening.
42.20: "He sees many things, but does not observe them; his ears are open, but he does not hear." The progressive hardening — the ongoing exposure to revelation that produces no response. Rom 1:18-20: "what can be known about God is plain to them... for his invisible attributes, namely, his eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world in the things that have been made. So they are without excuse." The "seeing many things" without perceiving is the state Paul diagnoses as the suppression of truth in unrighteousness. Heb 4:7 warns: "Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts" — the repeated opportunity to see and hear becomes the ground of greater accountability.
42.21: "YHWH was pleased, for his righteousness' sake, to magnify his law and make it glorious." Matt 5:17-18: "Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them. For truly, I say to you, until heaven and earth pass away, not an iota, not a dot, will pass from the Law until all is accomplished." The fulfillment of Torah by Christ is precisely the magnification and glorification of the law that Isaiah 42:21 anticipates. Rom 3:31: "Do we then overthrow the law by this faith? By no means! On the contrary, we uphold the law." The Sermon on the Mount is the enacted magnification of Torah — each "you have heard it said... but I say to you" deepens rather than annuls the law's demands.
42.22: "But this is a people plundered and looted; they are all of them trapped in holes and hidden in prisons; they have become plunder with none to rescue, spoil with none to say, 'Restore!'" The captive, plundered people awaiting rescue is the condition that Christ's mission addresses. Luke 4:18: "to proclaim liberty to the captives... to set at liberty those who are oppressed." Rev 13:10: "If anyone is to be taken captive, to captivity he goes" — under the beast's rule the situation of 42:22 is reproduced; but the Lamb who was slain has purchased the captives (Rev 5:9: "by your blood you ransomed people for God"). The "none to rescue" of 42:22 is answered by the one who "gave his life as a ransom for many" (Matt 20:28).
42.23: "Who among you will give ear to this, will attend and listen for the time to come?" The prophetic call for attentive hearing of the judgment/salvation announcement. Matt 11:15: "He who has ears to hear, let him hear." Rev 2-3 (the seven churches): "He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches" — the repeated call across the Apocalypse. The "time to come" (lĕʿēt ʾāḥôr, for the future) is the eschatological future that the Servant's mission inaugurates; those who hear now are positioned for what is coming. Heb 2:1: "we must pay much closer attention to what we have heard, lest we drift away from it."
42.24: "Who gave up Jacob to the looter, and Israel to the plunderers? Was it not YHWH, against whom we have sinned, in whose ways they would not walk, and whose law they would not obey?" The acknowledgment that YHWH himself gave his people up — the covenant judgment. Rom 1:24,26,28: "God gave them up to..." — Paul's three-fold use of the verb paredōken (handed over) in Romans 1 applies the same logic: the covenant God who hands over his sinful people to the consequences of their choices. The ultimate "giving over" is Christ: "he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up (paredōken) for us all" (Rom 8:32) — the Father gives the Son as the final answer to the handing-over that Israel's sin deserved.
42.25: "So he poured on him the heat of his anger and the might of battle; it set him on fire all around, but he did not understand; it burned him up, but he did not take it to heart." The repeated judgment that produces no comprehension — the fire of divine wrath that Israel endured without learning from it. Heb 12:5-11: "the Lord disciplines the one he loves" — discipline is meant to produce learning and righteousness. The failure of Israel in 42:25 (burning without understanding) is the cautionary context for the NT call to receive God's discipline fruitfully. In Christ, the fire fell on the one who did understand — who received the judgment he did not deserve so that those who deserved it might be spared. 2 Cor 5:21: "he made him to be sin who knew no sin."

52.13: A direct revelation: 'Behold, my servant shall act wisely; he shall be high and lifted up, and shall be exalted.' The Servant Songs reach their climax in the fourth song: the exaltation begins the song (52:13) and ends it (53:12: 'I will divide him a portion among the many, and he shall divide the spoil with the strong'). The Christological movement: wisdom → humiliation (disfigurement, rejection, death) → exaltation (he shall see his offspring, he shall prolong his days). The resurrection is implicit in 'he shall prolong his days' after 'he was cut off out of the land of the living' (53:8-10). The fourth Servant Song is the OT's clearest pre-figuration of the entire Christological narrative: incarnation, rejection, crucifixion, resurrection, and universal reign.
52.1: "Awake, awake, put on your strength, O Zion; put on your beautiful garments, O Jerusalem, the holy city." The double "Awake, awake" mirrors 51:9 (the arm of YHWH awakening) — now the community is called to respond. Rev 21:2: "the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband." Eph 5:27: "the church... in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, that she might be holy and without blemish." The "beautiful garments" are the righteousness Christ provides: Rev 19:8 (the fine linen, bright and pure, is "the righteous deeds of the saints"); Isa 61:10 ("he has clothed me with the garments of salvation"). The call to awake is both eschatological and present: Rom 13:11 ("it is time for you to wake from sleep") applies the same awakening call to the church awaiting Christ's return.
52.2: "Shake yourself from the dust and arise, O captive Jerusalem; loose the bonds from your neck, O captive daughter of Zion." The prisoner told to remove her own bonds — the paradox of liberation that requires the liberated to participate. John 11:44: Lazarus comes out of the tomb still bound, and Jesus says "Unbind him, and let him go" — the act of loosing is given to the community to perform what Christ has accomplished. Gal 5:1: "For freedom Christ has set us free; stand firm therefore, and do not submit again to a yoke of slavery." The neck-bonds are the yoke of the law and sin; rising from dust is the resurrection posture of those who belong to the risen Lord. The command to "shake off the dust" (Matt 10:14) is Jesus's instruction to the disciples as they go out — the liberation Zion is offered becomes the posture of the gospel mission.
52.3: "For thus says YHWH: 'You were sold for nothing, and you shall be redeemed without money.'" The costlessness of the sale and the costlessness of the redemption — but what costs nothing to the recipient cost everything to the Redeemer. 1 Pet 1:18-19: "you were ransomed from the futile ways inherited from your forefathers, not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ." Rev 22:17: "let the one who desires take the water of life without price." The "without money" (lōʾ bĕḵesef) redemption anticipates Isa 55:1 ("without money and without price") — the free offer of salvation that is free to receive but infinitely costly to provide. The apparent economy — sold for nothing, bought for nothing — conceals the price of the Son.
52.4: "For thus says the Lord YHWH: My people went down at the first into Egypt to sojourn there, and the Assyrian oppressed them for nothing." The review of Israel's exile history — Egypt and Assyria as types of captivity. Matt 2:13-15: the flight to Egypt and return fulfill Hos 11:1 ("out of Egypt I called my son") — Christ recapitulates Israel's Egypt sojourn in his infancy. The "for nothing" (ʾēpōʾ mâ-lî-p̄ōh — "what have I here?") oppression without cause is the oppression that Christ, the innocent one, endured supremely. The typological pattern: Egypt-bondage → Exodus-liberation is the meta-narrative that Christ's passion-resurrection enacts at cosmic scale (Luke 9:31: "his departure" — the Greek is exodon, exodus).
52.5: "Now therefore what have I here, declares YHWH, seeing that my people are taken away for nothing? Their rulers wail, declares YHWH, and continually all the day my name is despised." Paul quotes this verse in Rom 2:24: "The name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you" — applying the oracle to the moral failure of Jews in Rome whose conduct dishonors YHWH. The "name despised" because of the people's captivity becomes in the NT the name despised because of the community's hypocrisy. But the name is ultimately glorified through Christ: Phil 2:9-11 ("the name that is above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow"). The desecration of the name in exile is answered by the exaltation of the name in resurrection.
52.6: "Therefore my people shall know my name. Therefore in that day they shall know that it is I who speak; here I am." The self-revelation of YHWH in the eschaton — "here I am" (hinnēnî). John 17:6: "I have manifested your name to the people whom you gave me out of the world." John 17:26: "I made known to them your name, and I will continue to make it known." John 8:24,28: "unless you believe that I am he, you will die in your sins... when you have lifted up the Son of Man, then you will know that I am he." The "I AM" declarations of Christ are the fulfillment of 52:6 — the people will "know his name" through the one who says "I am the way, the truth, and the life" (John 14:6). "Here I am" is both the prophetic messenger's response and the divine self-disclosure that Christ embodies.
52.7: "How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace, who brings good news of happiness, who publishes salvation, who says to Zion, 'Your God reigns.'" The most celebrated gospel verse in the OT. Rom 10:15 quotes it as the basis for the preaching mission: "How are they to preach unless they are sent? As it is written, 'How beautiful are the feet of those who preach the good news!'" The content of the good news is threefold: šālôm (peace), ṭôb (happiness/good), yĕšûʿâ (salvation — the same root as "Jesus"). The announcement "Your God reigns" (YHWH mālāk) = "the kingdom of God has come near" (Mark 1:15). The herald who runs to declare the king's victory is Christ — and those who follow him become heralds of the same message (the Great Commission, Matt 28:19-20).
52.8: "The voice of your watchmen — they lift up their voice; together they sing for joy; for eye to eye they see the return of YHWH to Zion." The watchmen seeing YHWH return to Zion — the eschatological vision of divine return. Luke 2:25-32: Simeon, who had waited for "the consolation of Israel," takes the child Jesus and says "my eyes have seen your salvation." John 1:14: "we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father." Acts 1:10-11: two angels announce to the watching disciples that "this Jesus, who was taken up from you into heaven, will come in the same way as you saw him go." The watchmen of Isaiah 52:8 are simultaneously the prophets who saw Christ's coming, those who witnessed his incarnation, and the church that watches for his return.
52.9: "Break forth together into singing, you waste places of Jerusalem, for YHWH has comforted his people; he has redeemed Jerusalem." The desolate places breaking into song. Rev 21:1-5: "Behold, I am making all things new" — the waste places become the new Jerusalem. Isa 35:1-2: "the wilderness and the dry land shall be glad; the desert shall rejoice and blossom." Luke 19:40: "if these were silent, the very stones would cry out" — Jesus's response when the Pharisees demand silence from the disciples who are declaring him king; creation itself would join the singing of 52:9. The comfort and redemption of Jerusalem announced here is the subject of the entire second half of Isaiah (chs 40-66), fulfilled in Christ's "it is finished" (John 19:30).
52.10: "YHWH has bared his holy arm before the eyes of all the nations, and all the ends of the earth shall see the salvation of our God." The bared arm of YHWH — the divine warrior revealing his power. Luke 2:30-32: Simeon: "my eyes have seen your salvation that you have prepared in the presence of all peoples, a light for revelation to the Gentiles." John 12:32: "when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself." 1 Cor 1:24: "Christ crucified... the power of God" (dynamin Theou) — the cross is the bared arm of YHWH. The universal scope ("all the ends of the earth") is the Great Commission geography (Matt 28:19: "all nations") and the Pentecost reversal of Babel (Acts 2:5: "devout men from every nation under heaven"). The arm bared at the cross is the arm that gathers the nations.
52.11: "Depart, depart, go out from there; touch no unclean thing; go out from the midst of her; purify yourselves, you who bear the vessels of YHWH." The call to holy separation. 2 Cor 6:17 quotes this directly: "Therefore go out from their midst, and be separate from them, says the Lord." Rev 18:4: "Come out of her, my people, lest you take part in her sins." The "vessels of YHWH" — the temple vessels that the Babylonian priests were not to profane during the return — become in the NT the believers themselves: 2 Tim 2:21 ("a vessel for honorable use, set apart as holy, useful to the master of the house"). The call to depart from uncleanness is the holiness ethic of the new community, grounded in the purity Christ has provided.
52.12: "For you shall not go out in haste, and you shall not go in flight, for YHWH will go before you, and the God of Israel will be your rear guard." The new exodus that differs from the first: no haste, no panic (contrast Ex 12:11: "eat it in haste"). The un-panicked departure of those who trust in YHWH's protection before and behind. Phil 4:6-7: "do not be anxious about anything... and the peace of God... will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus." Matt 28:20: "I am with you always, to the end of the age" — the promise of divine presence going before and behind. Heb 13:5: "I will never leave you nor forsake you." The rear-guard YHWH is Christ who sent the Paraclete (John 14:16-17) as his presence after the ascension — the church moves through history neither in haste nor in fear because Christ guards both the advance and the retreat.
52.14: "As many were astonished at you — his appearance was so marred, beyond human semblance, and his form beyond that of the children of mankind." The Servant's disfigurement that shocks observers. John 19:1-5: after the scourging, Pilate presents the beaten Jesus to the crowd — "Behold the man!" (Ecce homo) — and the "man" is barely recognizable. Phil 2:7-8: "emptied himself, taking the form of a servant... he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death." Isa 53:2-3 extends the description: "he had no form or majesty... he was despised and rejected." The astonishment (šāmēm) of v.14 is the stupefied horror of those who encounter the spectacle of divine suffering in human flesh — it is the astonishment of the cross, which Paul describes as "folly to those who are perishing" but "the power of God" to those being saved (1 Cor 1:18).
52.15: "So shall he sprinkle many nations; kings shall shut their mouths because of him; for that which has not been told them they shall see, and that which they have not heard they shall understand." The Servant who sprinkles the nations — the priestly act (yazzeh) applied to the Gentiles at cosmic scale. 1 Pet 1:2: believers are "chosen... for obedience to Jesus Christ and for sprinkling with his blood." Heb 9:13-14: the blood of Christ purifies the conscience. Rom 15:20-21 quotes v.15 directly as the mandate for Paul's mission to the unevangelized Gentiles: "I make it my ambition to preach the gospel, not where Christ has already been named... as it is written, 'Those who have never been told of him will see, and those who have never heard will understand.'" The kings who shut their mouths is the NT pattern of earthly authority silenced before Christ: Matt 22:46 ("no one was able to answer him a word"), 27:12-14 (Jesus silent before Pilate; Pilate himself silenced by the crowd). The unanswerable Christ sprinkles all nations with his blood.

53.5: A direct revelation: 'He was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities; upon him was the chastisement that brought us peace, and with his wounds we are healed.' The substitutionary suffering of the Servant expressed in four parallel lines: (1) pierced for our transgressions; (2) crushed for our iniquities; (3) chastisement for our peace; (4) wounds for our healing. The preposition lemaan/min in Hebrew (for/because of/from our transgressions) carries causal weight — the Servant suffers not for his own sins but for others'. Peter cites v. 5b-c in 1 Pet 2:24 (by his wounds you have been healed) as the foundational text for Christ's atoning death. The fourfold parallelism is the OT's most precise statement of what the cross accomplishes.
53.11: A direct revelation: 'Out of the anguish of his soul he shall see and be satisfied; by his knowledge shall the righteous one, my servant, make many to be accounted righteous, and he shall bear their iniquities.' The justification-language of Isa 53:11 — yatsdiq (make righteous/justify) + the many — is Paul's foundation for Pauline justification theology. The righteous Servant making many righteous by bearing their iniquities is the atonement-justification nexus that Romans 3-5 develops: Christ's obedient death (bearing iniquities) is the ground of the justified status of many. Isaiah 53:11 is the OT's closest approximation to Paul's 'he made him to be sin so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Cor 5:21).
53.1: A direct prophecy. Mî hě'emîn lišmû'ātēnû ûzĕrôa' YHWH 'al-mî niglātāh — who has believed what he has heard from us? And to whom has the arm of YHWH been revealed? John 12:38 cites this verse as fulfilled in the aftermath of Jesus' signs: so that the word spoken by the prophet Isaiah might be fulfilled: 'Lord, who has believed what he heard from us?' Romans 10:16 cites it again: they have not all obeyed the gospel, for Isaiah says, 'Lord, who has believed what he has heard from us?' The Servant's reception begins with the question of belief — establishing that the scandal of the Messiah is the stumbling block the gospel announces.
53.2: A direct prophecy. No form or majesty that we should look at him, and no beauty that we should desire him — the Servant is not recognizable as a king, having no regal appearance. The hiddenness of the Messiah's glory is the Incarnation's signature: John 1:10-11 (the world did not know him; his own people did not receive him); Luke 2:7 (born in a stable, no room at the inn). The root from dry ground (kĕšōreš mē'ereṣ ṣiyyāh) is the emergence of new life from what appeared dead — the Davidic dynasty cut to a stump (Isa 11:1), from which the Servant-shoot emerges against all expectation.
53.3: A direct prophecy. Nib̠zeh waḥădal 'îšîm 'îš maḵ'ōb̠ôt — despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows (maḵ'ōb̠ôt — literally pains, griefs) and acquainted with grief. The passion narrative is the direct fulfillment: Luke 23:11 (Herod with his soldiers treated him with contempt and mocked him); John 1:11 (he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him); Mark 9:12 (how is it written of the Son of Man that he should suffer many things and be treated with contempt?). As one from whom men hide their faces — the disciples' flight (Matt 26:56) and Peter's denial (Matt 26:69-75) enact the hiding of faces from the suffering Servant.
53.4: A direct prophecy. 'ākēn ḥolāyēnû hû' nāśā' ûmaḵ'ōb̠ênû sĕb̠ālām — surely he has borne (nāśā') our griefs and carried our sorrows. Matthew 8:17 quotes this as fulfilled in Jesus' healing ministry: he took our illnesses and bore our diseases — applying the Servant's bearing not only to the cross but to the entire ministry of healing. 1 Peter 2:24 applies it to the atonement: he himself bore our sins in his body on the tree, that we might die to sin and live to righteousness. The verb nāśā' (to bear, to carry) is the sacrificial term for taking on what belongs to another — the same verb that describes the scapegoat carrying the people's sins (Lev 16:22).
53.6: A direct prophecy. Kullānû kĕṣō'n tā'înû 'îš lĕdarkô pānînû waYHWH hip̄gîa' b̠ô 'ēt 'ăwōn kullānû — all we like sheep have gone astray; we have turned every one to his own way; and YHWH has caused the iniquity of us all to fall on him. The total scope of sin (kullānû... kullānû — all of us... all of us) and the total scope of the bearing: every individual's sin laid on the Servant. 1 Peter 2:25 cites this directly: you were straying like sheep, but have now returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of your souls. John 10:11-15 (the Good Shepherd lays down his life for the sheep, knowing his sheep and known by them) is the narrative form of Isaiah 53:6 — the Shepherd who becomes the sacrificial lamb for the straying sheep.
53.7: A direct prophecy. Niggaś wĕhû' na'ăneh wĕlō' yip̄taḥ-pîw kĕśeh labbataḥ yûb̠al — he was oppressed and he was afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth; like a lamb led to slaughter, like a sheep silent before its shearers. Acts 8:32-35 records Philip using this exact verse to explain the gospel to the Ethiopian eunuch — the most explicit OT-to-gospel connection in Acts. The silent lamb is Jesus before the high priest (Matt 26:63: Jesus remained silent) and before Pilate (Matt 27:14: he gave him no answer, not even to a single charge). John 1:29 (behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world) and 1 Peter 1:19 (the precious blood of Christ like that of a lamb without blemish or spot) are the theological appropriation of the silent lamb of Isaiah 53:7.
53.8: A direct prophecy. Mē'ōṣer ûmimmiśpāṭ luqqāḥ ûb̠ĕdôrô mî yĕśôḥēaḥ kî niggĕzar mē'ereṣ ḥayyîm mippešā' 'ammî nĕga' lāmô — by oppression and judgment he was taken away; who considered that he was cut off out of the land of the living, stricken for the transgression of my people? The legal process culminating in death (oppression and judgment... taken away) is the trial and crucifixion: Pontius Pilate's judgment, the unjust verdict, the execution. Cut off out of the land of the living is death. Romans 4:25 (he was delivered up for our trespasses) and Acts 3:13-15 (you denied the Holy and Righteous One... and killed the Author of life, whom God raised) apply this verse to the cross and the resurrection that reverses it.
53.9: A direct prophecy. Wayyittēn 'et-rĕšā'îm qib̠rô wĕ'et-'āšîr b̠ĕmōtāyw — they made his grave with the wicked and with a rich man in his death. The double burial detail — among the wicked (crucified between two criminals, Luke 23:32-33) and with a rich man (Joseph of Arimathea's tomb, Matt 27:57-60) — is fulfilled with precision. Although he had done no violence, and there was no deceit in his mouth — 1 Peter 2:22 quotes this of Christ directly: he committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth. The innocence of the Servant established by the prophet becomes the foundation of his substitutionary efficacy: the innocent dies for the guilty.
53.10: A direct prophecy. waYHWH ḥāp̄ēṣ dakke'ô heḥĕlî 'im-tāśîm 'āšām nap̄šô yir'eh zera' ya'ărîk̠ yāmîm — yet it was the will (ḥāp̄ēṣ) of YHWH to crush him; he has put him to grief. When his soul makes a guilt offering ('āšām), he shall see his offspring; he shall prolong his days. Acts 4:28 (what your hand and your plan had predestined to take place — the crucifixion — is the fulfillment of YHWH's sovereign will). The 'āšām (guilt/reparation offering, Lev 5-6) applied to the Servant is the atoning sacrifice that satisfies divine justice. He shall prolong his days after making the offering for guilt is the resurrection: the one who was cut off (v. 8) lives again, seeing his offspring — the community of faith — as the fruit of his death. Hebrews 9:14 (Christ offered himself without blemish to God) and John 12:24 (the grain of wheat that falls and dies bears much fruit) apply both the sacrifice and the offspring-seeing.
53.12: A direct prophecy. lākēn 'ăḥalleq-lô b̠ārabbîm wĕ'et-'ăṣûmîm yĕḥallēq šālāl taḥat 'ăšer hě'ĕrāh lammāwet nap̄šô wĕ'et-pōšĕ'îm nimnāh wĕhû' ḥēṭ'-rabbîm nāśā' wĕlappōšĕ'îm yap̄gîa' — therefore I will divide him a portion with the many... because he poured out his soul to death and was numbered with the transgressors; yet he bore the sin of many, and makes intercession for the transgressors. Jesus cites this verse at the Last Supper: Luke 22:37 (for I tell you that this scripture must be fulfilled in me: 'and he was numbered with the transgressors') — identifying himself as the Servant before the arrest that makes it literal (crucified between criminals, Luke 23:32-33). He makes intercession for the transgressors is Hebrews 7:25 (he always lives to make intercession for them) and Luke 23:34 (Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do). The dividing of spoil (yĕḥallēq šālāl) with the strong is the resurrection triumph's fruits distributed to those who are his (Eph 4:8: when he ascended he gave gifts to his church).

61.1: A direct revelation: 'The Spirit of the Lord GOD is upon me, because the LORD has anointed me to bring good news to the poor; he has sent me to bind up the brokenhearted, to proclaim liberty to the captives and the opening of the prison to those who are bound; to proclaim the year of the LORD's favor.' Jesus's Nazareth declaration — 'Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing' (Luke 4:21) — is the most explicit Christological self-application of an OT text in the Gospels. Jesus is not a prophet announcing a future event; he is himself the eschatological year of favor. The Jubilee of Isa 61 (release from debt-slavery, return of land, restoration of community) is enacted wherever Christ's reign extends — in healing, exorcism, forgiveness, community inclusion.
61.2: A direct prophecy with a noted gap. Liqrō' šĕnat-rāṣôn laYHWH wĕyôm nāqām lē'lōhênû lĕnaḥēm kol-'ăb̄ĕlîm — to proclaim the year of YHWH's favor, and the day of vengeance of our God; to comfort all who mourn. Luke 4:20 records that Jesus rolled up the scroll and sat down after reading to proclaim the year of YHWH's favor — stopping before and the day of vengeance of our God. This mid-sentence pause is theologically precise: the year of favor is the first advent (grace and healing); the day of vengeance is the second advent (judgment). 2 Corinthians 6:2 (behold, now is the favorable time; behold, now is the day of salvation) applies the year of favor to the gospel age. The day of vengeance awaits Christ's return (Rev 19:11-21).
61.3: A shadow: to grant to those who mourn in Zion — a beautiful headdress (pĕ'ēr) instead of ashes, the oil of gladness (šemen śāśôn) instead of mourning, the garment of praise instead of a faint spirit. Hebrews 1:9 quotes Psalm 45:7 of Christ: God has anointed you with the oil of gladness beyond your companions — the anointed one who possesses the oil of gladness himself gives it to those who mourn. Revelation 21:4 is the eschatological fulfillment: God will wipe away every tear; there will be no more mourning or crying. The oaks of righteousness ('êlê haṣṣedeq) are those rooted in Christ (Col 2:7: rooted and built up in him), displaying the righteousness that is his gift.
61.4: A shadow: they shall build up the ancient ruins; raise up the former devastations; repair the ruined cities. The rebuilding theme of Isaiah 58:12 continues here. The community formed by the Servant's ministry becomes a restoring community — the church as rebuilder of what sin has destroyed. Ephesians 2:19-22 (built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ being the cornerstone; the whole structure joined together grows into a holy temple in the Lord). The ancient ruins are the shattered human condition; the rebuilt city is the community of the new covenant gathered in Christ.
61.5: A shadow: strangers shall tend your flocks; foreigners shall be your plowmen and vinedressers. The inclusion of foreigners in the service of Zion is the type of the Gentile mission: not the replacement of Israel by Gentiles but the incorporation of Gentiles into the covenant community. Romans 11:17 (Gentile branches grafted into the olive tree) and Ephesians 3:6 (the Gentiles are fellow heirs, members of the same body) describe the same dynamic. The foreigners who serve Zion are the Gentile believers whose inclusion fulfills Isaiah's vision of a united human family before YHWH.
61.6: A direct prophecy. Wĕ'attem kōhănê YHWH tiqāre'û mĕšārtê 'Ĕlōhênû yē'āmēr lākem — but you shall be called the priests of YHWH; they shall speak of you as the ministers of our God. 1 Peter 2:9 applies this directly to the church: you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession. Revelation 1:6 (he has made us a kingdom, priests to his God and Father) and 5:10 (you have made them a kingdom and priests to our God) cite the same prophetic promise as fulfilled in Christ's work. The universal priesthood — all the redeemed as priests — is what Exodus 19:6 promised and Isaiah 61:6 reaffirms, fulfilled through Christ who is both high priest and the one who makes his people priests.
61.7: A shadow: instead of your shame there shall be a double portion; instead of dishonor they shall rejoice in their lot; they shall have everlasting joy (śimḥat 'ôlām). The reversal of shame into double honor is the eschatological pattern that Christ enacts: the one who endured the shame of the cross (Heb 12:2: despising the shame) receives the honor of the exaltation at the Father's right hand. Those who share his shame share his honor: Romans 8:17 (we suffer with him in order that we may also be glorified with him). The everlasting joy of Isaiah 61:7 is the joy that no one can take away (John 16:22).
61.8: A direct prophecy. Kî 'ănî YHWH 'ōhēb̠ mišpāṭ śōnē' gāzēl bĕ'ôlāh ûnātattî pĕullah bě'emet lāhem ûb̠ĕrît 'ôlām 'ekrōt lāhem — for I, YHWH, love justice; I hate robbery and wrong; I will faithfully give them their recompense, and I will make an everlasting covenant (b̠ĕrît 'ôlām) with them. Hebrews 13:20 identifies this everlasting covenant as the new covenant sealed in Christ's blood: the God of peace who brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, the great shepherd of the sheep, by the blood of the eternal covenant. The same b̠ĕrît 'ôlām that Isaiah 24:5 describes as broken and Isaiah 55:3 promises to restore is established permanently through the blood of the Servant.
61.9: A shadow: their offspring shall be known among the nations, and their descendants in the midst of the peoples; all who see them shall acknowledge them as an offspring YHWH has blessed. The visible blessing of the covenant community among the nations — recognized even by outsiders as YHWH's blessed people — is the NT picture of the church's witness: Matthew 5:16 (let your light shine before others, so that they may see your good works and give glory to your Father who is in heaven). The offspring known among the nations are those born again through the Servant's gospel (John 1:12-13; 1 Pet 1:23), whose transformed lives testify that they are an offspring YHWH has blessed.
61.10: A shadow: śôś 'āśîś baYHWH tāgēl nap̄šî bē'lōhay kî hilbîšanî b̠igdê-yēša' mĕ'îl ṣĕdāqāh — I will greatly rejoice in YHWH; my soul shall exult in my God, for he has clothed me with the garments of salvation (bigdê-yēša'); he has covered me with the robe of righteousness (mĕ'îl ṣĕdāqāh), as a bridegroom decks himself, as a bride adorns herself. The clothing imagery is the NT's forensic justification in pictorial form: 2 Corinthians 5:21 (in him we might become the righteousness of God); Galatians 3:27 (you have clothed yourselves with Christ); Revelation 19:8 (the fine linen, bright and pure, represents the righteous deeds of the saints). The exultation of the one clothed in salvation is the joy of justification — what Paul describes in Romans 5:1-2 as peace with God and rejoicing in hope.
61.11: A shadow: as the earth causes its sprouts to spring up, and a garden causes what is sown in it to sprout up, so the Lord YHWH will cause righteousness and praise to sprout up before all the nations. The agricultural metaphor of righteousness sprouting before all nations is the gospel's universal scope: Romans 1:17 (the righteousness of God is revealed from faith to faith); John 12:24 (unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit). The mustard seed and leaven parables (Matt 13:31-33) use the same natural-growth imagery for the kingdom: the Servant's work, once planted, produces a harvest that fills the earth with righteousness and praise to YHWH.

2.1: The chapter's heading (haddābār ʾăšer ḥāzâ yĕšaʿyāhû) identifies prophetic vision as "the word that Isaiah saw" — vision (ḥāzôn) and word (dābār) are united. In the NT, this unity of seeing and hearing reaches its apex in the Prologue of John (1:1-14): the Word that was in the beginning, heard and seen by the eyewitnesses (1 John 1:1-3, "what we have heard, what we have seen with our eyes, what we looked upon and touched with our hands"). The prophetic word is proto-incarnational: it comes from YHWH, takes form in time, and demands reception.
2.2: "It shall come to pass in the latter days (bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm) that the mountain of YHWH's house shall be established as the highest of the mountains." The phrase bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm (in the last/latter days) is programmatically used in Acts 2:17 where Peter, citing Joel 2:28-32, announces "in the last days, God declares, I will pour out my Spirit" — the Pentecost is the inauguration of the ʾaḥărît hayyāmîm. Hebrews 1:2 announces "in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son." The "latter days" of Isaiah 2:2 = the age that began with the incarnation and Pentecost. The "mountain of YHWH's house" that all nations stream to is the eschatological Zion whose fullness Revelation 21:22-27 describes — the New Jerusalem needs no temple "for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb," and "the nations will walk by its light."
2.3: "Many peoples shall come and say: Come, let us go up to the mountain of YHWH, to the house of the God of Jacob, that he may teach us his ways and that we may walk in his paths. For from Zion shall go forth Torah (tôrâ), and the word of YHWH from Jerusalem." The going-forth of Torah from Zion is fulfilled in Christ who is the Word from the Father (John 1:1, 14) and the fulfillment of the Torah (Matt 5:17: "I have not come to abolish the Torah but to fulfill it"). The missionary sending of Acts 1:8 ("you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth") is the actualization of the word going forth from Jerusalem. Paul reads his own Gentile mission as the fulfillment of passages like this (Rom 15:18-21), going where the name of Christ has not been named in obedience to the eschatological gathering of nations.
2.4: "He shall judge between the nations and shall decide disputes for many peoples; and they shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks; nation shall not lift up sword against nation, neither shall they learn war anymore." The Christological reception is threefold. (1) Christ as eschatological judge: John 5:22 ("the Father judges no one, but has given all judgment to the Son"); Acts 17:31 ("he has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed"). (2) The peace achieved through Christ: Eph 2:14-15 ("he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility"). (3) The swords-to-plowshares reversal of Joel 3:10 ("beat your plowshares into swords") in Isaiah creates an eschatological polarity: the call to war before judgment, the transformation to peace after it — Christ stands at the fulcrum, first the servant who makes peace by the cross, then the judge who enforces it at his return.
2.5: "O house of Jacob, come, let us walk in the light of YHWH." The appeal to "walk in the light" of YHWH becomes in the NT the fundamental description of the Christian life. John 8:12: "I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life." 1 John 1:5-7: "God is light, and in him is no darkness at all. If we say we have fellowship with him while we walk in darkness, we lie and do not practice the truth. But if we walk in the light, as he is in the light, we have fellowship with one another." The light of YHWH in Isaiah 2:5 is the light that comes into the world in Jesus (John 1:9-11), which the darkness could not overcome (1:5).
2.6: "For you have rejected your people, the house of Jacob, because they are full of things from the east and of fortune-tellers like the Philistines, and they strike hands with the children of foreigners." The abandonment of the people to foreign religious practices is the judgment that precedes the Day of YHWH. In NT terms, this chapter's structure of rejection followed by eschatological vindication maps onto the pattern of Israel's rejection of Christ and the subsequent opening of the Gentile mission (Rom 11:11-12: "through their trespass salvation has come to the Gentiles, so as to make Israel jealous"). The "things from the east" and fortune-telling represent the syncretism that Jesus condemns in the religious leaders who "devour widows' houses" and "say a long prayer" while rejecting the covenant's weightier matters (Matt 23:23).
2.7: "Their land is filled with silver and gold, and there is no end to their treasures; their land is filled with horses, and there is no end to their chariots." The accumulation of wealth and military power violates the Deuteronomic restriction on the king (Deut 17:16-17: "he must not acquire many horses... he must not acquire much silver and gold"). Jesus's teaching inverts this entirely: "lay not up treasures on earth" (Matt 6:19-20); "you cannot serve God and money" (6:24); the rich young ruler who turns away from discipleship because of his possessions (Mark 10:22). The land filled with treasures and horses is the opposite of the kingdom of God, which belongs to the poor in spirit (Matt 5:3).
2.8: "Their land is filled with idols; they bow down to the work of their hands, to what their own fingers have made." Paul's Areopagus sermon (Acts 17:29-30) draws on this exact critique: "Being then God's offspring, we ought not to think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone, an image formed by the art and imagination of man. The times of ignorance God overlooked, but now he commands all people everywhere to repent." The idol as "work of human hands" (maʿăśēh yĕḏêhem) becomes in the NT a comprehensive description of all autonomous human construction that refuses to receive the living God. Col 3:5 identifies covetousness itself as idolatry, extending the concept beyond cultic images.
2.9: "So man is humbled, and each one is brought low — do not forgive them!" The humbling of humanity (wayyiššaḥ ʾādām wayyiššāpel ʾîš) in vv.9,11,12,17 establishes the chapter's dominant theme: the eschatological reversal of all human pretension. In Christ, this reversal takes the form of the Magnificat (Luke 1:51-53: "he has scattered the proud in the thoughts of their hearts; he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate"). The refusal of forgiveness in this verse must be read canonically — it is the appropriate response to specific, unrepented covenant violations. The cross, which this same Isaiah anticipates (ch. 53), is precisely where YHWH does not invoke the unforgiveness of 2:9 but absorbs the judgment himself.
2.10: "Enter into the rock and hide in the dust from before the terror of YHWH, and from the splendor of his majesty." Revelation 6:15-17, describing the sixth seal, reproduces this imagery with striking exactness: "Then the kings of the earth and the great ones and the generals and the rich and the powerful, and everyone, slave and free, hid themselves in the caves and among the rocks of the mountains, calling to the mountains and rocks, 'Fall on us and hide us from the face of him who is seated on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb, for the great day of their wrath has come, and who can stand?'" The "wrath of the Lamb" (Rev 6:16) is the NT's most striking Christological transformation of the Day of YHWH: the Servant of Isaiah 53 who bore the wrath is also the Lamb who executes it.
2.11: "The haughty looks of man shall be brought low, and the lofty pride of men shall be humbled, and YHWH alone will be exalted in that day." The elevation of YHWH alone (wĕniśgaḇ Yhwh lĕḇaddô) is the theological claim that the NT applies to Christ: Phil 2:9-11 ("God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow"). The humbling of human pride is the form Christ's exaltation takes — the cross is both the lowest point of human humiliation of Christ and the moment of his highest glorification (John 12:23-24: "the hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified"). Jas 4:6 and 1 Pet 5:5 both cite "God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble" (Prov 3:34) — the same wisdom principle that structures Isaiah 2.
2.12: "For YHWH of hosts has a day against all that is proud and lofty, against all that is lifted up — and it shall be brought low." The "Day of YHWH of hosts" against all pride is the theological engine of eschatology. In the NT, the Day of the Lord is the day of Christ's return: 1 Thess 5:2 ("the day of the Lord will come like a thief in the night"); 2 Pet 3:10 ("the day of the Lord will come like a thief, and then the heavens will pass away with a roar"). The list of specific things brought low in vv.12-16 (cedars of Lebanon, oaks of Bashan, high mountains, lofty towers) functions in NT preaching as the comprehensive catalog of human achievement — no earthly structure, however magnificent, escapes the day of reckoning.
2.13: "Against all the cedars of Lebanon, lofty and lifted up; and against all the oaks of Bashan." The cedars of Lebanon are the great trees used for Solomon's temple (1 Kings 5:6-10) and for palace construction — they represent civilization's highest achievements. In Jesus's saying about the Jerusalem temple: "There will not be left here one stone upon another that will not be thrown down" (Matt 24:2), the great structures of religious and human achievement — even the temple — are subject to the Day of the Lord. The "lifted up" (nĕśuʾôt) terminology is the same root used in 52:13 for the exaltation of the Servant — everything human is brought down so the Servant alone can be lifted up.
2.14: "Against all the lofty mountains, and against all the uplifted hills." Mountains in biblical cosmology are the places of divine manifestation — Sinai, Zion, Carmel. The eschatological leveling of every mountain (cf. 40:4, "every mountain and hill shall be made low; and the uneven ground shall become level") is the negative form of Zion's exaltation in 2:2. In Luke 3:5, the Baptist's announcement of the coming of the Lord cites Isaiah 40:3-5, and the mountain-leveling is part of the preparation for the Messiah's coming. Judgment and restoration are two sides of the same eschatological act.
2.15: "Against every high tower, and against every fortified wall." The tower and wall represent human defensive architecture — the attempt to secure one's own life against enemy and divine judgment. Luke 14:28 uses tower-building as a parable for counting the cost of discipleship; the foolish builder fails to account for what it will take. More directly, Jesus's pronouncement against the Jerusalem walls (Matt 24:2; Luke 19:43-44: "your enemies will set up a barricade around you and surround you and hem you in on every side") is the fulfillment of the "against every fortified wall" judgment of Isaiah 2:15.
2.16: "Against all the ships of Tarshish, and against all the beautiful craft." The ships of Tarshish (probably Tartessus in southern Spain, or a generic term for large ocean-going vessels) represent the reach and wealth of international trade. In Revelation 18:17-19, the merchants and sailors who weep over fallen Babylon are depicted in terms that echo Ezekiel 27 (the Tyre lament) but also this Isaianic tradition: "all shipmasters and seafaring men, sailors and all whose trade is on the sea, stood far off and cried out as they saw the smoke of her burning." Human commercial achievement and maritime power are included in the catalog of what the Day of the Lord sweeps away.
2.17: "And the haughtiness of man shall be humbled, and the lofty pride of men shall be brought low, and YHWH alone will be exalted in that day." The repetition of v.11's refrain (YHWH alone exalted) creates the chapter's structural spine. In NT Christology, the exclusive exaltation of YHWH and the exaltation of the risen Christ are not in tension but are identical — Phil 2:9-11 presents Christ's exaltation as the form of YHWH's vindication of humble obedience. The "one name" before which every knee bows (2:11, 17) is the name of Jesus, which is also the name above every name (Phil 2:9). The monotheistic intensification ("YHWH alone") becomes the Christological concentration in the NT.
2.18: "And the idols shall utterly pass away." The abolition of idols is an eschatological event in Isaiah (cf. 17:7-8; 31:7). In the NT, Paul declares this fulfilled in principle: "we know that an idol has no real existence, and that there is no God but one" (1 Cor 8:4). Col 2:15 reads the cross as the event in which Christ "disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him" — the powers behind the idols are already defeated, though their final abolition awaits the return. 1 Cor 15:24-26 traces the sequence: Christ reigns "until he has put all his enemies under his feet" — the idols and the powers they represent are part of this eschatological abolition.
2.19: "And people shall enter the caves of the rocks and the holes of the ground, from before the terror of YHWH, and from the splendor of his majesty, when he rises to terrify the earth." The cave-hiding imagery of vv.19-21 is directly quoted in Rev 6:15-16 (see note on v.10). Luke 23:30 applies the related Hosea 10:8 ("they will say to the mountains, 'Cover us,' and to the hills, 'Fall on us'") to the coming judgment on Jerusalem: Jesus tells the daughters of Jerusalem to weep not for him but for themselves and their children. The terror of YHWH rising to terrify the earth is the posture of the returning Christ who comes "in flaming fire, inflicting vengeance on those who do not know God" (2 Thess 1:8).
2.20: "In that day mankind will cast away their idols of silver and their idols of gold, which they made for themselves to worship, to the moles and to the bats." The casting away of idols — not voluntary devotion but panic-driven abandonment — contrasts with voluntary repentance. In Acts 19:18-19, those who convert in Ephesus burn their magical books publicly; the voluntary casting away of the instruments of idolatry is the repentance that the Isaianic "that day" judgment calls for before the terror comes. The NT consistently presents the church's present repentance from idols (1 Thess 1:9: "you turned to God from idols to serve the living and true God") as the anticipatory fulfillment of this eschatological casting away.
2.21: "To enter the caverns of the rocks and the clefts of the cliffs, from before the terror of YHWH, and from the splendor of his majesty, when he rises to terrify the earth." The threefold repetition (vv.10, 19, 21) of the cave-hiding formula intensifies the inexorability of the Day. In Rev 6:17 the question "who can stand?" has a Christological answer: "Stand firm in the faith" (1 Cor 16:13); "who are you to pass judgment on the servant of another? It is before his own master that he stands or falls" (Rom 14:4). Those who stand on the Day of the Lord are those who stand in Christ, rooted in the one who himself "rose to terrify the earth" in judgment and rose from the earth in resurrection.
2.22: "Stop regarding man in whose nostrils is breath, for of what account is he?" The concluding verse grounds the entire chapter's anti-pride argument in creatureliness: nišmāt ʾap (breath of the nostril) is the same phrase used in Gen 2:7 when YHWH breathes into Adam's nostrils — the human creature receives life as gift, not as possession. Paul's anti-boasting theology rests on this: "no flesh shall boast before God" (1 Cor 1:29); "what do you have that you did not receive? If then you received it, why do you boast as if you did not receive it?" (1 Cor 4:7). James 4:13-14 applies this directly to human planning: "what is your life? For you are a mist that appears for a little time and then vanishes." The entire chapter, in its two-part structure (eschatological pilgrimage, Day of humbling), finds its Christological resolution in the one who "though he was in the form of God, did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself" (Phil 2:6-7) — the one human who correctly understood that breath is received, not owned, and therefore became the source of life for all.

1.1: Isaiah (Yesha'yahu — YHWH saves) bears a name that is the Hebrew equivalent of Jesus (Yeshua). The book that opens with this prophet's name opens with the same salvific declaration that the angel gave Joseph: you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins (Matt 1:21). Hebrews 1:1-2 frames the entire prophetic tradition as God's speech that reaches its culmination in his Son — Isaiah's vision is one of those earlier, partial words now eclipsed by the final Word.
1.2: YHWH's lament — I reared sons and brought them up, yet they have rebelled — establishes the pattern of divine adoption met by human rebellion that reaches its climax in the Parable of the Tenants (Matt 21:33-43). Christ is the one Son who was not rebellious — the perfectly obedient Son (Phil 2:8; Heb 5:8: he learned obedience through what he suffered). His obedience is the answer to the generations of filial rebellion Isaiah catalogs.
1.3: The ox knows its owner and the donkey its master's feeding trough — but Israel does not know. The reversal of this ignorance is Jesus's triumphal entry on a donkey (Matt 21:1-11; fulfilling Zech 9:9): the very animal of humble service now carries the one Israel should have recognized. Those who cried Hosanna briefly acknowledged what the donkey embodied — the king who is the true master entering his city. The irony of Isaiah 1:3 is resolved when the beast recognizes what Israel continues to miss.
1.4: They have despised the Holy One of Israel — the title YHWH's holiness is applied to Jesus by the unclean spirit's testimony (the Holy One of God, Mark 1:24) and by Peter's confession (we believe and have come to know that you are the Holy One of God, John 6:69). The despising the nation directs toward YHWH in Isaiah 1 is redirected toward the incarnate Holy One in his earthly ministry — he came to his own and his own did not receive him (John 1:11).
1.5: The whole head diseased, the whole heart failing — the total-body diagnosis of sin's corruption is the medical situation that requires the Great Physician. Jesus identifies himself as the physician who came not for the healthy but for the sick (Matt 9:12; Mark 2:17). The disease Isaiah identifies as universal is the universal need that drives the incarnation: he came because every head and every heart was in the condition Isaiah 1:5-6 describes.
1.6: From the sole of the foot to the top of the head not one healthy spot — only wounds, bruises, and open sores. The total physical description of the wounded body anticipates Isaiah 53:5 (by his wounds we are healed). The humanity Christ assumes and redeems is this body — comprehensively wounded, without a healthy spot. His own body receives wounds, bruises, and lacerations (53:4-5; John 19:1-3) so that the wounds of the one whom he represents become the site of healing rather than abandonment.
1.7: Your land is desolate and your cities burned — foreigners strip your countryside. Jesus foresaw and lamented this precise judgment on Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44: they will not leave one stone upon another in you; Matt 23:37-38: your house is left to you desolate). The desolation Isaiah names as consequence of covenant unfaithfulness becomes Jesus's tear-stained prediction of 70 CE — the Messianic King weeping over the city he came to save.
1.8: The daughter of Zion left like a temporary shelter in a vineyard, like a watchman's hut in a cucumber field.
