The howling creatures in the ruins of Babylon are the dark inverse of the singing trees and clapping mountains of Isa 55:12 — creation mourning the fall of what God condemned.
13.22: Hyenas will cry out in her citadels and jackals in her splendid palaces. Her time is about to come; her days will not be drawn out. The urgency — about to come, days not drawn out — creates the eschatological pressure of the whole oracle. The certainty of Babylon's fall is certain enough that it is already as good as happened: this is the prophetic perfect. In the NT, the same certainty marks the proclamations of Rev 14:8 (Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the Great!) — announced before the final judgment as if already accomplished because YHWH's word is certain.

14.1: YHWH will show compassion to Jacob and again choose Israel — the restoration follows immediately upon Babylon's judgment (ch 13). Compassion and choice are the two covenant terms that ground Israel's existence (Deut 7:6-7) and that the exile seemed to negate. The choosing-again is the key phrase: YHWH's election of Israel was not revoked by judgment, only suspended. The NT interprets Israel's hardening and future restoration in Rom 11:28-29 through exactly this structure: the gifts and calling of God are irrevocable; the same compassion that chose Israel will ultimately restore them.
14.2: Peoples will escort them home; Israel will possess foreigners as servants. The reversal of exile: those who were carried away in captivity will be escorted home by those who carried them. The former captors become servants — not in perpetual servitude but in the sense of the nations serving Israel's restoration (as Cyrus does in Isa 45). The NT applies the nations-serving-Israel pattern to the ingathering of the Gentiles into the people of God (Rom 15:27: if the Gentiles have come to share in their spiritual blessings...).
14.3: On the day YHWH gives rest from pain, suffering, and cruel servitude — the day of rest (menuchah) is the sabbath-rest of the people of God (Heb 4:9-11). The rest from servitude echoes the Exodus: YHWH remembered Israel's suffering in Egypt (Exod 3:7-8). The greater rest that remains for the people of God (Heb 4) is the Christological fulfillment: the Servant's yoke-taking (Matt 11:28-30: come to me, all who labor, and I will give you rest) is the eschatological sabbath-gift of which this verse speaks.
14.4: The taunt-song against the king of Babylon: How the tyrant has come to his end! The taunt-song (mashal) is the literary form of prophetic reversal — the powerful mocked in their fall by those they oppressed. In the NT, the fall of Rome/Babylon is lamented by merchants (Rev 18:9-19) but celebrated by heaven (Rev 18:20, 19:1-4). The contrast — earth mourning, heaven celebrating — is the eschatological division that the judgment of the beast reveals. The king of Babylon's fall is the type of the fall of the beast (Rev 19:20).
14.5: YHWH has shattered the rod of the wicked, the scepter of rulers. The rod (matteh) is the symbol of authority and power — the same rod that beat down peoples (v 6). YHWH's shattering of it is the assertion that his sovereignty supersedes all human power structures. In the Christological framework, the rod that beats down is answered by the rod of iron that the Messiah wields (Ps 2:9, Rev 2:27, 12:5, 19:15) — not the rod of oppression but the rod of righteous judgment that shatters the nations' self-constructed powers.
14.6: The rod that beat peoples in relentless blows, that kept nations under tyranny without relief. The sustained oppression of Babylon describes the condition of all who are under any coercive power. The without-relief — the refusal of rest — is the anti-sabbath character of tyranny: it works the people to exhaustion. Jesus names this as what he has come to reverse: come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest (Matt 11:28) — the rest that the tyrant's rod refused.
14.7: The whole earth rests quietly in peace; they burst into song. The peace that follows Babylon's fall is the sabbath-rest of all creation. The singing of creation when the tyrant falls echoes the cosmic singing of 55:12 — the mountains and hills break into song when the people go out in peace. In the NT, creation's liberation (Rom 8:21: creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay) is the eschatological fulfillment of this cosmic peace.
14.8: The cypress trees and cedars of Lebanon rejoice over Babylon's fall: since you have been brought low, no axeman has come against us. The trees personified as persecuted by the tyrant (who cut them for building projects) now celebrate freedom from exploitation. The personified creation's liberation is another anticipation of Rom 8:19-22: the creation waits with eager longing for the revealing of the sons of God. Creation's suffering under tyranny and creation's joy at tyrants' fall are both part of the cosmic drama of redemption.
14.9: Sheol stirs to meet the Babylonian king — the spirits of dead rulers rise to greet him. The underworld scene is one of the most vivid in the OT: all the kings he dethroned now taunt him from Sheol. The NT doctrine of Hades as the realm of the dead is built on texts like this one. The king of Babylon's descent to Sheol is the antitype of the Servant's descent: the Servant goes to the grave (53:9) but rises — he sees offspring and prolongs his days (53:10). The Babylonian king goes to Sheol and stays. The contrast is resurrection vs. permanent death.
14.10: You too have been brought to nothing — you are exactly like us. The once-mighty tyrant is leveled to the common condition of all the dead. In the NT, the equality of death — all die — is the context from which resurrection is the decisive break: death is swallowed up in victory (1 Cor 15:54-57). The resurrection of Jesus is precisely the moment when the king of kings is not leveled to the common condition of the dead but is raised as firstfruits (1 Cor 15:20) — the exact opposite of the Babylonian king's fate.
14.11: Your magnificence has been dragged to Sheol — your harp music, your bed of maggots. The luxurious pomp of empire (music, splendor) is replaced by its exact opposite (decay, maggots). The radical inversion — glory to corruption — is the pattern of fallen human pride. The NT reverses this for the Servant: what went into the grave in weakness is raised in power (1 Cor 15:43). The resurrection body is not subject to decay (Acts 2:27, citing Ps 16:10: you will not let your Holy One see decay).
14.12: How you have fallen from heaven, O Day Star, son of the Dawn! You have been hurled to the ground — you who laid nations prostrate! The Day Star (helel ben shachar — Lucifer in Latin) is the morning star / Venus, used as a symbol for the king of Babylon's pretension to divine status. This verse became the central OT text for the tradition of Satan's fall from heaven (Luke 10:18: I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven; Rev 12:9: the great dragon was hurled down). The Babylonian king's pride is the human form of the angelic rebellion against YHWH — both are cast down from their presumed divine position.
14.13: For you said in your heart: I will climb to heaven; I will set my throne higher than the stars of God; I will sit on the mount of assembly in the far north. The five I will statements of Babylon's king are the grammar of the fall: five claims to divine prerogatives. The mount of assembly (har moed) is the cosmic mountain of the divine council. The NT background: Satan's temptation of Jesus in Matt 4:8-9 (all these kingdoms I will give you if you bow down to me) is the same bid for cosmic sovereignty — and Jesus' refusal is the reversal of Adam's and humanity's capitulation to the same temptation.
14.14: I will rise above the clouds; I will make myself like the Most High. The climax of the pretension: like the Most High (Elyon). The desire to be like God is the original sin (Gen 3:5 — you will be like God). The king of Babylon enacts in political form what Adam enacted in the garden. In contrast, Jesus — who being in the form of God did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped (Phil 2:6) — reverses the Edenic/Babylonian pattern: he did not grasp at divine status but emptied himself. The I will make myself like God is answered by the Word who was God and became flesh (John 1:1, 14).
14.15: But you are brought down to Sheol, to the depths of the pit. The reversal of the five I wills: you will be brought down. This is the judgment on all self-exaltation: everyone who exalts himself will be humbled (Luke 14:11, 18:14). The descent to the pit is the exact opposite of the ascension the king claimed. In the NT, this pattern reaches its culmination in the beast cast into the lake of fire (Rev 19:20) — the final fall of all who set themselves against the Most High.
14.16: Those who see him scrutinize: Is this the man who made the earth shake, who rattled kingdoms? The mockery at the fallen tyrant's insignificance in death shows that all imperial power is derivative and temporary — when YHWH removes it, nothing of the tyrant remains impressive. The NT applies this to the powers and principalities: the cross made a public spectacle of them (Col 2:15 — having disarmed the rulers and authorities, he put them to open shame by triumphing over them in the cross).
14.17: Who turned the world into a wasteland and demolished cities; who never released his prisoners to go home. The characterization of Babylon as a world-destroyer who keeps prisoners locked up is the profile of the power that Christ overcomes: he came to proclaim release to the captives (Isa 61:1 / Luke 4:18) — the exact reversal of the never-released-his-prisoners charge. The stronger man who binds the strong man and plunders his goods (Matt 12:29) is the messianic answer to this verse.
14.18: All the kings of nations lie in state, each in his own burial place. The proper burial of other kings — an honor given even to enemies — is contrasted with Babylon's shameful treatment (v 19-20). In the ancient world, proper burial was essential to dignity in death. The NT equivalent: Jesus — the innocent one condemned — is given a rich man's tomb (53:9, Matt 27:57-60), reversing the pattern; the king who falsely claimed divine status is denied even a proper grave.
14.19: But you are thrown out of your grave like a rejected branch (netser nival) — surrounded by slaughtered bodies. The rejected branch echoes and inverts the Branch of 4:2 (tsemach YHWH) and 11:1 (netser from Jesse's roots). The messianic Branch is the accepted, living shoot; the Babylonian king is the rejected, dead branch thrown out unburied. The contrast is total: both are called netser (shoot/branch) but with opposite outcomes — life vs. rejection, resurrection vs. unburied shame.
14.20: You will never be buried with them, because you destroyed your own country and murdered your own people. The ironic judgment: the one who killed his own people is denied the burial rites his murdered subjects received. The seed of evildoers will never be named again — generational extinction as the final form of the covenant curse. In contrast, the Servant who was cut off will see offspring and prolong his days (53:10) — the exact reversal of never be named again.
14.21: Prepare the execution ground for his sons — so they never rise to possess the land and fill the world with cities. The sons bear the fathers' guilt in the pattern of Deut 5:9 (visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation). In the NT, the judgment of generation on those who killed the prophets culminates in the judgment on Jerusalem in 70 CE (Matt 23:34-36: all this will come on this generation). The pattern: generational guilt accumulates until YHWH's patience ends and judgment falls completely.
14.22: I will rise up against Babylon — declares YHWH of hosts — and I will cut off its name and survivors, offspring and descendants. YHWH's personal rising against Babylon mirrors the Babylonian king's five I wills (vv 13-14) — but YHWH's I will actually stands while the tyrant's is cut off. In the NT, YHWH's rising to execute judgment is the Father's vindication of the Son: the one who was cut off (53:8) is raised, while the powers that cut him off are cut off in turn.
14.23: I will make it a haunt for the hedgehog and stagnant marshes; I will sweep it with the broom of annihilation. The broom of annihilation (matate hashmad) is the most complete image of purging: everything removed, nothing remaining. Rev 18:21 (a mighty angel picked up a boulder the size of a large millstone and threw it into the sea, saying: with such violence the great city Babylon will be thrown down) is the NT escalation of this image — catastrophic, irreversible, complete.
14.24: YHWH of hosts has sworn: Exactly as I have planned, so it will happen; exactly as I have purposed, so it will stand. The divine oath on the immutability of the divine plan grounds every subsequent oracle. Heb 6:17-18 applies this to the two immutable things (oath and promise) by which God confirmed his purposes — making it impossible for him to lie. The certainty of Babylon's fall is the same certainty as the certainty of Christ's triumph: YHWH's purpose is the foundation.
14.25: I will crush the Assyrian in my own land; I will trample him on my mountains. The oracle pivots from Babylon (vv 1-23) to Assyria — the immediate threat in Isaiah's time. YHWH's personal crushing in my own land signals that the holy land is YHWH's territory and cannot be permanently occupied by any foreign power. The yoke lifted from my people (v 25b) is the specific promise Jesus cites at Matt 11:28-30 — the yoke of Assyrian oppression is the type of the yoke of sin and death from which the Servant delivers his people.
14.26: This is the plan purposed for the whole earth, and this is the hand stretched out over all the nations. The plan (haetsah) of YHWH is singular and universal: one divine purpose governs all of world history. The stretched-out hand is the arm of YHWH that appears throughout Isaiah as the agent of salvation and judgment (51:5, 52:10, 53:1). In the NT, the same sovereign plan — pre-determined by God (Acts 2:23, 4:28: Jesus was handed over by God's deliberate plan) — governs even the cross: the apparent chaos of the passion is the outworking of the plan purposed for the whole earth.
14.27: YHWH of hosts has made this purpose, and who can overturn it? His hand is extended, and who can force it back? The rhetorical questions assert the absolute sovereignty of the divine will. Rom 8:31 applies the same logic: if God is for us, who can be against us? The irresistibility of YHWH's purpose is the ground of the believer's confidence — not in human strength but in the one whose outstretched hand no one can force back.
14.28: In the year King Ahaz died — the historical anchor for the Philistia oracle (vv 29-32). The dating to Ahaz's death links this oracle to the Immanuel-Emmanuel sign of chapters 7-8: Ahaz was the king who refused the sign, now his death marks a transition. The oracle that follows (vv 29-32) carries the messianic promise of 14:32 — the LORD has established Zion — into the context of the nations' question about Israel's fate.
14.29: Do not rejoice, O Philistia — because the rod that struck you is broken. For from the serpent's root a viper will come out, and its fruit will be a flying fiery serpent. The serpent's root is the Davidic line that will produce something more dangerous than the broken rod: the viper and flying fiery serpent are images of escalating threat. In the NT, the serpent/viper imagery is applied both to the enemies of God's people (Matt 3:7, 23:33: you brood of vipers!) and to the Serpent of Genesis whose head is crushed by the Seed of the woman (Gen 3:15, Rev 12:9). The flying fiery serpent becomes, in typological trajectory, the bronze serpent (Num 21:8-9) that Jesus applies to himself: just as Moses lifted up the serpent, so the Son of Man must be lifted up (John 3:14).
14.30: The poorest of the poor will have pasture and the needy will rest in safety. The poor and needy are the specific recipients of the messianic program: the Servant comes to bring good news to the poor (61:1 / Luke 4:18). Here, the fall of the threatening power (Philistia) is immediately connected to the flourishing of the poorest — YHWH's defense of the powerless is the positive side of his judgment on the powerful.
14.31: Wail, O gate! Cry out, O city! For a column of smoke rises from the north and there is no straggler in his ranks. The unstoppable army from the north — disciplined, with no stragglers — is YHWH's instrument against Philistia. The gates (places of civic life and commerce) wail because they will be the first breached. In the NT, the certainty of judgment on all who resist YHWH is the same: the gates of Hades will not prevail against the church (Matt 16:18) — but the gates of the world's cities are not guaranteed the same immunity.
14.32: What answer will be given to the envoys of the nations? The LORD has established Zion, and in her the poor of his people will take refuge. The closing verse is the theological heart: YHWH has established Zion — this is the answer to all geopolitical questions. Whatever the nations ask about Israel's fate, the answer is: Zion is YHWH's city and her poorest residents are under his protection. Jesus quotes this structure when he declares that the kingdom of heaven belongs to the poor in spirit (Matt 5:3) — the anawim of Zion are the first inheritors of the eschatological Zion. The established Zion of 14:32 is the new Jerusalem of Rev 21:2 — the city that comes down from heaven as the permanent dwelling of YHWH with his people.

15.1: The oracle concerning Moab: Ar and Kir, the two major cities, are destroyed in a single night. The speed — a single night — echoes the night of YHWH's judgments in Exodus (the angel of death at Passover) and the night fall of Babylon (Dan 5:30). Moab was Israel's neighbor and traditional enemy (Gen 19:37), yet also the homeland of Ruth — the Gentile ancestor of David. The ambiguity of Moab (enemy and ancestor of the messianic line) is part of the prophetic tension: even Moab's fall evokes sorrow (vv 5, 16:11).
15.2: The people of Moab go up to their temples and high places to weep, with shaved heads and cut-off beards — the bodily markers of extreme mourning in the ancient world. Their grief is religious: they seek their gods in the crisis. In the NT, the contrast is the mourning that leads to comfort: blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted (Matt 5:4) — but this is mourning for sin before YHWH, not religious mourning directed toward false gods. Moab's mourning is directed to the wrong address.
15.3: In the streets they wear sackcloth; on rooftops and squares everyone mourns and weeps uncontrollably. The public, comprehensive grief of Moab is genuine — Isaiah does not mock it but records it with sympathy (v 5: my heart cries out for Moab). The prophetic empathy for the nation under judgment anticipates the NT posture of mourning with those who mourn (Rom 12:15). YHWH's judgment is real and severe; the grief it produces is also real. The compassion of the prophet for the judged nation is a small image of the divine compassion that will ultimately extend even to Moab (Ruth's lineage reaching David and the Messiah).
15.4: Heshbon and Elealeh raise their cry — even Moab's soldiers shudder. The sound of lamentation spreads geographically through Moab's territory: city after city is engulfed. The soldiers' shuddering signals total military collapse — the army that might defend Moab is as demoralized as the civilian population. In the Christological reading, this comprehensive grief-spreading is the dark image of what judgment day looks like when it arrives before the kingdom is secured through the Servant's work.
15.5: My heart cries out for Moab. The speaker — Isaiah, and perhaps YHWH through him — mourns for the enemy nation. The divine mourning over judgment is a consistent thread in prophetic literature (Jer 48:31-36 expands this lament for Moab; Ezek 33:11: I take no pleasure in the death of the wicked). Jesus expresses the same grief over Jerusalem: how often I wanted to gather your children together, but you were unwilling (Matt 23:37). The God who judges is the same God who mourns the necessity of judgment — both are expressions of his holiness and love.
15.6: The pools of Nimrim have dried up — grass withered, new growth gone, nothing green. The drying up of water sources is the ecological sign of YHWH's withdrawal of blessing (cf. Amos 4:7-8). The greening of a land depended on its water sources; the drying up of Nimrim signals that the land itself is dying along with the civilization. In the Christological framework, the rivers of living water that the Messiah provides (John 7:38, Isa 44:3) are the positive reversal of this ecological death — the Servant brings the waters of life where judgment has dried up every spring.
15.7: Whatever they have accumulated and saved they carry across the Brook of the Willows. The refugees of Moab flee with their accumulated wealth across the border brook — carrying what they can before it is taken from them. The brook becomes the liminal boundary between the old life (destroyed Moab) and the uncertain future. In the NT, the possessions-carried-into-exile motif contrasts with the treasure stored in heaven that moth and rust cannot destroy (Matt 6:19-20) — the wealth the refugee truly needs is the one that cannot be taken at the border.
15.8: The sound of weeping has encircled the whole border of Moab — the wailing reaches even Eglaim and Beer-elim. The lamentation is geographically comprehensive: no part of Moab escapes it. The encircling grief mirrors the encircling army — YHWH's judgment is as complete in its mourning as it is in its military form. In the NT, the weeping and gnashing of teeth (Matt 8:12, 22:13, 25:30) that marks the outer darkness is the eschatological form of this comprehensive mourning: those outside the kingdom will mourn the loss of what they could have had.
15.9: For the waters of Dimon run red with blood; and I will bring more upon Dimon — a lion against the fugitives of Moab and against what remains in the land. The blood in the waters echoes the first plague of Egypt (Exod 7:17-21) — YHWH's judgment on Egypt is now applied to Moab. The lion against the fugitives is the predator that hunts down those who flee the military disaster: there is no safe exit. In the broader Isaianic trajectory, the lion that destroys is answered by the lion and lamb lying down together (11:6, 65:25) — the messianic peace transforms the predator. The eschatological reversal of this lion-against-fugitives is the vision of universal safety in the new creation.

16.1: A type: Moab is called to send the tribute-lamb (seh mōšēl 'āreṣ — the lamb of the ruler of the land) to the mount of the daughter of Zion. The lamb as tribute to the Davidic king in Zion anticipates Christ, who is simultaneously the Lamb (Rev 5:6) and the Davidic King who rules from Zion. What Moab is called to send becomes the very thing YHWH provides — his own lamb (Gen 22:8; John 1:29). The nations bringing tribute to the enthroned King is a recurring eschatological theme (Ps 72:10; Isa 60:6).
16.2: A revelation of God: Moab in flight like a scattered flock — the instability and vulnerability of nations that refuse to submit to Zion's ruler. The scattered-bird image is the anti-type of the gathering promised in Isaiah 11:12; those who gather to the messianic banner find security, while those who flee from it find only rootlessness. This verse establishes the necessity of the political submission that vv. 4-5 will define in messianic terms.
16.3: A theme: Zion is called to give shade and shelter to the fugitive — hābî'î 'ēṣāh 'ăśî mišpāṭ — give counsel, grant justice, make your shade like night at noon. The shelter of Zion from the destroyer is a messianic function: Christ is the one who shelters the fugitive (Heb 6:18 — we who flee to him for refuge). The shade at midday in the burning heat is an image of divine protection (Ps 121:6; Rev 7:16: the sun will not strike them).
16.4: A theme: the outcasts of Moab sheltered in Zion from the destroyer. The word nādîm (outcasts, the driven-away) are received, not expelled. This is the grace that runs counter to the ancient enmity between Israel and Moab — the same grace that the book of Ruth narratively demonstrates and that Paul proclaims: in Christ there is neither Jew nor Gentile, no outcast category that excludes from refuge (Rom 10:12-13). The condition given in v. 4b: this will be possible once the oppressor is removed.
16.5: A direct prophecy. When the oppressor vanishes, wĕhûkān bĕḥèsed kissē' — a throne shall be established in steadfast love — and on it shall sit in faithfulness in the tent of David (bĕ'ōhel dāwid) one who judges and seeks justice and is swift in righteousness. This is the messianic core embedded in the Moab oracle: the Davidic king whose throne is constituted by ḥesed (covenant love) and 'emet (faithfulness), who actively seeks justice. Luke 1:32-33 (throne of David; kingdom without end) and Revelation 4:2 (the established throne) both draw on this tradition. Christ is the king whose ḥesed-throne is the ground of all shelter for the outcast.
16.6: A revelation of God: Moab's pride (gē'āh... gā'ôn... gĕ'ûtô... 'ebrātô — the fourfold pride) is the ground of his destruction. The anti-type of the humble messianic King of v. 5 (who sits not in pride but in ḥesed) is Moab's boastful arrogance. This contrast — between the enthroned King of ḥesed and the proud nation that refuses him — runs through the NT's theology of the cross: God chose what is foolish and weak to shame the proud (1 Cor 1:27-28).
16.7: A revelation of God: Moab must wail for itself. The self-referential lament (lāk̠ēn yĕyêlîl mō'āb) describes a judgment that the nation brings upon itself — the internal logic of divine justice rendering to each what they have chosen. The NT framing: those who choose pride over the ḥesed-throne face the same trajectory — divine justice that confirms the choice already made.
16.8: A revelation of God: the vine of Sibmah and the fields of Heshbon languish — agricultural collapse as the concrete sign of covenant judgment. This verse does not directly anticipate Christ, but establishes the creation-theology context: sin destroys the fruitfulness YHWH intended, while Christ's redemption restores it (Rom 8:19-22; the vineyard parable of John 15 frames Christ himself as the true vine that produces what Moab's vines cannot).
16.9: A theme: the prophet weeps with the weeping of Jazer over Moab's destroyed vine — 'al-kēn 'eb̠keh bĕb̠ekî ya'zēr gèpen śib̠māh. This is divine solidarity with human grief, even grief over an enemy nation's judgment. Jesus wept over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41) in precisely the same pattern: grief for those facing a judgment they have brought on themselves. The God of Israel mourns what he must judge — a tension resolved at the cross where Christ bears the judgment he mourns.
16.10: A revelation of God: joy and gladness removed from the fruitful field — the vintage silenced, the treaders no longer treading with a shout. This removal of harvest joy is the covenant curse (Lev 26:20; Deut 28:30-40). Christ's work reverses this curse: he is the one who trampled the winepress alone (Isa 63:3; Rev 19:15), so that the covenant curse is absorbed and the harvest joy of the new creation can resume.
16.11: A theme: the divine inward parts (mê'ay — entrails, the seat of deep emotion) moan like a lyre for Moab. This is the most explicit statement in the Moab oracle of YHWH's personal grief over the enemy nation he must judge. Christ embodies this grief — he looked over Jerusalem and wept (Luke 19:41), he was moved with compassion for the crowds (Matt 9:36), he prays for those who crucify him (Luke 23:34). The weeping God of Isaiah 16 is fully present in the weeping Son of God.
16.12: A revelation of God: Moab wearies itself at the high place, comes to his sanctuary to pray, but does not prevail (wĕlō' yûk̠āl). False worship directed toward false gods — or toward YHWH through illicit means — cannot prevail. Christ as the one mediator (1 Tim 2:5) is the counterpart: through him alone do prayers prevail (John 16:23-24; Heb 4:16). Moab's futile sanctuary-approach points to the need for the true high priest who enters the true sanctuary (Heb 9:24).
16.13: A revelation of God: the oracle concerning Moab was spoken in the past — YHWH's word has a track record of fulfillment, and the prophetic word spoken here now has its own trajectory of completion. The reliability of prophetic word establishes the ground for trusting the messianic prophecies: the same God who precisely announced Moab's judgment announces the Davidic throne of ḥesed (v. 5) with equal certainty.
16.14: A revelation of God: in three years, like the years of a hired worker, Moab's glory will be contemptible and its remnant tiny and feeble. The divine time-constraint on judgment — three years — shows that YHWH's patience is not indefinite; judgment comes on the appointed schedule. This establishes the theological framework for Christ's in the fullness of time (Gal 4:4) — God operates on a precise temporal calendar, and the messianic throne of v. 5 arrives on that same schedule.

17.1: The oracle concerning Damascus — Damascus is about to cease being a city; it will become a pile of rubble. The judgment oracle against the region's dominant city-state belongs to Isaiah's panoramic survey of nations that frames YHWH's universal sovereignty. Christ as the eschatological judge of all nations (Matt 25:31-32; Rev 19:11-16) fulfills the pattern of divine judgment on proud cities. Damascus's fate also points toward the radical moment on the road there (Acts 9:3) — Saul's encounter with the risen Christ, a judgment-and-restoration event in miniature.
17.2: The towns of Aroer are abandoned — left for flocks to graze, with no one to disturb them. The pastoral image of abandoned human settlements reverting to sheep pasture is the inverse of the Edenic-restoration Christ brings. Desolation (the consequence of judgment) and Eden (the goal of redemption) share the same landscape — wilderness becomes either desolation without YHWH or garden with him. Christ as the Good Shepherd (John 10:11-16) leads his flock through the very terrain that human ambition has abandoned.
17.3: The fortified city will disappear from Ephraim and what remains of Syria will share the fate of the diminished glory (kavod) of the Israelites. The fading glory of Israel in the eighth century BCE is the shadow of the divine glory that YHWH's own presence was meant to be among them. John 1:14 declares that the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld his glory — the glory of the only Son. The glory that Ephraim and Syria lose is concentrated and restored in the incarnation of the one who is YHWH's own radiance (Heb 1:3).
17.4: In that day the splendor of Jacob will fade and his well-nourished frame will waste away. The wasting of Jacob's frame under divine judgment anticipates the Servant's description in Isaiah 52:14 and 53:2-4: appearance marred beyond human semblance, no beauty that would attract attention. The covenant nation's wasting away is taken into the Servant's body — Christ assumes the wasted, diminished form of covenant Israel in the incarnation and passion, so that through his wasting their wasting may be reversed (2 Cor 8:9).
17.5: It will be like a reaper sweeping in grain, gathering up the ears with his arm — like someone gleaning in the Valley of Rephaim. The harvest/gleaning imagery runs directly into Jesus's parables: Matthew 13:30 (gather the weeds first, then the wheat; the harvest is the end of the age). The gleaning in the Valley of Rephaim also connects to Ruth's gleaning in Boaz's fields (Ruth 2:2-3) — the foreign woman gathering what is left behind, who becomes through Boaz an ancestor of David and Christ (Matt 1:5). What appears as remnant-poverty is the seed of Messianic lineage.
17.6: Only gleanings will remain — two or three olives at the top of the highest branch, four or five on the outlying boughs. The surviving olives on the highest branch beyond the beaters' reach are the preserved remnant. Paul uses the olive-tree image in Romans 11:17-24 to describe the relationship of Jewish and Gentile believers to the covenant community: some natural branches broken off, wild branches grafted in. The few olives at the top of the highest branch are those preserved by divine grace, the remnant that receives and proclaims Christ.
17.7: In that day people will look to their Creator, and their eyes will be fixed on the Holy One of Israel. The turning from idols toward the Holy One of Israel is conversion language — and the Holy One of Israel is the title the NT applies to Jesus (Mark 1:24: the unclean spirit calls him the Holy One of God; John 6:69: Peter's confession). More specifically, Jesus draws on the parallel in Numbers 21:9 — the bronze serpent lifted up for all to look and live: as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up, that whoever believes in him may have eternal life (John 3:14-15). Looking to the Holy One finds its fulfillment in looking to the crucified Christ.
17.8: He will have no regard for the altars he made, or for the Asherah poles or incense altars that his own hands fashioned. The turning away from human-crafted religious systems and toward YHWH alone is the pattern the entire book of Hebrews develops: the old covenant's altars, priests, and sacrificial apparatus are superseded by Christ's once-for-all offering (Heb 10:1-14). The worshiper who previously made his own altars (human-initiated religious effort) now looks entirely to the Holy One — to the one offering that is not of human manufacture.
17.9: Their fortified cities will be like abandoned woodland shrines and deserted hilltops — utter desolation. The desolation of human security structures (fortified cities, Asherah shrines) anticipates Jesus's prediction of Jerusalem's coming desolation (Matt 23:38: your house is left to you desolate; 24:2: not one stone here will be left on another). The same judgment pattern — human security systems that replaced covenant faithfulness are abandoned — applies across Israel's history through to the final judgment on the religious-political structures that rejected Christ.
17.10: For you have forgotten the God who saves you and have not remembered the Rock who shelters you. The Rock title for YHWH is applied to Christ in the NT: the spiritual rock that accompanied them was Christ (1 Cor 10:4). Paul identifies the wilderness Rock of Exodus 17 as Christ himself — present throughout Israel's history as the one they kept forgetting. Isaiah 17:10's forgotten the Rock is the endemic covenant failure that makes the incarnation necessary: when Israel has forgotten the Rock for the last time, the Rock takes on flesh and becomes impossible to overlook (John 1:14).
17.11: Though you force them to sprout the very day you plant them — yet come the day of grief and desperate pain, the harvest will be gone. The forced, artificial growth that disappears at the first crisis maps directly onto Jesus's parable of the sower (Matt 13:5-6, 20-21): the seed on rocky soil springs up immediately but has no root and withers when trouble comes. Isaiah 17:11's ornamental gardens — planted for show, forced to sprout on demand — are the rocky-soil response to the word of the kingdom. Only the seed sown in good soil (deep covenant reception) produces lasting harvest.
17.12: Woe — the uproar of many peoples! They roar like the roaring of the seas! The nations' tumult figured as chaos-sea connects directly to Christ's authority over sea and storm. Mark 4:37-39 records Jesus rebuking the storm on Galilee (Peace! Be still!) — an exercise of the same divine authority that YHWH exercises over the roaring nations in verse 13. The one who silences the storm is the one who silences the uproar of the peoples (Rev 7:9 — the great multitude from all nations, no longer roaring but worshiping).
17.13: The nations surge like surging water — but he rebukes them and they scatter far away, driven like chaff before the wind. YHWH's rebuke (ga'ar) of the surging nations is the authority Christ exercises over demonic powers — the same verb pattern. Mark 1:25 uses the rebuke-formula (Be quiet! Come out!) when Jesus silences the unclean spirit, demonstrating the same authority over what surges against the covenant community. Paul declares that at the name of Jesus every knee shall bow (Phil 2:10) — the scattering of the nations in their pride before the sovereign rebuke.
17.14: Evening comes — sudden terror. Before morning, there is nothing left. This is the destiny of those who plunder us. The evening-terror / morning-destruction pattern is the narrative structure of the angel's destruction of Sennacherib's army (Isa 37:36: 185,000 dead in a single night). The same temporal structure governs the Passion and Resurrection: Friday's crucifixion brings sudden terror and apparent total defeat; by Sunday morning the enemy (death and sin) has been destroyed and nothing remains of its hold on the Messiah. Evening terror — morning triumph. The pattern of Isaiah 17:14 is the pattern of the cross and empty tomb.

18.1: A revelation of God: the oracle opens to the land of Cush (Ethiopia) — the most distant, feared people at the edges of the known world. YHWH's prophetic word reaches even there, establishing his universal sovereignty. This oracle prepares the ground for v. 7: the same distant people who are addressed in judgment become those who bring gifts to Zion. The God who speaks to the margins of the earth is the God who gathers those same margins to himself.
18.2: A revelation of God: swift messengers sent to a people tall and smooth, feared near and far (gôy yerā' ûmôrā' — a nation dreaded and awe-inspiring). YHWH's messengers go to every nation — not only Israel. The divine word is not geographically bounded. This anticipates the missionary sending of Acts 1:8 (to the ends of the earth) and the specific inclusion of Ethiopia in the early church (Acts 8:26-39: the Ethiopian eunuch).
18.3: A shadow: kol-yōšĕb̠ê tēb̠ēl wĕšōkĕnê 'āreṣ — all inhabitants of the world, all who dwell on the earth — when the signal (nēs) is lifted on the mountains, look! The banner raised on the mountain is the same nēs of Isaiah 11:10,12 — the messianic standard around which all nations gather. Christ identifies himself as the fulfillment of this signal in John 12:32: when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself. The elevation of the cross is the raising of the banner that summons all the earth.
18.4: A revelation of God: YHWH will be still and look from his dwelling — kĕḥōm ṣaḥ 'ălê-'ôr — like the clear heat of sunlight, like a cloud of dew in harvest heat. Divine stillness before judgment is not inactivity but sovereign watchfulness. This is the patience of God (2 Pet 3:9: not willing that any should perish) that the NT frames as the space between Christ's ascension and return — the appointed time when the gospel goes to all nations.
18.5: A revelation of God: at the right moment YHWH cuts the growing shoot, removes the spreading branches — wĕkārat 'et-hazyalzallîm b̠ammazmerôt. The precise timing of divine judgment — when the blossom is over and the flower becomes a ripening grape — models what the NT calls the fullness of time (Gal 4:4). YHWH acts not at human impatience but at the moment of maximum effectiveness. The vine-pruning imagery connects to John 15:1-2 (the Father prunes every branch).
18.6: A revelation of God: the pruned branches left to birds of prey and to the beasts of the earth — summer and winter alike. The abandoned branches are the aftermath of opposed divine purpose. This stark image of judgment establishes the reality that YHWH's patience (v. 4) has an endpoint — the pruning will come. Christ uses similar imagery (Matt 13:40-42: the weeds gathered and burned at the harvest).
18.7: A shadow: b̠ā'ēt hahî' yûb̠al-šay lĕYHWH ṣĕb̠ā'ôt — at that time gifts will be brought to YHWH of hosts from the tall and smooth people, the feared and conquering nation, to Mount Zion. The most distant, feared, and powerful nation becomes a gift-bearer to Zion — the complete reversal of the oracle's opening. This is the eschatological ingathering of all nations (Isa 60:6-7; Ps 72:10). Matthew 2:1-12 (the Magi from the east bringing gifts) is an initial fulfillment; Revelation 21:24-26 (the kings of the earth bringing glory and honor into the new Jerusalem) is its consummation. The feared nation of Isa 18:2 and 18:7 becomes the gift-bearing nation before the enthroned King.

23.1: Wail, ships of Tarshish — for Tyre is destroyed! The oracle against Tyre, the great Phoenician commercial empire, opens Isaiah's sequence of judgments on the nations. The wailing Tarshish ships mourning the fall of their commercial anchor directly prefigure Revelation 18:17-19: every sea captain, every passenger, every sailor... stood far off and cried out... Was there ever a city like this great city? John's Babylon-mourning draws explicitly on this Tyre-oracle, and both reach their resolution in Christ's final triumph over the commercial-political systems that substitute wealth for covenant.
23.2: Be still, you people of the coastland — you whom Sidon's merchants made wealthy. The silence commanded of the coastlands before Tyre's judgment is the awe-struck stillness before divine action. Be still and know that I am God (Ps 46:10) — the same stillness that follows Christ's rebuke of the sea (Mark 4:39: Peace! Be still!). The silence before YHWH's judgment on commercial empire prefigures the silence in heaven before the Lamb breaks the seventh seal (Rev 8:1) — the hush before the eschatological consummation.
23.3: The grain of Shihor — the Nile's harvest — crossed the great waters and became her income; she was the market town of nations. Tyre as the international grain-market that fed the known world makes her the commercial center of ancient civilization. Jesus as the true bread of life (John 6:35: I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me will never hunger) replaces the commercial grain-market with himself. The market town of nations that distributed Egypt's grain is transcended by the one through whom all true nourishment flows.
23.4: Be ashamed, O Sidon — the sea's own fortress declares: I have not travailed or given birth; I have raised no young men. The barren sea-city shamed by her inability to produce life. The barren powers that cannot generate covenant community contrast with the birth announced in Isaiah 7:14 and 9:6 — the virgin who conceives, the child who is born, the one through whom life enters what could not produce it. The sea's boast of barrenness meets its answer in the incarnation: God does what the powers cannot, taking the form of the son the sea could not birth.
23.5: When the news reaches Egypt, they will writhe with anguish at what has happened to Tyre. International grief at the commercial empire's collapse mirrors Revelation 18:9-10: the kings of the earth who committed adultery with her and shared her luxury will weep and mourn over her. The concentric circles of grief — Tyre's trade partners lamenting her fall — are the NT pattern for the judgment on Babylon, which Christ's return executes. The mourning is genuine but futile; no one can reverse what YHWH has decreed.
23.6: Sail across to Tarshish — wail, you people of the coastland! Tarshish, the western extremity of the trading world, is the destination of desperate flight. Jonah's attempt to flee YHWH's commission by taking ship to Tarshish (Jonah 1:3) is the individual acting out of the same reflex — the commercial escape route from covenant obligation. Jesus cites the sign of Jonah (Matt 12:39-41) as the definitive sign for his generation: three days in the fish, three days in the tomb. The Tarshish-ship that swallowed Jonah is the same commercial-maritime world that cannot hide from YHWH's purposes.
23.7: Is this the city so ancient in its origins — whose people sailed out to settle far-off shores? Tyre's founding-city status (she planted Carthage and other colonies across the Mediterranean) made her the originating power of the western commercial world. Against all claims of human antiquity and civilizational precedence, Jesus places his own: before Abraham was, I am (John 8:58). The city whose origins are ancient (miqedem) is nonetheless subject to the one who exists before all origins — the eternal I AM who entered human time as the Messiah.
23.8: Who planned this against Tyre — the crown-bestowing city, whose traders were princes? The merchant-princes of Tyre represent the fusion of commercial and political power that constitutes worldly empire. Jesus's teaching confronts this fusion directly: it is easier for a camel to pass through a needle's eye than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God (Matt 19:24). The crown-bestowing city gives crowns to its commercial elite; the Lamb gives his followers a crown of life (Rev 2:10) purchased by an entirely different economy.
23.9: The LORD of hosts planned it — to strip the pride from all magnificence and to humiliate all the famous of the earth. YHWH's explicit purpose in Tyre's fall is the comprehensive humiliation of human magnificence. Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:51-52) announces the identical program as the inaugurating act of Christ's coming: he has scattered the proud in the imagination of their hearts; he has brought down the mighty from their thrones. The incarnation itself — God entering the world as a helpless infant — is the enactment of what this verse prophesies: pride stripped, magnificence humiliated.
23.10: Flow through your land like the Nile, O daughter of Tarshish — nothing to hold you back any longer. The release of the commercial flow after Tyre's collapse — nothing restraining it — uses the Nile-flood image of abundance. Jesus's promise in John 7:38-39 reverses the commercial imagery: from his innermost being rivers of living water will flow — and this he said about the Spirit. Where commercial rivers of grain and gold flowed through Tyre, the Spirit-river now flows from those who believe. The unrestrainable flow of commerce becomes the image for the unrestrainable flow of the Spirit.
23.11: YHWH stretched his hand over the sea and shook the kingdoms — he issued his order against Phoenicia to demolish its fortresses. The hand stretched over the sea repurposes the Exodus gesture (Exod 14:21: Moses stretched his hand and the sea parted) for commercial-empire judgment. The same divine authority over the waters that rescued Israel from Egypt now dismantles the commercial fortresses of Phoenicia. Jesus's calming of the storm on Galilee (Mark 4:39) exercises this identical sovereignty — the creator's authority over the sea that was always his to command.
23.12: Your days of celebration are finished, O crushed virgin daughter of Sidon — even in Cyprus you will have no rest. The exile who finds no rest even in the refuge city — the perpetual restlessness of the displaced. Jesus's invitation (Matt 11:28-30: Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest) is the answer to exactly this condition. The Sidonian exile fleeing from city to city with no rest available finds in Christ the rest that no commercial-maritime refuge can provide. The yoke of empire is exchanged for the easy yoke of the Messiah.
23.13: Look at the land of the Chaldeans — a people who were nothing until Assyria raised them up. The Chaldeans (Babylonians) who destroyed Tyre were themselves a people of no account until YHWH used Assyria to elevate them as his instrument. The pattern of YHWH raising the nobodies to execute his purposes against the somebodies runs through the NT: God chose the foolish to shame the wise, the weak to shame the strong, the despised and the things that are not, to bring to nothing the things that are (1 Cor 1:27-28). The Chaldeans-from-nothing are the prototype of gospel reversal.
23.14: Wail, ships of Tarshish — your fortress harbour is destroyed! The second wailing of the Tarshish fleet brackets the oracle. Revelation 18:19 echoes this directly: they threw dust on their heads and cried out, weeping and mourning: Was there ever a city like this great city? The merchant ships mourning their commercial anchor is the pre-figuration of the final mourning over Babylon-the-great, which Christ's return brings to its conclusion in Revelation 18-19's sequence of judgment and triumph.
23.15: Tyre will be forgotten for seventy years — the span of one king's lifetime. The seventy years of Tyre's oblivion runs parallel to the seventy years of Babylon's dominance over Israel (Jer 25:11-12; Dan 9:2). Daniel's meditation on these seventy years leads Gabriel to extend the framework to seventy weeks of years (Dan 9:24-27) — the prophetic timeline within which NT scholarship has understood the Messianic fulfillment. The seventy-year exile-and-restoration template is the chronological frame through which Christ's coming is interpreted.
23.16: Pick up your harp, walk the streets, O forgotten prostitute — play your sweetest tune so someone might remember you. The commercial city reasserting herself through entertainment and seduction uses the harp as the instrument of self-advertisement. Revelation 18:22 announces the end of exactly this music: the sound of harpists and musicians, of flute players and trumpeters, will never be heard in you again. The harp music that sustains Babylon's self-promotional seduction is silenced by Christ's return, replaced by the new song of the redeemed (Rev 14:2-3: the sound of harpists playing their harps before the Lamb — the redemption of music itself).
23.17: At the end of seventy years YHWH will attend to Tyre and she will return to her trading — selling herself to all the kingdoms of the world. Tyre's restoration involves a return to the same prostitute-commerce with all the kingdoms. This is precisely Revelation's description of Babylon: all the nations have drunk the maddening wine of her adulteries... the kings of the earth committed adultery with her (Rev 17:2; 18:3). The commercial-political compromise that Isaiah names as prostitution is the defining feature of every earthly system that substitutes the kingdoms-of-the-world for the kingdom of God — the same temptation Satan offered Jesus (Matt 4:8-9).
23.18: Her merchandise and earnings will be set apart as holy to the LORD — not stored or hoarded; her profit will supply those who live before the LORD with abundant food and fine clothing. The oracle's redemptive conclusion: even Tyre's commercial wealth will ultimately be consecrated to YHWH. The wealth of nations flowing into the covenant community rather than being accumulated by commercial empire anticipates Revelation 21:24-26: the kings of the earth will bring their splendor into it [the New Jerusalem]... the glory and honor of the nations will be brought into it. The final transformation of commercial wealth into holy provision is the eschatological resolution of the entire Tyre-oracle: not destruction but conversion, under Christ's sovereign rule.

5.1: The Song of the Vineyard opens with the prophet speaking on behalf of his "beloved" (dôdî) — a term of intimate affection. Jesus retells this parable in Matt 21:33-41, making explicit that the vineyard is Israel, the owner is God, and the rejected stone (21:42) is the Son. In John 15:1-5 Jesus transforms the vineyard into a vine: "I am the true vine, and my Father is the vinedresser" — where Israel failed to yield the grapes of justice (5:7), Christ is the vine that produces fruit in those who abide in him. The beloved/owner distinction in Isaiah anticipates the Trinitarian relation: the Father tends the vineyard through the Son.
5.2: "He dug it and cleared it of stones, and planted it with choice vines; he built a watchtower in the midst of it, and hewed out a wine vat in it." The preparation of the vineyard is extravagant — every provision made for fruitfulness. In Matt 21:33, Jesus reproduces the tower and vat details from this verse exactly, making the connection unmistakable. The care invested in preparation is the theological ground of the owner's subsequent reckoning: the question in v.4, "what more was there to do for my vineyard that I have not done in it?" parallels the divine complaint underlying the incarnation — God gave everything, including his Son.
5.3: "And now, O inhabitants of Jerusalem and men of Judah, judge between me and my vineyard." The appeal to the hearers to judge their own case is a rhetorical trap similar to Nathan's parable (2 Sam 12:1-7), where David condemns himself before he realizes it. In the NT, the parable of the tenants (Matt 21:40-41) uses the same device: Jesus asks "what will the owner do to those tenants?" and the chief priests answer their own condemnation ("he will put those wretches to a miserable death"). The legal form — YHWH as plaintiff, Israel as defendant — is fulfilled in the passion narrative where the roles are inverted: the true Heir is put on trial by his own tenants.
5.4: "What more was there to do for my vineyard, that I have not done in it?" The rhetorical question has no answer — every provision was made. In the light of the incarnation, the NT reads this as the question behind the sending of the Son: "last of all he sent his son, saying, 'They will respect my son.'" (Matt 21:37). When the son is killed, the question of 5:4 is answered eschatologically: nothing more was withheld, and the rejection of the Son reveals the full depth of covenant unfaithfulness. Heb 1:1-2 frames the Son as the definitive final word after all the previous provisions of prophets.
5.5: "And now I will tell you what I will do to my vineyard. I will remove its hedge, and it shall be devoured; I will break down its wall, and it shall be trampled down." The removal of divine protection is the judgment. In Luke 13:6-9, the fig-tree parable extends the vineyard's reprieve: "let it alone this year also" — but the final removal remains possible. The trampling of Jerusalem by the nations in Luke 21:24 ("Jerusalem will be trampled underfoot by the Gentiles") echoes this Isaianic trampling-down; the siege of AD 70 is the historical fulfillment of the removed hedge.
5.6: "I will make it a waste; it shall not be pruned or hoed, and briers and thorns shall grow up; I will also command the clouds that they rain no rain upon it." The cessation of rain anticipates the drought-judgment pattern of Elijah's ministry (1 Kings 17:1; Jas 5:17-18) and the two witnesses of Rev 11:6 ("they have the power to shut the sky, that no rain may fall during the days of their prophesying"). The divine command over the clouds anticipates Christ who comes "on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory" (Matt 24:30) — cloud-command is a prerogative of the divine Son.
5.7: "For the vineyard of YHWH of hosts is the house of Israel, and the men of Judah are his pleasant planting; and he looked for justice (mišpāṭ), but behold, bloodshed (mišpāḥ); for righteousness (ṣĕdāqâ), but behold, an outcry (ṣĕʿāqâ)." The wordplay seals the parable's meaning. Jesus's woe against the scribes and Pharisees (Matt 23:23) — "you have neglected the weightier matters of the law: justice and mercy and faithfulness" — is a direct application of the vineyard's failure. The church as the new vineyard (Matt 21:43: "the kingdom will be given to a people producing its fruits") must not repeat the bloodshed/outcry pattern.
5.8: "Woe to those who join house to house, who add field to field, until there is no more room, and you are made to dwell alone in the midst of the land." The first woe targets covetous accumulation. Jesus's parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21: "I will tear down my barns and build larger ones") applies the same critique. The "woe" form itself reappears in Jesus's Sermon on the Plain (Luke 6:24-26: "woe to you who are rich") and the woes against the scribes (Matt 23:13-36), making the Isaianic woe-series the structural template for Christ's prophetic denunciations.
5.9: "YHWH of hosts has sworn in my hearing: Surely many houses shall be desolate, large and beautiful houses, without inhabitant." Jesus weeps over Jerusalem: "Behold, your house is left to you desolate" (Matt 23:38; Luke 13:35) — the temple itself, the ultimate "great and beautiful house," falls under the Isaianic judgment. The divine oath (nišbaʿ Yhwh ṣĕḇāʾôt bĕʾoznāy) emphasizes the certainty of the word; Heb 6:13-17 makes YHWH's oath-taking the ground of Christian hope in the unchangeable promise.
5.10: "For ten acres of vineyard shall yield but one bath, and a homer of seed shall yield but an ephah." The agricultural curse reverses covenant blessing (Lev 26:3-5). In the NT, the seed-parable tradition (Mark 4:8: thirty, sixty, and a hundredfold yield) is the opposite pole — the kingdom of God yields a hundredfold increase where covenant-breaking yields a hundredfold reduction. Christ is the one who reverses the curse: his death is the seed that falls into the ground and produces much fruit (John 12:24).
5.11: "Woe to those who rise early in the morning, that they may run after strong drink, who tarry late into the evening as wine inflames them!" The second woe targets the culture of revelry that obliterates discernment. Jesus's warning in Luke 21:34 — "watch yourselves lest your hearts be weighed down with dissipation and drunkenness... and that day come upon you suddenly like a trap" — applies this woe to eschatological watchfulness. Paul echoes it in 1 Thess 5:6-7: "let us not sleep, as others do, but let us keep awake and be sober."
5.12: "They have lyre and harp, tambourine and flute and wine at their feasts, but they do not regard the deeds of YHWH, or see the work of his hands." The inability to "see the works of YHWH" amid abundance is the diagnostic failure. Jesus's repeated call to "have eyes to see" (Matt 13:16; Mark 8:18) addresses this blindness. The works of God that are unregarded are Jesus's own miracles — he reproaches the cities where he did his mighty works "because they did not repent" (Matt 11:20). Luke 16:19-31 (the rich man who feasted daily and missed the beggar at his gate) embodies the failure of 5:12.
5.13: "Therefore my people go into exile for lack of knowledge; their honored men go hungry, and their multitude is parched with thirst." The lack of knowledge is remedied by Christ who is the wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:30) and who taught "as one who had authority" (Matt 7:29). The hunger and thirst of the exile are reversed: "blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied" (Matt 5:6); "I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger" (John 6:35).
5.14: "Therefore Sheol has enlarged its throat and opened its mouth beyond measure, and the nobility of Jerusalem and her multitude go down." Sheol expanding to receive the condemned is reversed in the resurrection. Christ descends to Sheol (Acts 2:27, 31: Peter quotes Ps 16:10) and conquers it from within. Rev 1:18 presents the risen Christ holding "the keys of Death and Hades" — the one who entered the enlarged Sheol now controls its gates, and the gates of Hell shall not prevail against his church (Matt 16:18).
5.15: "Man is humbled, and each one is brought low, and the eyes of the haughty are brought low." The repeated humbling theme of Isaiah is expressed in the Magnificat: "he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate" (Luke 1:52). The kenosis of Christ (Phil 2:6-8: "he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death") is the ultimate example of chosen lowering — the one who could not be brought low from without lowered himself voluntarily, and was therefore exalted above all.
5.16: "But YHWH of hosts is exalted in justice, and the Holy God shows himself holy in righteousness." John 5:22-27 states that the Father has given all judgment to the Son "so that all may honor the Son, just as they honor the Father." The cross is simultaneously the judgment of sin and the exaltation of the one who bears it: "now is the judgment of this world... And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself" (John 12:31-32). The "Holy God" who shows himself holy in judgment is the same Holy One whose holiness is mediated through the Servant.
5.17: "Then shall the lambs graze as in their pasture, and nomads shall eat among the ruins of the rich." John 10:1-18 applies the shepherd/sheep complex to Christ as the one who gives life to the sheep. Rev 7:17 pictures the Lamb at the center of the throne shepherding those who came out of the great tribulation to "springs of living water" — the lamb who was slain becomes the shepherd who leads. The nomads eating among the ruins is the reversal of the land-grabbers of v.8.
5.18: "Woe to those who draw iniquity with cords of falsehood, who draw sin as with cart ropes." The third woe characterizes sin as something actively dragged along — habituated transgression. John 8:34 — "everyone who practices sin is a slave to sin" — captures the bondage that the cord/rope image suggests. The "cords of falsehood" anticipates the binding of Jesus at Gethsemane (John 18:12: "they bound him") — the one who frees from sin's ropes is himself bound by those who serve sin.
5.19: "Who say: 'Let him be quick, let him speed his work that we may see it.'" The mocking demand for divine action is the voice of practical atheism. 2 Pet 3:3-4 identifies this as the eschatological mockers': "scoffers will come in the last days... saying, 'Where is the promise of his coming?'" The mockers of Isaiah 5:19 reappear beneath the cross: "he trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he desires him" (Matt 27:43) — the identical demand for immediate divine vindication, refused so that the deeper divine purpose can be accomplished.
5.20: "Woe to those who call evil good and good evil, who put darkness for light and light for darkness." The fourth woe targets moral inversion. Jesus's warnings about false prophets (Matt 7:15-23) and the Pharisees' attribution of his works to Beelzebul (Matt 12:24) — calling good works demonic — is the NT manifestation. The warning about the unforgivable sin (Matt 12:31-32) is the eschatological consequence of the Isaiah 5:20 inversion: calling the work of the Holy Spirit evil places one beyond the categories of repentance.
5.21: "Woe to those who are wise in their own eyes, and shrewd in their own sight!" The fifth woe inverts the wisdom tradition. Paul applies this directly: "Let no one deceive himself. If anyone among you thinks that he is wise in this age, let him become a fool that he may become wise. For the wisdom of this world is folly with God" (1 Cor 3:18-19). In 1:19-21, Paul argues the gospel exposes human wisdom's bankruptcy: "I will destroy the wisdom of the wise" (citing Isa 29:14). Christ crucified is the wisdom of God that makes human cleverness foolish.
5.22: "Woe to those who are heroes at drinking wine, and valiant men in mixing strong drink." The sixth woe targets false heroism — courage redirected from covenant faithfulness to competitive drinking. The Gethsemane scene inverts this: the disciples sleep while Jesus takes the cup of suffering alone (Matt 26:40). The cup of wine that the sixth-woe "heroes" master becomes the cup of the Lord's Supper (Luke 22:20) and the cup of suffering that Jesus drinks — the true heroism is receiving the cup from the Father.
5.23: "Who acquit the guilty for a bribe, and deprive the innocent of his right!" The judicial corruption condemned in the sixth woe is dramatized at the passion: Pilate, who "found no guilt" in Jesus (Luke 23:4), ultimately releases Barabbas (a guilty man) and condemns the innocent. The bribe in view is Judas's thirty pieces of silver (Matt 26:15). At the cross, Isa 53:11 is fulfilled: the servant "shall make many righteous" — the one unjustly condemned acquits the guilty, reversing the corruption described here.
5.24: "Therefore, as the tongue of fire devours the stubble... for they have rejected the Torah of YHWH of hosts, and have despised the word of the Holy One of Israel." John 12:48 — "the word that I have spoken will judge him on the last day" — presents Christ as the Torah/Word whose rejection is self-condemnation. The fire that devours stubble becomes John the Baptist's announcement: "he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire... he will burn the chaff with unquenchable fire" (Matt 3:11-12).
5.25: "Therefore the anger of YHWH was kindled against his people, and he stretched out his hand against them and struck them... For all this his anger has not turned away, and his hand is still stretched out." The "hand stretched out" refrain is a counterpoint to YHWH's hand stretched out in welcome (65:2; Rom 10:21). In the Passion, both stances are held simultaneously: the outstretched hand of welcome (Luke 15:20) and the hand of judgment are reconciled at the cross where God "did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all" (Rom 8:32).
5.26: "He will raise a signal for nations far away, and whistle for them from the ends of the earth; and behold, quickly, speedily they come!" The signal-raising to distant nations is the gathering of Gentiles — the great commission (Matt 28:18-20). Rev 14:6 presents "an angel flying directly overhead, with an eternal gospel to proclaim to those who dwell on earth, to every nation and tribe and language and people." The speed ("quickly, speedily") anticipates Christ's promised return: "Surely I am coming soon" (Rev 22:20).
5.27: "None is weary, none stumbles, none slumbers or sleeps, not a waistband is loose, not a sandal strap broken." The invading army's readiness is applied in the NT to spiritual watchfulness: "Dress yourselves for action and keep your lamps burning" (Luke 12:35); "stand therefore, having fastened on the belt of truth... and, as shoes for your feet, having put on the readiness given by the gospel of peace" (Eph 6:14-15). The soldier who never stumbles anticipates Jude 24: "to him who is able to keep you from stumbling."
5.28: "Their arrows are sharp, all their bows bent, their horses' hooves seem like flint, and their wheels like the whirlwind." The battle imagery foreshadows Revelation's war imagery: the four horsemen (Rev 6:1-8), the armies on white horses (Rev 19:14). In NT eschatology, the sharp arrows of divine judgment are overcome by the shield of faith (Eph 6:16: "with which you can extinguish all the flaming darts of the evil one").
5.29: "Their roaring is like a lion, like young lions they roar; they growl and seize their prey; they carry it off, and none can rescue." The lion imagery applies Christologically in two directions. As judge, Christ is "the Lion of the tribe of Judah" (Rev 5:5). As protector, John 10:28-29: "no one will snatch them out of my hand... no one is able to snatch them out of the Father's hand." The prey that "none can rescue" from the invader becomes the sheep that "none can snatch" from the divine hand.
5.30: "And they will growl over it on that day, like the growling of the sea. And if one looks to the land, behold, darkness and distress; and the light is darkened by its clouds." The darkness at the end of Isaiah 5 anticipates the darkness at the crucifixion (Matt 27:45) and the Day of the Lord (Joel 2:2). Luke 21:25 uses the sea-roar and distress as signs of the Son of Man's coming — the clouds that darken in 5:30 are the same clouds on which the Son of Man returns (Matt 24:30).

6.1: "In the year that King Uzziah died I saw the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up." John 12:41 — "Isaiah said these things because he saw his glory and spoke of him" — is the NT's explicit identification: the enthroned Lord of Isaiah 6:1 is Christ. John's context makes this unmistakable: he cites 6:10 in 12:40 and immediately identifies it as speaking of Christ's glory. The "high and lifted up" (rām wĕniśśāʾ) anticipates 52:13 where the Servant "shall be high and lifted up" using the same words — the throne vision and the Servant exaltation are the same divine glory seen from two vantage points.
6.2: "Above him stood the seraphim. Each had six wings: with two he covered his face, and with two he covered his feet, and with two he flew." The seraphim of Isaiah 6:2 become the four living creatures of Rev 4:8 ("each of them with six wings, full of eyes all around and within") who encircle the throne of God and the Lamb. The seraphim's covering of faces before divine glory anticipates the NT theme of beholding glory directly: "we all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image" (2 Cor 3:18).
6.3: "Holy, holy, holy is YHWH of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory!" The trisagion is taken up in Rev 4:8: "Holy, holy, holy, is the Lord God Almighty, who was and is and is coming!" — the Revelation version adds temporal extension that applies to Christ. "The whole earth full of his glory" is fulfilled in the incarnation: "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory" (John 1:14). The seraphic proclamation is the worship of the pre-incarnate Son; John saw it and testified.
6.4: "And the foundations of the thresholds shook at the voice of him who called, and the house was filled with smoke." The threshold-shaking at the divine voice anticipates: the earthquake at the resurrection (Matt 28:2); the Pentecost event where "suddenly there came from heaven a sound like a mighty rushing wind, and it filled the entire house" (Acts 2:2); and Rev 15:8 where "the sanctuary was filled with smoke from the glory of God" — a deliberate echo of Isaiah 6:4 applied to the return of Christ's glory.
6.5: "Woe is me! For I am lost; for I am a man of unclean lips... for my eyes have seen the King, YHWH of hosts!" The creature's dissolution before the holy presence is consistently present in NT encounters with Christ: Peter's "Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord" (Luke 5:8); John falling "as though dead" before the risen Christ (Rev 1:17); Paul blinded on the Damascus road (Acts 9:3-4). The "unclean lips" healed by the coal (v.7) anticipate Christ's purification of the unclean at his touch (Mark 1:40-42).
6.6: "Then one of the seraphim flew to me, having in his hand a burning coal that he had taken with tongs from the altar." The seraph mediates purification from the altar — the altar being the place of sacrifice. Heb 9:14 ("the blood of Christ... purify our conscience from dead works") continues this altar-to-purification logic. The flight of the seraph with the coal connects to the proclamation of the gospel by those sent (Rom 10:15, "how beautiful are the feet of those who preach the good news").
6.7: "He touched my mouth and said: 'Behold, this has touched your lips; your guilt is taken away, and your sin atoned for.'" The touch that removes guilt and atones is the purification that precedes commission. In the NT, forgiveness precedes and grounds mission: "your sins are forgiven... go in peace" (Luke 7:48-50). Jesus touches the untouchable (lepers, the dead) and pronounces forgiveness and healing — the coal-touch of Isaiah 6:7 becomes the healing touch of Christ. The atonement vocabulary (kuppār) connects to the priestly Christology of Heb 9:26.
6.8: "And I heard the voice of the Lord saying, 'Whom shall I send, and who will go for us?' Then I said, 'Here I am! Send me.'" The divine council's "us" and the prophet's commission anticipate the Trinitarian sending. John 17:18 — "as you sent me into the world, so I have sent them into the world" — continues the pattern: the Father sends the Son (John 20:21), the Son sends the Spirit (John 15:26). Christ's "Behold, I have come to do your will, O God" (Heb 10:7, citing Ps 40:7-8) is the full embodiment of the prophetic "Here I am."
6.9: "Go, and say to this people: 'Keep on hearing, but do not understand; keep on seeing, but do not perceive.'" This is the most frequently cited OT text in the NT: Matt 13:14-15; Mark 4:12; Luke 8:10; John 12:40; Acts 28:26-27; Rom 11:8. The programmatic hardening commission begins with the Servant's own ministry — the parables function to hide meaning from those who refuse to see. The hardening is judicial: persistent rejection of light results in inability to perceive it. John 12:40 adds: "Isaiah said these things because he saw his glory and spoke of him" — the one who hardens is Christ himself, as he was the Lord in 6:1.
6.10: "Make the heart of this people dull, and their ears heavy, and blind their eyes; lest they see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their hearts, and turn and be healed." John 12:40 quotes this verse and adds: "Isaiah said these things because he saw his glory and spoke of him" (12:41) — making the hardening the judgment of the pre-incarnate Christ. The healing that hardening prevents is the healing Jesus offers throughout his ministry. Matt 13:15 ends the citation with the positive: "lest they turn, and I would heal them" — the healing remains possible for those who turn; the hardening terminal only for final refusal.
6.11: "Then I said, 'How long, O Lord?' And he said: 'Until cities lie waste without inhabitant.'" The "how long?" question is the eschatological lament. In Rev 6:10, the souls under the altar cry "how long, O Lord, holy and true, before you will judge and avenge our blood?" — the prophetic "how long?" of Isaiah 6:11 becomes the martyrs' cry. Christ's own "how long am I to bear with you?" (Matt 17:17) echoes the divine patience. The answer in both Isaiah and Revelation: until the full measure of judgment is accomplished.
6.12: "And YHWH removes people far away, and the forsaken places are many in the midst of the land." The scattering judgment is the reverse of the promised regathering. In NT terms: "I have other sheep that are not of this fold. I must bring them also" (John 10:16). The "forsaken places" become the sites of the new community — Acts 8:4, "now those who were scattered went about preaching the word," turns the dispersion into mission, fulfilling the gathering of the scattered that Isaiah promised.
6.13: "And though a tenth remain in it, it will be burned again, like a terebinth or an oak, whose stump remains when it is felled. The holy seed is its stump." The holy seed (zeraʿ qōdeš) is the remnant Paul addresses in Rom 11:5: "at the present time there is a remnant, chosen by grace." The stump also anticipates the Servant: 11:1 describes "a shoot from the stump of Jesse" — the Messiah from the apparently dead royal line. The cutting-down and sprouting is the death-and-resurrection pattern: the holy seed that falls into the ground and dies bears much fruit (John 12:24).

8.1: "Then YHWH said to me, 'Take a large tablet and write on it in common characters, 'Maher-shalal-hash-baz.'" The writing of prophetic judgment on a large public tablet is an enacted oracle. Rev 1:11 commissions John to "write what you see in a book" — the same writing-as-public-witness function. The name Maher-shalal-hash-baz is the judgment counterpart to the salvation of "Immanuel" in 7:14; both are enacted in child-naming. Jesus's name (Matt 1:21) is the ultimate prophetic naming — the word of God compressed into a personal identity.
8.2: "And I will get reliable witnesses, Uriah the priest and Zechariah the son of Jeberechiah, to attest for me." The two witnesses required by Torah (Deut 17:6; 19:15) are the legal foundation for the prophecy's credibility. Jesus cites the two-witness principle in John 8:17-18: "I am the one who bears witness about myself, and the Father who sent me bears witness about me." Revelation's two witnesses (Rev 11:3-4) carry the same prophetic authentication function. At the passion, the false witnesses are "two" (Matt 26:60) — a perverse inversion of the Mosaic requirement against the innocent.
8.3: "And I went to the prophetess, and she conceived and bore a son. Then YHWH said to me, 'Call his name Maher-shalal-hash-baz.'" The prophetic family as a living enacted parable foreshadows the holy family of the NT. The incarnation is itself the ultimate enacted parable: the eternal Word takes flesh in a human family (Luke 2:1-7). Isaiah's family of sign-children (Shear-jashub, Maher-shalal-hash-baz) is the OT precursor to the incarnate Immanuel who is the sign of all signs.
8.4: "For before the boy knows how to cry 'My father' or 'My mother,' the wealth of Damascus and the spoil of Samaria will be carried away before the king of Assyria." The sign's historical fulfillment (Assyrian conquest of Damascus, 732 BCE) establishes the prophetic word's reliability. Luke 1:1-4 opens with the same concern for historical reliability. The historical grounding of Isaiah's near signs grounds the credibility of the far signs about Immanuel — if this came true in verifiable history, so will the rest.
8.5: YHWH spoke to Isaiah again, adding to the oracle. The cumulative speaking — oracle upon oracle — is the prophetic patience that Heb 1:1 describes: "Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets." The "many times and many ways" is the narrative of Isaiah 7-8 in miniature. "But in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son" — the multiplicity of prophetic words converges into the single Word made flesh.
8.6: "Because this people has refused the waters of Shiloah that flow gently, and rejoice over Rezin and the son of Remaliah." John 9:7 identifies the pool of Siloam (Siloam, translating šilōaḥ, "sent") as the site of the healing of the man born blind — Jesus sends him there to wash. The people who rejected the gentle waters of Shiloah (8:6) are the ones who reject the one who offers living water (John 4:10). The "sent" etymology connects to John's repeated use of "sent" for Jesus: "the Father who sent me."
8.7: "Therefore the Lord is bringing up against them the waters of the River, mighty and many, the king of Assyria and all his glory." The rejection of Shiloah's gentle waters brings the Euphrates flood. In NT apocalyptic, the demonic flood of Rev 12:15-16 parallels this: the dragon pours water like a river after the woman, to sweep her away — but the earth swallows the flood. Christ's mastery over the sea (Mark 4:39: "peace, be still"; Matt 14:25-26: walking on water) demonstrates sovereignty over the flood-waters that represent both judgment and hostile powers.
8.8: "And it will sweep into Judah, it will overflow and pass on, reaching even to the neck, and its outspread wings will fill the breadth of your land, O Immanuel." The flood reaching to the neck and the address "O Immanuel" is the pivot: the flood threatens the land but the land is named for Immanuel. John 16:33 — "in the world you will have tribulation, but take heart; I have overcome the world" — reproduces this logic exactly. The flood that reaches the neck of Zion is the cross that nearly swallowed the Christ — but did not.
8.9: "Be broken, you peoples, and be shattered; give ear, all you far countries; strap on your armor and be shattered." The repeated command to the nations to arm themselves and be shattered is prophetic irony. Rev 19:19-21 fulfills this: "the beast and the kings of the earth with their armies gathered to make war against him who was sitting on the horse and against his army. And the beast was captured."
