The nations gather against Immanuel and are shattered — the exact pattern of Psalm 2:2-4 ("the kings of the earth set themselves against YHWH and against his Anointed... he who sits in the heavens laughs").
8.10: "Take counsel together, but it will come to nothing; speak a word, but it will not stand, for God is with us (ʿimmānû ʾēl)." The Immanuel refrain is here explicitly rendered as a declaration: plans fail because "God is with us." Rom 8:31: "If God is for us, who can be against us?" Rom 8:38-39: "neither death nor life... nor any other created thing will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord." The ʿimmānû ʾēl is the Christological principle that no counsel against the people of Christ can prevail.
8.11: "For YHWH spoke thus to me with his strong hand upon me, and warned me not to walk in the way of this people." The divine grip on the prophet ("his strong hand upon me") is prophetic compulsion. Jesus's own "I must" language — "I must proclaim the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns also; for I was sent for this purpose" (Luke 4:43) — is the full embodiment of this compulsion. The Servant is driven by divine necessity, not by the desire for popular approval.
8.12: "Do not call conspiracy all that this people calls conspiracy, and do not fear what they fear, nor be in dread." The prophetic counter-culture: while the political class sees conspiracies and fears coalitions, the prophet is to fear YHWH alone. Jesus's "do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul" (Matt 10:28) is the NT expression. Acts 18:9-10: "do not be afraid, but go on speaking... for I am with you" — the Immanuel-presence banishes the political fears of Isaiah 8:12.
8.13: "But YHWH of hosts, him you shall honor as holy. Let him be your fear, and let him be your dread." 1 Pet 3:15 cites this verse directly: "in your hearts honor Christ the Lord as holy" (ton Christon hagiasate) — the YHWH-fear of Isaiah 8:13 is explicitly Christ-fear in Peter's application. Rev 1:17-18 — "when I saw him, I fell at his feet as though dead... 'Fear not, I am the first and the last, and the living one'" — the holy fear before the risen Christ resolves into reassurance.
8.14: "And he will become a sanctuary and a stone of offense and a rock of stumbling to both houses of Israel, a trap and a snare to the inhabitants of Jerusalem." Rom 9:33 and 1 Pet 2:8 both cite this verse as fulfilled in the rejection of Christ: "'Behold, I am laying in Zion a stone of stumbling, and a rock of offense.'" 1 Pet 2:6-8 weaves together Isaiah 28:16 (the cornerstone), Psalm 118:22 (the rejected stone becoming cornerstone), and Isaiah 8:14 (the stumbling stone) into a unified stone-theology: the same stone that is salvation's foundation is the stone that causes those who refuse it to fall.
8.15: "And many shall stumble on it. They shall fall and be broken; they shall be snared and taken." Luke 20:18 — "everyone who falls on that stone will be broken to pieces, and when it falls on anyone, it will crush him" — applies the stumbling-stone imagery to the parable of the tenants. The division between those built on the rock (Matt 7:24-25) and those who stumble on it is the Christological fork in every human destiny: the same stone that is the foundation of salvation is the stone of judgment for those who reject it.
8.16: "Bind up the testimony; seal the teaching among my disciples." The sealed testimony entrusted to disciples (limmudîm, the taught ones — same word as 50:4) is the prototype of apostolic tradition. 2 Tim 1:14 — "guard the good deposit entrusted to you, by the Holy Spirit who dwells within us" — continues this sealing-and-guarding function. Rev 5:1-5: the sealed scroll of divine counsel that only the Lion-Lamb can open. The disciples of the Servant are the bearers of the sealed testimony across time.
8.17: "I will wait for YHWH, who is hiding his face from the house of Jacob, and I will hope in him." Hebrews 2:13 explicitly quotes this verse as Christ speaking: "I will put my trust in him." The hiding of the divine face (hammastîr pānāyw) reaches its extreme expression in Jesus's cry from the cross: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matt 27:46). The Servant who waits for YHWH while his face is hidden is the Christ who maintains trust through the God-forsakenness of the cross.
8.18: "Behold, I and the children whom YHWH has given me are signs and portents in Israel from YHWH of hosts." Hebrews 2:13 quotes this verse immediately after 8:17, presenting Christ as the speaker: the Servant's sign-children are the type of Christ's "children" — those given to him by the Father (John 6:37: "all that the Father gives me will come to me"; 17:6: "the people whom you gave me out of the world"). The "children" of Isaiah 8:18 are the Church, given to the Son, who are signs of the new creation.
8.19: "And when they say to you, 'Inquire of the mediums and the necromancers who chirp and mutter,' should not a people inquire of their God? Should they inquire of the dead on behalf of the living?" The contrast between consulting the dead and inquiring of the living God is resolved definitively in the resurrection. John 11:25 — "I am the resurrection and the life; whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live" — establishes Christ as the answer to the "inquiry of the dead." Heb 7:25: "he always lives to make intercession for them" — the living one holds the keys to death (Rev 1:18).
8.20: "To the teaching and to the testimony! If they will not speak according to this word, it is because they have no dawn." The Torah and testimony as the standard — "if there is no light (šaḥar, dawn) in them." John 8:12 — "I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will not walk in darkness" — presents Christ as the dawn-light that the teaching pointed toward. 2 Pet 1:19: "you do well to pay attention to it, as to a lamp shining in a dark place, until the day dawns and the morning star rises in your hearts."
8.21: "They will pass through the land, greatly distressed and hungry. And when they are hungry, they will be enraged and will speak contemptuously against their king and their God." The hunger and rage of the judgment era — where distress produces cursing rather than repentance — contrasts with the hunger that produces trust. The crowd that follows Jesus "because they ate their fill of the loaves" (John 6:26) but turns away when the teaching becomes demanding (6:66) reproduces the turning-away of 8:21. Only the bread of life that Christ gives satisfies to eternal life (John 6:35).
8.22: "And they will look to the earth, but behold, distress and darkness, the gloom of anguish. And they will be thrust into thick darkness." The thick darkness that ends Isaiah 8 is immediately dispelled at the opening of 9:1-2 ("the people who walked in darkness have seen a great light") — the death-darkness/resurrection-light movement. Matt 4:15-16 quotes Isaiah 9:1-2 in connection with Jesus's ministry in Galilee. The darkness of 8:22 is not the final word — it is the darkness before the dawn that is Jesus, "the light that shines in the darkness, which the darkness has not overcome" (John 1:5).

19.1: YHWH rides on a swift cloud to Egypt — the theophanic cloud-chariot is the vehicle of the divine warrior (Ps 68:4, 104:3; Dan 7:13). The idols of Egypt tremble at his approach and the heart of Egypt melts. The coming-on-a-cloud image is the same vocabulary Jesus uses in the Olivet Discourse (Matt 24:30: the Son of Man coming on the clouds) and that Daniel 7:13 applies to the one like a son of man. YHWH's coming to Egypt in judgment is a type of the final coming of the Son: every idol that claims ultimate loyalty will tremble before the one who comes on the cloud.
19.2: YHWH stirs up Egyptians against Egyptians — civil war as covenant judgment. The internal collapse of a civilization is YHWH's judgment tool: he removes the social cohesion that holds a society together, and it fractures along its fault lines. The NT application: Jesus says he came not to bring peace but a sword, and that families will be divided against each other (Matt 10:34-36). The division YHWH brings is not cruelty but the inevitable consequence of rejecting the one who is the source of all true peace (shalom).
19.3: Egypt's spirit (ruach) will drain away; YHWH will confound its plans; the Egyptians will turn to idols and mediums. The spirit-draining is the withdrawal of the divine breath that sustains wisdom and governance. The flight to idols and necromancers follows: when YHWH withdraws, people fill the vacuum with occult practices. The NT pattern: the wisdom of this world is foolishness with God (1 Cor 3:19); those who suppress the truth in unrighteousness exchange the living God for images (Rom 1:21-23). Egypt's turn to mediums is the extreme form of what every society does when it rejects YHWH.
19.4: YHWH hands Egypt over to a harsh master, a cruel king. The handing-over (sakkar) is the same verb as the divine hardening that leads to judgment: YHWH gives a people over to the consequences of rejecting him (Rom 1:24, 26, 28: God gave them over). The cruel king is the form human rebellion produces when YHWH removes his restraining hand. In the Christological framework: the only alternative to the kingship of the Servant-King (9:6-7) is the tyranny of the cruel king.
19.5: The Nile will dry up. The river that was Egypt's life-source, the basis of its agriculture and economy, will be parched. The drying of Egypt's waters is the anti-creation judgment: the same God who made water teem with life (Gen 1:20-21) withdraws the water when his people are judged. In contrast, the promise to those who follow YHWH is the springs of salvation (12:3) and rivers of living water (John 7:38) — the Servant's presence reverses the parching that judgment produces.
19.6: The channels will become foul; the Nile's branches will shrink and dry up; reeds and rushes will wither. The ecological collapse of Egypt follows from the Nile's failure: everything dependent on the water-source dies. This is the prophetic image of what happens to any civilization when its spiritual foundation is removed — the surface vegetation survives briefly, then withers completely. The NT uses the same image: the branch that does not remain in the vine withers and is thrown into the fire (John 15:6).
19.7: Papyrus reeds and crops along the Nile will wither, blow away, and cease to be. The drying of the Nile produces a windswept desolation where Egypt's abundant agriculture once flourished. The contrast with the new-creation promise (35:7: the burning sand will become a pool; water will gush in the desert) marks this as the judgment-side of the Isaianic pattern: YHWH dries up when he judges, waters when he redeems.
19.8: Fishermen will mourn; those who cast hooks into the Nile and spread nets on the water will languish. Egypt's fishing economy was famous in the ancient world — the Nile's fish were a dietary staple mourned during the wilderness years (Num 11:5). The fishermen's mourning is the economic face of covenant judgment: the work of human hands fails when YHWH withdraws provision. In the NT, Jesus calls fishermen to become fishers of men (Matt 4:19) — the same economic vocabulary applied to the kingdom: the divine economy provides what the natural economy, under judgment, cannot.
19.9: Those who work with combed flax and weave fine linen will be put to shame. Egypt's linen industry was world-famous (Gen 41:42; Ezek 27:7). Its destruction is a specific economic judgment: the goods Egypt was proud of producing will be stopped. The shame is both economic and cultural: a civilization's identity is bound up in what it makes. The NT equivalent: those who build with wood, hay, and stubble will see their work burned at the judgment (1 Cor 3:12-15) — only what is built on the foundation of Christ survives.
19.10: Egypt's foundations will be crushed; all who work for hire will be sick at heart. The foundations (shototeha) — the structural supports of Egyptian civilization — are broken. The wage-worker who cannot earn is the last resort of the hierarchy, and when they despair, the whole system has collapsed. In the NT pattern: the laborers in the vineyard receive their wage from the master (Matt 20:1-16) — YHWH's economy pays justly, even unexpectedly. Egypt's wage-workers have no such master.
19.11: The princes of Zoan are utter fools; Pharaoh's wisest counselors give worthless advice. The wisdom tradition of Egypt was ancient and prestigious — yet it is confounded by YHWH's judgment. How can they claim to be wise? — the question is rhetorical: Egypt's wisdom cannot interpret what YHWH is doing. Paul applies this directly: where is the wise man? Where is the scholar? Has not God made foolish the wisdom of the world? (1 Cor 1:20). Isaiah 19:11-15 is the OT ground for Paul's theology of the cross as foolishness to the wise.
19.12: Where are your wise men now? Let them tell you what YHWH of hosts has planned against Egypt. The prophetic challenge to wisdom: if the wise men are truly wise, let them interpret the divine plan. The irony is that YHWH's plan (vv 19-25) is so unexpected — YHWH blessing Egypt as my people — that no human wisdom could have derived it. It took revelation, not reason. The NT ground is 1 Cor 2:6-10: what no eye has seen, ear heard, or mind conceived — God has revealed by his Spirit.
19.13: The princes of Zoan and Memphis have made themselves fools; the leaders of Egypt's tribes have led it astray. The leaders who should have guided the nation are themselves deluded. The leadership failure of Egypt mirrors the leadership failure of Jerusalem in chapters 1-6: when the guides are blind, the nation stumbles. Jesus applies this to the Pharisees: they are blind guides; if the blind lead the blind, both fall into the pit (Matt 15:14).
19.14: YHWH has poured into Egypt a spirit (ruach) of confusion; the Egyptians err in everything they do, like a drunkard reeling in his vomit. The divine sending of a spirit of confusion recalls the lying spirit in 1 Kgs 22:19-23 — YHWH uses spiritual confusion as a judgment instrument. The drunkard-in-vomit image is the most extreme of the confusion descriptions: complete disorientation, incapable of directed action. The NT equivalent: God sends a powerful delusion to those who refuse to love the truth (2 Thess 2:11) — the same pattern of judgment through confusional darkening.
19.15: Nothing Egypt can accomplish — neither head nor tail, neither palm branch nor reed. The head-to-tail formula (echoing 9:14-15) means from the highest to the lowest rank: every level of Egyptian society is incapacitated. The palm branch and reed symbolize height and humility, nobility and common folk. The total paralysis of Egypt by YHWH's judgment is the dark image of what the coming of the Branch (tsemach, 4:2) reverses: the Servant gives sight to the blind and freedom to the captive (61:1), restoring what YHWH's judgment removed.
19.16: In that day the Egyptians will be like frightened women, trembling because of the hand of YHWH of hosts that he is raising against them. The first in-that-day oracle (vv 16-25) begins with judgment but moves toward unprecedented blessing. The trembling of Egypt is not its final state — what begins in fear (v 16) ends in worship (vv 21-22). The same YHWH whose judgment produces trembling is the one whose salvation produces the doxology of 12:1-6. The fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom (Prov 9:10), and Egypt's trembling is the first step toward its restoration.
19.17: The land of Judah will become a source of dread to Egypt — whenever Judah is mentioned, Egypt will tremble because of what YHWH of hosts has planned against it. The reversal: Egypt, the superpower that held Israel as slaves, will fear the mention of Judah. This is the Deut 28:13 blessing: YHWH will make you the head, not the tail; you will be above, not below. In the Christological trajectory, the reversal reaches its completion when the nations stream to Zion (2:2-3) not in fear but in seeking YHWH's instruction — the same nations, but now drawn by glory rather than repelled by judgment.
19.18: In that day five cities in Egypt will speak the language of Canaan and swear loyalty to YHWH of hosts. The language of Canaan is Hebrew — the covenant tongue. Egypt's cities swearing allegiance to YHWH is the most radical reversal: the nation that enslaved Israel now adopts Israel's language and Israel's God. The NT fulfillment: at Pentecost, people from every nation hear the gospel in their own tongue (Acts 2:8-11), but all are united in calling on the name of the Lord (Joel 2:32, Acts 2:21). The five Egyptian cities are the type of the nations that will bear YHWH's name.
19.19: In that day there will be an altar to YHWH in the heart of Egypt and a standing stone at its border dedicated to YHWH. The altar in Egypt's heart is extraordinary: the place of Israel's slavery becomes a place of YHWH's worship. The altar is the intersection of human approach and divine presence — and YHWH places one in the center of the enemy empire. The NT: every place where the church gathers is an altar of the new covenant, and the church's mission (from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth, Acts 1:8) is the outworking of this oracle — taking the altar of YHWH to Egypt and beyond.
19.20: The altar will be a sign and witness to YHWH of hosts in Egypt. When they cry to YHWH because of oppressors, he will send them a savior and defender to rescue them. The savior-and-defender sent to Egypt in response to their cry is the exodus pattern applied to Egypt itself: YHWH heard Israel's cry in Egypt and sent Moses (Exod 3:7-8). Now YHWH will hear Egypt's cry in Egypt and send a savior. The one savior-and-defender who fulfills this universally is the Servant: he will not falter until he establishes justice in the earth; the islands will put their hope in his teaching (42:4).
19.21: YHWH will make himself known to Egypt, and the Egyptians will acknowledge YHWH in that day. They will worship with sacrifice and grain offering; they will make vows to YHWH and keep them. The Egyptians worshipping YHWH with proper sacrificial rites — the full covenant cultus — is the eschatological inclusion of Egypt in the covenant. The NT: Jesus commands baptizing and teaching all nations (Matt 28:19); Paul's mission in Acts includes Egypt's territory; the new Jerusalem has gates open to all nations (Rev 21:24-26). Egypt's worship of YHWH with sacrifice is fulfilled in the Messiah's sacrifice received by all peoples.
19.22: YHWH will strike Egypt — striking and healing — and they will turn back to YHWH. He will hear their pleas and heal them. The striking-and-healing dialectic is the pattern of covenant relationship: discipline that produces repentance that produces restoration. Hosea applies the same pattern to Israel (Hos 6:1: he has torn us to pieces, but he will heal us). The NT ground is Heb 12:6: the Lord disciplines the one he loves. The healing of Egypt by YHWH is the universal pattern of how YHWH redeems any people — through judgment toward repentance toward restoration.
19.23: In that day there will be a highway from Egypt to Assyria — the two great empires that bookended Israel's history, the oppressors of the south and the north — will be connected by a road of peaceful exchange. The highway reverses the roads of conquest: what was a path for invading armies becomes a route for mutual blessing. The great highway of 40:3 (prepare in the wilderness a highway for our God) is the spiritual counterpart. In the NT, the dividing wall of hostility between Jew and Gentile is abolished in Christ (Eph 2:14-16); the Egypt-Assyria highway is the type of the reconciliation of all enemies in the peace of the Messiah.
19.24: In that day Israel will be the third alongside Egypt and Assyria, a blessing in the midst of the earth. Israel — not the superpower, but the middle member of the triad — fulfills the Abrahamic commission: through you all families of the earth will be blessed (Gen 12:3). The three former enemies stand together as YHWH's community of blessing. The NT: the church as the body composed of Jew and Gentile (Eph 2:15-16) is the eschatological fulfillment of this triad — the middle wall broken down, former enemies joined in one new humanity.
19.25: Blessed be Egypt my people, and Assyria the work of my hands, and Israel my inheritance. The three titles used here — my people, the work of my hands, my inheritance — are the titles used throughout Isaiah and the OT for Israel alone. Now YHWH applies each to a different nation: Egypt receives the covenant-intimacy title (my people), Assyria receives the creation-relationship title (the work of my hands), and Israel keeps the inheritance title. The NT: the wall between Jew and Gentile, Israel and the nations, comes down in Christ; all who are in Christ are Abraham's seed and heirs according to the promise (Gal 3:29). This verse is the most universalist in Isaiah — and its fulfillment is the church gathered from every nation.

20.1: The historical anchor: the year Sargon of Assyria sent his commander-in-chief to attack Ashdod. The specific historical detail grounds the sign-act of vv 2-6 in a documented event (Sargon's campaign of 711 BCE). The prophet's body becomes the medium of revelation in the most visceral way: Isaiah's physical condition over three years is the message YHWH communicates to Israel. The NT equivalent: the incarnation itself is the ultimate sign-act in which the divine being takes on the conditions of human vulnerability to deliver a message that no verbal oracle could match.
20.2: YHWH speaks through Isaiah: go, remove the sackcloth and take off your sandals. Isaiah obeyed — he walked naked and barefoot. The sackcloth that Isaiah removes was probably his normal prophetic garb (cf. Zech 13:4). The nakedness and barefoot-ness are the marks of captivity and poverty — the condition of prisoners taken in war. By wearing these conditions for three years, Isaiah embodies the fate that awaits those who trust Egypt and Cush. The NT sign-act equivalent: Jesus' own body is the sign — the teacher who had nowhere to lay his head (Matt 8:20), stripped at Golgotha (John 19:23), is the living embodiment of what judgment looks like when it falls on the one who takes it on behalf of others.
20.3: YHWH interprets: just as Isaiah has walked naked and barefoot for three years as a sign and portent against Egypt and Cush. The three years are deliberate: not a brief symbolic action but a sustained embodiment. The sign (ot) and portent (mofet) are the two terms used for the plagues of Egypt (Exod 7:3) — the prophetic act recapitulates the exodus judgment, now applied to Egypt itself. Isaiah's body is a living prophecy: the prophet who speaks of the Servant's suffering (52:13-53:12) first enacts a lesser version of stripping and humiliation.
20.4: The king of Assyria will lead away Egyptian and Cushite captives naked and barefoot, with buttocks uncovered — the shame of Egypt exposed. The explicit shameful exposure of the captives is the judgment that Isaiah's three years of symbolic nakedness has been announcing. In the Christological pattern: the Servant is stripped and shamed in a way that should belong to others (52:14; 53:3-4) — his exposure is substitutionary, taking on what the rebels deserve. Isaiah's years of nakedness are a prophetic type of the Servant's ultimate stripping.
20.5: Those who looked to Cush with expectation and took pride in Egypt will be terrified and put to shame. The political trust in Egypt — the alliance that seemed pragmatically wise — is the precise thing condemned. The shame is proportional to the hope: the greater the expectation placed in Egypt, the greater the humiliation when Egypt falls. In the NT, the same logic applies to any trust placed in human strength: those who make flesh their strength (17:5) will be ashamed when the arm of flesh fails.
20.6: In that day the coastland people will say: This is what happened to those we relied on, to whom we fled for help and rescue from the king of Assyria — how will we escape? The confession of the coastlands is the universal admission that human political alliances cannot save. The three-year sign-act concludes with this admission. The NT ground: blessed are those who trust in YHWH (Jer 17:7); cursed is the one who trusts in man (Jer 17:5-6). The coastlands' question — how will we escape? — is the question that the gospel answers: not by any political alliance but by the one who has already faced the Assyrian-equivalent and overcome (cf. 37:36).

21.1: The oracle of the Desert of the Sea — a cryptic designation for Babylon. Like whirlwinds in the Negeb, this vision comes from the desert, from a dreadful land. The whirlwind from the south is YHWH's judgment-storm, and its direction (from the desert, the wilderness between Babylon and Canaan) signals the direction from which Cyrus will come. The oracle's cryptic title (Desert of the Sea) combines the Mesopotamian geography — Babylon sat in the alluvial lowlands between rivers — with the prophetic imagery of chaos-water. In the NT, Babylon the Great comes from the waters (Rev 17:1) — the same cryptic geography of the empire of chaos.
21.2: A grim vision: the traitor betrays, the plunderer plunders. March, O Elam! Besiege, O Media! All the sighing she has caused I will bring to an end. The command to the Medes and Elamites is YHWH's war-summons — the same pattern as 13:17 (I am rousing the Medes). The end of Babylon's sighing — the suffering it has caused — is the goal of the judgment. In the NT, Babylon's fall in Rev 18 similarly ends the suffering of those who were killed for the word of God (Rev 6:10: how long, O Lord?). The divine bringing-to-an-end of oppression is the Servant's mission enacted at the cosmic level.
21.3: Isaiah's anguish at the vision: my body is racked with labor pains; I am too shaken to hear, too disturbed to see. The prophetic intercession takes the form of bodily suffering — the prophet bears in his body the weight of the message he delivers. The same visceral response appears at 16:11 (my heart moans like a lyre for Moab). The prophet's suffering at the vision of judgment is a small image of the divine mourning over judgment (Ezek 18:23, 33:11) and the Servant's bodily bearing of others' pain (53:4-6).
21.4: My mind reels; the evening I had longed for has been turned into dread. The reversal of the evening from anticipated rest to dread is the experience of those who live under the threat of judgment's arrival: the night brings anxiety, not relief. In the NT, the disciples in Gethsemane experience the same turning of anticipated rest (sleeping) into anguish (Matt 26:36-46) — the hour that came with dread rather than peace. The prophet's night of dread anticipates the darkest night of the Servant's suffering.
21.5: Set the table, spread the rugs, eat and drink — then: Rise up, princes, oil the shields! The scene cuts from banquet to battle stations — the sudden interruption of celebration by the alarm of war. This is precisely the scene in Dan 5:1-6: Belshazzar's feast interrupted by the handwriting on the wall, followed by the Median conquest (Dan 5:28-30). The Danielic narrative fulfills the Isaianic prophecy in vivid detail. In the NT, Jesus warns that the day of the Son of Man will come like a thief in the night, interrupting those who are eating and drinking (Matt 24:38-39, Luke 17:28).
21.6: YHWH stations a watchman — let him report what he sees. The watchman metaphor is central to the prophetic vocation (Ezek 3:17, 33:7: I have made you a watchman for Israel). The watchman watches and waits, alert for the signal that others miss. In the NT, Jesus commands watchfulness repeatedly (Matt 24:42-44, 25:13): be on guard, keep watch, for you do not know the hour. The church's eschatological posture is the watchman's posture — alert to the signs of the coming Lord.
21.7: The watchman saw a column of cavalry — riders in pairs, a caravan of donkeys, a caravan of camels — and he listened intently. The watchman's careful observation of the approaching column is the discipline of discernment: identifying what approaches before it arrives. The column of riders prefigures the NT imagery of the rider on the white horse (Rev 19:11-14) — but here the approaching riders bring judgment on Babylon, not the final victory of the Lamb. The watchman's discipline of patient, attentive watching is the contemplative ground of prophetic ministry.
21.8: The lookout called: My lord, I stand on the watchtower continually by day, and I keep my post every night. The watchman's faithfulness — continually by day, every night — is the model of the intercessor who does not give up (Isa 62:6-7: you who remind YHWH, give him no rest). In the NT, the persistent widow who keeps coming to the judge (Luke 18:1-8) is the parable form of this faithful watchman-prayer. The watchman does not abandon his post even when the night is long and nothing seems to move.
21.9: Fallen, fallen is Babylon! All the carved images of her gods lie shattered on the ground. The watchman's proclamation is one of the most significant prophetic announcements in the OT — it is quoted verbatim in Rev 14:8 (Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the Great!) and Rev 18:2. The double Fallen! is the intensified announcement of irreversible collapse. The destruction of Babylon's gods (all the carved images) is the theological core: YHWH's victory over Babylon is simultaneously the defeat of everything Babylon's gods claimed to guarantee — fertility, security, wisdom, sovereignty. The NT application: at the cross, Christ disarmed the rulers and authorities (Col 2:15), and at his return, Babylon the Great falls finally and completely.
21.10: O my people, crushed and winnowed — what I have heard from YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel, I now declare to you. The prophetic word of comfort addressed to the oppressed community (my people) immediately follows the announcement of Babylon's fall. The crushing and winnowing of the covenant community is the experience of exile — they have been ground down under Babylon's weight. But the watchman's announcement (v 9) is directed toward them: the fall of Babylon is good news for those who have suffered under it. In the NT, the announcement of Babylon's fall in Rev 14:8 is immediately followed by the call to the saints to endure (Rev 14:12) and the proclamation of the harvest of the earth (Rev 14:14-16).
21.11: The oracle of Dumah — a name that sounds like the Hebrew for silence/death (dumah). A voice calls from Seir: Watchman, what is left of the night? Seir is Edom. The question about the night's length is the anxious question of one waiting for dawn: how much longer until the darkness ends? In the Christological reading, the night of the exile and of all suffering under anti-God power is the dark through which the watchman watches. The question — what is left of the night? — is the eschatological question of the persecuted community in Rev 6:10: how long, O Lord?
21.12: The watchman answers: Morning is coming, but so is the night. If you would inquire, inquire; come back again. The answer is oracular — morning and night coexist in the watchman's reply. The mixed answer is not evasion but honest prophetic vision: light and darkness alternate before the final dawn. The NT equivalent: signs of the kingdom are present even now, but the night is not yet over; the day of the Lord has not yet come fully. The inquiry is the right response: keep asking, keep coming back. Jesus says ask, and it will be given; seek, and you will find; knock, and the door will be opened (Matt 7:7).
21.13: The oracle concerning Arabia — addressed to caravans of the Dedanites taking shelter in the thickets. Arabia was the trading-caravan territory south of Palestine. The judgment that sweeps through the empires reaches even the commercial routes of the desert. In the Christological framework, the reach of YHWH's judgment and redemption is universal: no commercial network, no desert trade route, no distant people is outside YHWH's concern. The NT equivalent is Acts 1:8: witnesses in Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, and to the ends of the earth — the gospel moves through every trading network and every wilderness.
21.14: Bring water to the thirsty, O people of Tema; go out with food to meet the fugitives. The practical care for refugees — water to the thirsty, food to the fugitives — is the covenant obligation embedded in the prophetic oracle. In the NT, Jesus makes care for the hungry, thirsty, and stranger the criterion of judgment: as you did it to one of the least of these, you did it to me (Matt 25:35-40). The water and bread offered to Arabia's fugitives are a small type of the bread of life and living water that the Servant gives to all who are driven and desperate.
21.15: For they have fled from the swords, from the drawn bow, from the fury of battle. The refugees of Arabia are driven by the same military violence that sweeps through all the oracles of chapters 13-21. Their flight is neither political nor economic but existential: they flee for their lives. The NT: the disciples flee (Matt 10:23: when they persecute you in one city, flee to another) in the same pattern — the prophetic community takes refuge wherever it can until the greater city is established. The fugitives of Arabia point to the eschatological gathering of all refugees into the city that has foundations (Heb 11:10).
21.16: Within a year — reckoned as a hired worker counts a year — all the wealth of Kedar will come to an end. The hired-worker's year is the most precise measure of labor-time in the ancient world: a full year of work, counted to the day, with no extensions. YHWH's judgment is as precise as a work contract. In the NT, the appointed time of judgment is fixed: God has set a day on which he will judge the world with justice through the man he has appointed (Acts 17:31). The precision of prophetic timing reflects the precision of the divine calendar.
21.17: The remaining archers of Kedar will be greatly diminished, for YHWH the God of Israel has spoken. The oracle's ending is anchored in the divine identity: YHWH the God of Israel — not a local deity but the sovereign of all nations — has spoken. The nations of chapters 13-21 are all under the same divine sovereign whose word, once spoken, does not return empty (55:11). The God who judges Arabia is the God who redeems Israel and who, through Israel's Servant, will bless Egypt, Assyria, and Arabia alike.

22.1: The oracle of the Valley of Vision — the cryptic title for Jerusalem, the city that was the site of prophetic vision (cf. 2:1). Jerusalem, the city of YHWH's presence, is itself under judgment — the most painful oracle in the series, because this is the covenant community, not a foreign nation. What has come over you that you have all climbed up to the housetops? The rooftops in the ancient world were places of watching, of festivals, and of anxious observation. Jerusalem's people are on the rooftops — whether celebrating or in panic is ambiguous, which is part of the oracle's power.
22.2: You are a city full of uproar and celebration — but your slain were not killed by the sword, and your dead did not fall in battle. The deaths of Jerusalem's people were not honorable military deaths but the deaths of panic, flight, and starvation. The celebration (at apparent deliverance?) is hollow: those who died, died without the dignity of battle. The uproar of the city is the dark inverse of the joyful uproar of 12:6 (shout for joy, you who dwell in Zion). Jerusalem's wrong kind of noise shows that it missed the right moment for repentance.
22.3: All your commanders fled together; they were captured without a bow being drawn. The military leadership's cowardice — captured without a fight — is the specific shame of Jerusalem's leaders. In the NT, the disciples fled at Jesus' arrest (Matt 26:56: all the disciples deserted him and fled). The temporary failure of the disciples is the negative image of what the Servant accomplishes alone: when all fled, the Servant did not flee but went voluntarily to his suffering (53:7: he did not open his mouth).
22.4: I said: Turn away from me; let me weep bitterly; do not try to comfort me over the ruin of the daughter of my people. The prophet's grief over Jerusalem is the closest analogy in Isaiah to Jesus' weeping over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44: when Jesus saw the city, he wept over it). The daughter of my people in ruin is the same image as the desolate woman of 3:26 sitting on the ground. The refusal of comfort is the authentic grief of one who loves the city and sees what is coming — and the NT weeping of Jesus over Jerusalem is its fulfillment.
22.5: YHWH of hosts has a day of panic and trampling and confusion in the Valley of Vision — walls crashing down, a cry shattering against the mountain. The Day of YHWH applied to Jerusalem: the city of his presence is not exempt from his judgment. The shattering cry is the sound of the city's defense collapsing. The NT: Jesus warns explicitly that the stones of the temple will be thrown down (Matt 24:2), fulfilled in 70 CE. The Day of YHWH falls on Jerusalem precisely because it is the covenant city — the higher the privilege, the heavier the accountability.
22.6: Elam took up the quiver with chariots and cavalry, and Kir bared the shield. The armies surrounding Jerusalem are diverse — Elam to the east, Kir to the north. The coalition against Jerusalem is YHWH's instrument. In the NT, the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 CE by the Roman armies is interpreted by Jesus as YHWH's judgment (Luke 21:20-24: when you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, know that its desolation has come). The nations that besiege Jerusalem are always, in prophetic interpretation, the instruments of a higher justice.
22.7: Your finest valleys were choked with chariots, and cavalry were posted at the city gates. The agricultural valleys of Judah — the source of Jerusalem's food supply — are blocked by enemy forces. The siege cuts off both escape and provision: the city is both trapped and starved. The NT: those who do not come under the Servant's provision will face exactly this condition — cut off from the source of life. But those who come to the one who says come to the waters (55:1) will never be cut off.
22.8: He stripped away Judah's defenses. In that day you turned to the arsenal in the House of the Forest. The House of the Forest (1 Kgs 7:2-5) was the great armory Solomon built — the symbol of human military preparedness. Jerusalem's crisis response is to turn to its weapons cache. The condemnation is not of the arsenal itself but of the priority: weapons before YHWH. In the NT pattern: those who trust in weapons and walls miss the true defense; the one who guards the city watches in vain unless YHWH watches (Ps 127:1).
22.9: You saw the gaps in the wall of the city of David and collected water into the lower pool. The engineering response to the siege: Hezekiah's famous water tunnel (2 Kgs 20:20, 2 Chr 32:2-4) redirected the Gihon spring to the Pool of Siloam, denying water to the besieging Assyrians. The historical detail shows that the crisis of chapter 22 is connected to the Assyrian siege of 701 BCE — the same crisis resolved by the miraculous deliverance of chapter 37. The water-engineering that preserved Jerusalem physically is a type of the living water that the Servant provides permanently.
22.10: You took inventory of Jerusalem's houses and demolished buildings to strengthen the wall. The dismantling of houses to reinforce the city wall — sacrificing private dwellings for collective defense — is the desperate pragmatism of siege conditions. The NT: the kingdom is built not by demolishing houses but by building what cannot be shaken (Heb 12:28). The human effort to fortify what God has already committed to protect reveals the chronic failure to trust YHWH rather than human engineering.
22.11: You built a cistern between the two walls, but you did not look to the one who made all this; you took no account of the one who planned it long ago. The specific accusation: you looked to the water cistern (human engineering) but not to YHWH (who both made the city and planned its history). The double accusation — did not look, took no account — is the fundamental covenant failure: sight and consideration are directed at the wrong object. The NT: seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness, and all these things will be added to you (Matt 6:33) — the reversal of the Jerusalem leaders' priority inversion.
22.12: In that day the Lord GOD of hosts called for weeping and mourning, for shaving the head and sackcloth. The appropriate response to the coming judgment is prophetic lamentation — the bodily, public acknowledgment of grief and guilt that prepares for YHWH's intervention. The NT: blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted (Matt 5:4). The lamentation that YHWH calls for is the gateway to the comfort that the gospel brings. Jerusalem's failure to mourn rightly is the specific sin of v 13.
22.13: But instead there was only celebration — slaughtering oxen, killing sheep, eating and drinking: Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die. The famous phrase let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die is the response of those who, faced with imminent judgment, choose hedonism over repentance. Paul quotes it in 1 Cor 15:32 as the logical conclusion of a life without resurrection hope: if the dead are not raised, then eat, drink, and be merry. But because Christ has been raised (1 Cor 15:20), the right response to mortality is not hedonism but resurrection hope.
22.14: YHWH of hosts revealed this directly in my hearing: This guilt will never be atoned for while you live. The specific word about unforgivable guilt spoken against this community is one of the most severe in the prophetic corpus — stronger even than the hardening of 6:9-10. The guilt that cannot be atoned is not the guilt of ordinary sin but the guilt of willful, defiant, eyes-open rejection of YHWH's call to repentance (v 12) in favor of deliberate hedonism (v 13). The NT equivalent: the blasphemy against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven (Matt 12:31-32) — the same pattern of guilt that persists because there is no repentance, not because the atoning work is insufficient.
22.15: Go to Shebna the steward who is in charge of the palace. Shebna is the chief minister (steward = asher al-habayit, the one over the house) of Hezekiah's administration. He represents the self-aggrandizing official who uses public office for personal glory. The specific accusation (v 16) — carving himself a magnificent tomb — shows that his concern is his own legacy rather than the kingdom's welfare. In the NT, the contrast is the Servant who made himself nothing (Phil 2:7) — the steward of YHWH's household who did not use his position for personal glory.
22.16: What gives you the right to be here? Who do you think you are, carving yourself a tomb on the heights — hewing a burial place in the rock? The two rhetorical questions assault Shebna's self-constructed identity. The magnificent tomb he is building for himself on the heights (a prime Jerusalem location) is the sign of his self-importance. In the NT, the tombs of the prophets that the scribes and Pharisees build and adorn (Matt 23:29) are the same self-aggrandizing monument-building — honoring the dead to establish their own religious status while refusing to receive the living prophets.
22.17: See — YHWH is about to hurl you away with a violent throw, O man; he will take hold of you and fling you far. The violence of the divine action against Shebna is the severity appropriate to the severity of his self-aggrandizement: the self-exalted is hurled low. In the NT pattern: everyone who exalts himself will be humbled (Luke 14:11). Shebna's violent ejection from office is the type of the removal of all false stewards when the true Steward-Servant assumes the position that rightly belongs to him.
22.18: He will ball you up and fling you like a stone across a wide open land; there you will die and your splendid chariots will be your shame. Death in a foreign land — the worst fate for an ancient Israelite who had invested so heavily in a Jerusalem tomb — is the exact reversal of Shebna's aspiration. The chariots that were symbols of status become symbols of shame: what you used to display your power will mark your disgrace. In the NT: what the world counts as gain, the Servant counts as loss (Phil 3:7-8).
22.19: I will remove you from your position and pull you down from your office. The divine removal of Shebna is the condition for Eliakim's appointment (v 20). The removal of the false steward makes room for the true one — the same pattern as the removal of Eli's priesthood (1 Sam 2:30-35) to make room for the faithful priest. In the Christological trajectory: the removal of inadequate mediators makes way for the one perfect mediator (Heb 9:11-15).
22.20: In that day I will summon my servant Eliakim son of Hilkiah. The title my servant (avdi) applied to Eliakim is the same title applied to the great Servant of chapters 42-53. Eliakim is a type of the eschatological Servant: faithful in the household, given the master's authority, opened to what no one can shut. The individual historical steward becomes a prophetic type of the true and final Steward of YHWH's house.
22.21: I will dress him in your robe and fasten your sash around him; I will hand your authority over to him, and he will be a father to the inhabitants of Jerusalem and to the house of Judah. The investiture — robe, sash, authority — is royal language: Eliakim receives what a king confers on his prime minister. The father to Jerusalem is the paternal-care title that describes the king's protective responsibility. In the Christological reading: Jesus is given the robe and authority of the eternal King, and he is a father to Jerusalem in the eschatological sense — the one who wanted to gather Jerusalem's children like a hen gathers her chicks (Matt 23:37).
22.22: I will lay on his shoulder the key of the house of David; what he opens no one will shut, and what he shuts no one will open. The key of the house of David is the most Christologically explicit verse in chapter 22 — it is quoted verbatim in Rev 3:7 as the description of the risen Christ: these are the words of him who is holy and true, who holds the key of David. What he opens no one will shut, and what he shuts no one will open. The key is not a physical key but the authority to open and close the house of YHWH — access to the covenant community, to salvation, to the kingdom. Jesus holds this key as the true and final Eliakim.
22.23: I will drive him in like a peg into a solid place, and he will become a throne of glory to his ancestral household. The peg driven into a solid place — an immovable fastener — is a metaphor for permanent appointment. Eliakim's position is secure, in contrast to Shebna's ejection. He becomes a throne of glory: the official becomes a seat of honor for his whole family. In the NT, the risen and enthroned Christ is the solid place — the cornerstone that the builders rejected (Ps 118:22, Matt 21:42) — into which all who trust him are built as living stones (1 Pet 2:4-5).
22.24: They will hang on him all the honor of his father's household — descendants and offshoots, every vessel from the smallest cups to the largest jars. The peg that Eliakim has become carries the whole household's weight and glory. The image of vessels hung on the peg — from cups to jars — is an image of the full community's dependence on the one appointed. In the NT, the Servant bears the whole household of faith: he is the one shepherd (John 10:16), the vine on which all branches depend (John 15:5), the cornerstone holding the whole building (Eph 2:20).
22.25: In that day the peg driven into the solid place will fail; it will be cut down and fall, and the load hanging on it will be cut off. The oracle ends with judgment: even the faithful Eliakim-type will ultimately fail — the peg is cut down and the load falls. This is the dark word that qualifies the typology: Eliakim is a type, not the antitype. Every human steward, however faithful, is finite and mortal. The peg that never fails is only the one described in Rev 3:7 — the one who holds the key of David and whose appointment is not temporal but eternal. Human types point to the divine reality and then fail, ensuring that the hope is fixed not on the type but on the one who fulfills it.

25.1: LORD, you are my God — I will exalt you; I will praise your name, for you have done wonderful things: plans formed long ago, faithful and true. The doxology opening Isaiah 25 praises YHWH for enacting plans formed long ago. Paul identifies these long-ago plans as fulfilled in Christ: in accordance with the eternal purpose that he accomplished in Christ Jesus our Lord (Eph 3:11). The faithful-and-true character of YHWH's plans becomes the title of the returning Christ in Revelation 19:11: Faithful and True. The praise for what YHWH has done wonderful — pele, the same root as the Messianic child's name Wonderful Counselor (9:6) — resonates across the book's Christological arc.
25.2: For you have turned a city into a heap of rubble — the stronghold of foreigners will never be rebuilt. The judgment on the representative proud city (Babylon, Nineveh, or the composite ANE empire) is the necessary clearing for the eschatological vision that follows. Christ's judgment on cities that reject the gospel (Matt 11:23-24: Capernaum... you will go down to Hades) enacts this same pattern. Revelation 18:21-23's destruction of Babylon — thrown down like a great millstone, never to be found again — is the NT fulfillment: the stronghold of opposition to YHWH's covenant permanently demolished.
25.3: Therefore strong peoples will glorify you — cities of ruthless nations will stand in awe of you. The conversion of the ruthless through YHWH's judgments is the eschatological gathering of the nations. Revelation 15:3-4 presents the redeemed multitude singing the Song of Moses and the Lamb: Great and marvelous are your deeds... All nations will come and worship before you. The strong peoples who glorify YHWH are those who, having seen his judgment on the proud city, recognize that the one they resisted is also the one who rescues. Their awe becomes worship of the Lamb who was slain.
25.4: For you have been a refuge for the poor, a shelter from the storm, a shade from the scorching heat — the blast of the ruthless is like a driving rainstorm against a wall. The covenant theology of YHWH as the poor community's refuge finds its NT fulfillment in Matthew 11:28-30: Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Jesus is the shade from scorching heat and the shelter from the storm — the one to whom the blast of the ruthless has been directed in the cross, so that those sheltered by his atoning work no longer bear it. The poor for whom he is a refuge are the poor in spirit of the Beatitudes (Matt 5:3).
25.5: You silence the clamor of the foreigners as drought is broken by cloud-shadow — you subdue the triumph of the ruthless. The relief of cloud-shadow in scorching heat is the image of divine intervention as physical refreshment. Revelation 7:16-17 applies this directly to the redeemed multitude: the sun will not beat down on them, nor any scorching heat, for the Lamb at the center of the throne will be their shepherd and will lead them to springs of living water. The cloud-shadow that silences the foreigners' clamor is the Spirit-cloud that rested on Christ at his baptism and received him at the ascension — the divine presence that is also the eschatological shade.
25.6: On this mountain the LORD of hosts will prepare a feast for all peoples — a feast of rich food and well-aged wines, of choice meat full of marrow, of the finest strained vintages. The Messianic banquet — the fullest OT statement of the eschatological feast. Jesus draws on this vision across multiple teachings: the Parable of the Great Banquet where all are invited (Luke 14:15-24), the Wedding Banquet where invitations go to those on the roads (Matt 22:1-14), and the promise that many will come from east and west and recline at table with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven (Matt 8:11). At the Last Supper, Jesus explicitly anticipates this feast: I will not drink again of the fruit of the vine until that day when I drink it new in the kingdom of God (Mark 14:25). Revelation 19:9 declares: Blessed are those who are invited to the wedding supper of the Lamb.
25.7: On this mountain he will destroy the shroud cast over all peoples, the sheet spread over all nations. The death-shroud covering all humanity is the universal mortality that sin introduced (Gen 2:17; Rom 5:12). Christ's destruction of the death-shroud is enacted in the resurrection: at Lazarus's tomb, Jesus commands Take off the grave clothes and let him go (John 11:44), performing in miniature what the resurrection performs universally. The grave clothes left behind in the empty tomb (John 20:6-7) are the shroud removed from the one who first breaks through it. Paul: Since death came through a man, the resurrection of the dead comes also through a man (1 Cor 15:21).
25.8: He will swallow up death forever. The Lord GOD will wipe away every tear from every face; the disgrace of his people he will remove from all the earth. The three eschatological reversals — death swallowed, tears wiped, disgrace removed — are all attributed to Christ in the NT. Paul quotes this verse directly in 1 Corinthians 15:54: when the perishable puts on the imperishable... then shall come to pass the saying: Death is swallowed up in victory. O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting? (citing also Hos 13:14). Revelation 21:4 is the fullest realization: He will wipe every tear from their eyes. There will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain. Isaiah 25:8 is the hinge on which all NT eschatology turns.
25.9: In that day they will say: This is our God; we waited for him, and he has saved us. This is the LORD; we waited for him. Let us rejoice and be glad in his salvation. The recognition-exclamation at the consummation — This is our God! — is the moment of unveiled identity. John 1:14: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory. At the resurrection, Thomas's recognition: My Lord and my God! (John 20:28) is the individual's version of this communal cry. Revelation 21:3: the dwelling place of God is with man — the This is our God moment that the whole of Scripture has been building toward. The salvation (yeshu'ato) for which they waited is the name Jesus: you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins (Matt 1:21).
25.10: For the hand of the LORD will rest on this mountain, but Moab will be trampled in place as straw is trampled into a dung heap. Moab as the representative enemy stands in contrast to the covenant mountain under YHWH's protecting hand. The trampling of Moab under divine judgment while the feast occurs on the holy mountain is the eschatological pattern Paul describes in 1 Corinthians 15:25-27: Christ must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet (citing Ps 110:1). Hebrews 10:13 sees Christ waiting at the Father's right hand until his enemies are made a footstool for his feet — the divine hand resting on Zion while the enemies are progressively trampled.
25.11: He will spread his hands over Moab as a swimmer spreads his arms through water — and he will bring down their pride despite all their struggling. The image of YHWH's hands spread wide over the enemy to subdue their pride carries a quiet but striking resonance with the cross: the arms of the crucified one spread wide in the gesture of both total vulnerability and sovereign embrace. What appears as Rome's and Israel's victory over Jesus (his hands spread, nailed, helpless) is in fact the moment when YHWH spreads his hands over sin, death, and pride — bringing them down despite all their struggling against him. The swimmer's arms subdue the current; the crucified arms subdue the powers.
25.12: The high fortifications of your walls he will pull down, lay low, and cast to the ground — to the very dust. The demolition of Moab's pride-fortifications to the dust closes the chapter in the same key as the feast opened it: while the holy mountain hosts the banquet for all peoples, the enemy's greatest defenses become dust. Christ's promise in Matthew 16:18 — the gates of Hades will not prevail against my church — is the positive expression of what this verse promises negatively. The fortifications that seemed impregnable cannot stand before the one who swallows up death and wipes every tear.

24.1: A revelation of God: YHWH empties (b̄ôqēq) and lays waste (ûb̄ôl ĕqāh) the earth — the verbs are intensive Polel forms conveying thorough, complete action. The cosmic scope of this judgment (the whole earth, not one nation) establishes this as the final-judgment register. Revelation 6-19 draws on this imagery throughout the seal and trumpet and bowl judgments. The theology: sin's ultimate trajectory is the unraveling of creation, which only Christ's new creation (Rev 21:5: behold, I am making all things new) can reverse.
24.2: A revelation of God: all social distinctions are annulled — priest and people, servant and master, seller and buyer, borrower and lender — judgment is perfectly impartial. This is the NT principle: there is no partiality with God (Rom 2:11; Acts 10:34). At the judgment seat of Christ all will stand equally as individuals accountable to the same standard (2 Cor 5:10; Rev 20:12).
24.3: A revelation of God: the earth will be utterly emptied and utterly plundered — hib̄bôq tibb̄ôq hā'āreṣ wĕhib̄bôz tibb̄ôz — the doubled infinitive absolutes are emphatic. YHWH has spoken this word, and it is irrevocable. This establishes the certainty of the judgment that Christ will execute at his return — the same divine word that announced creation now announces its undoing and renewal.
24.4: A revelation of God: the earth mourns ('āb̄ĕlāh) and withers (nāb̄ĕlāh), the world languishes and withers, the high ones of the people languish. Creation's mourning anticipates the NT framework of Romans 8:19-22: the creation groans under the weight of human sin, waiting for the liberation that comes through the revelation of the children of God — liberation secured by Christ's redemption.
24.5: A shadow: the earth defiled under its inhabitants — they have transgressed the laws, violated the statutes, broken the everlasting covenant (b̄ārak û b̄ĕrît 'ôlām). The broken everlasting covenant is the ground of the curse. Christ establishes the new covenant in his blood (Luke 22:20; Heb 13:20: the everlasting covenant established by the blood of the eternal shepherd) — the same b̄ĕrît 'ôlām that Isaiah 24:5 describes as broken is the one Christ restores in an unbreakable form.
24.6: A revelation of God: the curse ('ālāh) devours the earth; few men are left. The divine curse is the outworking of covenant violation (cf. Deut 27-28 — the curses of covenant-breaking). Christ becomes a curse for us (Gal 3:13 — cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree), absorbing the covenant curse so that those who are in him receive the covenant blessing instead.
24.7: A revelation of God: the new wine mourns ('āb̄al tîrôš), the vine languishes, all the merry-hearted sigh. The joy of harvest — central to covenant life (Deut 16:13-15; cf. Ps 4:7) — fails under judgment. Christ transforms this mourning: John 2:1-11 (water into wine at Cana) signals that messianic abundance reverses this loss; John 15:11 promises that his joy remains in his disciples.
24.8: A revelation of God: the tambourines cease, the noise of the jubilant stops, the mirth of the lyre ceases. The complete silencing of celebration is the sign of total divine judgment (cf. Rev 18:22: the voice of harpists and musicians heard no more in Babylon). The absence of music signals the absence of shalom. Christ restores the song: Rev 5:9 (a new song sung before the Lamb).
24.9: A revelation of God: they no longer drink wine with singing; strong drink is bitter to those who drink it. The celebratory drink turned bitter is a reversal of covenant blessing. The NT picks up this inversion at the cross: Jesus was offered wine mixed with gall (Matt 27:34) — the bitter drink at the moment of maximum judgment, after which he promises the new wine of the kingdom (Matt 26:29).
24.10: A revelation of God: the city of chaos (qiryat-tōhû) is broken down — the word tōhû is the same as Genesis 1:2 (formless void). The city built on human pride reverts to pre-creation chaos. Christ is the one through whom all things were made (John 1:3) and by whom all things hold together (Col 1:17); apart from him, the Babel-cities collapse back into chaos.
24.11: A revelation of God: in the streets they cry out for wine, all joy has gone dark, the gladness of the earth is banished. The complete reversal of covenant blessing points forward to the consummation: Christ promises that in the kingdom the disciples will drink wine new with him (Matt 26:29) — the restoration of what judgment takes away.
24.12: A revelation of God: desolation is left in the city and the gate is battered to ruins. The city's destruction is comprehensive — it is the anti-type of the new Jerusalem (Rev 21:2) whose gates are never shut (Rev 21:25). The desolated city of Isaiah 24 gives way to the city of God as the ultimate telos.
24.13: A shadow: among the peoples it will be as at the gleaning of an olive tree (k̠ĕnōqēp̄ zayit) — a few left after the harvest. The remnant who survive judgment are those who lift their voices in praise (v. 14). Christ gathers this remnant: Luke 12:32 (fear not, little flock); Revelation 7:9 (a great multitude from every nation — the remnant that turns out to be vast). The gleaning remnant becomes a multitude no one can number.
24.14: A shadow: they lift up their voices and sing for joy, shouting from the west over the majesty of YHWH. The remnant's praise from the ends of the earth (east, v. 15; coastlands, v. 15; ends of the earth, v. 16) anticipates the universal worship of Revelation 7:9-12 — every nation, tribe, people, and language standing before the throne and before the Lamb, crying Salvation belongs to our God and to the Lamb.
24.15: A shadow: glorify YHWH in the east, and in the coastlands the name of YHWH God of Israel. The universal geographic scope of praise — east, west (v. 14), coastlands — is the eschatological worship that Christ's redemption makes possible. Acts 1:8 (to the ends of the earth) frames the missionary movement as the vehicle through which this east-west praise is gathered.
24.16: A revelation of God: from the ends of the earth songs are heard — glory to the Righteous One (ṣĕb̄î lĕṣaddîq). But the prophet breaks off in anguish: I pine away, I pine away. Woe is me! — the treacherous still deal treacherously. The tension between the eschatological praise and the present reality of ongoing wickedness is the tension of the NT's already and not yet — the songs of the kingdom are real, but the full resolution awaits the return of the Righteous One himself (cf. Jas 5:8: the coming of the Lord is near).
24.17: A revelation of God: terror, pit, and snare for the inhabitants of the earth — paḥad wāpaḥat wāpāḥ 'ālèkā — the wordplay (terror/pit/snare with overlapping sounds) emphasizes inescapability. The NT frames final judgment with the same inescapability: Hebrews 2:3 (how shall we escape if we neglect such a great salvation?); Revelation 6:15-17 (kings and generals hide but cannot escape the wrath of the Lamb).
24.18: A revelation of God: flight from one danger encounters another — the one who flees the sound of terror falls into the pit; the one who climbs out of the pit is caught in the snare. The windows of heaven are opened and the foundations of the earth shake — the flood imagery of Genesis 7:11 is invoked for the final judgment. Christ's cross is the one place of escape (Rom 5:9 — saved through him from the wrath to come).
24.19: A revelation of God: the earth is broken, split, shaken violently (rō'āh hit rō'ĕ'āh hā'āreṣ — intensive Hithpolel). The cosmic earthquake of final judgment appears throughout the prophets (Amos 8:8; Zech 14:4-5) and the NT (Matt 27:51 — earthquake at the crucifixion as anticipation; Rev 16:18 — greatest earthquake in history at the final bowl). The creation trembles at its Judge.
24.20: A revelation of God: the earth staggers like a drunkard (nûa' tānûa' hā'āreṣ k̠aššikkôr), sways like a hut. Its transgression lies heavy upon it — the weight of accumulated sin destabilizes creation itself. This verse does not directly anticipate Christ but establishes the necessity of the new creation: a creation staggering under sin cannot be reformed; it must be replaced (Rev 21:1 — the first earth passed away).
24.21: A shadow: YHWH punishes the host of heaven in heaven and the kings of the earth on earth. The judgment of cosmic powers (ṣĕb̄ā' hammārôm — host on high) alongside earthly rulers anticipates Paul's vision of Christ's triumph over all principalities and powers (1 Cor 15:24-25: he must reign until he has put all enemies under his feet; Eph 6:12 — rulers, authorities, powers of this dark world). Christ's cross is the decisive defeat of these powers (Col 2:15).
24.22: A shadow: they will be gathered as prisoners in a pit, shut up in a prison, and after many days they will be punished. Revelation 20:1-3 uses this framework directly: the angel seizes the dragon, binds him, and throws him into the Abyss for a thousand years — after many days will be punished corresponds to the release and final judgment of Revelation 20:7-10. The sequence of binding, imprisonment, and final punishment is the precise structure of both Isaiah 24:22 and the Revelation's millennial vision.
24.23: A shadow: the moon will be confounded and the sun ashamed when YHWH of hosts reigns on Mount Zion — the natural luminaries are superseded by the divine presence. Revelation 21:23 applies this directly to the new Jerusalem: the city does not need the sun or the moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and the Lamb is its lamp. Isaiah 60:19-20 develops the same theme. The reign of YHWH on Zion is fulfilled in the reign of Christ (Rev 22:3: the throne of God and of the Lamb will be in the city). The glory that makes moon and sun irrelevant is the glory of the incarnate, crucified, and risen Lord.

26.1: "We have a strong city; he sets up salvation as walls and bulwarks." The strong city (ʿîr ʿāz) with salvation (yĕšûʿâ) as its structural walls is the eschatological Zion. Heb 11:10: "he was looking forward to the city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God"; Heb 12:22: "you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem." Rev 21:10-12 presents the New Jerusalem descending, with a great wall — here in Isaiah the wall is salvation itself, meaning the walls are the saving act of God in Christ. The city is not fortified by stone but by the redemption accomplished at the cross.
26.2: "Open the gates, that the righteous nation that keeps faith may enter in." Rev 21:25 fulfills this: "its gates will never be shut by day — and there will be no night there"; Rev 22:14: "Blessed are those who wash their robes, so that they may have the right to the tree of life and that they may enter the city by the gates." The one who is the door of entry is Christ: "I am the door; if anyone enters by me, he will be saved" (John 10:9). The "righteous nation that keeps faith" (gôy ṣaddîq šōmēr ʾĕmunîm) is the community of those justified by faith — the NT's "righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe" (Rom 3:22).
26.3: "You keep him in perfect peace whose mind is stayed on you, because he trusts in you." The doubled šālôm šālôm (peace, peace — a superlative) is the peace that Christ gives. John 14:27: "Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. Not as the world gives do I give to you. Let not your hearts be troubled, neither let them be afraid." Phil 4:7: "the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus." The mind "stayed" on YHWH (sāmûk, leaning/resting upon) is the same posture as Paul's "in Christ" — the peace is not produced by the mind's effort but by its resting on the object of trust.
26.4: "Trust in YHWH forever, for in YHWH GOD is an everlasting rock." 1 Cor 10:4: "and the rock was Christ" — the spiritual rock that Israel drank from in the wilderness is identified as the pre-incarnate Christ. The ṣûr ʿôlāmîm (rock of ages/everlasting rock) is the foundation on which Christ builds his church (Matt 16:18) and on which those who hear his words build (Matt 7:24-25). Heb 13:8: "Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever" — the everlastingness of the rock is the unchanging faithfulness of Christ. "Rock of Ages, cleft for me" (Toplady) is the direct hymnodic application of this verse.
26.5: "For he has humbled the inhabitants of the height, the lofty city. He lays it low, lays it low to the ground, casts it to the dust." The humbling of the lofty city is fulfilled proleptically at the fall of Babylon (Rev 14:8; 18:2: "Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great!") and ultimately in the judgment of all earthly power systems. Phil 2:6-11 establishes Christ as the agent of this humbling: the one who "emptied himself, taking the form of a servant" (v.7) is the same one before whom "every knee should bow" (v.10). The pattern is kenosis-then-exaltation — the strong city of salvation (v.1) rises as the lofty city falls.
26.6: "The foot tramples it, the feet of the poor, the steps of the needy." The reversal of social order in the eschatological judgment is the Magnificat's content: "he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate; he has filled the hungry with good things, and the rich he has sent away empty" (Luke 1:52-53). Matt 5:3: "Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven." The lowly who trample the fallen city are those who were previously the humiliated poor — their elevation is the Servant's own mission (Isa 61:1: "to bring good news to the poor").
26.7: "The path of the righteous is level; you make level the way of the righteous." The level path (yĕšārîm, upright/straight) connects to Isa 40:3 ("make straight in the desert a highway") and Luke 3:5 (the Baptist's ministry of leveling). But the ultimate fulfillment is Christ himself: "I am the way, and the truth, and the life" (John 14:6). The "way" is not merely a method but a person — the level path of the righteous is not an ethical standard but the one who is himself righteousness and who, by union with him, becomes our path.
26.8: "In the path of your judgments, O YHWH, we wait for you; your name and remembrance are the desire of our soul." The posture of waiting for YHWH (cf. 40:31) is the NT community's posture regarding Christ's return. Rev 22:20: "Come, Lord Jesus!" The Maranatha prayer of 1 Cor 16:22 ("Our Lord, come!") is the NT's "your name and remembrance are the desire of our soul." The Acts community "waited" for the promised Spirit (Acts 1:4) as the first installment of the full waiting for Christ — the Advent posture governs both the Spirit's coming and the Son's return.
26.9: "My soul yearns for you in the night; my spirit within me earnestly seeks you." The night-seeking of God anticipates Jesus in Gethsemane (Matt 26:36-46: praying alone in the night before the crucifixion, "my soul is very sorrowful, even to death"). The "earnest seeking" (ʾăšaḥărĕkā, to seek early/diligently) is the prayer-posture of the one who comes to his Father. Heb 5:7: "In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence." The night-yearning of Isaiah 26:9 is the Servant's own spirituality.
26.10: "If favor is shown to the wicked, he does not learn righteousness; in the land of uprightness he deals corruptly and does not see the majesty of YHWH." The hardening of the wicked by grace misused parallels Rom 2:4-5 ("do you presume on the riches of his kindness... not knowing that God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance? But because of your hard and impenitent heart you are storing up wrath"). The failure to "see the majesty of YHWH" is the failure to see Christ: John 12:45, "whoever sees me sees him who sent me." The light of divine favor that the wicked do not use to see God becomes the ground of their condemnation.
26.11: "O YHWH, your hand is lifted up, but they do not see it. Let them see your zeal for your people, and be ashamed." The divine hand lifted in blessing and judgment that the wicked cannot perceive — at Christ's return, "then will appear in heaven the sign of the Son of Man, and then all the tribes of the earth will mourn" (Matt 24:30). The zeal for his people that will be made manifest is the same zeal that drove Jesus to cleanse the temple (John 2:17: "Zeal for your house will consume me"). 1 John 2:28: "so that when he appears we may have confidence and not shrink from him in shame at his coming."
26.12: "O YHWH, you will ordain peace for us, for you have indeed done for us all our works." The divine ordaining of peace and the acknowledgment that all works are from God is the theology of grace. Eph 2:8-10: "For by grace you have been saved through faith... not a result of works, so that no one may boast. For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand, that we should walk in them." The "peace ordained" is the peace established through the atonement (Rom 5:1: "we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ") — not achieved but received.
26.13: "O YHWH our God, other lords besides you have ruled over us, but your name alone we bring to remembrance." The exclusive naming of YHWH over former lords is the Christological confession of Phil 2:11: "every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father." Col 2:15: Christ "disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him" — the "other lords" that once ruled are the powers Christ has defeated at the cross. The Name (šēm) that alone is remembered is the name above every name (Phil 2:9).
26.14: "They are dead, they will not live; they are shades, they will not arise; to that end you have visited them with destruction and wiped out all remembrance of them." The dead-who-shall-not-rise (the former lords who ruled Israel) contrasts sharply with v.19 ("your dead shall live"). In NT terms, the powers and rulers who will not rise are the "rulers of this age, who are doomed to pass away" (1 Cor 2:6). Christ alone is "the first and the last, and the living one. I died, and behold I am alive forevermore" (Rev 1:17-18) — the one who died and rose stands over against those who died and will not rise.
26.15: "But you have increased the nation, O YHWH, you have increased the nation; you are glorified; you have expanded all the borders of the land." The multiplication of the nation anticipates the church's expansion to all peoples. Matt 28:18-20: "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations." Acts 1:8: "to the ends of the earth." The "borders of the land" are now the whole earth — Rom 4:13: "the promise that he would be heir of the world" was given to Abraham and his offspring. The physical borders of Canaan become the spiritual borders of the new creation: "the meek shall inherit the earth" (Matt 5:5, citing Ps 37:11).
26.16: "O YHWH, in distress they sought you; they poured out a whispered prayer when your discipline was upon them." The prayer in extremity — seeking God specifically under discipline (mûsār, chastisement). Heb 5:7: "In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence." The "whispered prayer" (ṣĕqôn lāḥaš, a barely audible murmur) is the prayer so pressed by suffering that it can only be whispered — Gethsemane and the cross are the ultimate fulfillment of this verse in the Servant who prays under the discipline that we deserved.
26.17: "Like a pregnant woman who writhes and cries out in her pangs when she is near to giving birth, so were we before you, O YHWH." The birth-pangs image of eschatological distress is directly used in John 16:20-21: "you will be sorrowful, but your sorrow will turn into joy. When a woman is giving birth, she has sorrow because her hour has come, but when she has delivered the baby, she no longer remembers the anguish." Matt 24:8: "all these are but the beginning of the birth pains" — the eschatological distress before Christ's return is labor that will end in the joy of resurrection and new creation (vv.18-19). Rom 8:22: "the whole creation has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth until now."
26.18: "We were pregnant; we writhed; but we have given birth to wind. We have accomplished no deliverance in the earth, and the inhabitants of the world have not fallen." The failed birth — human striving produces only wind. In Gal 2:16, Paul's whole argument against Torah-works-righteousness is the same point: "a person is not justified by works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ." Human religious effort to produce deliverance generates "wind" — it is the rûaḥ of the Spirit, given freely by God (John 3:8: "the wind blows where it wishes"), that produces the new birth (John 3:5-6). The contrast between vv.18 and 19 is the contrast between human striving and divine resurrection.
26.19: "Your dead shall live; their bodies shall rise. You who dwell in the dust, awake and sing for joy! For your dew is a dew of light, and the earth will give birth to the dead." The most explicit resurrection promise in the OT. John 5:28-29: "An hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out, those who have done good to the resurrection of life, and those who have done evil to the resurrection of judgment." 1 Cor 15:20-23: "in fact Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep... as in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive." Matt 27:52-53: at Jesus's resurrection, "the tombs also were opened... many bodies of the saints who had fallen asleep were raised" — the preliminary fulfillment of Isaiah 26:19. The "dew of light" (ṭal ʾôrōt) is the resurrection-dew, the divine life-moisture that awakens the dead — in John's Gospel, Christ is the resurrection and the life (11:25) who speaks and the dead come forth (11:43-44).
26.20: "Come, my people, enter your chambers, and shut your doors behind you; hide yourselves for a little while until the fury has passed by." The divine hiding-place during judgment connects to the Passover night (Exod 12:22-23: "none of you shall go out of the door of his house until the morning"). John 14:2-3: "In my Father's house are many rooms (monai). I go to prepare a place for you... I will come again and will take you to myself." The "chambers" (ḥăḏārîm) are the dwelling places Christ prepares — the believer's shelter in the eschatological judgment is union with Christ, hidden with him in God (Col 3:3: "your life is hidden with Christ in God").
