Rev 6:11: "They were told to rest a little longer" — the kĕmōʿeṭ (for a little while) of Isaiah 26:20.
26.21: "For behold, YHWH is coming out from his place to punish the inhabitants of the earth for their iniquity, and the earth will disclose the blood shed on it, and will no more cover its slain." The divine coming-out to judge is the parousia. Jude 14-15: "Behold, the Lord comes with ten thousands of his holy ones, to execute judgment on all and to convict all the ungodly." The earth disclosing its concealed blood is the revelation of all violence at the judgment: Rev 6:10 (the martyrs' blood crying out), Rev 16:6 ("for they have shed the blood of saints and prophets"), Rev 18:24 ("in her was found the blood of prophets and of saints"). The cross itself is the foundational disclosure: the earth swallowed no blood at Calvary — the body was taken down, the blood poured, the injustice recorded — and at the parousia, no blood will remain covered.

30.1: A revelation of God: woe to the rebellious children who carry out plans that are not from YHWH, adding sin to sin by going to Egypt — making an alliance without consulting YHWH's Spirit. This woe against the disobedient son is the anti-type of the obedient Son: Christ is the one who does nothing of his own accord but only what the Father shows him (John 5:19), who came not to do his own will but the will of the one who sent him (John 6:38). Philippians 2:8 frames his obedience explicitly: he humbled himself and became obedient to death. Israel adds sin to sin by consulting Egypt; Christ takes on Israel's sin to end the cycle.
30.2: A revelation of God: they set out for Egypt without asking YHWH, taking refuge in Pharaoh's shadow — seeking security in human power rather than divine provision. The theology of misplaced refuge runs through the Psalms (Ps 20:7: some trust in chariots, some in horses, but we trust in YHWH; Ps 146:3: do not trust in princes, in a son of man who cannot save). Christ is the true refuge: Hebrews 6:18 (we who flee to him for refuge), the one in whose shadow nations find protection (Isa 51:16; Ps 91:1-2).
30.3: A revelation of God: Pharaoh's protection will become Israel's shame; the shelter of Egypt's shadow will become humiliation. The anti-type is the cross: what appeared to be shame and defeat (1 Cor 1:23: Christ crucified, a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Gentiles) is the true shelter. The emperor who crucified appeared to be the protecting power; the crucified one proved to be the only one in whose shadow one is safe.
30.4: A revelation of God: even to Zoan and Hanes the officials have gone — the extent of the alliance demonstrates the depth of Egypt's failure as a refuge. The farther one travels from YHWH's provision to seek human alternatives, the greater the eventual humiliation. This dynamic anticipates the prodigal son (Luke 15:13-14) whose journey into the far country ends in famine — the distance from the Father equals the depth of need.
30.5: A revelation of God: all are put to shame by a people who neither help nor profit, bringing shame and also reproach. Egypt's help is empty and vain (hebel wārîq — vapor and emptiness). Every human savior-figure ultimately proves hebel: vapor, insubstantial. Only Christ is the substantive rescuer — the one who is not empty, whose help is not vapor, who actually delivers from the ultimate oppressor (Heb 2:14-15).
30.6: A revelation of God: the oracle of the animals of the Negev — the dangerous journey south through lion country, carrying treasures on camels and donkeys to Egypt for help that does not profit. The irony: they risk lions and vipers to reach a land that cannot help. Christ inverts this: he leaves the safety of the Father to enter the territory of the Adversary (the wilderness temptation, Matt 4:1-11) not to seek refuge from it but to overcome it.
30.7: A revelation of God: Egypt's help is utterly useless — šaw' wĕhebel — emptiness and vapor. The prophet names Egypt Rahab who sits still (Rahab-hem-shebeth) — the chaos monster who has no energy to act. Every substitute for YHWH proves to be a sleeping dragon, full of menacing appearance and empty of actual power. The gospel announces that only YHWH — incarnate in Christ — is the saving power that is not empty.
30.8: A revelation of God: write this on a tablet and inscribe it in a book so it endures as a witness. YHWH directs the prophet to preserve the indictment permanently — divine words outlast the moment. This is the principle behind scripture itself: 2 Timothy 3:16 (all scripture is God-breathed and profitable); John 5:39 (the scriptures bear witness about Christ). The permanent record becomes the testimony that ultimately points to the one who fulfills what the rebellious generation refused.
30.9: A revelation of God: a rebellious people, lying sons, sons who will not hear YHWH's instruction. The characterization of Israel as bĕnê mĕrî (rebellious sons) is the backdrop against which Christ as the faithful Son stands. Romans 5:19 frames the entire redemption in these terms: by one man's disobedience many were made sinners; by one man's obedience many will be made righteous. Christ is what the rebellious sons refused to be.
30.10: A revelation of God: they say to the seers do not see and to the prophets do not prophesy to us what is right; speak smooth things, prophesy illusions. The demand for comfort over truth is the perennial human temptation. Paul warns that a time will come when people will not endure sound teaching but have itching ears and accumulate teachers to suit their own passions (2 Tim 4:3). Christ is the prophet who speaks what is right regardless of reception (John 8:40: I have told you the truth that I heard from God).
30.11: A revelation of God: leave the way, turn aside from the path, let us hear no more about the Holy One of Israel. Rejecting the prophetic word is rejecting YHWH's identity as the Holy One. Jesus as the Holy One of God (John 6:69 — Peter's confession; Mark 1:24 — the demon's recognition) is precisely what the rebellious sons of Isaiah 30 refused. The rejection of Isaiah's word about the Holy One of Israel is the type of the rejection of the Holy One himself in the Gospel narrative.
30.12: A revelation of God: because you despise this word and trust in oppression and perverseness — the reliance on human crooked dealing rather than divine word is the ground of judgment. The NT indictment of the same pattern: those who suppress the truth in unrighteousness (Rom 1:18) face the wrath of God. The rejection of straight paths for crooked ones is the trajectory that makes a Redeemer necessary — one who straightens the crooked (Luke 3:5: the crooked shall become straight).
30.13: A revelation of God: this iniquity will be for you like a break in a high wall that bulges out and collapses suddenly. The metaphor of imminent structural collapse — the wall that appears solid but is already cracking — appears in Jesus' parable of the house on sand (Matt 7:26-27). Human structures built on anything other than YHWH's word collapse at the first storm. Christ is the cornerstone (Eph 2:20; 1 Pet 2:6) — the stone that prevents structural failure when built upon rightly.
30.14: A revelation of God: broken like a potter's vessel, shattered so completely that not a sherd remains useful — no sherd to carry fire, no sherd to scoop water from the cistern. The total brokenness anticipates the complete ineffectiveness of all human remedies for sin. Christ alone is the one who can provide living water (John 4:10-14; 7:37-38) — not scooped from a cistern in a broken sherd but flowing from within the one who is himself the source.
30.15: A shadow: bĕšûb̄āh wānāḥat tiwwāšĕ'ûn — in returning and rest you shall be saved; bĕhašqēṭ ûb̄ebiṭḥāh — in quietness and trust shall be your strength. This is the closest Isaiah gets to a statement of gospel in chapter 30. Jesus cites this verse's logic in Matthew 11:28-29: come to me, all who are weary and heavy-laden, and I will give you rest; take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly of heart, and you will find rest for your souls. Hebrews 4:1-11 develops the same connection, reading the Sabbath rest as the eschatological rest that Christ inaugurates. Israel said no (v. 15b) to the rest; Christ says come.
30.16: A revelation of God: Israel says we will flee on horses — and therefore they shall flee in defeat. The self-fulfilling irony of trusting in military speed: the very horses they trust in become the means of their rout. The NT framing: those who live by the sword die by the sword (Matt 26:52). Trusting human military capacity rather than YHWH results in experiencing that capacity turned against you. Christ's kingdom is not advanced by horses and chariots (Zech 9:9-10 — the king arrives on a donkey, a beast of peace, dismissing the war-horse).
30.17: A revelation of God: one pursuing a thousand, five putting ten thousand to flight, until you are left as a flagstaff on a mountaintop — the exposed remnant, vulnerable, visible, alone. The decimated remnant standing alone is the anti-type of the exalted Christ on the cross — the one who was left alone (John 16:32: you will all be scattered, each to his own home, and leave me alone), the flagstaff of v. 17 becoming the lifted-up banner of Isaiah 11:10 and John 12:32.
30.18: A shadow: therefore YHWH waits to be gracious to you (yĕḥakkeh YHWH lĕḥonnĕkem); he exalts himself to show mercy; blessed are all those who wait for him (kol-ḥôkê lô). The patient waiting of YHWH to be gracious is one of the most profound statements in Isaiah. 2 Peter 3:9 applies the same truth to the delay of Christ's return: he is not slow concerning his promises but patient, not willing that any should perish. The father running to meet the returning prodigal (Luke 15:20) is the narrative embodiment of YHWH who waits to be gracious. Blessed are those who wait for him — the Beatitudes re-express this (Matt 5:3-12).
30.19: A shadow: the people in Zion shall weep no more; YHWH will surely be gracious at the sound of your cry — as soon as he hears it, he answers. The promise of immediate divine responsiveness to the cry of the distressed is the backdrop for Jesus' teaching on prayer: ask and it will be given, seek and you will find (Matt 7:7-11); the Father who gives good gifts to his children. Revelation 21:4 fulfills v. 19 completely: God will wipe every tear from their eyes and mourning shall be no more.
30.20: A shadow: though the Lord give bread of adversity and water of affliction, your Teacher will no longer hide himself, but your eyes will see your Teacher (wĕlō'-yikkānēp̄ 'ôd môrekā wĕhāyû 'êneykā rō'ôt 'et-môrekā). The hiddenness of YHWH in the exile gives way to direct sight of the Teacher. John 1:14 is the fulfillment: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory. John 6:45 cites the principle (they will all be taught by God, Isa 54:13) and applies it to coming to Christ. 1 John 2:27 — the anointing teaches you about all things — is the Spirit-mediated continuation of what the visible Teacher established. The Teacher who was hidden is now seen, and through the Spirit continues to teach.
30.21: A shadow: your ears shall hear a word behind you saying this is the way, walk in it — divine guidance for every turning, left or right. Christ as the Way (John 14:6: I am the way, the truth, and the life) is the fulfillment of the guiding voice: the path behind, alongside, and ahead is Christ himself. The Spirit continues this guidance (John 16:13: the Spirit of truth will guide you into all truth). The voice behind you saying this is the way becomes the indwelling Spirit who leads the children of God (Rom 8:14).
30.22: A revelation of God: then you will defile your silver-covered idols and gold-plated images, throwing them away as unclean — wegone! The repentance that follows the experience of the Teacher's presence includes spontaneous idol-rejection. The NT pattern is identical: when the Thessalonians received the gospel, they turned to God from idols to serve the living and true God (1 Thess 1:9). The sight of Christ makes idols not merely wrong but disgusting — gēgone! — begone! (cf. Phil 3:7-8: Paul counts all things as rubbish because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ).
30.23: A shadow: YHWH gives rain for the seed, the bread of the ground will be rich and plenteous, livestock will graze in large pastures. The eschatological agricultural abundance is the material face of covenant restoration. The NT reads this as the new creation: Romans 8:21 (creation itself will be freed from corruption); Revelation 22:2 (the tree of life yielding fruit every month). Christ as the bread of life (John 6:35) is the spiritual reality of which this material abundance is the type — the one who satisfies not just physical hunger but the hunger that no harvest can meet.
30.24: A shadow: the oxen and donkeys will eat seasoned fodder — even the working animals share in the eschatological restoration. Creation-wide redemption is the scope: Romans 8:20-22 frames the created order as participating in the liberation from futility. Christ's lordship extends to all creation (Col 1:15-17: in him all things hold together; 1 Cor 15:28: God will be all in all). The working animals fed with fine fodder point to the comprehensive scope of the kingdom's shalom.
30.25: A shadow: on every lofty mountain and every high hill, brooks running with water on the day of the great slaughter when the towers fall. The paradox — abundant water on the day of great slaughter — is the NT paradox of the cross: on the day of maximum devastation (the crucifixion), the water of life flows (John 19:34; cf. John 7:37-38). The tower that falls is the power of death; the water that runs freely is the Spirit poured out on Pentecost (Acts 2:17-18).
30.26: A shadow: on the day YHWH binds up the brokenness of his people and heals the wound of their blow, the moon will be as bright as the sun and the sun will be sevenfold — as the light of seven days. The intensified light of the eschatological healing day is the backdrop for Revelation 21:23 and 22:5: the new Jerusalem has no need of sun or moon because the glory of God gives it light and the Lamb is its lamp. Christ's resurrection and the giving of the Spirit are the first rays of this sevenfold light; the consummation is its noon.
30.27: A revelation of God: the name of YHWH comes from afar, burning with anger, in thick rising smoke, lips full of fury, tongue a devouring fire. The theophany of YHWH's wrath coming against Assyria in vv. 27-33 is cast in the language of the divine warrior (cf. Ps 18:7-15; Deut 33:2). Christ at his return is described in exactly these terms: Revelation 19:11-16 (the warrior whose eyes are like a flame of fire, whose robe is dipped in blood, from whose mouth comes a sharp sword to strike down the nations). The YHWH of Isaiah 30 and the returning Christ of Revelation 19 are the same divine warrior.
30.28: A revelation of God: his breath like an overflowing stream, reaching to the neck, sifting the nations with the sieve of destruction, bridling the jaws of the peoples. The divine breath (nišmāt YHWH) as the instrument of judgment appears again in Isaiah 11:4 (breath of his lips slaying the wicked) and 2 Thessalonians 2:8 (Paul applies this to Christ destroying the lawless one with the breath of his mouth). The judgment that sifts the nations in Isaiah 30 is the final judgment that Christ executes at his return (Matt 25:31-46: sifting sheep from goats).
30.29: A shadow: you shall have a song as in the night when a holy feast is kept — the night song of pilgrims going to the Rock of Israel (Tsur Yisrael). Paul identifies the Rock that accompanied Israel in the wilderness as Christ: the Rock was Christ (1 Cor 10:4). The night pilgrimage song becomes the Easter morning song — the night of weeping and crucifixion gives way to the dawn of resurrection (Ps 30:5: weeping may endure for a night, but joy comes in the morning). The journey to the Rock of Israel is the journey to Christ.
30.30: A revelation of God: YHWH causes his majestic voice to be heard and the descending blow of his arm to be seen — in furious anger, devouring fire, cloudburst, storm, and hailstones against Assyria. The divine warrior acts with total, overwhelming force. The NT frames Christ's second advent in these theophanic terms (Rev 19:11-21; 1 Thess 4:16: the Lord descends from heaven with a cry of command). The blow of YHWH's arm against Assyria is the partial historical enactment of what will be complete at the consummation.
30.31: A revelation of God: the Assyrians will be terror-stricken at the voice of YHWH when he strikes with his rod (šēbeṭ mûsāh — the rod of punishment). The Assyrian superpower is brought down not by human military force but by the divine voice alone. This is the consistent NT pattern of ultimate judgment: the word of Christ is the weapon (Rev 19:15 — the sword from his mouth strikes the nations; cf. 2 Thess 2:8). Human powers that seem invincible are unmade by the divine word.
30.32: A revelation of God: every stroke of the appointed staff falls to the sound of tambourines and lyres — YHWH fights with a brandished arm. The paradox of joyful worship accompanying divine judgment on the enemy is the NT's righteous wrath theme: Revelation 18:20 (rejoice over Babylon, O heaven!) and 19:1-3 (the great hallelujah over the fall of the great harlot). The vindication of justice is cause for worship, not cold satisfaction — the songs of the redeemed celebrate the righteousness of divine judgment.
30.33: A shadow: Topheth has long been prepared (Tōp̄et min-'etmôl hûkān) — it is made ready for the king; its pyre is deep and wide; YHWH's breath like a stream of sulfur kindles it. The burning place prepared for the Assyrian king (Sennacherib or the archetype of the destroyer) becomes in the NT the Gehenna of Matthew (5:22; 18:9; 23:33) and the lake of fire of Revelation 20:10-15: the fire prepared for the devil and his angels (Matt 25:41). Christ's warning about Gehenna in the Gospels stands on the foundation of Isaiah 30:33 — the burning place that YHWH prepared is the ultimate destination of all who align with the destroyer-king.

27.1: "In that day the LORD with his hard and great and strong sword will punish Leviathan the fleeing serpent, Leviathan the twisting serpent, and he will slay the dragon that is in the sea" — the cosmic battle: YHWH's sword against Leviathan, the primordial sea-dragon of ancient Near Eastern myth (cf. Job 41; Ps 74:14; 89:10). The three descriptions (fleeing, twisting, dragon-of-the-sea) denote the chaos-force in all its manifestations. Revelation 12:9: "The great dragon was hurled down — that ancient serpent called the devil, or Satan." 20:2-3: "He seized the dragon, that ancient serpent, who is the devil, or Satan, and bound him for a thousand years." Colossians 2:15: "having disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross." The sword that slays Leviathan in Isaiah 27:1 is the cross — the instrument by which Christ disarms the primordial serpent that has held creation in thrall since Genesis 3.
27.2: "In that day: 'A pleasant vineyard, sing of it!'" — the dramatic reversal of Isaiah 5's Song of the Vineyard. Isaiah 5 began with a song about the vineyard that yielded wild grapes and was destroyed; Isaiah 27 opens a new song about the vineyard that is now pleasant (ḥemed). The structural echo is deliberate: same form (a song about a vineyard), opposite content (destruction vs. restoration). John 15:1-5: "I am the true vine, and my Father is the gardener." The Beautiful Vineyard of Isaiah 27 is the vineyard as it becomes through Christ — the true vine who produces genuine fruitfulness where Israel, the failed vine, produced only wild grapes. The song of restoration is the song of the true vine.
27.3: "I, the LORD, am its keeper; every moment I water it. Lest anyone punish it, I keep it night and day" — YHWH as the diligent vineyard-keeper: watering continuously, guarding unceasingly. The contrast with Isaiah 5:6 ("I will command the clouds that they rain no rain upon it") is total: there the rain was withheld as judgment; here YHWH waters "every moment." John 10:28-29: "I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand. My Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all; no one can snatch them out of my Father's hand." The unceasing guarding of the vineyard in Isaiah 27:3 is the security of the sheep in the Father's and Son's hands — the restoration-vineyard is the community that cannot be lost because its Keeper never sleeps.
27.4: "I have no wrath. Would that I had thorns and briers to battle! I would march against them, I would burn them up together" — the remarkable divine self-restraint: YHWH declares no wrath against the restored vineyard; the only thing he would attack is the thorns and briers (the enemy). The thorns that were the judgment of Isaiah 5:6 ("briers and thorns shall grow up") are now the targets, not the contents, of the vineyard. Galatians 3:13: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us." The crown of thorns on Christ's head is the judgment-thorns taken into Christ himself — he bears the briers of the curse so that the restored vineyard is free of them. The wrath that YHWH says he has none of (toward his vineyard) is the wrath that Christ absorbed at the cross.
27.5: "Or let them lay hold of my protection, let them make peace with me, let them make peace with me" — the double invitation to peace: the repeated "make peace with me" (yaʿăśeh šālôm lî) is an urgent, gracious offer. The enemy (the thorns and briers of v4) is invited to lay hold of divine protection rather than face divine fire. Romans 5:1: "Therefore, since we have been justified through faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ." Ephesians 2:14: "For he himself is our peace." The double peace-invitation of Isaiah 27:5 is fulfilled in the double grace of the gospel: Christ both is our peace (Eph 2:14) and makes peace through the blood of his cross (Col 1:20). The invitation is irresistible in its urgency and genuine in its offer.
27.6: "In days to come Jacob shall take root, Israel shall blossom and put forth shoots and fill the whole world with fruit" — the eschatological fruitfulness: from the covenant people flowing outward to fill the whole world (tēbēl, the inhabited earth). The vocabulary is horticultural, but the scope is universal. John 15:5: "I am the vine; you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit." John 15:16: "I chose you and appointed you so that you might go and bear fruit — fruit that will last." Matthew 28:19: "Therefore go and make disciples of all nations." The "fill the whole world with fruit" of Isaiah 27:6 is the commission of Matthew 28 — the restored vineyard/vine produces fruit that spreads to the nations through the missionary movement rooted in Christ.
27.7: "Has he struck them as he struck those who struck them? Or have they been slain as their slayers were slain?" — the measured judgment of Jacob compared to the full judgment of their oppressors. The rhetorical question expects the answer "no": YHWH's discipline of his people is milder than his full judgment of the nations that oppressed them. Hebrews 12:5-7: "'My son, do not make light of the Lord's discipline, and do not lose heart when he rebukes you, because the Lord disciplines the one he loves.'" The distinction between discipline and judgment is the distinction between covenant chastisement (which produces righteousness) and final condemnation (which does not). Christ's suffering was not discipline but substitutionary judgment — he received the full blow ("as their slayers were slain") so that his people receive only the measured discipline of the beloved.
27.8: "Measure by measure, by exile you contended with them; he removed them with his fierce breath in the day of the east wind" — the measured nature of the exile as discipline: not annihilation but contention (rîb, as in a legal dispute) administered by measure. The east wind (rûaḥ qādîm) is both the literal sirocco that desiccates and the instrument of divine judgment (cf. Exod 10:13; Jer 18:17). The breath/wind (rûaḥ) of YHWH blows in judgment — but the same rûaḥ breathes life in John 20:22 ("he breathed on them and said, 'Receive the Holy Spirit'"). The fierce breath of judgment becomes the life-giving breath of Pentecost: same divine rûaḥ, transformative direction.
27.9: "Therefore by this the guilt of Jacob will be atoned for (yĕkuppar), and this will be the full fruit of the removal of his sin: when he makes all the stones of the altars like chalkstones crushed to pieces, no Asherah poles or incense altars will remain standing" — the atonement (kāpar, H3722) language of covenant restoration: the guilt covered by the purging of false worship. Hebrews 9:22: "Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness." The crushed altar-stones of Isaiah 27:9 are the removal of the competing loyalties — atonement is not merely the covering of guilt but the transformation of the worshiper, the end of divided allegiance. Colossians 3:5: "Put to death, therefore, whatever belongs to your earthly nature: sexual immorality, impurity, lust, evil desires and greed, which is idolatry." The altar-crushing of Isaiah 27:9 is the mortification of idolatry that follows from atonement through Christ.
27.10: "For the fortified city is solitary, a habitation deserted and forsaken, like the wilderness; there the calf grazes; there it lies down and strips its branches" — the abandoned city: the strong place become a pasture for calves. The same city that was fortified is now grazed by cattle — the power of human construction reduced to a meadow. Revelation 18:2: "Babylon the great has fallen!... She has become a dwelling for demons and a haunt for every impure spirit... a haunt for every unclean bird." The abandoned fortified city of Isaiah 27:10 is the type of every fallen empire — cities built on human power become wildernesses when YHWH withdraws his presence. Only the city whose builder and maker is God (Heb 11:10) will endure.
27.11: "When its boughs are dry, they are broken; women come and make a fire of them. For this is a people without discernment; therefore he who made them will not have compassion on them; he who formed them will show them no favor" — the dry branches broken for fuel: the judgment-language of withered wood. John 15:6: "If you do not remain in me, you are like a branch that is thrown away and withers; such branches are picked up, thrown into the fire and burned." Christ takes the very image of Isaiah 27:11 and applies it to those who do not abide in him as the true vine — the dry branches broken for fire are the judgment on those who remain outside the restored vineyard. The "people without discernment" (ʾên bînôt lô) are those who cannot perceive the vine that is their only source of life.
27.12: "In that day from the River Euphrates to the Brook of Egypt the LORD will thresh out the grain one by one, and you, O people of Israel, will be gathered one by one" — the great harvest: the divine threshing across the whole promised land (from Euphrates to Egypt's border = the full Abrahamic territory), gathering the covenant people grain by grain. Matthew 13:30: "Let both grow together until the harvest. At that time I will tell the harvesters: First collect the weeds and tie them in bundles to be burned; then gather the wheat and bring it into my barn." Revelation 14:14-16: "the earth was harvested." The great threshing of Isaiah 27:12 is the eschatological harvest that Christ describes in his parables and John depicts in Revelation — gathering "one by one" is the personal, specific gathering of every individual whom the Father has given to the Son (John 6:39).
27.13: "And in that day a great trumpet will be blown, and those who were lost in the land of Assyria and those who were driven out to the land of Egypt will come and worship the LORD on the holy mountain at Jerusalem" — the great trumpet that ends the exile and gathers the scattered. Matthew 24:31: "And he will send his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds, from one end of the heavens to the other." 1 Corinthians 15:52: "in a flash, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet. For the trumpet will sound, the dead will be raised imperishable, and we will be changed." 1 Thessalonians 4:16: "For the Lord himself will come down from heaven... with the trumpet call of God, and the dead in Christ will rise first." The great trumpet of Isaiah 27:13 that ends the Assyrian and Egyptian exiles is the type of the last trumpet that ends the entire exile of death — gathering the scattered elect to the holy mountain of the new Jerusalem (Rev 21:10).

31.1: Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help, relying on horses and chariots — but who do not look to the Holy One of Israel or seek the LORD! The fundamental covenant sin: substituting military-political alliance for YHWH. The Holy One of Israel who they refuse to look to is identified in the NT as Jesus (Mark 1:24; John 6:69). His invitation in Matthew 11:28-30 (Come to me, all who are weary and burdened) is the positive form of Isaiah 31:1's negative: look to me rather than to Egypt's chariots. The NT equivalent is 1 Corinthians 1:24 — Christ as the true power and wisdom that replaces military and intellectual strength.
31.2: Yet he too is wise and brings disaster — he does not take back his word. YHWH's wisdom stands against and outlasts the wisdom of Egypt's strategists. This is the exact framework Paul develops in 1 Corinthians 1:18-25: the foolishness of God is wiser than human wisdom, and the weakness of God is stronger than human strength. The wisdom that appears as disaster (the cross) is YHWH's word that he does not take back — the eternal plan (Eph 1:11) that proceeds through apparent defeat to certain victory. Against those who plot against YHWH, he rises; his word is irrevocable.
31.3: The Egyptians are human, not God — their horses are flesh, not spirit. When YHWH stretches out his hand, both the helper and the helped will fall. The flesh/spirit contrast is foundational to NT anthropology: what is born of the flesh is flesh; what is born of the Spirit is spirit (John 3:6). Paul: those who live according to the flesh cannot please God (Rom 8:8). The Egyptian military power Israel trusted is flesh — capable of impressive feats but unable to produce the spiritual new birth that Christ offers. When YHWH stretches out his hand, the flesh-systems collapse; the same hand raised over Egypt at the Exodus is now the hand of the risen Christ who has all authority (Matt 28:18).
31.4: As a lion growls over its prey and no company of shepherds can deter it — so the LORD of hosts will come down to wage war on Mount Zion. The divine warrior-lion descending on Zion is unstoppable. Revelation 5:5 identifies Jesus as the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David who has conquered. The lion who cannot be intimidated by the shepherds' noise is the Messiah who moves with absolute sovereign determination toward his purpose — and whose resurrection-victory cannot be undone by any earthly or heavenly opposition. His coming down to wage war on Zion is, paradoxically, the cross: where YHWH executes judgment on sin through his own Son.
31.5: Like birds hovering over their young — so the LORD of hosts will shield Jerusalem; shielding, he will deliver it; passing over, he will rescue it. The Passover echo (passing over, pasach) in the bird-hovering image is explicit: YHWH passes over Jerusalem as he passed over the doorposts in Egypt. Jesus uses the identical mother-bird image for his own posture toward Jerusalem: how often I have longed to gather your children together as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, but you were not willing (Matt 23:37; Luke 13:34). The bird who shields her young is YHWH's self-description — and Jesus embodies it as he weeps over the city and then opens his arms on the cross to shield it from the judgment it would not accept.
31.6: Turn back to him from whom the people of Israel have so deeply defected. The call to return (shuvu) from deep apostasy is the prophetic message Jesus intensifies and centralizes: Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand (Matt 3:2; 4:17; Mark 1:15). The depth of the defection — amaq sarah, profound moral departure — mirrors what Jesus finds in his generation (Matt 23's extended condemnation). The return he calls for is not a minor adjustment but the turning back from deep defection that Isaiah 31:6 names — made possible by the Spirit who enables genuine teshuvah (Acts 5:31; 11:18).
31.7: In that day every person will throw away the idols of silver and gold their own hands have made — things that have been their sin. The eschatological abandonment of handmade idols is the conversion pattern the NT consistently describes. Paul at Athens: God commands all people everywhere to repent, having overlooked the times of idol-worship (Acts 17:29-30). The Thessalonians turned to God from idols to serve the living and true God (1 Thess 1:9). 1 John 5:21: little children, keep yourselves from idols. The idol-throwing of Isaiah 31:7 is what happens when the Holy One of Israel is finally looked to — the false gods become obviously worthless in his presence.
31.8: The Assyrian will fall by a sword that is not of any man — no human sword will consume him. The defeat of the great enemy without conventional military action is fulfilled historically in the angel's overnight destruction of 185,000 Assyrians (37:36). The theological principle — victory by the not-of-human-hands sword — governs Christ's method of defeating the powers. Paul: the weapons of our warfare are not of the flesh but have divine power to destroy strongholds (2 Cor 10:4). The weapon is the word of God (Eph 6:17) and the Spirit — the sword that proceeds from the mouth of the returning Christ (Rev 1:16; 19:15) rather than from any human forge.
31.9: His stronghold will crumble in terror at the signal standard (nes) — declares YHWH, whose fire is in Zion and whose furnace is in Jerusalem. The signal standard raised on the mountain is developed in Isaiah 11:10 as the Root of Jesse lifted as a banner to which the nations rally. Jesus claims this fulfillment in John 12:32: when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself — the cross as the signal standard that causes every stronghold of the enemy to crumble. YHWH's fire in Zion is the Pentecost fire that descended on the disciples gathered in Jerusalem (Acts 2:3) — the same divine fire that Isaiah sees as the presence consuming the enemy is the Spirit-fire that empowers the church.

32.1: "Behold, a king will reign in righteousness, and princes will rule in justice." The righteous king (melek lĕṣedeq) is the messianic portrait Isaiah develops across the book: 9:6-7 (Prince of Peace, reigning on David's throne), 11:1-5 (the Branch ruling with righteousness), 16:5 (the throne established in steadfast love). In the NT, Jesus enters Jerusalem as the king (Matt 21:5: "Behold, your king is coming to you" — citing Zech 9:9), is interrogated by Pilate as to whether he is the king of the Jews (John 18:37: "You say that I am a king. For this purpose I was born and for this purpose I have come into the world"), and will reign at his return (Rev 19:16: "King of kings and Lord of lords"). The "princes ruling in justice" are the apostles and elders who govern the new community under Christ's rule.
32.2: "Each will be like a hiding place from the wind, a shelter from the storm, like streams of water in a dry place, like the shade of a great rock in a weary land." The fourfold description of the righteous king as refuge — hiding place, shelter, streams, and shade — is applied Christologically across the NT. Rev 7:15: "he who sits on the throne will shelter them with his presence" (literally, "will spread his tent over them"). John 14:2-3: "In my Father's house are many rooms... I go to prepare a place for you." The "shade of a great rock in a weary land" connects to 1 Cor 10:4 (the rock in the wilderness was Christ). The streams of water in a dry place are the "rivers of living water" that Jesus promises in John 7:38 as the Spirit given to those who believe in him.
32.3: "Then the eyes of those who see will not be closed, and the ears of those who hear will give attention." The opened eyes and attentive ears under the righteous king directly reverse the hardening commission of 6:9-10. Matt 11:5 is Jesus's self-description fulfilling this reversal: "the blind receive their sight and the deaf hear." Isa 35:5-6 uses the same opened-eyes/opened-ears motif for the coming salvation — Jesus's healings of the blind (John 9) and the deaf (Mark 7:31-37) are the enacted demonstrations that the righteous king of Isaiah 32 has arrived. The kingdom opens what the hardening closes.
32.4: "The heart of the hasty will understand and know, and the tongue of the stammerers will hasten to speak distinctly." The transformation of the slow-of-understanding and the tongue-tied anticipates the Pentecost event (Acts 2:4: "they began to speak in other tongues as the Spirit gave them utterance") and the Great Commission's effect across languages and peoples. The "stammering tongue" (lāšôn ʿilgîm) connects to Isa 28:11 ("by people of strange lips and with a foreign tongue, YHWH will speak to this people") — the unintelligible becomes intelligible under the Spirit's giving. Rom 8:26 extends this: "the Spirit helps us in our weakness. For we do not know what to pray for as we ought, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words."
32.5: "The fool will no more be called noble, nor the scoundrel said to be honorable." The eschatological reversal of false social honor is a consistent NT theme. Luke 16:15: "what is exalted among men is an abomination in the sight of God." Matt 23:27: "Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! For you are like whitewashed tombs, which outwardly appear beautiful, but within are full of dead people's bones." 1 Cor 1:26-28: "not many of you were wise according to worldly standards, not many were powerful, not many were of noble birth... God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise." The reversal of fool/noble is enacted in the cross (the "foolishness" of God wiser than human wisdom, 1:25).
32.6: "For the fool speaks folly, and his heart is busy with iniquity, to practice ungodliness, to utter error concerning YHWH, to leave the craving of the hungry unsatisfied, and to deprive the thirsty of drink." The fool's heart busy with iniquity — specifically depriving the hungry and thirsty — is the anti-portrait of the righteous king who feeds and waters (v.2: streams in a dry place). Matt 25:41-45 presents the ultimate judgment on those who did not feed the hungry or give the thirsty a drink: "I was hungry and you gave me no food, I was thirsty and you gave me no drink." The fool of Isaiah 32:6 is the one who passes by the hungry in Matt 25 and the man beaten in the ditch (Luke 10:30-32).
32.7: "As for the scoundrel — his devices are evil; he plans wicked schemes to ruin the poor with lying words, even when the plea of the needy is right." The scoundrel who ruins the poor with lies is the anti-shepherd figure. John 10:10: "The thief comes only to steal and kill and destroy" — the thief who scatters and exploits the sheep. Matt 7:15-20: "Beware of false prophets, who come to you in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves." Rev 13:5-6: the beast who speaks arrogant words against God. The contrast between the scoundrel of Isaiah 32:7 and the righteous king of 32:1 is the contrast between the prince of this world and Christ.
32.8: "But he who is noble plans noble things, and on noble things he stands." The noble person (nāḏîḇ, generous/magnanimous) who plans and stands by generosity is the portrait of the servant-leader that Jesus embodies. Matt 5:3-12: the beatitudes describe the character of those in the kingdom — the poor in spirit, the meek, the merciful. Luke 8:15: the good soil that hears the word and holds it fast in "an honest and good heart" (kardia kalē kai agathē). The contrast with the fool (v.5-7) and the scoundrel (v.7) establishes the moral binary of Isaiah's wisdom theology, resolved in Christ who is the wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:30).
32.9: "Rise up, you women who are at ease, hear my voice; you complacent daughters, give ear to my speech." The prophetic address to the complacent women anticipates the parable of the ten virgins (Matt 25:1-13: five were foolish and unprepared; "watch therefore, for you know neither the day nor the hour") and the warnings against self-satisfied ease. Luke 12:19-20: the soul who says "eat, drink, be merry" is called a fool. Rev 3:17: the church at Laodicea "you say, I am rich, I have prospered, and I need nothing, not realizing that you are wretched, pitiable, poor, blind, and naked." The ease of Isaiah 32:9 is the spiritual complacency that all three texts warn against.
32.10: "In little more than a year you will shudder, you complacent women; for the grape harvest fails, the fruit harvest will not come." The sudden reversal of the comfortable harvest — "in a little more than a year" — is the pattern of sudden judgment. Luke 17:26-30: "Just as it was in the days of Noah... they were eating and drinking and buying and selling and planting and building, but on the day when Lot went out from Sodom, fire and sulfur rained from heaven." 1 Thess 5:3: "While people are saying, 'There is peace and security,' then sudden destruction will come upon them as labor pains come upon a pregnant woman." The comfortable harvest that suddenly fails is the unguarded moment of Christ's coming (Matt 24:42-44).
32.11: "Tremble, you women who are at ease! Shudder, you complacent ones! Strip, and make yourselves bare, and tie sackcloth around your waist." The call to grief and mourning that shakes the comfortable is the posture of repentance. Jas 4:9: "Be wretched and mourn and weep. Let your laughter be turned to mourning and your joy to gloom. Humble yourselves before the Lord." Rev 18:7-8: the mourning for Babylon contrasted with her former luxury — "as she glorified herself and lived in luxury, so give her a like measure of torment and mourning." The sackcloth of Isaiah 32:11 is the voluntary grief of repentance as against the forced grief of judgment.
32.12: "Beat your breasts for the pleasant fields, for the fruitful vine." The mourning over lost agricultural fruitfulness is the lament over what pride and complacency destroyed. The beating of breasts — a gesture of grief — appears in Luke 23:48 at the crucifixion: "all the crowds... returned home beating their breasts." Luke 18:13: "the tax collector, standing far off, would not even lift up his eyes to heaven, but beat his breast, saying, 'God, be merciful to me, a sinner!'" The voluntary breast-beating of repentance (Luke 18) is the appropriate response to the loss described in Isaiah 32 — grief that leads to the mercy Christ offers.
32.13: "For the soil of my people growing up in thorns and briers, yes, for all the joyful houses in the exultant city." The land reverting to thorns and briers — the judgment imagery of 5:6; 7:23-25 — connects directly to the curse of Gen 3:18 ("thorns and thistles it shall bring forth for you"). The crown of thorns at the crucifixion (Matt 27:29; John 19:2) is the symbolic condensation of the full curse: Christ wears the thorns of the judgment-land on his head. The "joyful houses" that fall silent anticipate the NT warnings about Jerusalem's coming desolation (Matt 23:38; Luke 19:43-44).
32.14: "For the palace is forsaken, the populous city deserted; the hill and the watchtower will become dens forever, a joy of wild donkeys, a pasture of flocks." The forsaken palace and deserted city is a type of the Jerusalem judgment that Jesus predicts (Luke 21:5-6: "As for these things that you see, the days will come when there will not be left here one stone upon another that will not be thrown down"). The desolation of what was formerly "the joy" of the city is the same pattern as Matt 23:38 ("your house is left to you desolate"). The watchtower that becomes a pasture for flocks anticipates the Good Shepherd who reclaims the abandoned land.
32.15: "Until the Spirit is poured upon us from on high, and the wilderness becomes a fruitful field, and the fruitful field is deemed a forest." The Spirit-pouring from on high is the Pentecost promise. Acts 2:17-18 applies Joel 2:28-32 to Pentecost, but the Isaianic background of Spirit-pouring upon the community (Isa 32:15; 44:3; Ezek 36:26-27) is the broader OT foundation. John 7:38-39: "'Whoever believes in me, as the Scripture has said, rivers of living water will flow from his heart.' Now this he said about the Spirit, whom those who believed in him were to receive, for as yet the Spirit had not been given, because Jesus was not yet glorified." The wilderness-to-fruitful-field transformation by the Spirit is the new creation that begins at Pentecost.
32.16: "Then justice will dwell in the wilderness, and righteousness abide in the fruitful field." The Spirit's transformation of the land produces justice and righteousness in the transformed landscape. Matt 6:33: "seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness, and all these things will be added to you." Rom 14:17: "the kingdom of God is not a matter of eating and drinking but of righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit." The justice and righteousness that abide in the Spirit-transformed field are the character of the kingdom — not imposed by law but produced by the Spirit. Gal 5:22-23: the fruit of the Spirit is the new harvest of the fruitful field.
32.17: "And the effect of righteousness will be peace, and the result of righteousness, quietness and trust forever." The righteousness → peace sequence is Paul's atonement logic: "since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ" (Rom 5:1). The righteousness achieved at the cross produces the peace — not the peace produced by human moral achievement but the peace given as the consequence of divine righteousness imputed to the believer. Jas 3:18: "a harvest of righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace." Heb 12:11: "it yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it." The "quietness and trust forever" is the eschatological security of those who are justified.
32.18: "My people will abide in a peaceful habitation, in secure dwellings, and in quiet resting places." The eschatological rest in peaceful dwellings is the fulfillment Christ brings. John 14:2-3: "In my Father's house are many rooms... I go to prepare a place for you." Heb 4:9-10: "there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God, for whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works as God did from his." Rev 21:3: "Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God." The peaceful habitation of Isaiah 32:18 is the final state of the new creation — God and his people dwelling together in the rest Christ has prepared.
32.19: "And it will hail when the forest falls down, and the city will be utterly laid low." The hailstorm judgment that precedes the ultimate peace anticipates the apocalyptic hail sequences of Revelation: 8:7 (first trumpet: hail and fire mixed with blood); 11:19 (the temple opened, hailstorm); 16:21 (the seventh bowl: great hailstones). The falling of the forest and city is the judgment that clears the way for the new creation. The pattern is always: judgment on the old order, then the establishment of the peaceful habitation (v.18). Christ comes first as the one who endures the storm (8:24: "he rebuked the wind and the sea"); at his return, the storm of judgment clears the way for the new dwelling.
32.20: "Happy are you who sow beside all waters, who let the feet of the ox and the donkey range free." The beatitude-form (ʾašrêkem, "happy/blessed are you") that closes the chapter echoes the beatitude tradition of Psalms and the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:3-12). "Sowing beside all waters" is the fruitful labor of the Spirit-filled new creation — Isa 55:10-11 (the rain that makes the earth fruitful, like the word that accomplishes its purpose); John 12:24 (the grain that falls into the ground and dies brings forth much fruit). The ox and donkey ranging free recall the animals of Christ's entry into Jerusalem (Matt 21:2-5: "untie the donkey and the colt") and the manger scene (Luke 2:7) — the humble animals of labor free in the new creation's rest.

28.1: Woe to the proud crown of Ephraim's drunkards — their glorious beauty is a fading flower on the head of the fertile valley of those overcome with wine. The northern kingdom (Ephraim/Israel) is addressed first: its capital Samaria sat on a hill above the fertile valleys, its wealth coming from agriculture, but its leaders are drunk. The proud crown (atarah ge'ut) is both the literal garland worn by drunken revelers and a metaphor for the kingdom's misplaced pride. In the NT, the pride that is a crown of fading flowers is contrasted with the crown of life (Rev 2:10) and the imperishable crown (1 Cor 9:25) that the faithful receive — the kind of glory that does not wither.
28.2: See, the Lord has a powerful agent — like a devastating hailstorm, a destructive tempest, a flood of overwhelming water — he will throw it down to the ground with his hand. The agent of judgment (Assyria historically; any instrument of YHWH's wrath) comes with all the force of natural catastrophe: hail, tempest, flood. The three images are the weapons of the divine warrior. In the NT, the same imagery appears in Rev 16:21 (huge hailstones from heaven) as the final judgment instrument. The one who can deploy hail, tempest, and flood is the same one who calmed the storm at a word (Mark 4:39) — his power over chaos is the power of the Creator exercising sovereignty in both salvation and judgment.
28.3: The proud crown of Ephraim's drunkards will be trampled underfoot. The trampling-underfoot is the final indignity for what was a crown: the symbol of pride becomes mud. In the NT, the church is the body of the one before whom every knee will bow (Phil 2:10) — the reversal: not pride trampled, but humility exalted. The crown that Ephraim made of pride is contrasted with the crown of thorns pressed on Jesus (Matt 27:29) — human pride expressed as a mock-crown becomes the actual mark of the King who absorbs the judgment that pride deserves.
28.4: The beautiful crown on the fertile valley will be like a first-ripe fig before summer — whoever sees it swallows it as soon as he has it in his hand. The first-ripe fig is the most desirable fruit — it disappears instantly when found. Samaria's wealth and beauty are consumed immediately by the conquering power that seizes it. Jesus curses the fig tree (Matt 21:19-20) for having no fruit — the inverse of the first-ripe-fig eagerness; the covenant community with the appearance of fruitfulness but no actual fruit is more condemned than the Ephraim that at least attracted legitimate desire.
28.5: In that day YHWH of hosts will be a glorious crown and a beautiful diadem for the remnant of his people. The crown of the remnant is YHWH himself — the reverse of the false crown of Ephraim (v 1). YHWH as crown-of-his-people is the ultimate honor: the people do not make YHWH their ornament; YHWH makes himself their glory. In the NT, the church's boast is in Christ (1 Cor 1:31; Jer 9:24): the crown that does not fade (1 Pet 5:4) is the crown of the Chief Shepherd himself.
28.6: He will be a spirit of justice for those who sit in judgment, and strength for those who repel battle at the gate. YHWH provides both judicial wisdom (for judges) and military strength (for defenders) — the two functions of covenant governance. In the NT, the Spirit given at Pentecost is the same spirit of justice: the Paraclete who convicts the world concerning sin, righteousness, and judgment (John 16:8). The strength for defenders at the gate is the spiritual armor of Eph 6:10-18, provided by the same YHWH who stands at the gate of history.
28.7: But priest and prophet stagger from strong drink; they are confused by wine; they err in vision, they stumble in judgment. The covenant community's leaders — specifically the spiritual leaders, priest and prophet — are the ones who have given themselves to drunkenness. The erring in vision and stumbling in judgment is the consequence: the very functions that qualify them for office (vision for the prophet, judgment for the priest) are destroyed by the very sin they should warn against. In the NT, the requirement of sobriety for elders and overseers (1 Tim 3:2-3, Titus 1:7-8) is the positive construction of what this verse describes negatively.
28.8: All the tables are covered with vomit and filth; there is no clean space anywhere. The graphic physical description is not gratuitous — it is the prophetic literalization of the metaphorical condition: the tables where covenantal meals should be celebrated (the feast of 25:6!) are defiled by the physical consequence of the leaders' spiritual condition. The tables of the covenant community, which should be set with YHWH's provision (23:5: a table before me in the presence of my enemies), have become what the leaders have made of them.
28.9: Who is he trying to teach? Who is he trying to make understand his message? Babies just weaned from milk, infants just taken from the breast? The scoffers mock the prophet's message as elementary — fit only for infants. The contempt is aimed at the prophet's simple, repetitive instruction (v 10). In the NT, Paul subverts this contempt: the wisdom of the cross is foolishness to the world (1 Cor 1:18-25), and spiritual things are spiritually discerned — the mature can receive solid food, but those who scoff at elementary things are themselves infants (1 Cor 3:1-3, Heb 5:11-14).
28.10: For it is rule on rule, rule on rule; line on line, line on line; a little here, a little there. The mocking imitation of the prophet's repetitive teaching style — the children's rhyme quality (tsav latsav tsav latsav in Hebrew, onomatopoeic and demeaning). The scoffers experience YHWH's instruction as infantilizing and tedious. In the NT, the same dynamic: the gospel's simplicity (the proclamation of Christ crucified, 1 Cor 2:2) is dismissed by the sophisticated as beneath consideration — yet it is the power of God to salvation (Rom 1:16).
28.11: For by people of strange lips and a foreign tongue YHWH will speak to this people. Because the covenant people rejected YHWH's instruction through the prophet's familiar voice, YHWH will speak to them through the alien voice of the conquering Assyrian army — an incomprehensible tongue that makes his message unmistakable by its very unintelligibility. Paul quotes this verse in 1 Cor 14:21-22 in his discussion of tongues: tongues are a sign not for believers but for unbelievers — the strange-lips pattern of judgment. The Pentecost phenomenon (Acts 2:4-12) is the redemptive inversion: the strange tongues are now a sign of blessing rather than judgment, understandable to those who receive the Spirit.
28.12: He said to them: This is the place of rest; give rest to the weary; this is the refreshing. But they refused to listen. The rest and refreshing that YHWH offered through the prophet's simple instruction is the same rest Jesus offers: come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest (Matt 11:28-30, citing this verse). The Israel that refused to listen to the prophetic invitation to sabbath-rest is the same Israel that Jesus weeps over — so often I wanted to gather your children, but you were unwilling (Matt 23:37). The refusal of rest-in-YHWH is the fundamental covenant failure that judgment reverses.
28.13: So the word of YHWH will become to them: rule on rule, rule on rule; a little here, a little there — so that when they walk they will stumble backward and be injured, snared and taken captive. The instruction they mocked as infantile becomes the very mechanism of their stumbling: hearing YHWH's word but not understanding it results in walking into judgment blindly. The stumbling-backward image anticipates 8:14-15 (he will be a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense) and its NT application: the stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone — but it causes those who reject it to stumble (Rom 9:32-33, 1 Pet 2:8).
28.14: So hear the word of YHWH, you scoffers who rule this people in Jerusalem. The oracle pivots south to Jerusalem, where the same contempt for YHWH's word governs the city. The scoffers who rule (anshei latson — men of scoffing) are the leadership class of Jerusalem. Their specific sin is political pragmatism in the face of Assyrian threat — making diplomatic arrangements that bypass YHWH. In the NT, the same category of scoffer appears as those who deny the coming of the Lord (2 Pet 3:3-4): the eschatological scoffers are the covenant pattern applied to the last age.
28.15: You have said: We have made a covenant with death; with Sheol we have an agreement. When the overwhelming flood sweeps through, it will not reach us, for we have made lies our refuge and falsehood our hiding place. The covenant with death is the political alliance with Egypt or Assyria — YHWH uses this striking metaphor to expose what the alliance really is: a deal with the anti-life powers of the underworld. The lies and falsehood (shav and sheqer) are the treaty guarantees that cannot be trusted. In the NT: those who trust in the powers of death and Sheol cannot escape them; only the one who holds the keys of death and Hades (Rev 1:18) can nullify the covenant with death.
28.16: So this is what the Lord GOD says: See, I am laying a stone in Zion — a tested stone, a precious cornerstone, a sure foundation. Whoever trusts in it will not be put to shame. The cornerstone oracle is one of the most NT-cited verses in Isaiah. Paul quotes it in Rom 9:33 (combined with 8:14) and 1 Cor 3:11; Peter quotes it in 1 Pet 2:6; Jesus cites Ps 118:22 which is the companion text. The tested stone, precious cornerstone, sure foundation is the Messiah himself: he is the foundation that cannot be shaken (1 Cor 3:11 — no one can lay a foundation other than the one already laid, which is Jesus Christ). Whoever trusts in it will not be put to shame: faith in the Cornerstone is the only alternative to the covenant with death (v 15). The not-put-to-shame is quoted in Rom 10:11 as the universal promise of gospel faith.
28.17: I will measure with justice as my plumb line and righteousness as my level. The hail will sweep away your refuge of lies, and water will overwhelm your hiding place. YHWH's architectural measurement uses his own character — justice and righteousness — as the standards. What is built on the cornerstone of v 16 will be measured by these standards and found true; what was built on lies (v 15) will be swept away. The hail and overwhelming water are the judgment instruments of v 2 now directed specifically at the false refuge. In the NT, the house built on the rock vs. the house built on sand (Matt 7:24-27) is the same measurement: the storm reveals what the foundation was.
28.18: Your covenant with death will be cancelled, and your agreement with Sheol will not hold. When the overwhelming flood sweeps through, you will be beaten down by it. The nullification of the covenant with death: what they thought was their insurance policy proves worthless when judgment arrives. The overwhelming flood cannot be stopped by diplomatic agreements. In the NT, the covenant with death is cancelled not by the judgment coming upon the scoffers but by the resurrection of the Servant: death is swallowed up in victory (1 Cor 15:54-57, citing Isa 25:8). The covenant with death is annulled not because it never existed but because the one who holds the keys of death has broken its power.
28.19: Every time it sweeps through, it will seize you — morning after morning, by day and by night. The very understanding of the message will bring only terror. The relentless coming of judgment — morning and night, again and again — gives no rest. The terror-understanding is the terrible irony: finally understanding what YHWH has been saying (rule on rule, line on line) only means understanding the judgment that follows rejection. In the NT: those who have heard and rejected cannot escape what follows (Heb 10:26-27 — if we deliberately keep on sinning after receiving knowledge of the truth, no sacrifice for sins is left).
28.20: The bed is too short to stretch out on, and the covering too narrow to wrap yourself in. The proverb of inadequate coverage: the false security of the political alliance is like a bed that doesn't fit, covers that don't reach. No human arrangement is adequate to cover the full extent of the need. In the Christological reading: the only covering that is truly adequate is the robe of righteousness (61:10) and the garment of salvation — the coverage provided by the Servant's atonement that covers sin completely (53:6: YHWH laid on him the iniquity of us all).
28.21: For YHWH will rise up as he did at Mount Perazim (2 Sam 5:20) and as in the valley of Gibeon (Josh 10:12-14) — to carry out his task, his strange task, and to do his work, his alien work. YHWH's judgment on his own people is his strange work (zar maasse) and alien work (nokri pe'ulato) — what he does with his enemies (destroying nations) he now does with his covenant community. The strangeness signals that judgment of the covenant community is not YHWH's preference but his necessity: the love that disciplines is a different expression of the same fidelity. In the NT: judgment begins with the household of God (1 Pet 4:17) — the same strange-work logic applied to the church age.
28.22: Now stop your mocking, or your bonds will grow tighter. I have heard from YHWH a decree of complete destruction for the whole land. The final warning: the scoffing makes the bonds tighter — the attitude of contempt for YHWH's word compounds the guilt. The decree of complete destruction (kalah venechcretset) is the most severe prophetic word — it echoes 10:23 and anticipates the 70 CE judgment on Jerusalem. In the NT: every careless word spoken will be judged (Matt 12:36); the hardening of the heart under divine warning produces increasingly irreversible consequences.
28.23: Listen and hear my voice; pay attention and hear what I say. The prophetic call to hearing, in the form of the wisdom tradition's summons to a parable (the farmer, vv 24-28). The hearing-call after the judgment oracle invites the community to find wisdom in the agricultural parable that follows. In the NT, Jesus similarly uses agricultural parables to invite hearing: whoever has ears to hear, let him hear (Matt 13:9). The parable is the form of revelation that both conceals (from those who mock) and reveals (to those who listen).
28.24: Does a farmer spend the whole day plowing in order to plant? Does he keep breaking up the clods of his field forever? The agricultural wisdom: plowing has a purpose (breaking up the ground) and a limit (it must be followed by planting). The farmer knows when each phase ends. The parable's logic is that YHWH's judgments — the plowing and threshing — are purposive and limited, not eternal or gratuitous. In the NT, the parable of the soils (Matt 13:3-9) is the wisdom extension of this agricultural theology: the soil is prepared so that seed can be sown and bear fruit.
28.25: When he has leveled the surface, he broadcasts caraway, scatters cumin, plants wheat in rows, barley in its proper place, and emmer in its border. The variety of seeds sown in the proper manner for each — the farmer's wisdom is in knowing which seed goes where and how. YHWH's wisdom in dealing with his people is similarly differentiated: different judgments and mercies are applied in the right measure at the right time. The diversity of seeds is the diversity of the covenant community — each receiving what is appropriate for growth.
28.26: His God instructs him in the right way and teaches him. The farmer's agricultural wisdom comes from God: it is not merely practical knowledge but divinely taught skill. This grounds the parable's theology: YHWH is the one who teaches both the farmer and the prophet, both agriculture and covenant relationship. In the NT, the Spirit is the divine teacher who guides into all truth (John 16:13) — the same divine instruction applied to the new covenant community.
28.27: Caraway is not threshed with a sledge, nor does a cartwheel roll over cumin — caraway is beaten with a stick, and cumin with a rod. The wisdom of proportional threshing: small seeds require gentle treatment. The heavy sledge and cartwheel appropriate for grain would crush the delicate caraway. YHWH's judgment is similarly calibrated: the measure of discipline is appropriate to what is being refined. The NT equivalent: God is faithful and will not let you be tempted beyond what you can bear (1 Cor 10:13) — the divine calibration of testing to the capacity of the one being tested.
28.28: Grain for bread must be crushed, but a farmer does not go on threshing it forever. He drives his cartwheel over it, but his horses do not crush it. The limit of threshing: grain must be processed, but not destroyed. The crushing is purposive (to produce flour for bread) and has an end-point. The bread that results from the crushed grain is the NT's broken bread: this is my body, broken for you (1 Cor 11:24). The Servant who is crushed (53:5 — he was crushed because of our iniquities) is the grain-for-bread that YHWH threshes with purpose — not for destruction but for the bread of life.
28.29: All of this too comes from YHWH of hosts, whose counsel (eitsah) is wonderful and whose skill (tushiyah) is great. The parable closes with a doxology of divine wisdom: the agricultural wisdom that gives the parable its form, the prophetic wisdom that judges and redeems, and the eschatological wisdom that governs all of history — all come from the same source. The wonderful-counsel of YHWH is the same wonderful-counselor attribute of the Davidic child-king (9:6) — the Messiah who embodies YHWH's wisdom is the fulfillment of the agricultural wisdom the farmer received from God.

29.1: Woe to Ariel — the mysterious title for Jerusalem (Ariel means either lion of God or altar hearth). Add year to year; let the festivals continue their cycle. The festivals continuing is bitter irony: Jerusalem keeps its religious calendar while continuing in the spiritual condition that makes the feasts hollow. Jesus makes the same charge: you travel land and sea to make a single proselyte (Matt 23:15), while maintaining the outward forms of devotion. The religious cycle without repentance is the covenant community's deepest self-deception.
29.2: Yet I will besiege Ariel; there will be mourning and lamentation, and she will become to me like an altar hearth. The altar-hearth (Ariel) becomes the city of destruction — a grim wordplay. The city built around the altar of sacrifice becomes itself an altar on which offerings are consumed. In the NT, the temple's destruction in 70 CE is the fulfillment: the place of sacrifice is itself sacrificed. Jesus weeps that Jerusalem did not know the time of her visitation (Luke 19:44) — the city that could not recognize YHWH's arrival becomes the city of mourning.
29.3: I will encamp against you on every side; I will besiege you with a mound and raise siege-works against you. The siege of Jerusalem by YHWH himself — using enemy armies as his instrument — is the supreme covenant-judgment: the divine protector becomes the divine besieger. The NT application: the siege of Jerusalem by Rome in 66-70 CE is described by Jesus as the days of vengeance to fulfill all that is written (Luke 21:22). The judgment-from-outside on the covenant community is YHWH's judgment executed through human agents.
29.4: Then you will be brought low; you will speak from the ground, and your words will come up from the dust; your voice will be like one from the dead, chirping from the ground. The low-speaking from the ground and dust is the posture of the defeated city: Jerusalem, which spoke from the heights of Zion (2:3: from Zion the law will go out), now speaks from the grave. The voice from the dust is the mourning whisper of the vanquished. Yet in the Christological pattern, it is the Servant who descends to the dust of death (Ps 22:15) and speaks from the lowest place — and is vindicated and raised from that same dust.
29.5: But the horde of your enemies will become like fine dust, and the multitude of the ruthless like blowing chaff. And in a sudden moment, YHWH will intervene. The sudden reversal: the besieging army that was about to consume Jerusalem will itself be consumed like chaff. The sudden-moment pattern (cf. 37:36 — the angel of YHWH put to death 185,000 Assyrians overnight) is the pattern of divine intervention that changes everything instantly. In the NT, the Son of Man coming on the clouds (Matt 24:30) is the ultimate sudden intervention — the parousia as the decisive reversal of all that opposes YHWH's people.
29.6: You will be visited by YHWH of hosts with thunder and earthquake and great noise, with storm and tempest and flame of devouring fire. The theophanic arsenal — thunder, earthquake, noise, storm, tempest, fire — is the same weapons-of-the-divine-warrior imagery as chapter 13. YHWH's visitation is always attended by these forces: they are the peripheral phenomena of his presence. The NT: at the transfiguration, a voice came from a bright cloud (Matt 17:5); at the cross, the earth shook and rocks split (Matt 27:51); at the end, the same cosmic phenomena attend the return of the Son of Man.
29.7: And the multitude of all the nations that fight against Ariel — all who fight against her and her fortifications — will be like a dream, like a vision in the night. The nations besieging Jerusalem will dissolve like a dream: what seemed overwhelmingly real (the siege, the threat) will prove insubstantial when YHWH acts. The dream-dissolution is the anti-Nebuchadnezzar image: his vision of the statue dissolved before the stone from heaven (Dan 2:34-35, 44-45). In the NT, the same dissolution of the enemies of YHWH's city: the new Jerusalem has no need for defense because its enemies have melted away before the glory of the Lamb (Rev 21:24-27).
29.8: As when a hungry man dreams of eating and wakes still hungry, or when a thirsty man dreams of drinking and wakes still craving — so the nations that fight against Zion will be when they wake. The hunger-and-thirst that cannot be satisfied by dreaming is the condition of those who oppose YHWH's purposes: they strain toward what they cannot attain. In the NT, the hunger and thirst that is satisfied is the hunger and thirst for righteousness (Matt 5:6: they will be filled) — the opposite of the frustrated dream-hunger of YHWH's enemies. The bread of life and living water that Jesus gives are the satisfaction that ends all dream-hunger.
29.9: Be stunned and bewildered; be blinded and stay blind! They are drunk, but not with wine; they stagger, but not with strong drink. The spiritual stupor of Jerusalem's people who have seen the signs and understood nothing. The drunk-but-not-with-wine is the same diagnostic as 28:1, 7 — spiritual confusion that mimics physical intoxication. The not-from-wine specification is crucial: no natural substance causes this condition; only the withdrawal of divine understanding can produce it. Paul applies this logic in Rom 11:7-8 (quoting Isa 29:10 and Deut 29:4): Israel has not obtained what it was seeking, but the elect obtained it — the rest were hardened.
29.10: For YHWH has poured out on you a spirit of deep sleep; he has shut your eyes — the prophets — and covered your heads — the seers. The divine role in the hardening: YHWH pours out the spirit of deep sleep on those whose repeated rejection of his word has brought them to the point of irreversibility. The closing of the prophets' eyes and seers' heads is the covenant community's judgment: the mediators of revelation are themselves shut down, leaving the community without guidance. Paul quotes this verse (Rom 11:8) in his discussion of Israel's hardening — the eyes closed so that they cannot see is the judgment side of the Servant's mission to open eyes (42:7).
29.11: And the vision of all this has become like the words of a sealed book. When someone gives it to a literate person, saying Read this, he answers I cannot, for it is sealed. The sealed book of prophetic vision makes reading impossible even for the literate: having the physical text is not the same as having access to its meaning. In the NT: the scroll of Revelation (Rev 5:1-5) is sealed with seven seals and no one in heaven or on earth can open it — until the Lamb who was slain takes it and opens it. The sealed book of Isaiah 29 finds its final form in the sealed scroll that only the Lion of Judah/Lamb can open.
29.12: And when the book is given to one who cannot read, saying Please read this, he answers I cannot read. The illiterate person also cannot access the sealed vision — neither literacy nor illiteracy can open what YHWH has sealed. The double impossibility (literate and illiterate both fail) establishes that no human capacity can unlock divine revelation. In the NT: flesh and blood did not reveal this to you, but my Father in heaven (Matt 16:17); the natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit of God (1 Cor 2:14). The Spirit's revelation is the only key to the sealed book.
29.13: And the Lord said: Because this people draw near with their mouth and honor me with their lips, while their heart is far from me, and their fear of me is a commandment of men learned by rote. The lips-heart gap is the prophetic diagnosis of religious formalism: elaborate verbal devotion that originates from human tradition rather than from genuine encounter with YHWH. Jesus quotes this verse directly in Matt 15:8-9 (and Mark 7:6-7) as his critique of the Pharisees' hand-washing tradition: in vain they worship me, teaching as doctrines the precepts of men. The application to the NT community is direct and intentional: this is the diagnostic that distinguishes living faith from religious performance throughout the canon.
29.14: Therefore, watch — I am about to do something astonishing among this people, something astonishing and astounding; the wisdom of the wise will perish, and the discernment of the discerning will be hidden. YHWH's response to the lips-heart religion is not refinement of the existing system but the radical new act that confounds all human wisdom. Paul quotes this verse in 1 Cor 1:19 (I will destroy the wisdom of the wise; the intelligence of the intelligent I will frustrate) as the description of what God did in the cross: the crucifixion of Christ is the wisdom-destroying astonishing-thing that makes all human religious calculation irrelevant. The cross is the fulfillment of this verse: the greatest wisdom-confounding event in history.
29.15: Woe to those who go to great lengths to hide their plans from YHWH, who work in darkness and say Who sees us? Who knows us? The theological assumption behind hiding from YHWH is that he is limited in sight and knowledge — the same error as Adam hiding in the garden (Gen 3:8-9). In the NT: there is nothing hidden that will not be disclosed (Luke 8:17, 12:2-3); God will bring to light what is hidden in darkness and expose the motives of the heart (1 Cor 4:5). The YHWH who sees in secret (Matt 6:4, 6, 18) is the one from whom nothing can be hidden.
29.16: How you have everything backwards! Should the potter be regarded as no different from the clay? Should what is made say of its maker He did not make me? Should the pot say of the potter He has no understanding? The potter-clay inversion is the fundamental ontological error: the creature claiming equality with or independence from the Creator. Paul uses this verse extensively in Rom 9:20-21 in his discussion of divine sovereignty in election: does not the potter have the right to make from the same lump of clay some pottery for special purposes and some for common use? The NT application is not about fatalism but about the creature's inability to overrule the Creator's purposes — including the purpose of the Servant's atoning work.
29.17: Is it not just a little while until Lebanon becomes a fruitful orchard, and the fruitful orchard is considered a forest? The reversal of nature — wilderness to orchard, orchard to forest — is YHWH's sovereign ability to transform the landscape of his purposes. The little-while urgency marks the imminent reversal that the following verses describe (vv 18-19): the deaf will hear, the blind will see, the meek will find joy. The transformation of Lebanon into orchard is the de-wildernessing of what was untamed — the same creational transformation of 32:15-16 and 35:1-7.
29.18: In that day the deaf will hear the words of a book, and out of gloom and darkness the eyes of the blind will see. The reversal of the sealed-book blindness of vv 11-12 and the spirit-of-deep-sleep of v 10: those who could not read the sealed book will hear it; those whose eyes were closed will see. Jesus applies these specific reversals to his own ministry: go tell John what you hear and see — the blind receive sight, the deaf hear (Matt 11:4-5, citing Isa 35:5-6 and 29:18). The opening of deaf ears and blind eyes is the Servant's commission (42:7: to open eyes that are blind and free captives from prison) and the sign that the Messianic age has arrived.
29.19: The meek will find renewed joy in YHWH, and the poorest among people will exult in the Holy One of Israel. The meek (anawim) and the poor (evyonim) — the same community as the afflicted of 61:1 — are the specific recipients of the eschatological reversal. They find joy in YHWH, not merely in improved circumstances: the source of the joy is YHWH's character (Holy One of Israel) not YHWH's gifts alone. The meek who inherit the earth (Matt 5:5) and the poor in spirit who inherit the kingdom (Matt 5:3) are the NT form of these same anawim-evyonim who exult in the Holy One.
29.20: For the tyrant will be no more, and the scoffer will vanish, and all who look for opportunity to do wrong will be cut off. The three classes eliminated: tyrant, scoffer (the mockiers of 28:14), and those who trap the innocent (v 21). The removal of these three produces the social conditions for the meek and poor to flourish (v 19). In the NT, the casting out of the devil (John 12:31: the ruler of this world will be driven out) and the final judgment on the beast and false prophet (Rev 19:20) are the eschatological fulfillment: the tyrant and scoffer of cosmic scope are finally removed.
29.21: Those who trap a man with a word, who lay snares for the one who rebukes corruption at the gate, and who use empty claims to turn away justice. The snare-setters who trap with words: the specific form of oppression in the judicial setting (the gate). The ones who rebuke corruption at the gate — the prophets and honest judges — are the ones being snared.
