The way-in-the-sea language is picked up in John the Baptist's role: Mark 1:2-3 cites both Malachi 3:1 and Isa 40:3 (prepare the way of the Lord, make straight paths for him) to describe John as the one making the eschatological way. The way-in-the-sea becomes the way in the wilderness (v 19), and both are fulfilled in the one who is himself the Way (John 14:6).
43.17: Who leads out chariot and horse, army and warrior; they lie down together, they will not rise up — they are extinguished, quenched like a wick. The Exodus destruction of Pharaoh's army (Exod 14:28) is the past event from which the new Exodus is predicted: the first enemy destroyed in the sea provides the template for the eschatological enemy's destruction. They will not rise up; they are extinguished. In the NT, the final enemy whose destruction mirrors this is death itself: the last enemy to be destroyed is death (1 Cor 15:26). The Exodus destruction of the Egyptian army is the type of the resurrection's destruction of death — the ultimate extinguishing of what opposed YHWH's people.
43.18: Do not remember the former things or consider the things of old. The command to forget the former Exodus is startling — YHWH's greatest redemptive act is being overshadowed by a coming act. The former-thing forgetting is not historical amnesia but the recognition that the new act will be so much greater that the old becomes the lesser type. In the NT: Paul counts his former things as loss for the sake of gaining Christ (Phil 3:7-8) and forgets what is behind while straining toward what is ahead (Phil 3:13). The do-not-remember-the-former-things is the eschatological posture of the one who lives in the new creation.
43.19: See, I am doing a new thing; now it springs up — do you not perceive it? I am making a way in the wilderness and streams in the wasteland. The new thing (hadashah — the eschatological novelty) is the second great NT theme verse: if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; the old has passed away, the new has come (2 Cor 5:17). The way-in-the-wilderness anticipates John the Baptist making the way straight (Isa 40:3, cited in all four Gospels) and Jesus declaring himself the Way. The streams-in-the-wasteland is the living water Jesus offers (John 4:10, 7:37-38). This verse is the seed from which the entire NT theology of newness — new covenant, new creation, new heart — grows.
43.20: The wild animals will honor me, the jackals and the ostriches — because I provide water in the wilderness, rivers in the wasteland, to give drink to my chosen people. The creation-response to YHWH's provision extends even to the wilderness creatures: the jackals and ostriches (symbols of desolation in Isaiah's judgment oracles, 13:21-22, 34:13) now honor YHWH because the wilderness they inhabit has been watered by his provision. In the NT, the restoration of creation is the scope of the gospel: the creation waits in eager expectation for the children of God to be revealed (Rom 8:19). The wild animals honoring YHWH anticipate the new creation's harmony — the wolf lying with the lamb (65:25).
43.21: The people I formed for myself will declare my praise. The formed-for-myself people are the chosen witnesses of vv 10-12, now formed specifically for doxology: their purpose is praise. In the NT: you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession, that you may declare the praises of him who called you out of darkness into his wonderful light (1 Pet 2:9). The praise-declaring purpose of the covenant community is the NT form of YHWH's word here — the community exists to proclaim what YHWH has done.
43.22: Yet you have not called on me, Jacob; you have grown weary of me, Israel. The indictment: the people formed for praise (v 21) have not prayed. The weariness of Israel toward YHWH inverts the weariness the Servant never showed (42:4: he will not grow faint or be discouraged). In the NT, the contrast is between Israel's failure to call and the NT community's commission to pray without ceasing (1 Thess 5:17); between growing weary of YHWH and running with endurance the race set before us, fixing our eyes on Jesus (Heb 12:1-2). The community that forgets to call on its Creator is the shadow that the Servant's perseverance corrects.
43.23: You have not brought me sheep for burnt offerings or honored me with your sacrifices. I have not burdened you with grain offerings or wearied you with frankincense. The accusation is that Israel's neglect of YHWH is not YHWH's fault — he made worship accessible and undemanding, but they still did not come. In the NT, Jesus makes the same point differently: come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest — my yoke is easy and my burden is light (Matt 11:28-30). The true sacrifice that YHWH desires (a broken spirit, a broken and contrite heart — Ps 51:17) is not burdensome; the failure to worship is the community's willful neglect, not YHWH's inaccessibility.
43.24: You have not bought any fragrant calamus for me or lavished the fat of your sacrifices on me. Instead, you have burdened me with your sins and wearied me with your offenses. The reversal: Israel accuses YHWH of making worship burdensome (the common complaint against religious obligation) but the indictment flips it — they have burdened YHWH with their sins. The weight of sin borne by YHWH is the image that becomes explicit in 53:4-6: surely he bore our infirmities... YHWH laid on him the iniquity of us all. The sin-burden that Israel placed on YHWH is borne by the Servant as the culmination of what YHWH bore throughout the covenant's history.
43.25: I, even I, am he who blots out your transgressions for my own sake, and I will not remember your sins. The emphatic double I (anî hû anî) — the same structure as v 11 and v 13 — now applied to forgiveness: YHWH is the sole forgiver as he is the sole savior. The for-my-own-sake grounds forgiveness in YHWH's character, not in Israel's merit. The blotting-out (mocheh) is the image of wiping a slate clean — the same image in Col 2:14 (having blotted out the handwriting of ordinances that was against us). The I-will-not-remember-your-sins is the new covenant promise of Jer 31:34 (I will forgive their wickedness and will remember their sins no more). The scribes at Capernaum ask the right question: who can forgive sins but God alone? (Mark 2:7) — and Jesus answers by forgiving, claiming the authority reserved for the anî hû of Isa 43:25.
43.26: Remind me; let us argue the case together; state your case, that you may be proved right. YHWH invites Israel to the court: if you have a counter-argument, make it. The legal-argument invitation exposes the emptiness of any self-justification attempt — not because the process is rigged but because Israel has no case. In the NT: if we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves and the truth is not in us (1 John 1:8). The court that YHWH opens in Isa 43:26 is the court in which the entire human race stands — the day of judgment at which no one can stand on their own righteousness. Only the righteousness credited through faith (Rom 3:21-22) provides ground to stand in that court.
43.27: Your first father sinned, and your representatives have rebelled against me. The first father — either Adam (whose sin in the garden established the pattern) or Jacob (whose history of striving-with-God prefigures Israel's) — sinned from the beginning. The mediators (representatives — the priests and prophets) amplified rather than corrected the sin. In the NT, Paul traces the same genealogy of sin: sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death spread to all people (Rom 5:12). The first father's sin is the original problem for which the Servant is the solution: just as through one man's sin, so through one man's righteous act, life comes to all (Rom 5:18-19).
43.28: So I will bring disgrace on the dignitaries of your sanctuary, and I will devote Jacob to total destruction and Israel to scorn. The judgment that the entire chapter moves toward: the sanctuary's dignitaries (the priesthood that failed to mediate) are disgraced; the covenant community is given to destruction (cherem — the holy war ban). The destruction of the sanctuary and its leadership is the 70 CE event that Jesus predicted (Matt 24:2: not one stone will be left on another). But the destruction of the old system opens the way for the new: the veil torn from top to bottom (Matt 27:51) when the Servant dies is the disgracing of the old sanctuary's boundary that makes YHWH's presence accessible to all.

46.1: A revelation of God: Bel bows down, Nebo stoops (Bēl kāra' Nĕb̠ô šōaḥ) — the chief gods of Babylon collapse as their images are loaded onto animals for deportation. The irony is devastating: gods who should carry their worshippers are themselves carried, a burden for exhausted beasts. Paul echoes this when he describes what the Corinthians were: led astray to mute idols, however you were led (1 Cor 12:2). The idol-god that must be carried to safety is the anti-type of the God who carries his people — established in vv. 3-4. Christ who leads captivity captive (Eph 4:8, citing Ps 68:18) is the antitype of Bel and Nebo going into captivity.
46.2: A revelation of God: they stoop and bow down together; they cannot save the burden — they themselves go into captivity. The total impotence of Babylon's gods at the moment of crisis — they cannot even save themselves, let alone their worshippers — is the verdict on every false absolute. Revelation 18:2 applies this logic to the fall of spiritual Babylon: fallen, fallen is Babylon the great! Every power that claims divine authority but cannot deliver at the crisis moment collapses under the weight it claimed to bear.
46.3: A shadow: šimĕ'û 'ēlay b̠êt ya'ăqōb̠... haśśûnim min-habeṭen hannĕśū'im min-rāḥam — listen to me, house of Jacob, all who remain of Israel, who have been borne by me from before birth, carried from the womb. The contrast with the idol of vv. 1-2 is complete: the idol must be carried; YHWH carries. He has borne Israel from the womb — the parent-who-carries image. This is the God who carries that Christ reveals: Matthew 11:28-30 (come to me, all who are weary and heavy-laden, and I will give you rest); 1 Peter 5:7 (cast all your anxieties on him, because he cares for you). The God who carried Israel from the womb is the God who carries his people through the Son.
46.4: A shadow: wĕ'ad-ziqnāh 'ănî hû' wĕ'ad-śêb̄āh 'ănî 'esb̄ōl 'ănî 'āśîtî wĕ'ănî 'eśśā' wĕ'ănî 'esb̄ōl wĕ'ămallēṭ — even to old age I am he; even to gray hairs I will carry you. I have made, and I will bear; I will carry, and I will save. The threefold carrying-promise (I will bear / I will carry / I will save) framed by the absolute 'ănî hû' (I am he). Hebrews 13:8 (Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever) is the NT application: the faithfulness that carries Israel from womb to gray hair is the faithfulness that never changes in the incarnate Son. Christ who is the same from before creation to the last day carries his people through the whole span of existence.
46.5: A revelation of God: to whom will you liken me and make me equal? — lĕmî tedammĕyûnî wĕtašwû wĕtamdĕlûnî wĕnimsāl. The incomparability challenge that Isaiah 40:18,25 has already raised is pressed again here after the idol-polemic. The NT's answer to the incomparability question is Christological: Philippians 2:9-11 (the name above every name); Colossians 2:9 (in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily); Hebrews 1:3-4 (having become as much superior to angels as the name he has inherited is more excellent than theirs). There is no one like YHWH — and the fullness of that incomparable YHWH is present in Christ.
46.6: A revelation of God: those who lavish gold and weigh silver hire a goldsmith to make a god — then they bow down and worship. The economic investment in the idol (gold, silver, craftsman's fee) followed by worship is the description of all idolatry: human resources poured into what cannot save. Paul diagnoses the same dynamic in Colossians 3:5: greed is idolatry — the investment of ultimate desire in finite objects. The idol-maker of Isaiah 46:6 and the greedy man of Colossians 3:5 are engaged in the same act: constructing and worshipping what they have made.
46.7: A revelation of God: they lift it to their shoulder, carry it, set it in its place — it stands there; it cannot move from its place. One cries out to it; it does not answer or save him from his trouble. The idol that cannot move, answer, or save is the consistent verdict of the idol-polemic (Ps 115:4-7; Isa 44:17-18). Christ as the true answerer: John 11:43-44 (Jesus calls, Lazarus comes out); John 14:14 (if you ask me anything in my name, I will do it); Hebrews 4:16 (that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need). The idol cannot save from trouble; Christ saves from the ultimate trouble.
46.8: A revelation of God: remember this and consider; recall it to mind, you transgressors. The call to memory (zikrû-zō't wĕhitbōnānû) is a call to intellectual engagement with the evidence — YHWH does not demand faith without reason but with it. The same epistemological seriousness underlies the NT: 2 Peter 1:16 (we did not follow cleverly devised myths, but were eyewitnesses of his majesty); Acts 1:3 (he presented himself alive with many proofs). The God who demands that Israel remember and reason is the God who provides evidence through the resurrection.
46.9: A direct Christological claim grounding. 'ănî 'ēl wĕ'ên 'ôd 'Ĕlōhîm wĕ'ep̄es kāmônî — I am God, and there is no other; I am God, and there is none like me. The absolute monotheism of Isaiah 46:9 is the very claim that the NT applies to Christ: John 10:30 (I and the Father are one); John 14:9 (whoever has seen me has seen the Father); Colossians 1:19 (in him all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell). The 'ên 'ôd (none other) of Isaiah 46:9 is not abandoned in the NT but filled with Trinitarian content — the same YHWH who is none other now reveals himself through the Son.
46.10: A direct reference. maggîd mē'rēšît 'aḥărît ûmiqkedem 'ăšer lō'-na'ăśû — declaring the end from the beginning, and from ancient times things not yet done. The divine capacity to declare the future and fulfill it is YHWH's unique credential. Jesus applies this to himself: John 13:19 (I am telling you this now, before it takes place, so that when it does take place you may believe that I am he). Luke 24:44-45 (everything written about me in the Law, the Prophets, and the Psalms must be fulfilled). The one who declares the end from the beginning in Isaiah 46:10 is the Christ who fulfills every word spoken about him.
46.11: A revelation of God: the bird of prey from the east — a man of YHWH's counsel from a far country — YHWH has spoken and will bring it to pass; he has purposed and will do it. The irresistibility of divine purpose ('ānî dibbartî 'ap̄-'ăb̄î'ennāh yāṣartî 'ap̄-'e'eśennāh — I have spoken, and I will bring it; I have purposed, and I will do it) is the ground of eschatological confidence. Acts 4:28 applies this to the cross: those who crucified Christ did what YHWH's hand and plan had predestined. Ephesians 1:11 (in him we have an inheritance, having been predestined according to the purpose of him who works all things according to the counsel of his will) is the NT form of Isaiah 46:11's irresistibility.
46.12: A direct prophecy. šimĕ'û 'ēlay 'abbîrê lēb̠ hārĕḥôqîm min-haṣṣĕdāqāh — listen to me, you stubborn of heart, you who are far from righteousness. The call to the hard-hearted who are far from righteousness is the posture of grace that Christ embodies: he came not to call the righteous but sinners (Matt 9:13); he ate with tax collectors and sinners (Luke 15:1-2) precisely because they were far from righteousness. The God who calls to those far from righteousness in Isaiah 46:12 is the God who draws near to them in Christ.
46.13: A direct prophecy. qērab̠tî ṣidqātî lō' tirḥāq ûtĕšû'ātî lō' tĕ'aḥēr — I bring near my righteousness; it is not far off, and my salvation will not delay. I will put salvation in Zion for Israel my glory. This is the compressed gospel announcement: YHWH's righteousness (ṣidqāh) and salvation (tĕšû'āh) are near, not delayed, and are placed in Zion. Romans 3:21-26 announces the fulfillment: now the righteousness of God has been manifested apart from the law... the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ. 1 Corinthians 1:30 (Christ Jesus became to us wisdom from God, and righteousness and sanctification and redemption). The salvation placed in Zion is the crucified and risen Christ — Israel's glory and the world's hope.

45.1: Thus says the LORD to his anointed, to Cyrus, whose right hand I have grasped, to subdue nations before him. A pagan king receives the title mashiach — anointed — making Cyrus the only non-Israelite in Scripture explicitly called YHWH's messiah. He is a type, not the fulfillment: where Cyrus frees physical exiles for earthly return, the true Anointed One (the Christ, the Messiah) frees humanity from the exile of sin for return to the Father. The grasped right hand of Cyrus anticipates Isaiah 53:10-11 where YHWH's own hand accomplishes what no pagan king ultimately can: the willing offering of the Servant who makes many righteous.
45.2: I will go before you and level the exalted places; I will break in pieces the doors of bronze and cut through the bars of iron. YHWH going before his agent — the divine vanguard-pattern that runs from the Exodus pillar of cloud through the Good Shepherd who goes before his sheep (John 10:4). The leveling of exalted places and the breaking of bronze doors is the language of liberation. Hebrews 2:14-15: Christ destroyed the one who has the power of death, and delivered all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery. The bronze doors that imprisoned the exiles are the death-doors that Christ breaks through at the resurrection.
45.3: I will give you the treasures of darkness and the hoards in secret places, that you may know that it is I, the LORD, the God of Israel, who call you by your name. Hidden treasures given so that the recipient may know YHWH. Colossians 2:3: in him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge. The treasures of darkness that YHWH reveals to Cyrus become, in Christ, the full disclosure of divine wisdom. John 17:3: this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent. The ultimate goal of divine revelation — knowing YHWH — reaches its telos in the knowledge of Christ.
45.4: For the sake of my servant Jacob, and Israel my chosen, I have called you by your name; I have named you, though you do not know me. Cyrus named before he was born — a pattern that runs through Scripture's covenant theology: JEREMIAH named before birth (Jer 1:5), JOHN the Baptist named before conception (Luke 1:13), and Jesus himself named before birth: you shall call his name Jesus (Matt 1:21). The naming-before-knowing is the pattern of divine election — YHWH knows and names those who have not yet come to know him. The covenant community named in Christ are those who were chosen in him before the foundation of the world (Eph 1:4).
45.5: I am the LORD, and there is no other; besides me there is no God. I equip you, though you do not know me. The foundational monotheism of the OT — pressed into service as the ground of Christology. 1 Corinthians 8:6: for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist. Paul preserves the strict monotheism of Deuteronomy by placing Christ within the identity of the one God — not alongside him as a second deity. The one God equips his agents, including the one Agent who is himself divine.
45.6: That people may know, from the rising of the sun and from the west, that there is none besides me. The universal geographic scope — sunrise to sunset — maps directly to the Great Commission. Matthew 28:19: go and make disciples of all nations. Acts 1:8: you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth. The proclamation that there is none besides YHWH is the proclamation that goes to every sunrise and sunset through the apostolic mission — the mission that announces Jesus as Lord (the one who shares YHWH's identity, Phil 2:11).
45.7: I form light and create darkness; I make well-being and create calamity; I am the LORD who does all these things. YHWH's comprehensive sovereignty over all opposites. John 1:1-5: in him was life, and the life was the light of men. The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it. The creator of both light and darkness is the one through whom all things were made (John 1:3). The "calamity" that YHWH creates reaches its most concentrated point at the cross — where the darkness covers the land (Luke 23:44-45) and the greatest disaster in history is simultaneously YHWH's greatest act of salvation (Acts 2:23-24).
45.8: Shower, O heavens, from above, and let the clouds rain down righteousness; let the earth open, that salvation and righteousness may bear fruit; let the earth cause them both to sprout. The rain of righteousness from heaven. Romans 3:21-22: the righteousness of God has been manifested apart from the law... the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe. The salvation that sprouts from the earth is the name Jesus — Yeshua, YHWH saves — which Mary is told to give her son (Matt 1:21). The heavens-rain-righteousness image is the Incarnation: the righteousness of God descending from above to take root in human flesh.
45.9: Woe to him who strives with him who formed him, a pot among earthen pots! Does the clay say to him who forms it, What are you making? or Your work has no handles? Romans 9:20-21 quotes this verse directly in the context of divine election and justification: But who are you, O man, to answer back to God? Will what is molded say to its molder, Why have you made me like this? Has the potter no right over the clay? Paul applies the potter-clay polemic to the controversy over whether God may justify Gentiles and unilaterally choose some over others. The answer is the cross: the Potter's supreme work is the vessel that appears broken but holds resurrection life.
45.10: Woe to him who says to a father, What are you begetting? or to a woman, What are you in labor with? The challenge to divine parenting — who questions what God brings forth? The Son who never raises this question: John 4:34 — my food is to do the will of him who sent me and to accomplish his work. John 5:19: the Son can do nothing of his own accord, but only what he sees the Father doing. The one who has every right to question — the eternal Son — instead perfectly embodies the clay-posture before the Potter, becoming obedient even to death on a cross (Phil 2:8).
45.11: Thus says the LORD, the Holy One of Israel, and the one who formed him: Ask me of things to come; will you command me concerning my children and the work of my hands? The Holy One of Israel — identified in the NT as Jesus (Mark 1:24; John 6:69) — invites petition about things to come. John 14:13-14: whatever you ask in my name, this I will do, that the Father may be glorified in the Son. If you ask me anything in my name, I will do it. The invitation to ask concerning coming things is the invitation of the risen Christ to his church: pray, ask, petition — I am the Holy One who answers.
45.12: I made the earth and created man on it; it was my hands that stretched out the heavens, and I commanded all their host. The creator declaration. John 1:3: all things were made through him, and without him was not anything made that was made. Colossians 1:16: all things were created through him and for him. Hebrews 1:10: You, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning, and the heavens are the work of your hands — cited of Jesus. The one who stretched out the heavens with his hands is the one whose hands were stretched out on the cross: the creator bearing the cost of creation's redemption.
45.13: I have stirred him up in righteousness, and I will make all his ways level; he shall build my city and set my exiles free, not for price or reward, says the LORD of hosts. The messianic-Cyrus who builds the city and frees the exiles at no cost. Matthew 16:18: I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not prevail against it. Luke 4:18: he has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives. The liberation not for price or reward becomes the grammar of grace: Ephesians 2:8-9 — by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God. Cyrus frees exiles at Persia's expense; Christ frees exiles at the cost of his own life.
45.14: Thus says the LORD: The wealth of Egypt and the merchandise of Cush... shall come over to you and be yours; they shall follow you; they shall come over in chains and bow down to you. They will plead with you, saying, Surely God is in you, and there is no other, no god besides him. The nations coming to YHWH's people with the confession God is in you. John 1:14: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us. Matthew 1:23: Immanuel, which means God with us. 1 Corinthians 14:25: when an unbeliever enters the assembly and hears prophecy, he will worship God and declare that God is really among you. The nations' discovery that God is present in the covenant community is the discovery of the incarnation.
45.15: Truly, you are a God who hides yourself, O God of Israel, Savior. The hidden God — deus absconditus — who is simultaneously the Savior. The cross is the supreme hiding of God: in what appears to be total defeat and abandonment (my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?, Matt 27:46), YHWH is acting as Savior. Martin Luther's theologia crucis (theology of the cross) is rooted in this verse: God's power is made known precisely in weakness, his glory in shame, his salvation in death. The hiddenness of God is not his absence but his most concentrated presence.
45.16: All of them are put to shame and confounded; the makers of idols go in confusion together. The shame of the idol-makers. 1 Corinthians 1:27-28: God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise; God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong... God chose what is low and despised in the world, even things that are not, to bring to nothing things that are. The cross is the instrument of shame that shames the shame-makers: the idol-system of human achievement and power is confounded by the crucified Messiah who is the wisdom and power of God (1 Cor 1:24).
45.17: But Israel is saved by the LORD with an everlasting salvation; you shall not be put to shame or confounded to all eternity. The everlasting salvation that reverses shame permanently. Romans 1:16: I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes. Hebrews 5:9: Christ became the source of eternal salvation to all who obey him. Romans 10:11: everyone who believes in him will not be put to shame. The everlasting salvation without shame or confusion is the eschatological state that Christ's resurrection inaugurates — the justification that holds eternally and cannot be reversed.
45.18: For thus says the LORD, who created the heavens (he is God!), who formed the earth and made it (he established it; he did not create it empty, he formed it to be inhabited!): I am the LORD, and there is no other. The earth formed to be inhabited — not chaos (tohu) but ordered for human dwelling. Revelation 21:1-3: then I saw a new heaven and a new earth... and I heard a loud voice from the throne saying, Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. The creator who formed the original earth for human habitation creates the new earth for the permanent dwelling of God with his people — and the dwelling is the Lamb himself, in whom God and humanity are permanently united.
45.19: I did not speak in secret, in a land of darkness; I did not say to the offspring of Jacob, Seek me in vain. I the LORD speak the truth; I declare what is right. YHWH speaks openly, not esoterically. John 18:20: Jesus says I have spoken openly to the world; I have always taught in synagogues and in the temple, where all Jews come together. I have said nothing in secret. Jesus explicitly echoes Isaiah 45:19 in his trial before the high priest. The God who does not speak in secret becomes the Son who teaches openly in public — the anti-mystery, anti-esoteric revelation who says follow me in the marketplace and on the lakeside.
45.20: Assemble yourselves and come; draw near together, you survivors of the nations! They have no knowledge who carry about their wooden idols, and keep on praying to a god that cannot save. The summons to all surviving nations, away from wooden idols that cannot save. Acts 17:30: God commands all people everywhere to repent. The wooden idol that cannot save stands in ironic contrast to the wooden cross from which Christ saves: the idol's wood is carried around in human hands, helpless to rescue; the cross's wood is the instrument of the one who will not stay dead. 1 Corinthians 1:18: the word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God.
45.21: Declare and present your case; let them take counsel together! Who told this long ago? Who declared it of old? Was it not I, the LORD? And there is no other god besides me, a righteous God and a Savior; there is none besides me. The only righteous God who is simultaneously Savior. Romans 3:26: God is just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus. 1 Timothy 2:5: there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. The problem Isaiah 45:21 names — how can the God who is righteous also be the Savior? — is answered at the cross: Christ's atoning death satisfies divine justice and provides the basis for justification. God is both just and the one who justifies.
45.22: Turn to me and be saved, all the ends of the earth! For I am God, and there is no other. The most universal salvation invitation in the OT — all the ends of the earth summoned to turn and be saved. Acts 4:12: there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved. John 3:16: whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life. The all the ends of the earth becomes the geographic scope of the Great Commission (Matt 28:19; Acts 1:8). The turning (panah) to YHWH is the repentance Jesus first proclaims: Repent and believe the gospel (Mark 1:15).
45.23: By myself I have sworn; from my mouth has gone out in righteousness a word that shall not return: To me every knee shall bow, every tongue shall swear allegiance. The supreme verse for Christological identification. Philippians 2:10-11 quotes this directly of Jesus: that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father. Romans 14:11: it is written, As I live, says the Lord, every knee shall bow to me, and every tongue shall confess to God. The NT applies YHWH's own sworn oath from Isaiah 45 to Jesus — making this the most explicit OT basis for the NT claim that Jesus shares the divine identity. The one to whom every knee bows in Isaiah is the one to whom every knee bows in Paul.
45.24: Only in the LORD, it shall be said of me, are righteousness and strength; to him shall come and be ashamed all who were incensed against him. Righteousness and strength in YHWH alone — and all who were incensed against him coming, shamed, to him. 2 Corinthians 5:21: for our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God. The righteousness that is only in YHWH (v24a) becomes the imputed righteousness that believers receive in Christ (Phil 3:9: not having a righteousness of my own that comes from the law, but that which comes through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God). Those incensed against him will stand ashamed at the final judgment (Rev 20:11-15).
45.25: In the LORD all the offspring of Israel shall be justified and shall glory. The justification of all Israel's seed is the NT doctrine of justification by faith. Romans 2:29; 9:6-8: the true Israel is not ethnic but covenant. Galatians 3:29: if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's offspring, heirs according to promise. Romans 5:1: since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ. The final verse of Isaiah 45 is the thesis statement of Paul's letter to the Romans: the offspring of Israel, defined by faith in the Messiah rather than ethnic descent, are justified in YHWH — in Christ — and glory in him.

47.1: "Come down and sit in the dust, O virgin daughter of Babylon; sit on the ground without a throne." The humiliation of the enthroned queen — forced from her throne to the dust. Rev 18:7-8 quotes and expands this scene directly: Babylon "glorified herself and lived in luxury" but in one day comes mourning, famine, and fire. The contrast with Christ is the structural heart of Second Isaiah: Babylon descends from throne to dust because of pride; Christ descends from throne to manger and cross (Phil 2:6-8: "though he was in the form of God, he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself"), and is consequently exalted above all (Phil 2:9-11). The dust-throne reversal is the anti-pattern of Christological kenosis-and-exaltation.
47.2: "Take the millstones and grind flour; remove your veil, strip off your robe, uncover your legs, pass through the rivers." The forced labor and stripping of Babylon's queen — what Babylon imposed on Israel's captives is now imposed on her. Matt 18:33-34: the unmerciful servant who was forgiven but showed no mercy is handed over to the torturers — the principle of reciprocal judgment. Rev 17:16: "the ten horns that you saw, they and the beast will hate the prostitute. They will make her desolate and naked, and devour her flesh and burn her up with fire." The stripping is the eschatological reversal administered by the very powers Babylon used: the woman stripped to shame is the anti-type of those whom Christ clothes in white garments of righteousness (Rev 3:18; 7:9).
47.3: "Your nakedness shall be uncovered, and your disgrace shall be seen. I will take vengeance, and I will spare no one." The divine declaration of vengeance — YHWH himself will expose Babylon's shame. Rom 12:19: "Beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave it to the wrath of God, for it is written, 'Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord.'" Rev 6:10: the martyrs under the altar cry "how long before you will judge and avenge our blood?" The divine vengeance that God reserves to himself is executed through Christ at the parousia (2 Thess 1:8: "inflicting vengeance on those who do not know God"). The statement "I will spare no one" is the counterpoint to "he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all" (Rom 8:32) — divine wrath either falls on the Son or on the unrepentant.
47.4: "Our Redeemer — YHWH of hosts is his name — is the Holy One of Israel." The doxological aside in the middle of the taunt song announces the identity of Babylon's adversary. The term gōʾēl (kinsman-redeemer) denotes the family member who pays the debt, redeems the enslaved, and avenges the murdered. Luke 1:68: Zechariah's Benedictus: "he has visited and redeemed his people." Gal 3:13: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us." Rev 5:9: "by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation." The Redeemer who stands against Babylon is Christ — YHWH of hosts in human flesh — who paid the price Babylon cannot.
47.5: "Sit in silence, and go into darkness, O daughter of the Chaldeans; for you shall no more be called the mistress of kingdoms." The kingdom that called itself eternal is silenced. Rev 18:2: "Babylon the great has fallen, has fallen." Rev 11:15: "the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he shall reign forever and ever." Dan 7:14 (the Son of Man): "his dominion is an everlasting dominion, which shall not pass away, and his kingdom one that shall not be destroyed." The silence and darkness into which Babylon sinks is the permanent end of the counterfeit kingdom — replaced by the eternal reign of Christ who is "Lord of lords and King of kings" (Rev 17:14).
47.6: "I was angry with my people; I profaned my heritage; I gave them into your hand; you showed them no mercy; on the aged you made your yoke exceedingly heavy." YHWH gave his people into Babylon's hand as an instrument of judgment — but Babylon exceeded her mandate by showing no mercy to the vulnerable. Matt 18:32-35: the unmerciful servant parable — "I forgave you all that debt because you pleaded with me. And should not you have had mercy on your fellow servant, as I had mercy on you?" Jas 2:13: "judgment is without mercy to one who has shown no mercy." The instrument of judgment that shows no mercy to the aged is judged without mercy. Christ, who bore the full weight of judgment, is the one who establishes a new community that shows mercy precisely because mercy has been shown to them (Matt 5:7).
47.7: "You said, 'I shall be mistress forever,' so that you did not lay these things to heart or remember their end." The refusal to think about one's end (ʾaḥărîtāh) — the last things — is the spiritual blindness Christ repeatedly warns against. Luke 12:19-20: the rich fool who builds barns and says "eat, drink, be merry" does not "lay to heart" his end (God says "Fool! This night your soul is required of you"). Heb 2:1: "we must pay much closer attention to what we have heard, lest we drift away from it." Rev 18:7: "she says in her heart, 'I sit as a queen, I am no widow, and mourning I shall never see'" — the direct NT echo of Isaiah 47:7 in the eschatological vision. The eternal mastership claimed by Babylon is the self-deification that Christ alone legitimately possesses.
47.8: "Now therefore hear this, you lover of pleasures, who sit securely, who say in your heart, 'I am, and there is no one besides me; I shall not sit as a widow or know the loss of children.'" The divine-name formula "I am, and there is no one besides me" (ʾănî wĕʾap̄sî ʿôḏ) appears in Isaiah as YHWH's exclusive self-declaration (45:5-6,18,21-22; 46:9). Here Babylon steals it. John 8:58: "before Abraham was, I am (egō eimi)" — Christ's claim to the name, which the crowd treats as blasphemy (8:59). The entire chapter is structured around the contrast: the one who legitimately says "I AM" (Christ/YHWH) vs. the one who blasphemously usurps it (Babylon/every worldly power). Rev 18:7 quotes v.8b directly as Babylon's fatal self-deception.
47.9: "Both these things shall come upon you suddenly, in one day: the loss of children and widowhood shall come upon you in full measure, in spite of your many sorceries and the great power of your enchantments." The suddenness of Babylon's fall — "in one day." Rev 18:8: "For this reason her plagues will come in a single day, death and mourning and famine, and she will be burned up with fire; for mighty is the Lord God who has judged her." Luke 17:29-30: "on the day when Lot went out from Sodom, fire and sulfur rained from heaven and destroyed them all — so will it be on the day when the Son of Man is revealed." The "one day" of Babylon's judgment is the template for the eschatological day of Christ's coming — sudden, total, irresistible despite all protective powers.
47.10: "You felt secure in your wickedness; you said, 'No one sees me.'" The delusion of invisible wickedness — "no one sees me." Heb 4:13: "no creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account." John 2:25: Jesus "knew what was in man" — he saw Nicodemus's real question (3:2), the woman at the well's actual history (4:17-18), and the Pharisees' murderous thoughts (8:40). Prov 15:3: "the eyes of YHWH are in every place, keeping watch on the evil and the good." The illusion of concealed wickedness that Babylon maintains is shattered by the one who is the Word that penetrates to the division of soul and spirit (Heb 4:12). "I am, and there is no one besides me" appears again (v.10b) — the second instance of the stolen divine name.
47.11: "But evil shall come upon you, which you will not know how to charm away; disaster shall fall upon you, for which you will not be able to atone (kappĕrāh); and ruin shall come upon you suddenly, of which you know nothing." The disaster that cannot be atoned for — kappĕrāh, the atonement/ransom word. No ritual, no payment, no magic can avert it. Heb 10:26-28: "if we go on sinning deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins, but a fearful expectation of judgment." The contrast: Christ's atonement (kippēr) covers the sin of his people (Rom 3:25: hilastērion, the propitiation/mercy-seat); Babylon has no atonement available because she has rejected the Redeemer (v.4). The "disaster for which you cannot atone" is only avoided by those for whom Christ has provided the atonement.
47.12: "Stand fast in your enchantments and your many sorceries, with which you have labored from your youth; perhaps you may be able to succeed; perhaps you may inspire terror." The ironic divine permission — "try all your powers." Rev 9:20-21: after the plagues of the trumpets, "the rest of mankind, who were not killed by these plagues, did not repent of the works of their hands nor give up worshiping demons and idols... nor did they repent of their murders or their sorceries." Acts 8:9-13: Simon Magus, who "amazed the nation of Samaria, saying that he himself was somebody great," is himself amazed when Philip performs signs in Christ's name — the sorcery tradition silenced before the power of the gospel. The enchantments that cannot save Babylon are the "empty philosophy" (Col 2:8) that Christ disarmed at the cross (Col 2:15).
47.13: "You are wearied with your many counsels; let them stand forth and save you, those who divide the heavens, who gaze at the stars, who at the new moons make known what shall come upon you." The astrologers and star-dividers — the counselors who read the heavens but cannot save. Matt 2:1-12: the Magi (astrologers from the East) follow a star — but the star leads not to Babylon's counsel but to Christ. The heavens that Babylon's specialists "divide" are the heavens that declare the glory of God (Ps 19:1) and that pointed the Gentile wise men to the child who is "the light of the world" (John 8:12). Col 2:8: "elemental spirits of the world" (stoicheia tou kosmou) — the cosmic powers that Babylon's astrologers consulted are the same powers that Christ triumphed over at the cross (Col 2:15). The star-gazers are weary; the star leads to rest (Matt 2:9-10).
47.14: "Behold, they are like stubble; the fire consumes them; they cannot deliver themselves from the power of the flame. No coal for warming oneself is this, no fire to sit before!" The fire that consumes the counselors — not a useful fire for warmth, only the fire of judgment. Matt 3:12: "the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire." Heb 12:29: "our God is a consuming fire." The distinction between the fire that warms and the fire that destroys is the distinction between the Spirit's fire (Matt 3:11: "he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire") that refines, and the eschatological fire that consumes the stubble. Rev 20:15: "if anyone's name was not found written in the book of life, he was thrown into the lake of fire." The counselors consumed by fire are the contrast to those warmed by the Spirit's fire in Acts 2 (tongues of fire, Acts 2:3).
47.15: "Such to you are those you have labored with, who have done business with you from your youth; they wander about, each in his own direction; there is no one to save you." The abandonment at the moment of judgment — all trading partners scatter, no one saves. Rev 18:9-19: the kings, the merchants, the seafarers all "stand far off" and weep for Babylon but cannot help — the desertion is exact. The final words "there is no one to save you" (ʾên môšîaʿ lāk) are the negative of the promise of the Servant/Messiah whose very name "Jesus" (Yēšûaʿ) means "YHWH saves." Isa 43:11: "I, I am YHWH, and besides me there is no savior." Rom 8:38-39: "neither angels nor rulers, nor things present nor things to come, nor powers... will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord." What no one can do for Babylon — save her — the Redeemer (v.4) accomplishes for those who are his.

48.1: Hear this, O house of Jacob, who are called by the name of Israel and who have come forth from the waters of Judah, who swear by the name of YHWH and invoke the God of Israel — but not in truth nor in righteousness. The hearing-call opens the oracle of indictment: Israel's liturgical identity (called by YHWH's name, swearing by YHWH's name) is not matched by truth and righteousness. They perform covenant-identity markers while the substance is absent. In the NT: not everyone who says to me Lord, Lord will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father (Matt 7:21). The gap between name-bearing and truth-and-righteousness is the problem Jesus names as the fundamental failure of nominal religion.
48.2: For they call themselves after the holy city and lean on the God of Israel — YHWH of hosts is his name. The irony is compounded: they name themselves after the holy city and lean on YHWH — but the leaning is not faith; it is presumption. The false security of geographical and ethnic covenant identity is the target. In the NT, John the Baptist addresses the same presumption: Do not think you can say to yourselves, We have Abraham as our father — I tell you that out of these stones God can raise up children for Abraham (Matt 3:9). The holy-city identity is not transferable to those who do not produce fruit.
48.3: I declared the former things long ago; they went out from my mouth and I made them known; then suddenly I acted, and they came to pass. YHWH's pattern of announce-then-fulfill is the epistemological basis for trusting the new declarations (vv 6-8): what he foretold happened; therefore what he is now foretelling will happen. The suddenly-I-acted pattern is the NT's pattern of divine intervention — the appointed time of Gal 4:4 (when the fullness of time had come, God sent his Son) and the sudden-coming of the Son of Man (Matt 24:27).
48.4: Because I knew that you are obstinate, and your neck is an iron sinew and your forehead is bronze. The diagnoses of Israel's spiritual condition (obstinate neck, iron sinew, bronze forehead) are the physical metaphors for spiritual hardening: the animal that will not be led, the metal that cannot be bent. In the NT, Stephen cites this tradition before the Sanhedrin: stiff-necked people with uncircumcised hearts and ears! You always resist the Holy Spirit (Acts 7:51). The iron-and-bronze obstinacy is the condition that the Spirit's coming is designed to overcome: I will remove from you your heart of stone and give you a heart of flesh (Ezek 36:26).
48.5: Therefore I told you these things long ago; before they happened I announced them to you, so that you could not say, My idol did them; my image and my metal god ordained them. The pre-announcement strategy is YHWH's argument against idol-attribution: if the events had been announced after the idols were made, the people could have credited the idols. The advance notice removes that option. In the NT, Jesus pre-announces his death and resurrection (Matt 16:21, 17:22-23, 20:18-19) so that when it happens the disciples will believe that I am he (John 13:19 — ego eimi, the divine self-identification) — not that the event was coincidence or another power's work.
48.6: You have heard; look at all of this. Will you not acknowledge it? I am making new things known to you from now on, hidden things you have not known. The transition from the former-things (already announced and fulfilled) to the new things: YHWH now declares hidden things that have not yet been revealed. The new things are the eschatological events that the rest of Second Isaiah announces: the new Exodus, the Servant's mission, the cosmic redemption. In the NT: what no eye has seen, what no ear has heard, what no human mind has conceived — these are the things God has prepared for those who love him, and God has revealed them to us through his Spirit (1 Cor 2:9-10). The hidden-things-now-revealed pattern is the NT's understanding of the apostolic gospel.
48.7: They are created now, not long ago; before today you have not heard of them, so that you could not say, I already knew them. The new things are genuinely new: not the recovery of old information but the novel act of YHWH in the eschatological age. The you-did-not-know defense prevents the claim of prior knowledge. In the NT, the resurrection of the Messiah was not knowable before it happened — even the disciples did not understand that he must rise from the dead (John 20:9). The new thing that YHWH creates is always beyond what the community could have predicted from its existing categories.
48.8: Yes, you have not heard, you have not known, and from of old your ear has not been opened; for I knew that you were completely treacherous, and that you were called a rebel from the womb. The radical diagnosis: Israel was treacherous from the womb — the same framing as Jacob-the-supplanter (Gen 25:26), the rebel who later became Israel. The from-the-womb framing anticipates the Servant's calling from the womb (49:1, 5) as the contrast: where Jacob was a rebel from the womb, the Servant is consecrated from the womb. In the NT, Paul applies from-the-womb election to his own calling (Gal 1:15: from my mother's womb God set me apart) — but the supreme example is the Son himself (Heb 10:5-7: when Christ came into the world, he said... I have come to do your will, my God).
48.9: For the sake of my name I delay my anger; for the sake of my praise I restrain it for you, so that I do not cut you off. YHWH's restraint of deserved judgment is grounded in his own name-reputation, not in Israel's merit — the same as 43:25 (I blot out your sins for my own sake). The delay of wrath for the sake of YHWH's name is the theological foundation of the NT's patience: the Lord is not slow in keeping his promise, as some understand slowness; instead he is patient with you, not wanting anyone to perish, but everyone to come to repentance (2 Pet 3:9). The restraint of anger is the expression of the same long-suffering love that drives the Servant's mission.
48.10: See, I have refined you, but not as silver; I have tested you in the furnace of affliction. The refinement is real — the exile was the furnace of affliction — but the qualification not as silver signals that the refining is not the standard metallurgical process of eliminating impurities through heat alone. YHWH refines through suffering for the purpose of relationship, not merely purification. In the NT: the testing of your faith — of greater worth than gold, which perishes even though refined by fire — results in praise, glory, and honor when Jesus Christ is revealed (1 Pet 1:6-7). The furnace-of-affliction is the NT church's school of faith.
48.11: For my own sake, for my own sake I act — for how could my name be profaned? And my glory I will not give to another. The double for-my-own-sake (twice, emphatic) grounds every act of YHWH — judgment, refinement, salvation — in divine self-consistency: YHWH cannot deny himself (2 Tim 2:13). The I-will-not-give-my-glory-to-another is the monotheistic claim that the NT surprisingly applies to Jesus: the Father who sends the Son shares his glory with him — the glory you have given me I have given to them (John 17:22). The glory that YHWH refuses to share with idols he gives to the Son — because the Son is not another but is within YHWH's own identity.
48.12: Listen to me, O Jacob, and Israel, whom I called: I am he; I am the first; I am also the last. The third statement of the first-and-last title in Isaiah (also 41:4 and 44:6) — here with the emphatic I am he (anî hû) preceding it. This triple occurrence establishes the title as a central identity marker. The Risen Christ applies it to himself three times in Revelation: I am the First and the Last (Rev 1:17), I am the First and the Last, who died and came to life again (Rev 2:8), I am the Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last, the Beginning and the End (Rev 22:13). No other OT title is more explicitly transferred from YHWH to the glorified Christ in the NT.
48.13: Indeed my own hand laid the foundations of the earth, and my right hand spread out the heavens; when I call to them, they stand up together. The Creator's right-hand act of laying earth's foundations and spreading out the heavens is applied to the Son in Heb 1:10-12 (citing Ps 102:25-27: In the beginning, Lord, you laid the foundations of the earth, and the heavens are the work of your hands). The Psalm is applied to the Son — the one through whom YHWH made the universe (Heb 1:2). The right-hand Creator of Isa 48:13 is identified by the NT with the pre-incarnate Son who is the agent of creation (John 1:3, Col 1:16).
48.14: All of you, assemble and listen! Who among them has foretold these things? YHWH loves the one he has chosen — he will carry out his purpose against Babylon, and his arm will be against the Chaldeans. The one whom YHWH loves and has chosen — here likely Cyrus as his instrument, though the language overlaps with the Servant's election — will execute YHWH's purpose against Babylon. The loved-and-chosen pattern links Cyrus as political type to the Servant as the supreme loved-and-chosen. In the NT, the Father declares his love for the Son at both the baptism and transfiguration: this is my Son, whom I love (Matt 3:17, 17:5) — the beloved and chosen who carries out YHWH's ultimate purpose.
48.15: I, even I, have spoken and called him; I brought him, and he will succeed in his mission. The emphatic I-even-I (anî anî) — the same structure as 43:11 and 43:25 — applied to the calling-and-sending of the chosen instrument. The success-in-mission guarantee is the divine sovereignty over the one sent: what YHWH sends, he brings to its purpose. In the NT: Jesus says of his own mission: I have completed the work you gave me to do (John 17:4); it is finished (John 19:30). The mission given by YHWH succeeds because YHWH is its ground and guarantor.
48.16: Come near to me and hear this: from the beginning I have not spoken in secret; from the time it came to be, I have been there. And now the Lord YHWH has sent me, and his Spirit. The dramatic shift to the speaker saying the Lord YHWH has sent me — a voice within the divine speech that is distinct from YHWH and yet speaks for him. This is the most explicit Servant/Spirit-sending in Isaiah before ch 61: the Spirit-sent envoy of YHWH who can say the Lord YHWH has sent me. Jesus applies the parallel text in 61:1-2 to himself at Nazareth (Luke 4:18): the Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to proclaim. The triune shape of v 16 — YHWH sends the envoy-with-the-Spirit — is the proto-Trinitarian mission structure that the NT makes explicit.
48.17: Thus says YHWH, your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel: I am YHWH your God, who teaches you for your good, who directs you in the way you should go. The teaching-for-your-good is the description of the Torah's purpose — not burden but benefit. YHWH as teacher (Rabbi) is the role that Jesus explicitly takes in the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7) and in John's Gospel (John 13:13: you call me Teacher and Lord, and rightly so). The one who teaches-for-your-good is the Good Shepherd who leads in the right paths (Ps 23:3) — the same YHWH who directs in the way-you-should-go now incarnate as the one who is the Way (John 14:6).
48.18: Oh, that you had paid attention to my commandments! Then your peace would have been like a river, and your righteousness like the waves of the sea. The divine lament — oh-that-you-had-listened — is the most tender verse in ch 48. The peace-like-a-river and righteousness-like-the-sea are the full-and-overflowing images of shalom: not merely the absence of war but the presence of flourishing life at every level. Jesus cites this tradition when he weeps over Jerusalem: if you, even you, had only known on this day what would bring you peace — but now it is hidden from your eyes (Luke 19:42). The oh-that of Isa 48:18 becomes the weeping lament of Jesus over the city that did not hear.
48.19: Your offspring would have been like the sand and your descendants like its grains; their name would not have been cut off or destroyed from before me. The potential-that-was-forfeited: the Abrahamic promise of descendants like the sand of the sea (Gen 22:17) could have been fully realized if Israel had listened. The cut-off name is the reversal of the eternal-name of 56:5. In the NT: the promises to Abraham are fulfilled in Christ (Gal 3:16 — the seed is one person, Christ) and in the community of faith (Gal 3:29: if you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham's seed, heirs according to the promise). What was forfeited by natural Israel's disobedience is inherited by the Servant's community through faith.
48.20: Go out from Babylon, flee from the Chaldeans! Declare this with a shout of joy, proclaim it; send it out to the end of the earth; say, YHWH has redeemed his servant Jacob. The exodus-from-Babylon command is both historical (return from exile) and eschatological (the ultimate coming-out from the powers of this age). Paul cites the underlying theology in 2 Cor 6:17 (come out from them and be separate, says the Lord) and John's Apocalypse makes it explicit: Come out of her, my people, so that you will not share in her sins (Rev 18:4). The exodus-from-Babylon is the NT's pattern of the Christian's separation from the worldly system that opposes YHWH's reign — the final-Exodus begun in the cross and completed at the parousia.
48.21: They did not thirst when he led them through the deserts; he made water flow for them from the rock; he split the rock and the water gushed out. The new Exodus recalls the original Exodus water-from-the-rock provision (Exod 17:6): YHWH's past faithfulness in the wilderness guarantees his faithfulness in the new wilderness journey. Paul identifies the Rock of the wilderness with Christ: they all drank from the spiritual rock that accompanied them, and that rock was Christ (1 Cor 10:4). The water-from-the-rock is the living water that flows from the side of the crucified Jesus (John 19:34) — the fulfillment of the rock-struck-in-the-desert that accompanied Israel through the wilderness.
48.22: There is no peace, says YHWH, for the wicked. The refrain that closes both the first and second sections of the latter part of Isaiah (57:21 repeats it). The no-peace-for-the-wicked is the negative form of the gospel's shalom: the peace that passes understanding (Phil 4:7) is available to those who come to YHWH through the Servant; those who reject the Servant's mediation remain in the unresolved state of v 18's tragedy. The peace-like-a-river that was available to obedient Israel (v 18) but forfeited by disobedience is the same peace that Jesus gives: my peace I give you, not as the world gives (John 14:27) — but only to those who receive the Servant-given shalom.

49.1: A direct prophecy. The Servant addresses the coastlands and peoples from afar: YHWH called me from the womb, from the body of my mother he named my name. The pre-birth calling and naming of the Servant — womb-vocation and name-before-birth — is applied directly to Jesus: Luke 1:31 (you shall call his name Jesus); Matthew 1:21 (you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins). The naming before birth is the Incarnation's announcement that the Servant's identity and mission are entirely determined by YHWH, not by circumstance or achievement.
49.2: A direct prophecy. YHWH made the Servant's mouth like a sharp sword (wayāśem pî kĕḥereb̠ ḥaddāh), hid him in the shadow of his hand, made him a polished arrow, and concealed him in his quiver. The mouth as a sharp sword is applied explicitly to Christ in Revelation 1:16 (from his mouth came a sharp two-edged sword) and 19:15 (from his mouth comes a sharp sword with which to strike down the nations). The concealment in the quiver — the Servant hidden through most of history, prepared and released at the appointed moment — is the mystery of the Incarnation's timing (Gal 4:4: in the fullness of time).
49.3: A direct prophecy. YHWH says to the Servant: you are my servant, Israel, in whom I will be glorified. The Servant is identified with Israel — yet is distinct from the nation (v. 5: the Servant restores Jacob). Christ is the true Israel: Matthew 2:15 applies Hosea 11:1 (out of Egypt I called my son) to Jesus — the Servant-Israel who undergoes the whole Israel-story in his own person. John 15:1 (I am the true vine) and the I am sayings similarly claim that Jesus is the fulfillment of Israel's symbolic identity. The one in whom YHWH is glorified (John 17:4: I glorified you on earth, having accomplished the work you gave me to do) is the Servant of Isaiah 49:3.
49.4: A shadow: the Servant's lament — rîq yāga'tî lĕtōhû wĕheb̄el kōḥî killêtî — I have labored in vain; I have spent my strength for nothing and vanity. But my recompense is with YHWH and my reward with my God. The apparent failure of the Servant's mission — labor without visible fruit — is the experience of the cross: the crowds scatter, the disciples flee, the mission appears to end in execution. Yet the Servant's confidence (my recompense is with YHWH) is the faith that underlies Christ's cry on the cross — even in the darkness of Psalm 22:1, the Servant entrusts himself to the Father (Luke 23:46: Father, into your hands I commit my spirit).
49.5: A direct prophecy. YHWH says: he who formed me from the womb to be his servant, to bring Jacob back to him, and that Israel might be gathered to him — the Servant's first mission is the restoration of Israel. But this is not all. Acts 2:36-41 shows the fulfillment of the first phase: Peter's Pentecost sermon brings three thousand Jews to faith. The regathering of Israel to YHWH through the Servant is the first movement of the gospel, accomplished in Jerusalem before the mission expands outward (Acts 1:8: Jerusalem first, then Judea, Samaria, and the ends of the earth).
49.6: A direct prophecy. It is too small a thing that you should be my servant to raise up the tribes of Jacob and to restore the survivors of Israel; I will make you as a light for the nations (lĕ'ôr gôyim), that my salvation may reach to the end of the earth. This is one of the most explicit universal-mission texts in the OT. Luke 2:32 quotes it as Simeon applies it to the infant Jesus: a light for revelation to the Gentiles. Acts 13:47 is Paul and Barnabas citing this verse explicitly as their mandate for the Gentile mission: I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth. The Servant's mission is explicitly too large for Israel alone.
49.7: A direct prophecy. Thus says YHWH, the Redeemer of Israel and his Holy One, to one deeply despised, abhorred by the nation, the servant of rulers — kings shall see and arise, princes shall prostrate themselves. The paradox of the despised Servant honored by kings is the pattern of Philippians 2:5-11: Christ humbles himself to the point of the cross (deeply despised, servant of rulers) — therefore God has highly exalted him and given him the name above every name, so that every knee shall bow. Isaiah 49:7 provides the narrative structure that Paul theologizes in Philippians 2: humiliation followed by universal royal recognition.
49.8: A direct prophecy. In a time of favor I have answered you; in a day of salvation I have helped you; I will keep you and give you as a covenant for the people (bĕrît 'ām). Paul cites the opening in 2 Corinthians 6:2: behold, now is the favorable time; behold, now is the day of salvation — applying it to the gospel moment. The Servant given as a covenant is the NT's new covenant: Luke 22:20 (this cup is the new covenant in my blood); Hebrews 8:6-13 (Christ is the mediator of a better covenant). The Servant is not merely the messenger of the covenant — he is the covenant itself, the binding tie between YHWH and his people.
49.9: A shadow: saying to the prisoners, come out; to those who are in darkness, appear. The release of prisoners and those in darkness is the messianic proclamation of Luke 4:18 (the Spirit of the Lord... to proclaim liberty to the captives and recovering of sight to the blind). Peter's liberation from prison (Acts 12:7-11) and Paul's from the Philippian jail (Acts 16:26) are enacted parables of the Servant's prisoner-releasing work. The ultimate liberation is from the prison of sin and death (Heb 2:14-15: Christ delivers those enslaved through the fear of death).
49.10: A shadow: they shall not hunger or thirst; neither scorching wind nor sun shall strike them — for the one who has compassion on them will lead them, and guide them to springs of water. This is the shepherd-care of the messianic age. Revelation 7:16-17 quotes this verse of the redeemed multitude before the throne: they shall hunger no more, neither thirst anymore; the sun shall not strike them, nor any scorching heat. For the Lamb in the midst of the throne will be their shepherd, and he will guide them to springs of living water. The compassionate guide of Isaiah 49:10 is explicitly identified as the Lamb.
49.11: A shadow: YHWH turns the mountains into roads, his highways are raised up. The great highway imagery of Isaiah 40:3-4 continues here — the exodus-road made smooth for the returning exiles. The NT applies it to the spread of the gospel: Acts 8 (Philip on the road to Gaza meets the Ethiopian eunuch; the road is the place of conversion); Romans 10:15 (how beautiful are the feet of those who preach good news). The mountain-roads of Isaiah 49:11 are the mission routes of the apostolic church.
49.12: A shadow: the returnees come from the north, from the west, and from the land of Sinim (the far east). The four-direction return from exile anticipates the eschatological gathering of Matthew 8:11 (many will come from east and west and recline at table with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven) and Revelation 7:9 (a great multitude from every nation, tribe, people, and language). The Servant's restored community is gathered from the whole earth, not from one land.
49.13: A shadow: sing for joy, O heavens! Shout, O earth! Break forth, O mountains, into singing! For YHWH has comforted his people and will have compassion on his afflicted. The cosmic joy at YHWH's comfort is the eschatological register of Revelation 19:1-7 (the great multitude in heaven crying hallelujah, for the Lord reigns). The angels who sang at Christ's birth (Luke 2:13-14) are the first fulfillment of this cosmic praise; the final fulfillment is the new creation's full-throated song. Christ's birth, resurrection, and return all generate this heaven-earth-mountain praise.
49.14: A revelation of God: Zion says, YHWH has forsaken me; my Lord has forgotten me. The lament of abandonment — the darkest spiritual experience — is voiced by YHWH's chosen city. Christ takes this lament to its absolute extreme: Matthew 27:46 (My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?) — where Zion's complaint becomes the Son's cry from the cross. The divine answer in v. 15-16 is the gospel's answer to the cross's cry of abandonment.
49.15: A shadow: Can a woman forget her nursing child, that she should have no compassion on the son of her womb? Even these may forget, yet I will not forget you. The nursing-mother image of divine faithfulness establishes that YHWH's compassion is stronger than the strongest human bond. The NT framing: Romans 8:38-39 (nothing in all creation will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord). The God who cannot forget is the God who sent his Son into the very place of forsakenness (v. 14) to ensure that no one who trusts him is ultimately forgotten.
49.16: A shadow: behold, I have engraved you on the palms of my hands (ḥaqqōtîk̠ 'al-kappāyim). The image of YHWH's people permanently inscribed on his hands is fulfilled in the most concrete way imaginable in the resurrection: John 20:27 (Jesus shows Thomas the nail-marks in his hands and his side — put your finger here and see my hands). The nail-prints in Christ's glorified hands are the eternal engraving of his people on the palms of YHWH's hands. The physical mark of the cross carried into resurrection glory is the fulfillment of Isaiah 49:16.
49.17: A shadow: your builders come quickly; your destroyers and those who laid you waste go away. The reversal — destroyers flee, builders arrive — is the pattern of the gospel's work in community: Ephesians 2:19-22 (you are built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone). The church as the rebuilt Zion, assembled from scattered exiles, is the living fulfillment of the restored city whose builders come quickly.
49.18: A shadow: lift up your eyes and see — all these gather and come to you. As I live, declares YHWH, you shall put them all on as an ornament; you shall bind them on as a bride does. The adorning of Zion with her returning children — like a bride adorning herself — anticipates Revelation 21:2 (the holy city, the new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband) and 21:9-11 (the Bride, the wife of the Lamb). The adorned Zion of Isaiah 49:18 is the Bride of the Lamb.
49.19: A revelation of God: the land previously desolate and ruined will now be too cramped for the inhabitants; the devourers will be far away. The abundance-after-desolation pattern is the consistent shape of divine restoration — what appeared irrecoverable becomes overflowing. Paul applies this logic spiritually in 2 Corinthians 4:17 (this light momentary affliction is preparing for us an eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison). The too-crowded city is the NT's picture of the final redeemed community — the vast multitude no one can number (Rev 7:9).
49.20: A revelation of God: the children born in the time of your bereavement shall yet say, the place is too narrow for me — make room for me to dwell. The children of the bereaved Zion who arrive and fill the land are the Gentile nations who pour into the people of God through the gospel. Paul in Galatians 4:27 quotes Isaiah 54:1 (which develops this same theme) and applies it explicitly: the desolate woman who was childless has more children than the married one. The Gentile multitude fulfills Zion's overcrowded house.
49.21: A revelation of God: Zion will say, who bore me these? I was bereaved and barren, exiled and put away — who has brought these up? The amazement of the restored Zion at her unexpected children is the amazement of the church at the scope of Gentile inclusion. Romans 9-11 is Paul's extended meditation on this amazement: the surprising addition of the nations to YHWH's people, which Paul calls the mystery hidden for ages (Eph 3:6: the Gentiles are fellow heirs, members of the same body, partakers of the promise in Christ Jesus).
49.22: A shadow: YHWH will lift up his hand to the nations and raise his signal to the peoples — they shall bring your sons in their arms and carry your daughters on their shoulders. The signal (nēs) lifted to the nations is the messianic banner of Isaiah 11:10,12 and the cross of John 12:32 (when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself). The nations bringing Israel's children in their arms describes the Gentile mission's strange reversal: non-Jews become the carriers of the gospel back to Jewish communities. Acts 15 narrates the beginning of this pattern.
49.23: A shadow: kings shall be your foster fathers and queens your nursing mothers — they shall bow down to you with face to the earth. The political reversal — kings as nursemaids to Zion — is the pattern of eschatological submission anticipated in Revelation 21:24-26 (the kings of the earth bring their glory into the new Jerusalem). The NT does not allegorize this into purely spiritual terms: Revelation's kings bringing honor to the city is the literal fulfillment of Isaiah 49:23's royal submission.
49.24: A revelation of God: can the prey be taken from the mighty? Can the captives of the righteous be delivered? The rhetorical question seems to demand a negative answer — no one can take back what a powerful enemy has taken. YHWH's answer in v. 25 reverses it completely. Christ's work is precisely this impossible liberation: Hebrews 2:14-15 (that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death, the devil, and deliver all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery). The prey is delivered from the mighty — the impossible rescue accomplished by the cross.
49.25: A direct promise. Even the captives of the mighty shall be taken, and the prey of the tyrant shall be rescued; for I will contend with those who contend with you, and I will save your children. The divine warrior who contends personally against the oppressor of his people is the theological ground of Christ's warfare against Satan: Colossians 2:15 (he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, triumphing over them in him). 1 John 3:8 (the Son of God appeared to destroy the works of the devil). YHWH who promises to contend with the mighty comes to do it himself in the Son.
49.26: A direct promise. I will make your oppressors eat their own flesh, and they shall be drunk with their own blood as with wine. Then all flesh shall know that I am YHWH your Savior, and your Redeemer, the Mighty One of Jacob. The universal knowledge of YHWH as Savior and Redeemer — the final outcome of all the battles and reversals — is the eschatological horizon of the gospel: Philippians 2:10-11 (every knee shall bow and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father). The all flesh shall know of Isaiah 49:26 is the every tongue shall confess of Philippians 2:11.

50.1: Where is your mother's bill of divorce, with which I sent her away? Or which of my creditors is it to whom I have sold you? Behold, for your iniquities you were sold, and for your transgressions your mother was sent away. YHWH opens the Third Servant Song by establishing that Israel's exile was self-inflicted — no divorce certificate, no creditor's sale. The covenant relationship, though fractured by Israel's sin, was never abandoned by YHWH. John 1:11: he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him. The Servant who speaks in vv4-9 comes into the very situation this verse describes — not a deserted wife but a people who deserted YHWH. The Incarnation is YHWH not accepting the separation but coming himself to restore what human sin broke.
50.2: Why, when I came, was there no man? Why, when I called, was there no one to answer? Is my hand shortened, that it cannot redeem? Or have I no power to deliver? Behold, by my rebuke I dry up the sea, I make the rivers a desert. YHWH came and found no one. John 1:11: he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him. The arm that is not too short to redeem (Exod 6:6: I will redeem you with an outstretched arm) is identified in Isaiah 53:1 as the arm of the LORD revealed in the Servant. The power to dry the sea (the Exodus) is the same power exercised by Jesus who rebukes the storm (Mark 4:39: peace, be still!) — the creator's arm in covenant action, coming to redeem though met with silence.
50.3: I clothe the heavens with blackness and make sackcloth their covering. Creation's mourning-garment of darkness — the cosmic dimension of divine grief over covenant rupture. The darkening of the heavens finds its NT fulfillment in the crucifixion: Luke 23:44-45 records that darkness came over the whole land from the sixth to the ninth hour, and the sun's light failed. The heavenly sackcloth of Isaiah 50:3 is the darkness of Golgotha — creation itself donning mourning clothes as the one through whom it was made bears its sin.
50.4: The Lord GOD has given me the tongue of those who are taught, that I may know how to sustain with a word him who is weary. Morning by morning he awakens; he awakens my ear to hear as those who are taught. The Servant speaks as a disciple — the tongue of the learned, the ear awakened daily. The content of the Servant's speech is sustaining the weary: Matthew 11:28-30: Come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Hebrews 5:8-9: although he was a son, he learned obedience through what he suffered; and being made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation. Jesus, the eternal Son, took on the form of a disciple — learning, growing, sustained morning by morning by the Father's word.
50.5: The Lord GOD has opened my ear, and I was not rebellious; I turned not backward. The Servant's perfect obedience contrasted with Israel's persistent rebellion (the entire preceding chapters). Philippians 2:8: and being found in human form, he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross. Hebrews 5:8: although he was a son, he learned obedience through what he suffered. In Gethsemane Jesus expresses the ultimate form of the opened ear: not my will, but yours, be done (Luke 22:42). The non-rebellious, non-turning Servant is the one who kept his face set toward Jerusalem even when all others fled.
50.6: I gave my back to those who strike, and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard; I hid not my face from disgrace and spitting. The most direct Passion prophecy before chapter 53 — each detail fulfilled with precision. Matthew 26:67: they spat in his face and struck him. And some slapped him. Mark 14:65: some began to spit on him and to cover his face and to strike him. Matthew 27:26: Pilate having scourged Jesus, delivered him to be crucified. The giving of the back to the smiters, the cheeks to those who pull the beard, the face to spitting and disgrace — the Passion narratives fulfill Isaiah 50:6 with such specificity that the disciples would have recognized the echo immediately after Pentecost.
50.7: But the Lord GOD helps me; therefore I have not been disgraced; therefore I have set my face like a flint, and I know that I shall not be put to shame. The Servant's resolve — face set like flint — is the language Luke uses for Jesus's final journey: Luke 9:51: when the days drew near for him to be taken up, he set his face to go to Jerusalem. This is a direct lexical echo of Isaiah 50:7's set my face like flint. The certainty of divine help grounds the Servant's determination; the certainty of resurrection grounds Jesus's determination. He sets his face because he knows what the Father will do — the shame absorbed at the cross will not be the final word.
50.8: He who vindicates me is near. Who will contend with me? Let us stand up together. Who is my adversary? Let him come near to me. The Servant's legal confidence in divine vindication.
