Romans 8:33-34 applies this directly to the risen Christ: Who shall bring any charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies. Who is to condemn? Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised — who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us. Paul uses the Servant's courtroom-language of Isaiah 50:8 to describe the position of the risen Christ before the divine court — and by extension the legal status of all who are in him.
50.9: Behold, the Lord GOD helps me; who will declare me guilty? Behold, all of them will wear out like a garment; the moth will eat them up. The Servant's accusers will wear out while he endures. Hebrews 1:10-12 uses the garment-wearing-out image for the creation that passes away while the Son remains unchanged: they will wear out like a garment... but you are the same, and your years will have no end. The Servant's legal vindication — no one can declare him guilty — becomes the doctrine of Christ's sinlessness: 2 Corinthians 5:21 — him who knew no sin. His accusers (Caiaphas, the Sanhedrin, Pilate) have been consumed while the one they condemned is still worshipped.
50.10: Who among you fears the LORD and obeys the voice of his servant? Let him who walks in darkness and has no light trust in the name of the LORD and rely on his God. The covenant community is now defined by obedience to the Servant's voice. John 10:27: my sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me. Hebrews 1:2: in these last days God has spoken to us by his Son. The walking-in-darkness person who trusts in YHWH's name is the person who trusts in the name of Jesus: Acts 4:12 — there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved. The Servant's voice is now the voice of Christ.
50.11: Behold, all you who kindle a fire, who equip yourselves with burning torches! Walk by the light of your fire, and by the torches that you have kindled! This you have from my hand: you shall lie down in torment. The warning to those who choose self-generated light over YHWH's provision. John 8:12: I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life. The self-kindled fire is the human achievement-system: human reason, human righteousness, human religion manufactured without the Servant. Revelation 20:14-15: the lake of fire is the endpoint of those who chose their own light. The torment that comes from one's own hand (the fire returns on its kindlers) is the ironic justice of rejecting the Light of the world for self-made illumination.

51.1: Listen to me, you who pursue righteousness, you who seek the LORD: look to the rock from which you were hewn, and to the quarry from which you were dug. The call to look backward to origins — to Abraham and Sarah from whom YHWH carved the covenant people. The NT applies the Abraham-pattern to Christ's community: Romans 4:16-17 — Abraham is the father of us all — of all who share his faith. Galatians 3:16: the promises were made to Abraham and to his offspring... who is Christ. The rock from which believers are hewn is Abraham; but behind Abraham stands Christ, in whom all the Abrahamic promises are yes and amen (2 Cor 1:20).
51.2: Look to Abraham your father and to Sarah who bore you; for he was but one when I called him, that I might bless him and multiply him. One called and blessed to become many — the pattern of election and multiplication that the NT applies to Christ's work. Galatians 3:29: if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's offspring, heirs according to promise. The one-called-to-multiply is also the pattern of the grain of wheat: John 12:24 — unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit. The fruitful multiplication of the covenant community comes through death-and-resurrection, as Isaac's birth came through the as-good-as-dead bodies of Abraham and Sarah (Heb 11:12).
51.3: For the LORD will comfort Zion; he will comfort all her waste places and will make her wilderness like Eden, her desert like the garden of the LORD; joy and gladness will be found in her, thanksgiving and the voice of song. The Eden-restoration — wilderness becoming paradise. Revelation 22:1-5 presents the new Jerusalem explicitly as a restored Eden: the river of life, the tree of life, no more curse. Christ as the new Adam (Rom 5:14-19; 1 Cor 15:45) through whom what Adam lost is fully restored. The comfort of Zion is the Spirit whom Christ sends: John 14:16-18 — the Paraclete (Comforter) who creates the community of joy, gladness, and thanksgiving.
51.4: Give attention to me, my people, and give ear to me, my nation; for a law will go out from me, and I will set my justice for a light to the peoples. The law and light going out to the peoples — the universal mission of the covenant community. John 8:12: I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness. Matthew 5:17: I have not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets but to fulfill them. The Sermon on the Mount is the law going out from the Messianic hill (Matt 5:1) as the universal charter of the kingdom — and Christ himself is the light that goes out to the peoples (John 1:9: the true light, which gives light to everyone).
51.5: My righteousness draws near, my salvation has gone out, and my arms will judge the peoples; the coastlands hope for me, and for my arm they wait. The arm of YHWH bringing salvation — and the Gentile coastlands waiting for it. Matthew 12:21 quotes Isaiah 42:4 — and in his name the Gentiles will hope — as the summary of Jesus's mission. Isaiah 53:1 asks: to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed? — and answers: in the Servant's suffering. The arm that judges and saves is Christ at the right hand of the Father (Acts 2:33): the risen Christ who poured out the Spirit and who will return to complete the judgment of the peoples.
51.6: Lift up your eyes to the heavens, and look at the earth beneath; for the heavens vanish like smoke, the earth will wear out like a garment, and they who dwell in it will die in like manner; but my salvation will be forever, and my righteousness will never be dismayed. The permanent salvation contrasted with the perishing creation. Hebrews 1:10-12 applies the wearing-out-like-a-garment image directly to the Son: You, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning... they will perish, but you remain; they will all wear out like a garment... but you are the same, and your years will have no end. 1 Peter 1:24-25: all flesh is grass, but the word of the Lord — the gospel — endures forever. Christ is both the eternal Son (who outlasts the creation) and the eternal salvation (who never ends).
51.7: Listen to me, you who know righteousness, the people in whose heart is my law; fear not the reproach of man, nor be dismayed at their revilings. The people whose hearts have the law — the definitive mark of the new covenant community. Jeremiah 31:33: I will put my law within them and write it on their hearts. Hebrews 8:10 quotes this as the new covenant's central promise, fulfilled in the Spirit Christ sends. The freedom from fear of human reproach is the freedom of justification: Romans 8:1 — there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus. Those who know righteousness (in Christ, 2 Cor 5:21) are no longer dismayed by human opinion.
51.8: For the moth will eat them up like a garment, and the worm will eat them like wool; but my righteousness will be forever, and my salvation to all generations. The eternal righteousness against the moth-eaten opposition — the same image as Isaiah 50:9 (the Servant's accusers who wear out while he endures). Romans 1:16-17: I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes... in it the righteousness of God is revealed from faith for faith. The righteousness that endures to all generations is not a human achievement but the righteousness of God — the salvation accomplished at the cross that no opposition can ultimately threaten.
51.9: Awake, awake, put on strength, O arm of the LORD; awake, as in days of old, the generations of long ago. Was it not you who cut Rahab in pieces, who pierced the dragon? The appeal for the arm of YHWH to repeat the Exodus-miracle that split the chaos-sea-dragon. Isaiah 53:1 asks to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed? — answered in the Servant's suffering. Revelation 12:9: the great dragon — that ancient serpent who is the devil — is thrown down at the cross by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony (Rev 12:11). Colossians 2:15: Christ disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, triumphing over them in him. The arm that pierced Rahab pierces the last enemy at the resurrection.
51.10: Was it not you who dried up the sea, the waters of the great deep, who made the depths of the sea a way for the redeemed to pass over? The Exodus-path through the sea as the type of redemption. 1 Corinthians 10:1-4: our fathers were all under the cloud, and all passed through the sea... and all drank the same spiritual drink. For they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ. The way through the sea is the way Christ makes through death — the pattern of the resurrection. Hebrews 11:29: the Israelites passed through the Red Sea; Christ passes through the sea of death and opens a new and living way for his people (Heb 10:20).
51.11: And the ransomed of the LORD shall return and come to Zion with singing; everlasting joy shall be upon their heads; they shall obtain gladness and joy, and sorrow and sighing shall flee away. This verse echoes Isaiah 35:10 almost verbatim — the joy of the ransomed reaching Zion. Revelation 5:9: you were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation. The ransom is Christ himself: Matthew 20:28 — the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many. The everlasting joy with sorrow and sighing fled away is the eschatological state described in Revelation 21:4: he will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more.
51.12: I, I am he who comforts you; who are you that you are afraid of man who dies, of the son of man who is made like grass? YHWH as the sole Comforter — and the absurdity of fearing mortal man while forgetting him. John 14:16-18: Jesus promises the Paraclete (Comforter/Advocate) and says I will not leave you as orphans; I will come to you. 2 Corinthians 1:3-4: the God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our affliction, so that we may be able to comfort those who are in any affliction. The fear of dying men that Isaiah 51:12 diagnoses is the anxiety that the risen Christ addresses at every resurrection appearance: do not be afraid (Matt 28:10; Rev 1:17).
51.13: And you have forgotten the LORD, your Maker, who stretched out the heavens and laid the foundations of the earth, and you fear continually all the day because of the wrath of the oppressor, when he sets himself to destroy? And where is the wrath of the oppressor? The diagnostic — forgetting the Creator while fearing the oppressor. Matthew 10:28: do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. Rather fear him who can destroy both soul and body in hell. Jesus reorients fear from the human oppressor to the living God — the identical move Isaiah 51:13 makes. The oppressor's wrath is temporary; YHWH's comfort is eternal. The question where is the wrath of the oppressor? anticipates: it will not last.
51.14: He who is bowed down shall speedily be released; he shall not die and go down to the pit; neither shall his bread fail. The release of the prisoner from death in the pit. Luke 4:18: Jesus reads to proclaim liberty to the captives and recovery of sight to the blind, to set at liberty those who are oppressed — his mission statement. Acts 2:27: Peter quotes Psalm 16:10 of the resurrection: you will not abandon my soul to Hades, nor will you let your Holy One see corruption. Christ himself is the primary fulfillment of he shall not die and go down to the pit — and then, through him, neither will those who are in him (John 11:25-26: whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live).
51.15: I am the LORD your God, who stirs up the sea so that its waves roar — the LORD of hosts is his name. YHWH who commands the sea — enacted by Jesus in Mark 4:37-39: the storm on the Sea of Galilee, rebuked with peace, be still! The disciples' astonished question: who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him? (Mark 4:41) is answered by Isaiah 51:15: the one who stirs up and calms the sea is the LORD of hosts. Jesus's storm-rebuke is one of the clearest exercises of YHWH's own divine identity in the Gospels — the Creator exercising authority over what he made.
51.16: And I have put my words in your mouth and covered you in the shadow of my hand, establishing the heavens and laying the foundation of the earth, and saying to Zion, You are my people. The word put in the Servant's mouth — the same image as the sharp sword-mouth of 49:2. Hebrews 1:1-2: God who spoke through the prophets has in these last days spoken to us by his Son. John 1:1: in the beginning was the Word. The word placed in the Servant's mouth is ultimately the eternal Word himself — the Son who is both the speaking and the spoken. The shadow of YHWH's hand covering the Servant is the divine protection: John 10:28-29 — no one will snatch them out of my hand... no one can snatch them out of the Father's hand.
51.17: Wake yourself, wake yourself, stand up, O Jerusalem, you who have drunk from the LORD's hand the cup of his wrath, who have drunk to the dregs the bowl, the cup of staggering. Jerusalem has drunk the cup of YHWH's fury to the dregs — and now is called to wake up. The cup of wrath is the central image of Christ's Gethsemane prayer: Matthew 26:39 — if it is possible, let this cup pass from me. The cup that Jerusalem has drunk is the cup that Christ takes up — drinking the wrath that they deserved so that it may be removed from them (v22). The sleeping/waking language is also resurrection language: Ephesians 5:14 — Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you.
51.18: There is none to guide her among all the sons she has borne; there is none to take her by the hand among all the sons she has brought up. Jerusalem without a guide or a hand to grasp — utterly alone in her suffering. This is the condition of abandonment that Christ enters in the incarnation: he comes as the guide Israel never had. Hebrews 2:10: it was fitting that he, for whom and by whom all things exist, in bringing many sons to glory, should make the founder of their salvation perfect through suffering. The one who brings many sons to glory is the one who takes them by the hand — who holds them in a grip that no one can break (John 10:28).
51.19: These two things have come upon you — who will grieve for you? — devastation and destruction, famine and sword; who will comfort you? The double disaster — devastation and famine — with no comforter. 2 Corinthians 1:3-4: God is the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our affliction. The absence of human comforters sets up YHWH's own arrival as comforter — and Christ who promises: John 16:33 — in the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world. The one who overcomes the world is the comforter who addresses every possible affliction.
51.20: Your sons have fainted; they lie at the head of every street like an antelope in a net; they are full of the wrath of the LORD, the rebuke of your God. The sons of Jerusalem fallen under divine rebuke — lying exhausted in the streets. This is the condition of desolation that Christ's lament addresses: Matthew 23:37-38 — O Jerusalem, Jerusalem... how often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing! See, your house is left to you desolate. The fainted sons lying in the streets is the desolation that results from rejecting the one who would have gathered them.
51.21: Therefore hear this, you who are afflicted, who are drunk, but not with wine. The afflicted and drunk — not with wine but with suffering and divine judgment. The first beatitude addresses exactly this condition: Matthew 5:3-4 — blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted. The sermon to the afflicted who are drunk on suffering is the Sermon on the Mount — the announcement that the kingdom belongs to precisely those who have nothing left of their own strength.
51.22: Thus says your Lord, the LORD, your God who pleads the cause of his people: Behold, I have taken from your hand the cup of staggering; the bowl of my fury you shall drink no more. YHWH removes the cup of wrath from Jerusalem's hand — and the mechanism is the cross. The cup that Jesus takes in Gethsemane (Matt 26:39) is the bowl of divine fury — he drinks it so that his people need drink it no more. Romans 5:9: since we have been justified by his blood, how much more shall we be saved by him from the wrath of God. 1 Thessalonians 1:10: Jesus delivers us from the wrath to come. The cup-removal of Isaiah 51:22 is the atonement accomplished at Calvary.
51.23: And I will put it into the hand of your tormentors, who have said to you, Bow down, that we may pass over; and you have made your back like the ground and like the street for them to pass over. The cup transferred from the oppressed to the oppressors — the eschatological reversal of all history's humiliations. Revelation 18:6: pay her back as she herself has paid back others, and repay her double for her deeds. The final judgment transfers the cup of wrath to all who enforced the humiliation of YHWH's people. The covenant community whose back was made into a road for the oppressor to walk over (the slave-posture of maximum degradation) is vindicated when the oppressor receives what they gave. The last are first; the humiliated are exalted (Matt 20:16; Luke 1:52).

54.1: Sing, O barren one who has not borne; break out into singing and cry aloud, you who have not been in labor! For the children of the desolate one will be more than the children of the married woman, says YHWH. This verse opens Isaiah 54 as the direct continuation of the Servant's self-offering in ch 53: the barren woman (desolate Zion) who lost her children to exile now sings because the Servant's atoning work has opened the womb of the covenant community. Paul quotes this verse directly in Gal 4:27 as the oracle of the Jerusalem above — she is the mother of us all. He applies it to the contrast between Hagar (the slave, earthly Jerusalem under the law) and Sarah (the free woman, the new covenant community). The barren woman who has more children than the married woman is the community of the Servant — the church — whose children come through the new birth of the Spirit, not natural generation. The impossible fertility promised to the barren is the pattern of the gospel's expansion: the community that seemed most depleted (post-cross, post-exile) produces the most children.
54.2: Enlarge the place of your tent, and let the curtains of your habitations be stretched out; do not hold back; lengthen your cords and strengthen your stakes. The expand-your-tent command is the logistical consequence of v 1's unexpected fertility: the community must prepare for an influx it did not anticipate. The tent imagery is both nomadic (wilderness-covenant) and the tabernacle (YHWH's dwelling among his people). The stretching of cords and strengthening of stakes is the infrastructure of a growing community. In the NT: Pentecost (Acts 2:41 — three thousand added in one day) and the subsequent Gentile mission (Acts 13-28) are the historical fulfillment of the tent-expansion command. The stakes-and-cords work of Paul's missionary journeys is the literal stretching of the tent across the Mediterranean world.
54.3: For you will spread out to the right and to the left; your offspring will possess the nations and will people the desolate cities. The right-and-left expansion is the geographic promise: Zion will inherit what was empty. The desolate cities — the ruins of judgment oracles throughout Isaiah — will be repopulated by the children of the barren one. In the NT: the Great Commission (Matt 28:18-20: go and make disciples of all nations) is the Servant-community's possession of the nations. The desolate cities of the Gentile world are repeopled by the church's mission. Paul's calling to proclaim the gospel where Christ was not known (Rom 15:20) is the right-and-left spread of the expanded tent.
54.4: Do not fear, for you will not be ashamed; do not be humiliated, for you will not be disgraced. For you will forget the shame of your youth, and the reproach of your widowhood you will remember no more. The shame-removal promise follows the three do-not-fears of Second Isaiah (41:10, 43:1, 44:2) and echoes the cornerstone promise of 28:16 (whoever trusts in it will not be put to shame). The shame-of-your-youth (Egypt's slavery) and the reproach-of-widowhood (the exile) are the two great traumas whose memory is erased by YHWH's restoration. In the NT: there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Rom 8:1) — the shame and reproach of sin's verdict is removed by the Servant who bore it in ch 53. Those who trust in the cornerstone will not be put to shame (Rom 10:11).
54.5: For your Maker is your husband — YHWH of hosts is his name — and the Holy One of Israel is your Redeemer; he is called the God of all the earth. The Maker-as-husband metaphor is the covenant relationship expressed as marriage: YHWH is not merely Zion's protector but her closest kin. In the NT, Paul develops this metaphor into the church-as-bride of Christ (Eph 5:25-32: husbands, love your wives as Christ loved the church and gave himself for her). The husband-metaphor applied to YHWH in Isa 54 becomes the husband-metaphor applied to Christ in Eph 5 — the NT's way of placing Christ in the position that only YHWH occupies. The God of all the earth title anticipates the universal scope of the covenant of peace (v 10): what began as Israel's covenant now encompasses all the earth.
54.6: For YHWH has called you like a wife deserted and grieved in spirit, like a wife of one's youth when she is rejected, says your God. The tender-rejection-and-recall metaphor is the covenant's emotional register: YHWH does not abandon in indifference but in grief, and the return is not merely legal but relational recovery. The wife of youth rejected and then recalled is the Hosea pattern (Hos 2:14-16: I will allure her; I will lead her into the desert and speak tenderly to her). In the NT: God demonstrates his own love for us in this — while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Rom 5:8). The recalled-wife is the sinner reconciled not by her own improvement but by the husband's initiative and cost (the Servant of ch 53).
54.7: For a brief moment I deserted you, but with great compassion I will gather you. The brief-moment abandonment and the great-compassion restoration are asymmetric: the exile (however long it felt) is brief against the eternal compassion. The great compassion (rachamim gedolim) is the womb-love (racham — the maternal love YHWH uniquely bears). In the NT: I consider that our present sufferings are not worth comparing with the glory that will be revealed in us (Rom 8:18) — Paul applies the same asymmetry. The brief-moment darkness of the cross (My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? — Matt 27:46) gives way to the resurrection's great-compassion gathering: the Father does not abandon the Son to the grave (Acts 2:24).
54.8: In a surge of wrath I hid my face from you for a moment, but with everlasting kindness I will have compassion on you, says YHWH your Redeemer. The hidden face of v 8 is the divine-abandonment language: when YHWH hides his face, the covenant community is exposed to chaos and enemy attack (Ps 30:7: when you hid your face, I was dismayed). The surge-of-wrath moment is real — the exile, the judgment — but it is temporary against the everlasting kindness (chesed olam). In the NT: nothing in all creation will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord (Rom 8:39). The everlasting-kindness guarantee of Isa 54:8 is the OT form of Rom 8's nothing-can-separate — both ground the eternal covenant-love against the temporary experience of absence.
54.9: For this is like the days of Noah to me: as I swore that the waters of Noah should no more go over the earth, so I have sworn that I will not be angry with you and will not rebuke you. The covenant with Noah (Gen 9:11-17) is the template for the covenant of peace: just as YHWH swore never to flood the earth again, so he swears never again to abandon Zion in wrath. The rainbow-covenant becomes the type of the new covenant. In the NT: the blood of Jesus speaks a better word than the blood of Abel (Heb 12:24) — the new covenant's oath is sealed not by a rainbow but by the Servant's blood. The I-will-not-be-angry-with-you is the assurance that rests on the Servant's atonement of ch 53 — the wrath that was poured out there will not be poured out again on those in the Servant's community.
54.10: For the mountains may depart and the hills be removed, but my steadfast love shall not depart from you, and my covenant of peace shall not be removed, says YHWH, who has compassion on you. The covenant of peace (berit shalom) is YHWH's guarantee that outlasts the most permanent features of creation (mountains, hills). The shalom-covenant is the new covenant that Jeremiah and Ezekiel also anticipate (Jer 31:31-34, Ezek 37:26: I will make a covenant of peace with them — it will be an everlasting covenant). In the NT: the God of peace, who through the blood of the eternal covenant brought back from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great Shepherd of the sheep (Heb 13:20-21) — the covenant of shalom is the covenant sealed in the Servant's blood and ratified by the resurrection. The mountains-may-depart permanence is the NT's eternal covenant.
54.11: O afflicted one, storm-tossed and not comforted, behold, I will set your stones in antimony, and lay your foundations with sapphires. The storm-tossed-and-not-comforted Zion (the exile's condition) is now addressed as the recipient of jeweled reconstruction. The precious-stone foundations echo the high priest's breastplate (Exod 28:17-21) and anticipate the New Jerusalem's foundations: the wall of the city had twelve foundations, decorated with every kind of precious stone (Rev 21:19-20). The precious-stone city in Isa 54 is the prophetic type of the New Jerusalem in Rev 21 — the same architectural vision of YHWH's restored community expressed in cosmic materials.
54.12: I will make your pinnacles of rubies, your gates of crystal, and all your wall of precious stones. The four precious materials (antimony, sapphires, rubies, crystal) are continued in the gates-of-crystal and precious-stone walls. Every architectural element is precious-stone: the foundation, the pinnacles, the gates, the walls. In Rev 21:21: the twelve gates were twelve pearls, each gate made of a single pearl; the great street of the city was of gold, as pure as transparent glass. The New Jerusalem's architecture is the eschatological fulfillment of the precious-city rebuilt here. The afflicted, storm-tossed Zion becomes the most glorious city in all creation — the pattern of the resurrection: the body sown in dishonor is raised in glory (1 Cor 15:43).
54.13: All your children will be taught by YHWH, and great will be the peace of your children. Jesus quotes this verse in John 6:45 in his discourse on the Bread of Life: It is written in the Prophets, they will all be taught by God. Everyone who listens to the Father and learns from him comes to me. The taught-by-YHWH community of Isa 54:13 is the community that comes to Jesus — because Jesus is where the Father's teaching leads. The great peace of the children is the fruit of YHWH's instruction: shalom is the product of being taught by the source of shalom. In the NT: the Spirit of truth who guides you into all truth (John 16:13) is the Spirit who carries out the taught-by-YHWH promise — the Paraclete as the continuing fulfillment of Isa 54:13 in the community of Jesus.
54.14: In righteousness you will be established; you will be far from oppression, for you will not fear; and from terror, for it will not come near you. The righteousness-as-foundation (established in righteousness) is the covenant community's structural stability: when the foundation is YHWH's own righteousness (cf. the cornerstone of 28:16), oppression and terror cannot reach it. In the NT: the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe (Rom 3:22) is the foundation on which the church is established — not the community's moral achievement but the Servant's righteous act credited to those who trust in him. The far-from-terror community is the community that has been given the peace that surpasses understanding (Phil 4:7).
54.15: If anyone stirs up strife, it is not from me; whoever stirs up strife with you shall fall because of you. The attack-will-fail guarantee: any who assemble against Zion do so without YHWH's authorization and will therefore fail. This is not a blanket promise against difficulty but a specific guarantee that no assault against the covenant community has divine backing. In the NT: the gates of Hades will not prevail against the church (Matt 16:18) — the attacks on YHWH's community are real (the book of Acts, the letters to persecuted churches) but they cannot achieve their purpose of destroying what YHWH has built.
54.16: Behold, I have created the smith who blows the fire of coals and produces a weapon for its purpose. I have also created the ravager to destroy. YHWH is the Creator even of the weapons-maker and the destroyer: the smith who forges the weapon and the warrior who wields it are both YHWH's creations. This is the sovereignty-over-evil claim: YHWH does not merely react to human threats but governs the entire ecology of power including those who forge weapons against his community. In the NT: he works all things together for good for those who love God (Rom 8:28) — the Sovereign who created the smith who forges the weapon is the same one who uses every adversarial force for the ultimate good of his covenant community.
54.17: No weapon formed against you shall prosper, and every tongue that rises against you in judgment you will condemn. This is the heritage of the servants of YHWH and their vindication from me, declares YHWH. The no-weapon-shall-prosper and every-tongue-condemned are the two domains of hostile action — physical violence and verbal accusation — both of which YHWH nullifies for his servants. The heritage of the servants is the entire promise-set of this chapter: the enlarged tent, the covenant of peace, the precious-stone city, the YHWH-taught children. In the NT: who will bring any charge against those whom God has chosen? It is God who justifies. Who is the one who condemns? It is Christ Jesus who died — and who is at the right hand of God and is also interceding for us (Rom 8:33-34). The every-tongue-that-accuses is condemned by the Servant's own advocacy — the one who bore the accusations of ch 53 now intercedes against every accusation brought against his servants.

55.1: "Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters; and he who has no money, come, buy and eat! Come, buy wine and milk without money and without price" — the great grace-market: thirst without cost, wine and milk without payment. The economy of grace inverts market logic entirely: the more valuable the commodity (water, wine, milk — the necessities and delights of life), the less it costs the recipient. John 7:37-38: "On the last day of the festival, the great day, while Jesus was standing there, he cried out, 'Let anyone who is thirsty come to me, and let the one who believes in me drink. As the scripture has said, "Out of the believer's heart shall flow rivers of living water."'" Revelation 22:17: "The Spirit and the bride say, 'Come.' And let everyone who hears say, 'Come.' And let everyone who is thirsty come. Let anyone who wishes take the water of life as a gift." Jesus's Feast of Tabernacles cry and Revelation's closing invitation are the Isaiah 55:1 offer universalized — the cosmic marketplace where the water of life is given without price because the price has already been paid at the cross (1 Pet 1:18-19).
55.2: "Why do you spend your money for that which is not bread, and your labor for that which does not satisfy? Listen diligently to me, and eat what is good, and delight yourselves in rich food" — the futility of spending on non-sustaining commodities contrasted with free real food. The economic-spiritual paradox: money spent on substitutes, while the genuine article is free. Matthew 6:19-33: "Do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth... seek first his kingdom and his righteousness, and all these things will be given to you as well." John 6:27: "Do not work for food that spoils, but for food that endures to eternal life, which the Son of Man will give you." John 6:35: "I am the bread of life. Whoever comes to me will never go hungry." The non-satisfying labor of Isaiah 55:2 is the labor that produces "food that spoils" — Jesus identifies himself as the real food that satisfies the hunger which every substitute fails to address. The free invitation of v1 becomes the satisfying feast of v2 for those who come to Christ.
55.3: "Incline your ear, and come to me; hear, that your soul may live; and I will make with you an everlasting covenant, my steadfast, sure love for David" — the everlasting covenant (bĕrît ʿôlām) with David's sure mercies (ḥasdê Dāwid hanneʾĕmānîm). Acts 13:34: "The fact that God raised him from the dead, never to decay, is stated in these words: 'I will give you the holy and sure blessings promised to David.'" Paul's quotation of Isaiah 55:3 (LXX) is the only NT citation, applied to Christ's resurrection as the fulfillment of the Davidic covenant. The "steadfast sure love for David" = the indestructible covenant faithfulness that the resurrection proves — death could not hold the one to whom David's mercies belong. Hebrews 13:20: "the God of peace, who through the blood of the eternal covenant brought back from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great Shepherd of the sheep." The everlasting covenant of Isaiah 55:3 is the eternal covenant sealed in Christ's blood.
55.4: "Behold, I made him a witness to the peoples, a leader and commander for the peoples" — the Davidic king as witness-leader-commander to the nations. The royal titles go beyond Israel to include the peoples (lĕʾummîm). Revelation 1:5: "and from Jesus Christ, the faithful witness, the firstborn from the dead, and the ruler of the kings of the earth." Revelation 19:16: "On his robe and on his thigh he has this name written: King of Kings and Lord of Lords." The threefold description of the Davidic king in Isaiah 55:4 (witness, leader, commander) maps onto the Revelation 1:5 description of Christ: "faithful witness" (= witness), "firstborn from the dead" (= the everlasting covenant confirmed by resurrection), "ruler of the kings of the earth" (= leader/commander to the peoples). The fulfillment is total and explicit in Revelation's identification of Christ with the titles of Isaiah 55:4.
55.5: "Behold, you shall call a nation that you do not know, and a nation that did not know you shall run to you, because of the LORD your God, and of the Holy One of Israel, for he has glorified you" — the Gentile mission: unknown nations running to the glorified Servant-King. The running is initiated by YHWH's glorification of the king. Romans 15:9-12: Paul's catena of OT texts for the Gentile mission, including "Praise the Lord, all you Gentiles." Acts 28:28: "Therefore I want you to know that God's salvation has been sent to the Gentiles, and they will listen!" John 12:20-23: "Now there were some Greeks among those who went up to worship at the festival. They came to Philip with a request. 'Sir,' they said, 'we would like to see Jesus.' Jesus replied, 'The hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified.'" The Greeks coming to see Jesus triggers his announcement of glorification — Isaiah 55:5's nations running to the glorified one enacted in John 12. The glorification is the cross-and-resurrection (John 12:24: the grain of wheat falling into the ground).
55.6: "Seek the LORD while he may be found; call upon him while he is near" — the urgent invitation with temporal limits: there is a window; seek while the seeking can succeed. The eschatological urgency is the flip side of the free invitation of v1. 2 Corinthians 6:2: "For he says, 'In the time of my favor I heard you, and in the day of salvation I helped you.' I tell you, now is the time of God's favor, now is the day of salvation." Hebrews 3:13-15: "But encourage one another daily, as long as it is called 'Today,' so that none of you may be hardened by sin's deceitfulness... Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts." The "while he may be found" of Isaiah 55:6 is the "Today" of Hebrews — the time-limited seeking that requires present response. The proclamation of Christ creates the window (the age of grace, the day of salvation); the return of Christ closes it (Matt 25:10-12: "the door was shut").
55.7: "Let the wicked forsake his way, and the unrighteous man his thoughts; let him return to the LORD, that he may have compassion on him, and to our God, for he will abundantly pardon" — the repentance-invitation with the promise of abundant pardon (yarbeh lisĕlôaḥ, the pardon-multiplication). The returning of v7 echoes Israel's return-narrative (Deut 30:2; Jer 3:22). Luke 15:17-20: "'I will set out and go back to my father and say to him: Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you'... But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion for him." 1 John 1:9: "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just and will forgive us our sins and purify us from all unrighteousness." The "abundant pardon" of Isaiah 55:7 is the father-running-to-meet-the-returning-son of Luke 15 — and the forgiveness available because Christ absorbed the punishment is what makes the pardon both abundant and just (1 John 1:9 grounds forgiveness in Christ's faithfulness and justice).
55.8: "For my thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways, declares the LORD" — the opening of the divine transcendence argument (vv8-9): the foundational epistemological gap between divine and human reason. 1 Corinthians 1:18-21: "For the message of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God... For the foolishness of God is wiser than human wisdom, and the weakness of God is stronger than human strength." Romans 11:33-34: "Oh, the depth of the riches of the wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable his judgments, and his paths beyond tracing out! Who has known the mind of the Lord?" The "thoughts not your thoughts" of Isaiah 55:8 is the hermeneutic that explains the cross: no one reasoned their way to a crucified Messiah as the center of salvation history. The cross is the supreme instance of divine thoughts that are not human thoughts — wisdom concealed in apparent foolishness (1 Cor 2:7-8).
55.9: "For as the heavens are higher than the earth, so are my ways higher than your ways and my thoughts than your thoughts" — the cosmic scale of the gap: heaven-to-earth distance measures the divine-human thought-gap. The transcendence is vertical and absolute. Romans 9:20: "But who are you, a human being, to talk back to God? 'Shall what is formed say to the one who formed it, "Why did you make me like this?"'" Isaiah 45:9 (the potter-clay analogy) underlies Paul's argument. The height-of-heavens gap of Isaiah 55:9 is the gap that makes election mysterious (Romans 9-11) and the cross counter-intuitive (1 Cor 1). Ephesians 3:18-19: "may have power, together with all the Lord's holy people, to grasp how wide and long and high and deep is the love of Christ, and to know this love that surpasses knowledge." The love that surpasses knowledge (Eph 3:19) is the Isaiah 55:9 ways that are higher than our ways — and its full expression is the cross.
55.10: "For as the rain and the snow come down from heaven and do not return there but water the earth, making it bring forth and sprout, giving seed to the sower and bread to the eater" — the rain-analogy for the divine word: rain descends purposefully and does not return until it has accomplished its purpose (vegetation, seed, bread). The analogy establishes the effectiveness of the word. John 1:14: "The Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us." Hebrews 4:12: "For the word of God is alive and active. Sharper than any double-edged sword, it penetrates even to dividing soul and spirit, joints and marrow; it judges the thoughts and attitudes of the heart." The rain/snow that comes from heaven and produces fruit is the type of the Word who descended from heaven (John 6:38: "For I have come down from heaven not to do my will but to do the will of him who sent me") and never returned empty — the resurrection is the return with abundant fruit (John 12:24: the grain of wheat that dies and bears much fruit).
55.11: "So shall my word be that goes out from my mouth; it shall not return to me empty, but it shall accomplish that which I purpose, and shall succeed in the thing for which I sent it" — the word's certain effectiveness: the divine speech-act that always accomplishes its intention. John 1:1-14: "In the beginning was the Word... the Word became flesh." John 6:63: "The Spirit gives life; the flesh counts for nothing. The words I have spoken to you — they are full of the Spirit and life." Isaiah 55:11 is the ground of missional confidence: the word preached cannot fail. Romans 10:14-17: "How, then, can they call on the one they have not believed in? And how can they believe in the one of whom they have not heard?... faith comes from hearing the message, and the message is heard through the word about Christ." The word that "shall not return empty" is the proclaimed gospel — but behind the proclaimed word is the incarnate Word (John 1:14) who accomplished the Father's purpose perfectly and returned to the Father (John 17:4: "I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do").
55.12: "For you shall go out in joy and be led forth in peace; the mountains and the hills before you shall break forth into singing, and all the trees of the field shall clap their hands" — the creation-chorus of the new exodus: mountains singing, trees applauding. The non-human creation participates in the homecoming. Romans 8:19-22: "For the creation waits in eager expectation for the children of God to be revealed... the creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay and brought into the freedom and glory of the children of God... the whole creation has been groaning as in the pains of childbirth right up to the present time." Revelation 5:13: "Then I heard every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and on the sea, and all that is in them, singing: 'To him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be praise and honor and glory and power, for ever and ever!'" The singing mountains and clapping trees of Isaiah 55:12 are the creation-wide praise of Revelation 5:13 — triggered by the Lamb's victory that sets creation free. The new creation in Christ (2 Cor 5:17) is the down payment on the cosmic liberation that culminates in every creature singing.
55.13: "Instead of the thorn shall come up the cypress; instead of the brier shall come up the myrtle; and it shall make a name for the LORD, an everlasting sign that shall not be cut off" — the thorn-to-cypress reversal: the curse-plants (thorns, briers — Genesis 3:18) replaced by noble trees (cypress, myrtle). The reversal of the curse. Galatians 3:13: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, for it is written: 'Cursed is everyone who is hung on a pole.'" The crown of thorns on Christ's head (John 19:2) is the thorns of Genesis 3 taken into himself — and the cypress-and-myrtle replacement is the new creation in which the curse is reversed. Revelation 22:3: "No longer will there be any curse." The thorn-to-cypress of Isaiah 55:13 is the curse-to-blessing of the new creation, accomplished by Christ who wore the thorns and rose as the everlasting sign that shall not be cut off (the risen Christ who "dies no more", Rom 6:9).

56.1: Thus says the LORD: Keep justice, and do righteousness, for soon my salvation will come, and my righteousness be revealed. The urgency of near salvation — the same eschatological pressure that frames every NT ethical exhortation. Romans 13:11-12: salvation is nearer to us now than when we first believed. The night is far gone; the day is at hand. 1 Peter 1:5: a salvation ready to be revealed in the last time. The salvation that is about to come is the revelation of Jesus Christ himself — the one Paul calls our righteousness (1 Cor 1:30). Isaiah 56:1 is the text whose urgency the entire NT inhabits: the kingdom is at hand, act accordingly.
56.2: Blessed is the man who does this, and the son of man who holds it fast, who keeps the sabbath, not profaning it, and keeps his hand from doing any evil. The beatitude-form connects to Jesus's Beatitudes (Matt 5:3-12). Matthew 12:8: the Son of Man is lord of the Sabbath — Jesus claims sovereignty over the institution Isaiah 56:2 treats as the covenant marker. Hebrews 4:9-11: there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God, for whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works as God did from his. Christ is both the Lord of the Sabbath and the Sabbath-rest itself — the one who fulfills the Sabbath's purpose so that his people cease their own works of merit and rest in his.
56.3: Let not the foreigner who has joined himself to the LORD say, The LORD will surely separate me from his people; and let not the eunuch say, Behold, I am a dry tree. The radical inclusion of those physically disqualified by the Mosaic code (Deut 23:1 excluded eunuchs from the assembly). Acts 8:26-40 narrates the direct fulfillment: Philip baptizes the Ethiopian eunuch on the road from Jerusalem — a physical eunuch, a foreigner, joining the covenant community through faith in Christ. The barrier that Isaiah 56:3 begins to dismantle is the barrier that the cross removes entirely: Galatians 3:28 — there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus.
56.4: For thus says the LORD: To the eunuchs who keep my sabbaths, who choose the things that please me and hold fast my covenant. The qualification shifts from physical status to covenant faithfulness — keeping sabbaths and holding fast the covenant, not biological wholeness. This is the logic of the new covenant: what matters is not circumcision or ethnic descent but new creation (Gal 6:15). Philippians 3:3: we are the circumcision, who worship by the Spirit of God and glory in Christ Jesus and put no confidence in the flesh. The eunuch who holds the covenant is more fully in the assembly than the physically whole person who despises it.
56.5: I will give in my house and within my walls a monument and a name better than sons and daughters; I will give them an everlasting name that shall not be cut off. The everlasting name given in YHWH's house — better than biological legacy. Revelation 2:17: the overcomer receives a white stone with a new name written on it. Revelation 3:12: I will write on him the name of my God and the name of the city of my God... and my own new name. In Christ every believer receives an everlasting name inscribed by the risen Lord — an identity that death cannot cut off and infertility cannot diminish. The dry tree of verse 3 produces fruit that outlasts any family line.
56.6: And the foreigners who join themselves to the LORD, to minister to him, to love the name of the LORD, and to be his servants, everyone who keeps the sabbath and does not profane it, and holds fast my covenant. Full inclusion of Gentiles who love YHWH's name. Ephesians 2:12-13: those who were once alienated from the commonwealth of Israel and strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world... have now been brought near by the blood of Christ. Romans 15:8-12: Christ became a servant to confirm the promises to the patriarchs so that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy — citing Isaiah 11:10 (the Root of Jesse as banner to the nations). Isaiah 56:6 is the OT ground of the Gentile mission.
56.7: These I will bring to my holy mountain, and make them joyful in my house of prayer; their burnt offerings and their sacrifices will be accepted on my altar; for my house shall be called a house of prayer for all peoples. Jesus quotes this verse directly at the temple cleansing: Mark 11:17 — Is it not written, My house shall be called a house of prayer for all the nations? But you have made it a den of robbers. Matthew 21:13; Luke 19:46 both record the citation. The temple action is Jesus's enacted commentary on Isaiah 56:7: the house of prayer for all peoples has been turned into an exclusionary marketplace. The court of the Gentiles — the only space where non-Jews could pray — had been commercialized. Christ restores the universal scope that Isaiah promised.
56.8: The Lord GOD, who gathers the outcasts of Israel, declares, I will gather yet others to him besides those already gathered. The promise of gathering beyond Israel — the inclusive scope of eschatological salvation. John 10:16: I have other sheep that are not of this fold. I must bring them also, and they will listen to my voice. So there will be one flock, one shepherd. John 11:51-52: Caiaphas unwittingly prophesies that Jesus would die to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad. The gathering of others beyond the gathered-Israel is the Gentile mission — and the Good Shepherd who gathers them is the fulfillment of the YHWH who gathers in Isaiah 56:8.
56.9: All you beasts of the field, come to devour — all you beasts in the forest. The summons of the devouring beasts shifts abruptly from the inclusive gathering (vv3-8) to predator-judgment language — setting up the indictment of faithless shepherds in vv10-12. Acts 20:29: Paul warns the Ephesian elders — after my departure fierce wolves will come in among you, not sparing the flock. Matthew 7:15: beware of false prophets, who come to you in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves. The beasts of the field that Isaiah summons are the false shepherds-turned-predators who devour what Christ gathers — the same contrast Jesus draws in John 10:10-12.
56.10: His watchmen are blind; they are all without knowledge; they are all silent dogs; they cannot bark, warning the people. The corrupt watchmen — blind, ignorant, silent where they should warn. Jesus's condemnation of the scribes and Pharisees in Matthew 23 covers this explicitly: you blind guides (Matt 23:16, 24), blind fools (23:17), leaders who shut the kingdom in people's faces and do not enter themselves (23:13). The silent dogs who cannot bark are the religious leaders who see judgment approaching and give no real warning — absorbed in their own status rather than protecting the people. The contrast is the watchman-shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep (John 10:11).
56.11: The dogs have a mighty appetite; they never have enough. But they are shepherds who have no understanding; they have all turned to their own way, each to his own gain, one and all. The self-serving shepherds — Ezekiel 34:2-4 develops this theme (shepherds who feed themselves, not the flock). John 10:12-13: the hired hand, who is not the shepherd and does not own the sheep, sees the wolf coming and leaves the sheep and flees... he cares nothing for the sheep. The Good Shepherd who is the explicit contrast to this verse is Christ: I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep (John 10:11). Every self-serving religious leader is measured against the one who gave everything for those in his care.
56.12: Come, they say, let me get wine; let us fill ourselves with strong drink; and tomorrow will be like this day, great beyond measure. The corrupt leadership's hedonism — filling themselves with wine while judgment approaches. Luke 12:19-20: the rich fool says eat, drink, be merry and dies that night. Luke 12:45-46: the servant who says my master is delayed and begins to eat and drink and get drunk is cut in pieces when the master returns unexpectedly. Isaiah 56:1 opens with my salvation is near — the eschatological urgency that makes Isaiah 56:12's attitude criminal. The antithesis is the watching servant of Luke 12:35-40 who keeps his lamps burning and waits for the master's return.

58.1: A revelation of God: cry aloud, spare not; lift up your voice like a trumpet; declare to my people their transgression — yet they seek YHWH daily and delight to know his ways, as if they were a nation that did righteousness. The exposure of religious performance that masks ethical failure is the consistent concern of the prophets. Jesus applies the same critique to his contemporaries: Matthew 15:8 (this people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far from me); Matthew 23:23 (you tithe mint and dill and cumin but have neglected the weightier matters of the law: justice and mercy and faithfulness). The God who sees through form to substance is the God who reveals his Son as the one who does the substance perfectly.
58.2: A revelation of God: they ask of YHWH righteous judgments and delight to draw near to God — yet without the righteousness the delight-claims presuppose. The disconnect between expressed delight in God and actual righteousness is the hypocrite's signature. Christ exposes it directly: Matthew 7:21-23 (not everyone who says to me 'Lord, Lord' will enter the kingdom of heaven, but the one who does the will of my Father). The one who does the Father's will in full, and calls others to do the same, is the antidote to the formal religion of Isaiah 58:2.
58.3: A revelation of God: why have we fasted, and you see it not? — because on your fast day you seek your own pleasure and oppress all your workers. The fasting that accompanies exploitation is not fasting before YHWH. James 2:14-17 applies the same principle: faith without works is dead — the one who says go in peace, be warmed and filled while doing nothing for the needy has not met the requirement. Christ's critique of the Pharisees' ostentatious fasting (Matt 6:16-18) targets the same self-displaying devotion that Isaiah 58:3 exposes.
58.4: A revelation of God: your fasting leads to quarreling and strife, even to striking with a wicked fist — fasting like this is not heard on high. The performance of religious discipline that fuels rather than restrains conflict is a contradiction in terms. Paul frames the NT version: Galatians 5:14-15 (the whole law is fulfilled in love your neighbor as yourself — but if you bite and devour one another...). The Spirit produces peace, not quarreling — the fruit of genuine encounter with God (Gal 5:22-23) is what fasting is meant to cultivate.
58.5: A revelation of God: is this the fast I choose — a day for a man to humble himself, to bow his head like a reed and to spread sackcloth and ashes? Is this what you call a fast? The external gestures without internal and ethical reality are the target. Christ does not abolish fasting but reframes it entirely: Matthew 6:17-18 (when you fast, anoint your head and wash your face, that your fasting may not be seen by others but by your Father who is in secret). The fast that YHWH chooses is defined in vv. 6-7; the fast Christ exemplifies is his entire self-giving life (cf. Phil 2:7: he emptied himself).
58.6: A direct typological connection. Hălō' zeh ṣôm 'eb̠ĕḥārēhû pittēaḥ ḥarsinnôt reša' hattēr 'aguddôt môṭāh wĕšallaḥ rĕṣûṣîm ḥop̄šîm wĕk̠ol-môṭāh tĕnatēqû — is this not the fast I choose: to loose the bonds of wickedness, to undo the straps of the yoke, to let the oppressed go free, and to break every yoke? Luke 4:18-19 quotes the related passage of Isaiah 61:1-2 as Jesus' programmatic announcement: he has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives and recovering of sight to the blind, to set at liberty those who are oppressed. Hebrews 2:14-15 frames Christ's death as the liberation of all who were enslaved through fear of death. The fast YHWH chooses is the shape of Christ's entire mission: the undoing of bondage in all its forms.
58.7: A shadow: is it not to share your bread with the hungry and bring the homeless poor into your house; when you see the naked, to cover him? Matthew 25:35-40 makes this the criterion of judgment: I was hungry and you gave me food, I was a stranger and you welcomed me, I was naked and you clothed me — and identifies Christ with the needy neighbor. James 2:14-17 frames it as the test of living faith. Christ as the bread of life (John 6:35) and the incarnate God who takes in the outcast (Luke 14:21-23: bring in the poor and crippled) is the fulfillment of the true fast at the personal level, which he then extends through his community.
58.8: A shadow: then shall your light break forth like the dawn, and your healing shall spring up speedily — your righteousness shall go before you; the glory of YHWH shall be your rear guard. The light breaking forth like dawn is the imagery that the NT applies to Christ: Luke 1:78-79 (the sunrise from on high will visit us, to give light to those who sit in darkness); John 8:12 (I am the light of the world); 2 Corinthians 4:6 (the light of the knowledge of God's glory in the face of Jesus Christ). The healing that springs up speedily is the sign of the messianic age: Matthew 11:5 (the blind receive their sight, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed).
58.9: A revelation of God: then you shall call and YHWH will answer; you shall cry and he will say, here I am (hinnēnî). The divine hinnēnî — here I am, I am present, I am available — is the summit of covenant relationship. Christ as the one in whom YHWH is permanently present: John 14:9 (whoever has seen me has seen the Father); Matthew 18:20 (where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them); John 14:21 (I will manifest myself to him). The God who says hinnēnî in Isaiah 58:9 says it definitively and permanently in the incarnate Son.
58.10: A shadow: if you pour yourself out for the hungry and satisfy the desire of the afflicted, then your light shall rise in the darkness. The self-pouring (tāp̄ēq — to pour out, empty) for the needy is the shape of agape love. Paul describes Christ's self-giving in the same language: Philippians 2:7 (he emptied himself, taking the form of a servant). 1 John 3:17 applies this to the community: whoever has the world's goods and sees his brother in need yet closes his heart against him — how does God's love abide in him? The community formed by the self-poured Christ practices the self-pouring of Isaiah 58:10.
58.11: A shadow: YHWH will guide you continually and satisfy your desire in scorched places — you shall be like a watered garden, like a spring whose waters do not fail (wĕk̠ĕmôṣā' mayim 'ăšer lō' yĕk̠azzĕb̠û mêmāyw). The never-failing spring is Christ's promise: John 4:14 (the water that I will give him will become a spring of water welling up to eternal life); John 7:38-39 (rivers of living water will flow from within — which he said about the Spirit). The guidance continually given (the pillar of cloud and fire in Exodus) is now the Spirit of truth who guides into all truth (John 16:13). YHWH's unfailing spring and guide are embodied in Christ and given through the Spirit.
58.12: A shadow: your ancient ruins shall be rebuilt; you shall be called the repairer of the breach (gōdēr peretz), the restorer of streets to dwell in. Ephesians 2:14-16 describes Christ as the one who has broken down the dividing wall of hostility — making peace, reconciling both to God in one body through the cross. The breach (peretz) between God and humanity is the gap Christ's cross spans; the breach between Jew and Gentile is the wall Christ demolishes. The rebuilding of ancient ruins points to the new creation (Rev 21:5: behold, I am making all things new) in which every ruin is restored and every breach repaired.
58.13: A revelation of God: if you turn back your foot from the Sabbath and call the Sabbath a delight ('ōneg), the holy day of YHWH honorable — then you shall take delight in YHWH. Christ as Lord of the Sabbath (Mark 2:28) does not abolish the Sabbath principle but fulfills it: Hebrews 4:9-10 (a Sabbath rest remains for the people of God; whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works as God did from his). The delight in YHWH that true Sabbath-keeping produces is the participation in the divine rest that Christ opens — the rest of completed work, finished redemption.
58.14: A revelation of God: then you shall take delight in YHWH, and I will make you ride on the heights of the earth, and feed you with the heritage of Jacob your father — for the mouth of YHWH has spoken. The promised blessing of riding the heights and inheriting the full covenant heritage is the eschatological reward of those who keep covenant faithfully. Revelation 21:7 frames the same promise: the one who conquers will inherit these things. Christ is the one who rides the heights (Rev 19:11-16) and leads those who are his into the same inheritance (1 Pet 1:4; Heb 6:17). The mouth of YHWH has spoken — and that Word took flesh and dwelt among us (John 1:14).

57.1: The righteous man perishes, and no one lays it to heart; devout men are taken away, while no one understands that the righteous man is taken away from calamity. The paradox of the righteous death: the community does not perceive that the taking-away of the righteous is actually merciful removal from coming judgment. The righteous perishing-without-being-understood is the shape of the Servant's death: he was led like a sheep to the slaughter, and as a sheep before its shearers is silent, so he did not open his mouth — in humiliation his justice was denied (Acts 8:32-33, citing Isa 53:7-8). The no-one-understands the righteous man's death in 57:1 is the same incomprehension that surrounds the Servant's death — after his suffering he will see light (53:11) but no one sees that his removal was for our healing.
57.2: He enters into peace; they rest in their beds, each who walked in his uprightness. The upright man's death is an entry into peace (shalom) and rest (menucha). The Servant who was denied peace in his humiliation (53:8: by oppression and judgment he was taken away) enters the peace of the vindicated righteous after death. In the NT: blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from now on; they will rest from their labor (Rev 14:13). The rest-in-their-beds of the righteous departed is the theological category for the faithful dead who have entered their Sabbath rest (Heb 4:9-10: there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God; anyone who enters God's rest also rests from their own work). The shalom-entry at death anticipates the resurrection's fullness.
57.3: But you, come here, you children of a sorceress, you offspring of an adulterer and a prostitute. The oracle pivots to the idolaters: the sorceress-children are the covenant community that has pursued the occult religious practices of the Canaanites. The offspring-of-an-adulterer is the covenant-faithlessness language of Hosea — spiritual adultery as the category for idol-worship. In the NT: you adulterous people, don't you know that friendship with the world means enmity against God? (Jas 4:4). The children of the sorceress are the NT's children of the devil (John 8:44: you belong to your father, the devil) — defined by what/whom they serve rather than by ethnic origin.
57.4: Who are you mocking? At whom do you sneer and stick out your tongue? Are you not children of transgression, offspring of deceit? The mocking-and-sneering is directed at the righteous who suffer (vv 1-2) — the idolaters mock the very ones whose death is entered into peace. The children-of-transgression are defined by their parentage in sin: they mock the righteous because they are the offspring of deception. In the NT, the same dynamic is the crowd mocking Jesus at Golgotha: he trusts in God; let God rescue him now if he wants him, for he said I am the Son of God (Matt 27:43). The mocking of the dying righteous is the historical expression of the spiritual mockery that Isa 57:4 diagnoses.
57.5: You who burn with lust among the oaks, under every green tree, who slaughter children in the valleys, under the clefts of the rocks. The specific idolatry indicted: the fertility-cult rites under trees (Baal-worship, with sexual and child-sacrifice components) are the covenant violations that define the sorceress-children. The slaughter-of-children in valleys (likely the Valley of Hinnom, Gehenna — 2 Kgs 23:10, Jer 7:31) is the most extreme covenant violation. In the NT: Jesus's warnings about Gehenna (Matt 5:22, 29-30, 10:28, 23:33) are the eschatological judgment-language derived from this valley — the place of child-sacrifice becomes the image for the final judgment of those who sacrificed the innocent.
57.6: Among the smooth stones of the valley is your portion; they, they are your lot; to them you have poured out a drink offering, you have brought a grain offering. Shall I relent concerning these things? The smooth-stones-as-portion is the ironic assignment: the idolater who chose smooth stones as gods receives smooth stones as their inheritance. The grain offerings and drink offerings poured out to stones are the inverted worship that belongs to YHWH alone. The shall-I-relent rhetorical question implies the answer: no, not without repentance. In the NT: what a person sows, they will also reap (Gal 6:7). The portion-you-chose is the portion-you-receive — the assignment of inheritance mirrors the object of ultimate loyalty.
57.7: On a high and lofty mountain you have set your bed, and there you went up to offer sacrifice. The high-mountain bed is the Canaanite high place of fertility-cult worship — the sexual-cultic imagery of the bed on the mountain is the covenant-faithlessness expressed physically. YHWH is the one who dwells on the high and holy mountain (v 15); the idolater's setting-a-bed-on-the-mountain is the parody-worship that inverts YHWH's genuine dwelling. In the NT, the distinction between the mountain of YHWH (Zion, the new Jerusalem, Heb 12:22) and the mountains of idolatrous worship is resolved: true worshipers will worship the Father in Spirit and truth, neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem (John 4:21-24).
57.8: Behind your door and doorpost you have set up your memorial; forsaking me, you uncovered your bed; you made it wide; you made a covenant with them; you loved their bed; you looked on their nakedness. The memorial behind the door-post inverts the mezuzah (Deut 6:9 — write YHWH's commands on your doorposts): instead of covenant commands, the idol-memorial marks the home. The covenant-with-lovers is the classic Hosea-Ezekiel language of spiritual adultery (Ezek 16, Hos 2). In the NT, the household devoted to YHWH is the church (1 Tim 3:15: the household of God), and the faithfulness required in the household is the chastity of loyalty to one master: no one can serve two masters (Matt 6:24).
57.9: You journeyed to Molech with oil and multiplied your perfumes; you sent your envoys far off, and sent them down even to Sheol. The extreme extent of the idolaters' pursuit: they go as far as Sheol (the realm of the dead) in their devotion to Molech. The going-down-to-Sheol is both literal (the child-sacrifice rites associated with Molech are a descent into death) and spiritual (the direction of all idol-devotion is downward, toward death). In the NT: the wages of sin is death (Rom 6:23). The journeying-to-Sheol for Molech is the directional opposite of the Servant's descent-to-Sheol on behalf of others — Christ descended into the lower parts of the earth (Eph 4:9) not to worship death but to conquer it.
57.10: You were wearied with the length of your way, but you did not say, It is hopeless; you found new strength, and so you did not grow faint. The relentlessness of idolatry: even when exhausted by the effort, the idolater finds new energy to continue. The not-saying-it-is-hopeless is the stubborn persistence of the devoted-to-the-wrong-thing. This is the inverse of YHWH's servant who will not grow faint or be discouraged (42:4). In the NT: those who run after other gods find no satisfaction (cf. the Prodigal Son's persistence in the far country before coming to himself, Luke 15:17). The energy for idol-pursuit that could have been directed toward YHWH is the theological waste that the gospel addresses: let us run with perseverance the race marked out for us (Heb 12:1) — but in the right direction.
57.11: Whom did you dread and fear, so that you lied, and did not remember me, did not lay it to heart? Have I not held my peace, even for a long time, and you do not fear me? The diagnosis of the root cause: not remembering YHWH drove the idolatry. The long-divine-silence was misread as YHWH's absence or indifference. In the NT, the apparent silence of God during suffering is the test of faith — Jesus cries My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? (Matt 27:46), but the silence is not abandonment: you who fear him, trust in him — for he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one; he has not hidden his face from him but has listened to his cry for help (Ps 22:24). The silence of YHWH is not his absence but his providence.
57.12: I will reveal your righteousness and your deeds, but they will not benefit you. The exposure of the idolater's so-called righteousness is the judgment of the divine court: your deeds-done-in-worship-of-idols will be held up and will be shown to profit nothing. In the NT: each one's work will become clear; the Day will bring it to light; it will be revealed by fire, and the fire will test the quality of each person's work (1 Cor 3:13). The works that cannot endure the light-of-judgment are the equivalents of the idol-works that YHWH promises to reveal as empty.
57.13: When you cry out, let your collection of idols rescue you! The wind will carry them all off; a breath will take them away. But whoever takes refuge in me will inherit the land and will possess my holy mountain. The contrast is absolute: the wind-blown-idols versus the refuge-in-YHWH that inherits the land. The whoever-takes-refuge has the same force as the gospel's whoever believes: the inheritance is available to all who trust in YHWH and is denied to those who trust in manufactured things. In the NT: whoever believes in him will not perish but will have eternal life (John 3:16) — the inheritance-of-the-land is the NT's eternal-life inheritance (Matt 5:5: the meek will inherit the earth). The wind that blows away the idols cannot blow away what YHWH establishes.
57.14: And it shall be said: Build up, build up, prepare the way; remove every obstruction from my people's way. The highway-preparation oracle echoes 40:3 (prepare the way of YHWH in the wilderness) and 62:10. The removal of obstructions from the people's way is the work of the precursor — in the NT, John the Baptist (Matt 3:3: prepare the way of the Lord; make straight paths for him). But the ultimate obstacle-remover is the Servant himself: the sin and guilt that blocked humanity's access to YHWH are removed by the atonement of ch 53. The way-cleared-for-the-people is the new-and-living way opened through the veil, that is his body (Heb 10:20).
57.15: For this is what the high and exalted One says — he who lives forever, whose name is holy: I live in a high and holy place, but also with the one who is contrite and lowly in spirit, to revive the spirit of the lowly and to revive the heart of the contrite. The double-dwelling of YHWH — high-and-holy and contrite-and-lowly — is the theological climax of the chapter and one of the most Christologically significant verses in Isaiah. YHWH who lives in the high and holy place also dwells with the lowly: this is the condescension of the divine nature. Jesus is the incarnate form of this double-dwelling: the one who was in the form of God emptied himself, taking the form of a servant (Phil 2:6-7) — the high-and-exalted-one who lived in the lowest place. The blessed-are-the-poor-in-spirit (Matt 5:3) and the learn-from-me, for I am gentle and humble in heart (Matt 11:29) are the Christological fulfillment of YHWH's dwelling-with-the-contrite-and-lowly. God opposes the proud and gives grace to the humble (Jas 4:6, 1 Pet 5:5, citing Prov 3:34): the principle of Isa 57:15 applied as ethical instruction to the NT community.
57.16: For I will not contend forever, nor will I always be angry, for the spirit would grow faint before me — the breath of life that I made. The limit of divine anger: YHWH's anger has a built-in mercy-limit because the spirit of man would expire if YHWH's contention had no end. The breath-of-life made by YHWH (Gen 2:7: YHWH breathed into his nostrils the breath of life) is too fragile to sustain infinite divine wrath. In the NT: God did not appoint us to suffer wrath but to receive salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ (1 Thess 5:9). The I-will-not-contend-forever is the theological ground for the atonement: if YHWH's anger were eternal and absolute against all sinners, there would be no possibility of covenant restoration. The finite-anger that makes space for mercy is the prerequisite for the gospel's rescue.
57.17: Because of the sin of his covetousness I was angry; I struck him; I hid my face and was angry, but he went on backsliding in the way of his own heart. The divine discipline that did not produce repentance: I struck him — the exile, the affliction — but he continued in his own way. The hiding-of-the-face is the covenant-withdrawal of 54:8, here described as consequent to sin. The continuing-in-one's-own-way after discipline is the stubbornness that 48:4 diagnosed as bronze-foreheaded. In the NT, the same stubborn-persistence-despite-discipline is addressed in Heb 12:5-11: do not despise the Lord's discipline — it is for your good, so that you may share in his holiness.
57.18: I have seen his ways, but I will heal him; I will lead him and restore comfort to him and to his mourners. The divine initiative in healing: YHWH has seen the continuing backsliding (v 17) and chooses to heal anyway — the same logic as 43:25 (I blot out your sins for my own sake). The I-will-heal is the Servant's mission explicitly: he was pierced for our transgressions; by his wounds we are healed (53:5). The healing, leading, and comforting — three dimensions of restoration — are the work of the Spirit (John 14:16-17: the Paraclete who comforts; the Spirit who leads you into all truth; the healing of the new heart). The mourners who receive comfort are the community of Matt 5:4: blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted.
57.19: Creating the fruit of the lips. Peace, peace, to those far and near, says YHWH, and I will heal him. Paul applies this verse directly to the crucified Christ in Eph 2:17: he came and preached peace to you who were far away and peace to those who were near. The peace-to-those-far-and-near in Isa 57:19 was understood in Judaism as the inclusion of Gentiles (those far away) alongside Jews (those near): YHWH's shalom extends beyond the covenant community to the nations. Paul's argument in Eph 2:14-18 is that Christ himself is our peace, who has made the two groups one and has destroyed the barrier, the dividing wall of hostility — and has come and preached peace to those far and near (quoting v 19 exactly). The fruit-of-the-lips is the gospel proclamation through which this peace is announced: the lips that confess Christ as Lord make real the peace that Isa 57:19 promises.
57.20: But the wicked are like the tossing sea, which cannot rest, whose waves cast up mire and mud. The no-rest of the wicked contrasts with the entering-into-peace of the righteous (v 2) and the peace-peace to those near and far (v 19): the wicked have neither the rest of the righteous dead nor the shalom of the reconciled. The tossing sea is the primordial chaos-water of Gen 1:2 that YHWH ordered at creation — the wicked inhabit the un-creation of moral chaos. In the NT: the sea gave up the dead that were in it (Rev 20:13) and then the sea was no more (Rev 21:1) — the tossing-sea of wickedness's final home is removed in the new creation. What the wicked inhabit is what YHWH's new creation abolishes.
57.21: There is no peace, says my God, for the wicked. The closing refrain (repeated verbatim from 48:22) is the negative form of the chapter's positive theme: shalom is the inheritance of those who take refuge in YHWH (v 13) and the proclamation to those far and near who are reconciled through the Servant (v 19); the wicked who remain in their idolatry and rebellion have no access to this shalom. In the NT: there is no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Rom 8:1) — the positive form of the promise; its negative: the cowardly, the unbelieving, the vile, the murderers, the sexually immoral, those who practice magic arts, the idolaters and all liars — they will be consigned to the fiery lake (Rev 21:8). The no-peace-for-the-wicked is the final canonical word on the condition of the unreconciled.

59.1: Behold, the LORD's hand is not shortened, that it cannot save, or his ear dull, that it cannot hear. The not-shortened arm of YHWH — the arm that is powerful enough to save. Isaiah 53:1 asks: to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed? — and answers with the Servant's suffering. The arm that appears shortened and defeated at the cross is the arm of YHWH acting in its fullest capacity: Hebrews 2:14-15 — Christ destroyed the one who has the power of death through death. What looks like the arm failing is the arm reaching all the way into death to bring out the captives. Acts 2:24: God raised Jesus, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it.
59.2: But your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God, and your sins have hidden his face from you so that he does not hear. The fundamental problem of human existence: sin as separation. This is the diagnosis that makes the gospel necessary. Romans 5:10: while we were enemies we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son. 2 Corinthians 5:18-19: all this is from God, who through Christ reconciled us to himself... in Christ God was reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them. The separation of Isaiah 59:2 is precisely what Ephesians 2:13-16 says has been overcome: those who were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ who is our peace.
59.3: For your hands are defiled with blood and your fingers with iniquity; your lips have spoken lies; your tongue mutters wickedness. The comprehensive indictment of the sinning body — hands, fingers, lips, tongue — every instrument of human action corrupted.
