Paul's sin-catena in Romans 3:10-18 weaves together texts from Psalms and Isaiah precisely because Isaiah 59 provides a full-body inventory of human depravity. The same Spirit who reveals this indictment provides the remedy through Christ: Titus 3:5 — he saved us, not because of works done by us in righteousness, but according to his own mercy, by the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Spirit.
59.4: No one enters suit justly; no one goes to law honestly; they rely on empty pleas, they speak lies, they conceive mischief and give birth to iniquity. The corrupt legal system — a sign of total social breakdown. This is the context for Paul's charge in Romans 3:10-18 that none is righteous, no, not one. John 5:22: the Father judges no one, but has given all judgment to the Son. The legal corruption of Isaiah 59:4 makes necessary the appointment of the Son as the one true just judge — the one in whose court no empty plea succeeds and no false testimony stands.
59.5: They hatch adders' eggs; they weave the spider's web; he who eats their eggs dies, and from one that is crushed a viper is hatched. The poisonous work of the wicked — eggs that kill, webs that trap. Matthew 3:7; 23:33: Jesus calls the Pharisees and Sadducees a brood of vipers — using the serpent-imagery of Isaiah 59:5. Luke 10:19: Jesus gives the disciples authority to tread on serpents and scorpions — the reversal of the viper-web trap. The work of Christ is the crushing of the serpent's head (Gen 3:15) — the one who destroys the egg-layers and dismantles the web.
59.6: Their webs will not serve as clothing; men will not cover themselves with what they make. Their works are works of iniquity, and deeds of violence are in their hands. The self-made covering that fails — human attempts to clothe themselves with their own works. Genesis 3:7: Adam and Eve made fig-leaf coverings — the first human attempt at self-cover after the fall. Genesis 3:21: YHWH made garments of skin — the first divine provision of covering through sacrifice. Christ is the true garment: Romans 13:14 — put on the Lord Jesus Christ. Galatians 3:27: as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ. The failed webs of human self-righteousness are replaced by the robe of Christ's righteousness.
59.7: Their feet run to evil, and they are swift to shed innocent blood; their thoughts are thoughts of iniquity; desolation and destruction are in their highways. Romans 3:15-17 quotes this verse directly as part of the comprehensive OT indictment of universal human sinfulness: their feet are swift to shed blood; in their paths are ruin and misery, and the way of peace they have not known. Paul uses Isaiah 59:7-8 as the culminating evidence in his argument that all — Jew and Gentile — are under sin and need the righteousness that comes through faith in Christ (Rom 3:21-22). The feet swift to shed blood include those that carried Jesus to Calvary.
59.8: The way of peace they have not known, and there is no justice in their paths; they have made their roads crooked; no one who treads on them knows peace. Romans 3:17 cites this as the final word in Paul's universal-sin indictment: the way of peace they have not known. Ephesians 2:14-17: he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility... and he came and preached peace to you who were far off and peace to those who were near. Christ is the way of peace that Isaiah 59:8 says they have not known — the one who makes crooked roads straight (Isa 40:4) and who is himself the Way (John 14:6).
59.9: Therefore justice is far from us, and righteousness does not overtake us; we hope for light, and behold, darkness, and for brightness, but we walk in gloom. The communal lament of those who expected righteousness and received darkness. Matthew 4:16 (citing Isa 9:2): the people dwelling in darkness have seen a great light; and for those dwelling in the region and shadow of death, on them a light has dawned. John 1:4-5: in him was life, and the life was the light of men. The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it. The one who comes into the community groping in darkness (vv9-10) is the Light of the world whose arrival Isaiah 59 anticipates.
59.10: We grope for the wall like the blind; we grope like those who have no eyes; we stumble at noon as in the twilight, among those in full vigor we are like dead men. The blindness-at-noon image — the darkness so total that even daylight gives no help. John 9 is the enacted Isaiah 59:10: Jesus heals the man born blind and then declares I am the light of the world (John 9:5). The Pharisees who can physically see are spiritually blind (John 9:39-41: for judgment I came into this world, that those who do not see may see, and those who see may become blind). The stumbling-at-noon is the human condition that Christ's coming light addresses.
59.11: We all growl like bears; we moan and moan like doves; we hope for justice, but there is none; for salvation, but it is far from us. The animal-sounds of lament — the failure of hope deferred. Hebrews 11:13: these all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar. The people of Isaiah 59:11 moan for salvation that is far off — and then it arrives in the person of Jesus. Luke 2:25-32: Simeon, who had hoped to see the Lord's salvation, holds the infant Christ and says my eyes have seen your salvation. The far-off salvation comes near in the incarnation.
59.12: For our transgressions are multiplied before you, and our sins testify against us; for our transgressions are with us, and we know our iniquities. The honest communal confession — sins that testify against us. Luke 18:13: the tax collector's prayer — God, be merciful to me, a sinner! — is the model response to this condition. 1 John 1:9: if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness. The confession of Isaiah 59:12 is the necessary posture that receives the forgiveness Christ provides. Romans 10:9-10: with the heart one believes and is justified, and with the mouth one confesses and is saved.
59.13: Transgressing, and denying the LORD, and turning back from following our God, speaking oppression and revolt, conceiving and uttering from the heart lying words. The full description of covenant apostasy — turning from YHWH while harboring deceit in the heart. John 8:44: Jesus tells the opponents: you are of your father the devil... there is no truth in him. When he lies, he speaks out of his own character, for he is a liar and the father of lies. The lying words conceived in the heart (Isa 59:13) trace back to the father of lies. Christ is the counter-word: John 1:14 — the Word became flesh, full of grace and truth. Truth embodied confronts the lying words conceived in the heart.
59.14: Justice is turned back, and righteousness stands far away; for truth has stumbled in the public squares, and uprightness cannot enter. Truth stumbling in the public square — the ultimate portrait of systemic covenant breakdown. John 18:38: Pilate asks what is truth? — the Roman governor unable to recognize truth when Truth himself stands before him (John 14:6). The trial of Jesus is Isaiah 59:14 enacted in history: justice is turned back (the acquitted is crucified), righteousness stands far away (Barabbas is released), truth stumbles (perjured testimony accepted), uprightness cannot enter (the innocent is condemned). The cross is the supreme instance of justice inverted — and simultaneously YHWH's plan for the supreme justice to be accomplished through it.
59.15: Truth is lacking, and he who departs from evil makes himself a prey. The LORD saw it, and it displeased him that there was no justice. The divine displeasure at the absence of justice — YHWH sees and is moved to act. Hebrews 4:13: no creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account. The one who sees all corruption and is displeased is the Father who sends the Son to address what cannot be addressed from within the human system. The cross is the divine response to divine displeasure at injustice — not by ignoring it but by absorbing it.
59.16: He saw that there was no man, and wondered that there was no one to intercede; then his own arm brought him salvation, and his righteousness upheld him. No human intercessor — so YHWH's own arm acts. Isaiah 53:1: to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed? The arm that saves when no human can is the arm of the Servant — Christ who is both the divine arm and the human intercessor. Hebrews 7:25: Christ always lives to make intercession for those who draw near to God through him. 1 Timothy 2:5: there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. When there was no human intercessor, YHWH himself became one — by taking on flesh.
59.17: He put on righteousness as a breastplate, and a helmet of salvation on his head; he put on garments of vengeance for clothing, and wrapped himself in zeal as a cloak. The divine warrior's armor. Ephesians 6:14-17 distributes this imagery to the believer: the breastplate of righteousness... the helmet of salvation... the sword of the Spirit. The armor that YHWH wears in Isaiah 59 becomes the armor he gives to those in Christ — because Christ wore it first. 1 Thessalonians 5:8: putting on the breastplate of faith and love, and for a helmet the hope of salvation. The believer's armor is derivative: they are equipped with what the divine warrior wore when he came to save.
59.18: According to their deeds, so will he repay, wrath to his adversaries, repayment to his enemies; to the coastlands he will render repayment. The eschatological repayment to all who opposed the covenant. Revelation 22:12: Behold, I am coming soon, bringing my recompense with me, to repay each one for what he has done. The returning Christ is the executor of the judgment Isaiah 59:18 announces. The scope — to the coastlands (Gentile nations) — is the global scope of the final judgment when every nation stands before the Son of Man (Matt 25:31-32). The wrath of YHWH absorbed at the cross by Christ is the same wrath that returns at the parousia for those who rejected him.
59.19: So they shall fear the name of the LORD from the west, and his glory from the rising of the sun; for he will come like a rushing stream, the Spirit of the LORD driving it. The universal east-to-west proclamation of YHWH's glory, driven by the Spirit. Acts 2:2-4: at Pentecost, suddenly there came from heaven a sound like a mighty rushing wind, and it filled the entire house where they were sitting. The Spirit as a rushing stream driving the proclamation of YHWH's glory from Jerusalem (Pentecost) to the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8). John 3:8: the wind [Spirit] blows where it wishes, and you hear its sound, but you do not know where it comes from or where it goes.
59.20: And a Redeemer will come to Zion, to those in Jacob who turn from transgression, declares the LORD. Romans 11:26-27 quotes this verse at the climax of Paul's argument about Israel's eschatological salvation: and in this way all Israel will be saved, as it is written, The Deliverer will come from Zion, he will banish ungodliness from Jacob. The Redeemer who comes from Zion is Christ — and Paul's use of Isaiah 59:20 grounds his confidence that the partial hardening of Israel is not permanent: the Deliverer will come again, and at his coming, the remnant of Israel who turn from transgression will be saved. Isaiah 59:20 is thus both a prophecy of the first coming and the second.
59.21: And as for me, this is my covenant with them, says the LORD: my Spirit that is upon you, and my words that I have put in your mouth, shall not depart out of your mouth, or out of the mouth of your offspring, or out of your children's offspring, says the LORD, from this time forth and forevermore. The Spirit-and-word new covenant — permanently placed on the community and their children. Acts 2:16-18 (citing Joel 2): the Pentecostal Spirit-outpouring is the fulfillment. Acts 2:39: the promise is for you and for your children and for all who are far off. Ephesians 6:17: the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God. The Spirit and the word placed permanently on the covenant community are the new covenant gift that Christ secures and pours out — the Spirit he sends (John 16:7) and the word he is (John 1:1).

60.1: "Arise, shine, for your light has come, and the glory of YHWH has risen upon you." The call to arise and shine is simultaneously an announcement and a command — the light has come, therefore the arising is possible. John 8:12: "I am the light of the world." John 1:9: "the true light, which gives light to everyone, was coming into the world." Rev 21:23: "the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb." Eph 5:14: "Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you" — an early Christian hymn echoing Isaiah 60:1, applying it to the believer's encounter with the risen Christ. The glory of YHWH that "rises upon" Zion is the same glory that "rose upon" the shepherds' fields (Luke 2:9: "the glory of the Lord shone around them").
60.2: "For behold, darkness shall cover the earth, and thick darkness the peoples; but YHWH will arise upon you, and his glory will be seen upon you." The contrast between cosmic darkness and the divine light. John 1:5: "the light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it." John 3:19: "the light has come into the world, and people loved the darkness rather than the light because their works were evil." Matt 4:16 (quoting Isa 9:2): "the people dwelling in darkness have seen a great light, and for those dwelling in the region and shadow of death, on them a light has dawned." The thick darkness (ʿărāp̄el) that covers the peoples is the condition that the incarnation addresses — YHWH arising on Zion in Christ, his glory visible to those who looked on him (John 1:14: "we have seen his glory").
60.3: "And nations shall come to your light, and kings to the brightness of your rising." Rev 21:24: "by its light will the nations walk, and the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it." Matt 2:1-12: Magi from the East coming toward a light (a star) — the partial first-fulfillment at the nativity. Rev 21:26: "they will bring into it the glory and the honor of the nations." The nations coming to the light is the entire Gentile mission in nuce: Acts 13:47 (Paul quoting Isa 49:6: "I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth"); Acts 28:28 (the gospel going to the Gentiles who "will listen"). The brightness of Christ's rising is the resurrection light that draws all nations.
60.4: "Lift up your eyes all around, and see; they all gather together, they come to you; your sons shall come from afar, and your daughters shall be carried on the hip." The gathering of scattered children. John 11:51-52: Caiaphas's unwitting prophecy that "Jesus would die for the nation, and not for the nation only, but also to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad." John 10:16: "I have other sheep that are not of this fold. I must bring them also, and they will listen to my voice. So there will be one flock, one shepherd." Rev 21:2: the new Jerusalem coming down — the gathering-point of all the scattered children. The daughters "carried on the hip" (ʿal-ṣad tiṭṭāʾanāh, borne on the side) is the tender image of safe transport — the Good Shepherd who carries the lost sheep on his shoulders (Luke 15:5).
60.5: "Then you shall see and be radiant; your heart shall thrill and exult, because the abundance of the sea shall be turned to you, the wealth of the nations shall come to you." The transformation of the seeing community — not just sight but radiance. 2 Cor 3:18: "we all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another." The "abundance of the sea" (hmōn yām) is the commerce of the Gentile world redirected to serve God's city. Rev 21:26: "they will bring into it the glory and the honor of the nations." The economic imagery throughout Isaiah 60 anticipates the universal scope of the new creation — all human culture redeemed and brought into the City of God.
60.6: "A multitude of camels shall cover you, the young camels of Midian and Ephah; all those from Sheba shall come. They shall bring gold and frankincense, and shall bring good news, the praises of YHWH." The gold and frankincense that the Sheba caravans bring are the specific gifts the Magi offer to the infant Christ: Matt 2:11: "they offered him gifts, gold and frankincense and myrrh." Isaiah 60:6 is the OT foundation for the Magi narrative. The Sheba connection also activates Matt 12:42: "the queen of the South will rise up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and behold, something greater than Solomon is here." The Sheba journey that was about Solomon's wisdom is fulfilled in the Magi's journey to the one who is Wisdom incarnate (1 Cor 1:30: "Christ Jesus, who became to us wisdom from God").
60.7: "All the flocks of Kedar shall be gathered to you; the rams of Nebaioth shall minister to you; they shall come up with acceptance on my altar, and I will beautify my beautiful house." Gentile sacrifice accepted on the altar. Rom 15:16: Paul describes his apostolic ministry as "the priestly service of the gospel of God, so that the offering of the Gentiles may be acceptable, sanctified by the Holy Spirit" — Paul understands his mission as fulfilling Isaiah 60:7. 1 Pet 2:5: "you yourselves like living stones are being built up as a spiritual house, to be a holy priesthood, to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ." The "beautiful house" made beautiful by the Gentile offerings anticipates the new Jerusalem that needs no temple "for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb" (Rev 21:22).
60.8: "Who are these that fly like a cloud, and like doves to their windows?" The rapid, simultaneous ingathering of peoples from all directions — the speed of their coming likened to clouds and homing doves. Acts 2:5-11: "devout Jews from every nation under heaven" converging on Jerusalem at Pentecost — the first mass gathering of the dispersed nations to Zion. Rev 7:9: "a great multitude that no one could number, from every nation, from all tribes and peoples and languages, standing before the throne and before the Lamb." The question "who are these?" is the Seer's question before the innumerable multitude, answered in Rev 7:14: "these are the ones coming out of the great tribulation." The cloud of witnesses rushing home.
60.9: "For the coastlands shall hope for me, the ships of Tarshish first, to bring your children from afar, their silver and gold with them, for the name of YHWH your God, and for the Holy One of Israel, because he has made you beautiful." The coastlands that wait (42:4: "the coastlands wait for his Torah") now actively hope and send ships. Acts 13:47: Paul and Barnabas turn to the Gentiles at Antioch of Pisidia quoting Isa 49:6 — "I have made you a light for the Gentiles" — and the Gentiles "began rejoicing and glorifying the word of the Lord." The ships of Tarshish recall Jonah's attempted escape — the same vessels that carried the prophet away from God's mission are now the vessels of the ingathering. Christ redirects even resistance into mission.
60.10: "Foreigners shall build up your walls, and their kings shall minister to you; for in my wrath I struck you, but in my favor I have had mercy on you." The enemies who demolished now rebuild. Eph 2:12-14: Gentiles "who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ. For he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility." The foreigners building Zion's walls is the reversal of Babylon's conquest — the very nations that destroyed the temple become the nations that contribute to the new Jerusalem (Rev 21:24-26). The divine wrath/favor dialectic: "in my wrath I struck you, but in my favor I have had mercy" = Rom 11:22: "the kindness and the severity of God." The mercy in Christ's favor is the permanent condition.
60.11: "Your gates shall be open continually; day and night they shall not be shut, that people may bring to you the wealth of the nations, with their kings led in procession." Rev 21:25: "its gates will never be shut by day — and there will be no night there." John's vision copies Isaiah 60:11 almost verbatim. The perpetually open gates are the eschatological hospitality of the city of God — no night (when gates would be shut for safety), no exclusion of those who come bearing the nations' glory. John 10:9: "I am the door; if anyone enters by me, he will be saved and will go in and out and find pasture." Christ is the open gate — the permanent access to the Father (John 14:6: "no one comes to the Father except through me") that never shuts.
60.12: "For the nation and kingdom that will not serve you shall perish; those nations shall be utterly laid waste." The judgment on those who refuse the city. Matt 25:31-46: the judgment of the nations — those who served the least of Christ's brothers ("I was hungry and you gave me food") enter the kingdom; those who refused are sent to eternal punishment. Rev 19:15-21: the destruction of the nations that gathered to make war against the Lamb. The universal welcome of the open gates (v.11) has its dark counterpart in the universal accountability of v.12 — the same city that receives the repentant excludes the impenitent (Rev 21:27: "nothing unclean will ever enter it, nor anyone who does what is detestable or false").
60.13: "The glory of Lebanon shall come to you, the cypress, the plane, and the pine, to beautify the place of my sanctuary, and I will make the place of my feet glorious." The temple wood of Lebanon (1 Kings 5:6-10 — the cedar and cypress for Solomon's temple) now coming willingly. Rev 21:10-11: the new Jerusalem "having the glory of God, its radiance like a most rare jewel." John 14:2-3: "In my Father's house are many rooms... I go to prepare a place for you." The "place of my feet" (mĕqôm raglāy) is the footstool, the earthly temple-dwelling — but in Christ's incarnation, the place of God's feet is Galilee, Bethlehem, Gethsemane. Col 1:19: "in him all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell" — the sanctuary-place where YHWH's feet rest is the flesh of Christ.
60.14: "The sons of those who afflicted you shall come bending low to you, and all who despised you shall bow down at your feet; they shall call you the City of YHWH, the Zion of the Holy One of Israel." Phil 2:10-11: "every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father." Rev 3:9: the letter to Philadelphia — "I will make them come and bow down before your feet and they will learn that I have loved you." The reversal of social power — former oppressors in prostration before the formerly despised — is the eschatological vindication that follows the pattern of Christ: "despised and rejected by men" (53:3) becoming "the Lord of glory" (1 Cor 2:8) before whom all will bow.
60.15: "Whereas you have been forsaken and hated, with no one passing through, I will make you majestic forever, a joy from age to age." The transformation from abandoned to eternally majestic. Ps 22:1,24: the one who cried "my God, why have you forsaken me?" is also the one of whom it is said "he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one." The abandoned Zion parallels the abandoned Christ — "forsaken" (ʿăzûḇâ) is Christ's Gethsemane experience (Matt 26:56: "all the disciples left him and fled") and the cry of dereliction (Matt 27:46). The "majestic forever" is the exaltation that follows the abandonment: Phil 2:9: "God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name." Rev 21:11: the new Jerusalem radiates "the glory of God."
60.16: "You shall drink the milk of nations; you shall nurse at the breast of kings; and you shall know that I, YHWH, am your Savior and your Redeemer, the Mighty One of Jacob." The nursing metaphor of divine provision — Zion nourished by what was once threatening. Rev 21:26: the nations bringing their glory into the city. The title "Savior and Redeemer" (mōšîaʿ and gōʾēl) is the double divine identity — Savior = the name Jesus (Yēšûaʿ); Redeemer = the kinsman who pays the price (Gal 3:13; 1 Pet 1:18-19). John 4:42: "we know that this is indeed the Savior of the world." The "knowing that I, YHWH, am your Savior" is the knowledge that John's Gospel aims to produce through its signs: "these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God" (John 20:31).
60.17: "Instead of bronze I will bring gold, and instead of iron I will bring silver; instead of wood, bronze, instead of stones, iron. I will make your overseers peace and your taskmasters righteousness." The eschatological upgrade from inferior to precious. Heb 12:18-24: the contrast between Mount Sinai ("blazing fire and darkness and gloom and a tempest") and Mount Zion ("the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem"). The governance of the new city — "overseers of peace" (šōṭĕrê šālôm) and "taskmasters of righteousness" — is the reversal of Egypt's taskmasters (Exod 5:10-14) and Babylon's oppressors. Isa 9:6: "Prince of Peace" — Christ is the one whose name is shalom, the true overseer. Rev 22:5: his servants will "reign forever and ever" under the one who is righteousness itself (1 Cor 1:30).
60.18: "Violence shall no more be heard in your land, devastation or destruction within your borders; you shall call your walls Salvation, and your gates Praise." The walls named Yĕšûʿâ (Salvation — the same Hebrew root as "Jesus") and the gates named Tĕhillâ (Praise). John 10:9: "I am the door; if anyone enters by me, he will be saved" — Christ himself is the gate named Salvation. Rev 19:1: "Hallelujah! Salvation and glory and power belong to our God" — the praise that names the gates resounds in the heavenly worship. The absence of violence, devastation, and destruction anticipates Rev 21:4: "death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore." The walls and gates are Christ himself — he is both the protection (Salvation) and the worship-response (Praise) of the new city.
60.19: "The sun shall be no more your light by day, nor for brightness shall the moon give you light; but YHWH will be your everlasting light, and your God will be your glory." Rev 21:23: "the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb." John's apocalyptic vision is a near-verbatim quotation of Isaiah 60:19-20. John 1:4-5: "in him was life, and the life was the light of men. The light shines in the darkness." Rev 22:5: "they will need no light of lamp or sun, for the Lord God will be their light, and they will reign forever and ever." The lamp of the new Jerusalem is the Lamb — Christ is the eternal light that makes the created light of sun and moon unnecessary. 1 John 1:5: "God is light, and in him is no darkness at all."
60.20: "Your sun shall no more go down, nor your moon withdraw itself; for YHWH will be your everlasting light, and your days of mourning shall be ended." The setting sun that marks the end of days is abolished — the eternal day of YHWH's presence. Rev 21:4: "death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away." John 11:25-26: "I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live, and everyone who lives and believes in me shall never die." The "days of mourning ended" is the resurrection promise — the grief of death and exile permanently reversed. 1 Thess 4:17-18: "we will always be with the Lord. Therefore encourage one another with these words." The everlasting light of 60:20 is the comfort Paul draws from the resurrection.
60.21: "Your people shall all be righteous; they shall possess the land forever, the branch of my planting, the work of my hands, that I might be glorified." The eschatological community defined by righteousness — not self-achieved but divinely planted. John 15:1-5: "I am the true vine, and my Father is the vinedresser... every branch in me that does not bear fruit he takes away, and every branch that does bear fruit he prunes, that it may bear more fruit." The people as "the branch of my planting, the work of my hands" is the planting metaphor of John 15. Rom 5:19: "by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous." 2 Cor 5:21: "so that in him we might become the righteousness of God." The "all righteous" community is the church clothed in Christ's righteousness — "for my glorification" is the telos of the whole redemptive plan (Eph 1:12,14).
60.22: "The least one shall become a clan, and the smallest one a mighty nation; I am YHWH; in its time I will hasten it." The divine acceleration of growth from smallest to greatest. Mark 4:30-32: "with what can we compare the kingdom of God, or what parable shall we use for it? It is like a grain of mustard seed, which, when sown on the ground, is the smallest of all the seeds on earth, yet when it is sown it grows up and becomes larger than all the garden plants." Matt 13:31-32 applies the same mustard-seed parable — the smallest beginning (a handful of disciples in an obscure corner of the empire) becomes a community that fills the earth. "I am YHWH; in its time I will hasten it" — the sovereign divine timing. Acts 1:7: "it is not for you to know times or seasons that the Father has fixed by his own authority." The 'hastening' of the eschaton is the prerogative of the Father who keeps the timing of the new creation's completion.

62.1: For Zion's sake I will not keep silent, and for Jerusalem's sake I will not be still, until her righteousness goes out like brightness and her salvation like a burning torch. The divine resolve to not-keep-silent is the prophetic mission statement: YHWH will speak and act until the full promise is realized. The brightness-going-out and the burning-torch-of-salvation are the missionary images: the light does not stay local but goes out. In the NT, Paul expresses the same apostolic compulsion: it has always been my ambition to preach the gospel where Christ was not known (Rom 15:20); I am obligated both to Greeks and non-Greeks (Rom 1:14). The light-going-out-from-Zion is the Pentecost mission: from Jerusalem, then to Judea, then Samaria, then the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8).
62.2: The nations will see your righteousness, and all the kings will see your glory; and you will be called by a new name that the mouth of YHWH will designate. The new-name promise is the covenant-identity transformation: what was called Forsaken and Desolate (v 4) will receive a YHWH-given name that reflects the restored relationship. In the NT, the new-name theology appears in Rev 2:17 (I will give him a white stone with a new name written on it, known only to the one who receives it), Rev 3:12 (I will write on him the name of my God and the new Jerusalem and my own new name), and the renaming pattern of Peter (Simon → Rock), Paul (Saul → Paul), and the community called Christians (Acts 11:26). The mouth-of-YHWH-designates the name in Isa 62 is fulfilled in the Spirit who gives new identity at baptism: you are now called by the name of Christ.
62.3: You will be a crown of beauty in the hand of YHWH, and a royal diadem in the palm of your God. The crown-of-beauty in YHWH's hand is the reversal of the fading crown of Ephraim (28:1): what was a crown of pride that fell is now a crown of beauty held securely in YHWH's own hand. In the NT, the royal-diadem imagery applied to the community appears in 1 Pet 2:9 (a royal priesthood) and Rev 4:10-11 (the twenty-four elders cast their crowns before the throne). The crown held in YHWH's hand is the community kept by his power — the same keeping that Jesus promises: no one will snatch them out of my hand (John 10:28).
62.4: You will no longer be called Forsaken, and your land will no longer be called Desolate; but you will be called My-Delight-Is-in-Her (Hephzibah), and your land Married (Beulah), for YHWH delights in you and your land will be married. The name-replacement is the theological heart of the chapter: the two names of exile-judgment (Forsaken, Desolate) are replaced by two names of covenant-restoration (My-Delight-Is-in-Her, Married). The marriage metaphor for covenant relationship — YHWH as husband, Zion as wife — is the Hosea-Ezekiel-Jeremiah theology applied eschatologically. In the NT: the church as the bride of Christ (Eph 5:25-32: Christ loved the church and gave himself for her); the New Jerusalem as the bride adorned for her husband (Rev 21:2) — no longer Forsaken (Matt 27:46: my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?) but Hephzibah: the Beloved community in whom the Father delights.
62.5: For as a young man marries a virgin, so your sons will marry you; and as the bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so your God will rejoice over you. The double marriage image — sons marrying Zion (the community reconstituting the city) and YHWH as bridegroom rejoicing over the bride — is the most intimate covenant description in Isaiah. The bridegroom-rejoices image is the NT's wedding-feast metaphor: the kingdom of heaven is like a king who prepared a wedding banquet for his son (Matt 22:2); the friend of the bridegroom listens for the bridegroom's voice and is full of joy when he hears it — this joy is mine and it is now complete (John 3:29). The eschatological form is the marriage supper of the Lamb (Rev 19:7-9): rejoice! For the wedding of the Lamb has come, and his bride has made herself ready.
62.6: On your walls, O Jerusalem, I have set watchmen; all day and all night they will never fall silent. You who call on YHWH, take no rest for yourselves. The watchmen on the walls are the intercessors whose prayer sustains the city — they give YHWH no rest (v 7) until he establishes Jerusalem. The prayer-without-ceasing of the watchmen is the NT's pray without ceasing (1 Thess 5:17) and the persistent widow who gives the unjust judge no rest (Luke 18:1-8: always pray and do not give up). The watchmen who intercede for Zion are the NT community's prayer-watch: the Gethsemane command — watch and pray (Matt 26:41) — is the Isaianic watchman's commission applied to the disciples.
62.7: And give him no rest until he establishes and until he makes Jerusalem a praise on earth. The persistent-until-established prayer is the eschatological intercession: the watchmen do not stop until the promise is fully realized. In the NT: your kingdom come, your will be done on earth as in heaven (Matt 6:10) — the Lord's Prayer is the watchman's prayer of Isa 62:7. The until-he-establishes timeframe is the until-he-comes of the Lord's Supper (1 Cor 11:26: you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes) — the community maintains its watchful intercession until the full establishment of the Jerusalem-above (Heb 12:22, Rev 21:2) arrives.
62.8: YHWH has sworn by his right hand and by his mighty arm: Surely I will no longer give your grain as food for your enemies, and foreigners will not drink your new wine for which you have toiled. The oath-by-his-right-hand is the highest form of divine guarantee: YHWH swears by himself (Heb 6:13: since there was no one greater for him to swear by, he swore by himself). The promise that the harvest will not be taken by enemies reverses the covenant-curse of Lev 26:16 and Deut 28:33 (a nation you do not know will eat what your land produces). In the NT, the harvest theology is eschatological: the fields are ripe for harvest (John 4:35) and the harvest is the end of the age (Matt 13:39) — the grain that was taken by enemies will ultimately be gathered by the Lord of the harvest (Matt 9:38).
62.9: But those who harvest it will eat it and praise YHWH, and those who gather it will drink it in the courts of my sanctuary. The restoration of the harvest-into-worship cycle: the eating-and-praising in the sanctuary courts is the covenant-feast that the earlier thefts had interrupted. In the NT: the eschatological feast where the redeemed eat in the Father's house (Luke 22:30: I confer on you a kingdom... you may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom) is the sanctuary-courts meal of Isa 62:9 fulfilled. The Lord's Supper itself is the anticipatory participation in this feast: the church eats bread and drinks wine until the harvest-feast of the kingdom comes in its fullness.
62.10: Go through, go through the gates! Prepare the way for the people! Build up, build up the highway; clear it of stones; lift up a banner over the peoples. The highway-preparation oracle (echoing 40:3 and 57:14) is the infrastructure of the eschatological homecoming: the road is built, the stones are cleared, the banner is raised. In the NT: John the Baptist is the voice preparing the way (Matt 3:3 citing Isa 40:3) and the Great Commission is the raising of the banner over the peoples. The banner (nes) is the signal-flag that rallies the scattered peoples to YHWH's assembly — the NT's equivalent is the cross lifted up: when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself (John 12:32).
62.11: Behold, YHWH has proclaimed to the end of the earth: Say to Daughter Zion, See, your salvation comes! See, his reward is with him, and his recompense before him. The say-to-Daughter-Zion oracle is the announcement that Matthew applies to the Triumphal Entry: say to Daughter Zion, See, your king comes to you (Matt 21:5, combining Isa 62:11 with Zech 9:9). The salvation that comes is Jesus himself — the name Jesus (Yeshua) means YHWH saves — riding into Jerusalem as the fulfillment of the Savior-who-comes announced here. His reward and recompense are the judgment-and-salvation he brings: his reward with him anticipates Rev 22:12 (I am coming soon; my reward is with me, to give to each person according to what they have done).
62.12: And they shall be called the Holy People, the Redeemed of YHWH; and you shall be called Sought Out, a City Not Forsaken. The four new names at the chapter's end are the covenant-identity of the restored community: Holy People, Redeemed of YHWH, Sought Out, City Not Forsaken. In the NT: you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession (1 Pet 2:9) — the Holy People; the Redeemed by Christ's blood (1 Pet 1:18-19); Sought Out by the Good Shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to find the one lost sheep (Luke 15:4-7); Not Forsaken: I will never leave you nor forsake you (Heb 13:5). The four names of Isa 62:12 are the NT community's four-fold identity granted by the Savior who came to Daughter Zion (v 11).

64.1: "Oh that you would rend the heavens and come down, that the mountains might quake at your presence" — the anguished theophany-petition: the community pleads for YHWH to tear open the boundary between heaven and earth. The rending of the heavens is the spatial miracle that divine absence requires. Mark 1:10: "And when he came up out of the water, immediately he saw the heavens being torn apart (schizomenous) and the Spirit descending on him like a dove." Matthew 27:51: "And behold, the curtain of the temple was torn in two, from top to bottom." The two tearings of Mark and Matthew answer Isaiah 64:1 — at the baptism, the heavens are rent (the petition granted) and the Father declares the beloved Son; at the cross, the temple curtain is rent (the access granted). The incarnation is the tearing-open of the cosmic boundary that Isaiah 64:1 petitioned — God rending heaven to come down in the person of Jesus.
64.2: "As when fire kindles brushwood and the fire causes water to boil — to make your name known to your adversaries, and that the nations might tremble at your presence!" — the purpose of the theophany: divine-name recognition among adversaries, trembling among nations. The fire and water imagery of terrifying divine power. Hebrews 12:29: "for our God is a consuming fire." Revelation 1:14-15: "The hairs of his head were white, like white wool, like snow. His eyes were like a flame of fire, his feet were like burnished bronze, refined in a furnace." The risen Christ of Revelation 1 is the answered theophany of Isaiah 64:2 — the divine presence that causes trembling among adversaries. Acts 2:37: "When the people heard this, they were cut to the heart." The Pentecost sermon causes the trembling that Isaiah petitioned — nations hearing the mighty works of God in their own languages (Acts 2:11), which is a fulfillment of the divine-name-made-known petition.
64.3: "When you did awesome things that we did not expect, you came down, the mountains quaked at your presence. From of old no one has heard or perceived by the ear, no eye has seen a God besides you, who acts for those who wait for him" — the remembered theophanies (Sinai above all) as ground for the present petition. The God who did the unexpected in the past can do it again. The phrase "no eye has seen a God besides you" anticipates the v4 statement that Paul will cite directly. Luke 1:49: "for he who is mighty has done great things for me, and holy is his name." Mary's Magnificat recalls the "awesome things that we did not expect" — the incarnation was precisely the unexpected thing (the virgin birth, the Bethlehem manger, the census). 1 Corinthians 1:27: "But God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise; God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong." The unexpected divine action of Isaiah 64:3 is the pattern of all divine action, culminating in the cross.
64.4: "From of old no one has heard or perceived by the ear, no eye has seen a God besides you, who acts for those who wait for him" — the uniqueness-declaration: no God besides YHWH acts on behalf of those who wait for him (lĕmĕḥakkeh lô). 1 Corinthians 2:9: "But, as it is written, 'What no eye has seen, nor ear heard, nor the heart of man imagined, what God has prepared for those who love him.'" Paul cites Isaiah 64:4 (in LXX form) as the source for the Spirit-revealed wisdom of the gospel — the things God has prepared are the eschatological realities unveiled by the Spirit of Christ. The hidden things of Isaiah 64:4 are the mystery of Christ revealed in the gospel (1 Cor 2:10: "these things God has revealed to us through the Spirit"). Ephesians 3:4-5: "the mystery of Christ, which was not made known to the sons of men in other generations as it has now been revealed to his holy apostles and prophets by the Spirit." The "no eye has seen" of Isaiah 64:4 is the not-yet of prophecy; the Spirit's revelation in Christ is its yes.
64.5: "You meet him who joyfully works righteousness, those who remember you in your ways. Behold, you were angry, and we sinned; in our sins we have been a long time, and shall we be saved?" — the communal self-indictment: the transition from praise of divine action (vv3-4) to confession. YHWH meets the righteous worker — but "we sinned." The honesty of the prayer: chronic sinfulness, long-continued. Romans 3:23: "for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." Romans 5:8: "but God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us." The "shall we be saved?" of Isaiah 64:5 is the existential question that the gospel answers: yes — not because we have worked righteousness but because Christ, the one who "joyfully works righteousness" perfectly (Heb 4:15: without sin), meets us in our sin and absorbs the anger we incurred.
64.6: "We have all become like one who is unclean, and all our righteous deeds are like a polluted garment. We all fade like a leaf, and our iniquities, like the wind, take us away" — the total moral bankruptcy: not just sinners but the community's righteousnesses (its best efforts) are like a "polluted garment" (bĕged ʿiddîm). Romans 3:10-12: "None is righteous, no, not one; no one understands; no one seeks for God. All have turned aside; together they have become worthless; no one does good, not even one." Philippians 3:8-9: "I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord... that I may gain Christ and be found in him, not having a righteousness of my own that comes from the law, but that which comes through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God that depends on faith." Paul's self-application of Isaiah 64:6 logic: his Pharisaic righteousness (his best efforts) is counted as dung so that Christ's righteousness replaces it. The polluted garments of Isaiah 64:6 are replaced by the white robes of Revelation 7:14 — "washed... in the blood of the Lamb."
64.7: "There is no one who calls upon your name, who rouses himself to take hold of you; for you have hidden your face from us, and have made us melt in the hand of our iniquities" — the spiritual paralysis: no one calls, no one stirs himself to pray, because YHWH has hidden his face and iniquity has produced a spiritual immobility. Romans 3:11: "no one seeks for God." Romans 8:26: "Likewise the Spirit helps us in our weakness. For we do not know what to pray for as we ought, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words." The "no one calls" of Isaiah 64:7 is the condition of humanity without the Spirit — and the Spirit's intercession of Romans 8:26 is the divine answer to the failure to call. Christ makes intercession at the right hand of the Father (Heb 7:25: "he always lives to make intercession for them") — the one who calls on our behalf when we cannot call for ourselves.
64.8: "But now, O LORD, you are our Father; we are the clay, and you are our potter; we are all the work of your hand" — the pivot to petition on the basis of the Father-creator relationship: "but now" (wĕʿattāh) — the same hinge word as Isaiah 43:1. Despite the moral bankruptcy of vv5-7, the community appeals to the relationship that creation and covenant establish: Father, potter. Romans 9:20-21: "But who are you, O man, to answer back to God? Will what is molded say to its molder, 'Why have you made me like this?' Has the potter no right over the clay, to make out of the same lump one vessel for honorable use and another for dishonorable use?" Paul uses the potter-clay image of Isaiah 64:8 in his argument for divine sovereignty in election — the same imagery, applied not to petition but to the silencing of human objection. The cross is the decisive potter-act: God reshapes broken human clay into the image of the risen Son (Rom 8:29: "conformed to the image of his Son").
64.9: "Be not so terribly angry, O LORD, and remember not iniquity forever. Behold, please look, we are all your people" — the intercession: "all your people" is the ground of appeal. The covenant community presses its covenant membership as the reason for divine restraint. Romans 11:1-2: "I ask, then, has God rejected his people? By no means! For I myself am an Israelite, a descendant of Abraham, a member of the tribe of Benjamin. God has not rejected his people whom he foreknew." Hebrews 7:25: "Consequently, he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them." The "remember not iniquity forever" petition of Isaiah 64:9 is the petition that Christ grants through his blood — Hebrews 8:12 (quoting Jer 31:34): "I will remember their sins no more." The new covenant is the answer to Isaiah 64:9's request: YHWH has covenanted to no longer remember the iniquity of the forgiven.
64.10: "Your holy cities have become a wilderness; Zion has become a wilderness, Jerusalem a desolation" — the devastation of the exile described: the holy places reduced to wilderness. The most sacred geography has become desert. Luke 21:20-22: "But when you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, then know that its desolation has come near. Then let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains... For these are days of vengeance, to fulfill all that is written." Revelation 21:2: "And I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband." The Jerusalem made desolate in Isaiah 64:10 is the Jerusalem made desolate in AD 70 (fulfilling Luke 21) — and both prepare the way for the new Jerusalem coming down from heaven (Revelation 21), which is the city that the desolated earthly city was always pointing toward. Christ weeps over Jerusalem's desolation (Luke 19:41) and then promises its eschatological renewal (Rev 21).
64.11: "Our holy and beautiful house, where our fathers praised you, has been burned by fire, and all our pleasant places have become ruins" — the temple destroyed: the specific focus narrows from holy cities (v10) to the temple itself. The place of praise is ash. The keenness of the loss — "holy and beautiful" (qōdĕšēnû ûṯipaʾartēnû) — captures the community's grief. John 2:19-21: "Jesus answered them, 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up.'... But he was speaking about the temple of his body." 1 Corinthians 3:16-17: "Do you not know that you are God's temple and that God's Spirit dwells in you?... God's temple is holy, and you are that temple." The burned beautiful house of Isaiah 64:11 anticipates the temple that Christ's body is — which is destroyed (crucified) and raised in three days, becoming the foundation of the new temple that is the community of believers indwelt by the Spirit. Revelation 21:22: "And I saw no temple in the city, for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb." The earthly temple's destruction is the precondition for the eternal temple.
64.12: "Will you restrain yourself at these things, O LORD? Will you keep silent, and afflict us so terribly?" — the closing petition: will YHWH remain passive? The silence of God in affliction is the community's sharpest pain. The question hangs unanswered — which makes Isaiah 65's response (God who was always ready to be found) all the more dramatic. Psalm 22:1-2: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? Why are you so far from saving me, so far from my cries of anguish? My God, I cry out by day, but you do not answer, by night, but I find no rest." Matthew 27:46: Jesus quotes Psalm 22:1 from the cross. The divine silence of Isaiah 64:12 reaches its deepest point at the cross — when the Son himself cries out "why have you forsaken me?" and receives silence in return. But the silence is not the end: Psalm 22:24: "For he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one; he has not hidden his face from him but has listened to his cry for help." Isaiah 65:1: "I was ready to be sought by those who did not ask for me." The silence breaks at the resurrection.

63.1: Who is this coming from Edom, from Bozrah, with his garments stained crimson? Who is this, robed in splendor, striding forward in the greatness of his strength? It is I, proclaiming victory, mighty to save. The divine warrior returning from battle with garments stained in blood — one of the OT's most arresting images. Revelation 19:13 applies it directly to the returning Christ: he is clothed in a robe dipped in blood, and the name by which he is called is The Word of God. Revelation 19:11: I saw heaven opened, and behold, a white horse! The one sitting on it is called Faithful and True, and in righteousness he judges and makes war. The crimson garments of Isaiah 63 belong to the same figure as the bloodstained robe of Revelation 19 — Christ returning as conquering judge.
63.2: Why is your apparel red, and your garments like those of one who treads in the wine press? The question about the bloodstained garments that receives the winepress answer in verse 3. Revelation 14:19-20: the angel swung his sickle across the earth and gathered the grape harvest of the earth and threw it into the great winepress of the wrath of God. Revelation 19:15: the returning Christ will tread the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God the Almighty. The winepress of divine judgment that Isaiah 63 describes is the instrument of Christ's eschatological justice — the same warrior who absorbed divine wrath at the cross executes divine justice at the parousia.
63.3: I have trodden the winepress alone, and from the peoples no one was with me; I trod them in my anger and trampled them in my wrath; their lifeblood spattered on my garments, and stained all my apparel. The warrior trodding the winepress alone — no one was with me. This solitude resonates powerfully with Gethsemane and Golgotha: all the disciples left him and fled (Matt 26:56). Jesus drank the cup of wrath alone: Is it not the cup the Father has given me to drink? (John 18:11). The solitary treading is the aloneness of the cross in its first form; in its eschatological reversal, the same warrior alone executes the judgment no human power can accomplish (2 Thess 1:7-9).
63.4: For the day of vengeance was in my heart, and my year of redemption has come. The day of vengeance and the year of redemption held together — divine justice and divine mercy as simultaneous realities. This is the logic of the atonement: Romans 3:25-26 — God presented Christ as a propitiation to show his righteousness at the present time, so that he might be just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus. Isaiah 61:2, which Jesus reads in Luke 4:18-19, stops at the year of the LORD's favor — omitting the day of vengeance — reserving that element for the second coming. The first coming is the year of redemption; the second is the day of vengeance. Both are in the divine heart simultaneously.
63.5: I looked, but there was no one to help; I was appalled, but there was no one to uphold; so my own arm brought me salvation, and my wrath upheld me. No helper — so YHWH's own arm saves. This is the parallel of Isaiah 59:16: there was no man... then his own arm brought him salvation. Hebrews 1:3: the Son upholds the universe by the word of his power. 1 Timothy 2:5: there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. When no human arm could accomplish what was required, the divine arm itself became flesh — the incarnation as YHWH's response to the absence of any human redeemer. The arm that was not shortened (Isa 59:1) is the arm that brings salvation alone.
63.6: I trampled down the peoples in my anger; I made them drunk in my wrath, and I poured out their lifeblood on the earth. The divine execution of eschatological judgment on the nations. Revelation 14:20: the blood from the winepress flows as high as a horse's bridle, for 1,600 stadia. 2 Thessalonians 1:7-9: the Lord Jesus will be revealed from heaven in flaming fire, inflicting vengeance on those who do not know God and on those who do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus. The judgment of Isaiah 63:6 is the judgment of the returning Christ — the one who absorbed the cup of wrath at the cross now administers it to those who refused the cup of salvation.
63.7: I will recount the steadfast love of the LORD, the praises of the LORD, according to all that the LORD has granted us, and the great goodness to the house of Israel that he has granted them according to his compassion, according to the abundance of his steadfast love. The pivot from judgment (vv1-6) to recollection of YHWH's hesed. Titus 3:4-5: when the goodness and loving kindness of God our Savior appeared, he saved us, not because of works done by us in righteousness, but according to his own mercy. 2 Corinthians 1:3: the Father of mercies and God of all comfort. The steadfast love recounted in Isaiah 63:7 finds its supreme expression in John 3:16: the love that gave the only Son is the fullness of the hesed the chapter recalls.
63.8: For he said, Surely they are my people, children who will not deal falsely. And he became their Savior. The covenant declaration — my people — followed immediately by the Savior-title. Matthew 1:21: you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins. Hebrews 2:13 cites Isaiah 8:18 of Christ: Behold, I and the children God has given me — identifying the covenant community as the children the Son of God calls his own. 1 Peter 2:10: once you were not a people, but now you are God's people. The Savior-declaration of Isaiah 63:8 is the naming-commission given to Joseph in Matthew 1: Jesus is the Savior who fulfills what YHWH declared in Isaiah 63.
63.9: In all their affliction he was afflicted, and the angel of his presence saved them; in his love and in his pity he redeemed them; he lifted them up and carried them all the days of old. In all their affliction he was afflicted — the divine solidarity with suffering. Hebrews 4:15: we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin. Matthew 25:40: as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me. The incarnation is YHWH's affliction-solidarity taken to its ultimate form: the divine suffering-with is enacted when the eternal Son bears our griefs and carries our sorrows (Isa 53:4) — not from a distance but from within human experience.
63.10: But they rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit; therefore he turned to be their enemy, and himself fought against them. Grieving the Holy Spirit — the only explicit OT occurrence of this concept. Ephesians 4:30: do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, by whom you were sealed for the day of redemption. Paul's prohibition is rooted in Isaiah 63:10's history: the Spirit who can be grieved is the same Spirit who seals new covenant believers. The rebellion-that-grieves is not an abstract theological category but a concrete covenant pattern — and its consequence (YHWH becoming their enemy) is the pattern that the cross reverses: Romans 5:10 — while we were enemies we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son.
63.11: Then he remembered the days of old, of Moses and his people. Where is he who brought them up out of the sea with the shepherds of his flock? Where is he who put in the midst of them his Holy Spirit. The appeal to Exodus memory — the Spirit who was present in the cloud-pillar and through the wilderness. 1 Corinthians 10:1-4: the Israelites all passed through the sea, all ate spiritual food, all drank from the spiritual Rock — and the Rock was Christ. The Spirit in the midst of them (v11) is the Spirit that Pentecost pours out in new covenant fullness — the same divine Presence who accompanied the first exodus now accompanying the new-exodus community gathered around the risen Christ.
63.12: Who caused his glorious arm to go at the right hand of Moses, who divided the waters before them to make for himself an everlasting name. The glorious arm at Moses's right that split the sea — the arm of YHWH in Exodus action. Acts 2:33: being therefore exalted at the right hand of God, and having received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, he has poured out this that you yourselves are seeing and hearing. Christ at the Father's right hand exercises the same arm-authority that divided the sea. Philippians 2:9: God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name — the everlasting name made at the Exodus is the name above all names given to the one who bore the cross.
63.13: Who led them through the depths? Like a horse in the desert, they did not stumble. The safe passage through the depths without stumbling. 1 Corinthians 10:5-10: despite passing through the sea and eating spiritual food (Christ), many of Israel stumbled — as a warning to the church. Jude 24: Christ is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy. The one who led Israel through the depths without stumbling is the one who promises: no one will snatch them out of my hand (John 10:28). The ability to lead through the depths without stumbling is the pastoral power of the Good Shepherd who goes before his flock.
63.14: Like livestock that go down into the valley, the Spirit of the LORD gave them rest. So you led your people, to make for yourself a glorious name. The Spirit-given rest — the Exodus settlement as a type of the eschatological Sabbath-rest. Hebrews 4:8-11: if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day — there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God. Matthew 11:28-29: come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest... for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. The Spirit who gave rest in the valley-settlement is the Spirit that Christ sends (John 14:16-17) to give the deeper rest that Joshua's conquest could not provide.
63.15: Look down from heaven and see, from your holy and beautiful habitation. Where are your zeal and your might? The stirring of your inner parts and your compassion are held back from me. The prayer for YHWH to look down from his holy habitation and act from compassion. John 3:16-17: God so loved the world that he gave his only Son... for God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but in order that the world might be saved through him. The prayer of Isaiah 63:15 is answered beyond what the prayer could imagine: YHWH does not merely look down from his holy habitation — he enters human history in the person of the Son. The compassion that appeared withheld is poured out at the incarnation and cross.
63.16: For you are our Father, though Abraham does not know us and Israel does not acknowledge us; you, O LORD, are our Father, our Redeemer from of old is your name. The covenantal Abba-cry — you are our Father. Romans 8:15-16: you have received the Spirit of adoption as sons, by whom we cry, Abba! Father! The Spirit himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God. Galatians 4:6: because you are sons, God has sent the Spirit of his Son into our hearts, crying, Abba! Father! The cry of Isaiah 63:16 is the cry the Spirit enables believers to make through union with the Son — who is himself the eternal Son who in Gethsemane prays Abba, Father, all things are possible for you (Mark 14:36). Our Abba-cry is a participation in his.
63.17: O LORD, why do you make us wander from your ways and harden our heart, so that we fear you not? Return for the sake of your servants, the tribes of your heritage. The lament over hardening — YHWH's mysterious judicial action. Romans 11:25: a partial hardening has come upon Israel, until the fullness of the Gentiles has come in. Romans 9:18: God hardens whom he wills — but always in service of ultimate mercy (Rom 11:32: he has consigned all to disobedience, that he may have mercy on all). The hardening that Isaiah 63:17 laments is the same partial hardening whose resolution Paul sees in the Redeemer coming from Zion (Rom 11:26, citing Isa 59:20). The return for the sake of YHWH's servants is the return of Christ.
63.18: Your holy people held possession for a little while; our adversaries have trampled down your sanctuary. The sanctuary trampled by enemies — the desecration of the holy place. Mark 13:14: Jesus predicts the abomination of desolation in the holy place. Luke 21:20-24: armies surrounding Jerusalem, Gentiles trampling it until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled. The 70 CE destruction of the temple is the historical fulfillment — and Jesus's prediction of it links Isaiah 63:18 to the eschatological timetable: the trampling of the sanctuary ends when the times of the Gentiles are complete and the Deliverer comes from Zion (Rom 11:26).
63.19: We have become like those over whom you have never ruled, like those who are not called by your name. The lament of lost identity — not called by your name. 1 Peter 2:10: once you were not a people, but now you are God's people; once you had not received mercy, but now you have received mercy. In Christ, every believer is restored to the status of those-called-by-his-name. Revelation 22:4: they will see his face, and his name will be on their foreheads. Revelation 3:12: Christ writes on the overcomer the name of my God and the name of the city of my God... and my own new name. The lost name of Isaiah 63:19 is restored and surpassed in Christ — those who were not his people become the ones who bear the name of the Son himself.

65.1: I revealed myself to those who did not ask for me; I was found by those who did not seek me. To a nation that did not call on my name I said: Here am I, here am I! The divine self-disclosure to the non-seeking is the Gentile-mission oracle. Paul quotes this verse directly in Rom 10:20 and says Isaiah boldly declares what happened through his own apostolic mission: the gospel went to those who had not been looking for YHWH — the Gentiles. The here-am-I-here-am-I is the insistent self-offering of YHWH to those who had no prior category for him. In the NT: God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance (Rom 2:4); the gospel goes to the ends of the earth precisely to reach those who have not asked — the prodigal son who did not know he could return home until the word reached him (Luke 15:20: while he was still a long way off, his father saw him).
65.2: All day long I have held out my hands to an obstinate and rebellious people, who walk in ways not good, pursuing their own imaginations. Paul quotes this verse in Rom 10:21 as the counterpoint to v 1: while Israel had YHWH's arms extended toward them all day, the Gentiles received what Israel forfeited. The all-day-long-outstretched-hands is the image of unrequited divine love — the same image as Luke 13:34 (how often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing). The obstinate-and-rebellious people are not permanently abandoned — Romans 11 shows that their stumbling is not final (Rom 11:11-12) — but the all-day-outstretched-arms that Israel ignored are accepted by the Gentiles who were not looking.
65.3: A people who continually provoke me to my face, sacrificing in gardens and burning incense on altars of brick. The specific idolatry: garden-sanctuaries and brick-altars are the Canaanite practices that violated the Mosaic prohibition of unauthorized worship sites. The provoke-to-my-face is the directness of the offense: this is not ignorant worship of another god but deliberate rejection of YHWH's prescribed worship while claiming covenant identity. In the NT: they are enemies of the cross of Christ (Phil 3:18) — the community that has seen the gospel and turned away is more guilty than those who never heard.
65.4: Who sit in tombs and spend their nights keeping secret vigil; who eat the flesh of pigs, and whose pots hold broth of impure meat. The three covenant violations — sitting among tombs (ritual impurity), necromantic vigils (seeking the dead for guidance), and eating pork (Levitical prohibition) — are the explicit abandonment of covenant identity. The contrast with the Servant who did not commit violence or speak deceit (53:9) and the community of the new covenant that keeps YHWH's ways is total. In the NT: purity is redefined from external (Levitical food laws) to internal (what comes out of a person's heart — Matt 15:19-20) by Jesus — but the radical violation of covenant in all three dimensions here is the mark of apostasy, not Gentile ignorance.
65.5: Who say, Keep to yourself, don't come near me, for I am too holy for you! These people are smoke in my nostrils, a fire that keeps burning all day. The keep-away-I-am-too-holy is the supreme irony: the idolaters who sit among tombs eating pig's flesh claim to be more holy than others — the holiness-claim that is smoke-in-the-nostrils of the actual Holy One. Jesus addresses the same irony: woe to you, teachers of the law and Pharisees, you hypocrites! You are like whitewashed tombs, which look beautiful on the outside but on the inside are full of the bones of the dead (Matt 23:27). The smoke-in-the-nostrils-all-day is the irritating persistence of false-holiness that claims to speak for YHWH while rejecting his actual presence and instruction.
65.6: See, it stands written before me: I will not keep silent but will repay — I will repay into their laps both your sins and the sins of your ancestors together, says YHWH. The written-before-me is the divine record that will be opened at judgment: YHWH is not improvising his response but executing a verdict already established. The repay-into-their-laps is the measurement-back-into-the-bosom image (Luke 6:38: good measure, pressed down, shaken together, running over, will be put into your lap). In the NT: the books were opened (Rev 20:12), and the dead were judged according to what was written in the books, according to what they had done. The written-before-me of Isa 65:6 is the ledger opened at the great white throne.
65.7: Both your own sins and the sins of your ancestors together, says YHWH; because they burned incense on the mountains and defied me on the hills, I will repay them in full for their former deeds. The accumulated-sin of multiple generations: the judgment falls on the community that perpetuated the pattern of its ancestors. The mountain-idolatry is the high-place worship that the covenant forbade and that every generation of unfaithful Israel continued. In the NT: fill up the measure of the sin of your ancestors (Matt 23:32) — Jesus addresses the same generational-sin-accumulation to the Jerusalem leadership of his day, the final generation before the judgment of 70 CE.
65.8: Thus says YHWH: As the new wine is found in the cluster, and they say, Do not destroy it, for there is a blessing in it, so I will do for my servants' sake — I will not destroy them all. The grape-cluster parable: the entire cluster is not discarded because some good grapes remain within it. The faithful remnant within Israel is the preserving agent that prevents total destruction. In the NT: so too, at the present time there is a remnant chosen by grace (Rom 11:5) — the remnant of Jewish believers (Paul, the apostles, the Jerusalem church) is the good-grape-cluster that preserves the continuity of the covenant people. The do-not-destroy-for-there-is-blessing is the logic of intercession: Abraham arguing for Sodom (Gen 18:23-32), Moses for Israel (Exod 32:11-14).
65.9: I will bring forth descendants from Jacob, and from Judah those who will possess my mountains; my chosen people will inherit them, and there my servants will live. The chosen-people who inherit the mountains are the faithful remnant who receive what the idolaters forfeited. The inheritance of the mountains — Zion, the holy mountain — is the land-promise fulfilled in the community of the Servant. In the NT: the meek will inherit the earth (Matt 5:5) — the same inheritance-language applied to the community of the Beatitudes, whose poverty-of-spirit and mourning and hunger for righteousness mark them as the chosen-people-who-will-possess.
65.10: Sharon will become a pasture for flocks and the Valley of Achor a place for herds to lie down, for my people who seek me. The Sharon plain (fertile coast) and the Valley of Achor (where Achan's sin brought judgment, Josh 7:26) become pastoral blessing: the place of judgment becomes the place of rest. The Hosea tradition applies this transformation: I will give her the Valley of Achor as a door of hope (Hos 2:15) — the place of covenant violation becomes the gateway of restoration. The my-people-who-seek-me are the counterpart of the Gentiles who found YHWH without seeking (v 1): the new covenant community is both found by the unseeker and made up of seekers. Seek and you will find (Matt 7:7) is the call to become the my-people-who-seek.
65.11: But as for you who forsake YHWH and forget my holy mountain, who spread a table for Fortune and fill bowls of mixed wine for Destiny. The forsakers of YHWH are those who spread the table for Fortune (Gad) and Destiny (Meni) — the gods of luck and fate. The table-for-Fortune inverts the covenant table (23:5: YHWH prepares a table before me in the presence of my enemies). In the NT: the table of the Lord and the table of demons are mutually exclusive (1 Cor 10:21) — you cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons. The fate-and-fortune table is the NT's mammon-worship: the service of money as an alternative to YHWH's provision (Matt 6:24).
65.12: I will destine you for the sword, and you will all bend down for the slaughter; for I called and you did not answer; I spoke and you did not listen; you did evil in my sight and chose what I did not desire. The called-and-did-not-answer echoes the covenant-complaint pattern of 50:2 (when I came, was there no one there? When I called, was there no one to answer?). The choose-what-I-did-not-desire is the free-will aspect of judgment: the idolaters could have chosen YHWH's desire but chose otherwise. In the NT: you did not recognize the time of God's coming to you (Luke 19:44) — Jesus weeps over Jerusalem for the same called-and-did-not-answer failure. The destined-for-the-sword is the consequence, not the cause, of the refusal.
65.13: Therefore this is what the Lord YHWH says: My servants will eat, but you will go hungry; my servants will drink, but you will go thirsty; my servants will rejoice, but you will be put to shame. The servants-vs.-forsakers contrast is expressed as a feast-and-famine reversal: everything the forsakers expected from their Fortune-tables (v 11) will be denied them, while YHWH's servants receive the full covenant feast. In the NT: Jesus uses the same feast-reversal language: many who are first will be last, and many who are last will be first (Matt 19:30). The Beatitudes are the servants' side of the reversal: blessed are the hungry, for they will be satisfied (Matt 5:6); blessed are the mourning, for they will be comforted (Matt 5:4).
65.14: My servants will sing from the joy of their heart, but you will cry from the anguish of your heart and wail from a crushed spirit. The singing-from-joy vs. crying-from-anguish is the ultimate reversal: the two communities end in diametrically opposite emotional states. The crushed spirit of the forsakers inverts the crushed spirit of the contrite that YHWH dwells with (57:15) — the contrite who came to YHWH receive the revived spirit; those who forsook YHWH end with the spirit crushed by judgment. In the NT: there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth (Matt 8:12, 22:13, 25:30) — the anguish of those outside the feast is the forsakers' wailing of Isa 65:14.
65.15: You will leave your name as a curse to my chosen ones, and the Lord YHWH will put you to death; but to his servants he will give another name. The forsakers' name becomes a curse-formula (as bad as so-and-so) while the servants receive a new name — the reversal of identity. In the NT: the new name given to the servants (Rev 2:17: I will give him a white stone with a new name written on it; Rev 3:12: I will write on him the name of my God and my own new name) is the fulfillment of the other-name promise here. The servants who bear the name of Christ (Christian — Acts 11:26) carry the new-name-of-the-Servant as their identity, replacing whatever names they bore before.
65.16: Whoever invokes a blessing in the land will do so by the God of truth (Elohim amen); whoever takes an oath in the land will swear by the God of truth. For the former troubles are forgotten and hidden from my eyes. The God of truth (literally, God of Amen) is the divine self-designation as the reliable witness — the one whose words are always true. In the NT: Jesus uniquely introduces his teaching with Truly (Amen) I say to you — the Amen-formula is Jesus speaking with the authority of the God of Amen himself. John's Gospel uses I am the Truth (John 14:6) — Jesus is not only the truth-teller but the truth-embodied. The former-troubles-forgotten is the same promise as Isa 43:18 (do not remember the former things) — the new creation makes the old grief irrelevant.
65.17: See, I will create new heavens and a new earth. The former things will not be remembered, nor will they come to mind. The new-creation oracle is the theological apex of Isaiah and one of the most directly NT-cited verses in the OT. Peter uses it in 2 Pet 3:13 (in keeping with his promise we are looking forward to a new heaven and a new earth, where righteousness dwells). John sees it in Revelation: then I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth had passed away (Rev 21:1). Paul frames the transition theologically: the present form of this world is passing away (1 Cor 7:31); if anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come (2 Cor 5:17). The new-heavens-and-new-earth of Isa 65:17 is both the goal of history and the already-present reality inaugurated in the resurrection.
65.18: But be glad and rejoice forever in what I will create, for I will create Jerusalem to be a delight and its people a joy. The Jerusalem-of-delight is the new covenant community's identity: the city that was called Forsaken (62:4) and was lamented as a widow (Lamentations) is now created-to-be-delight by YHWH's own creative act. In the NT: the new Jerusalem coming down from God out of heaven, prepared as a bride beautifully dressed for her husband (Rev 21:2) is the final form of the Jerusalem-of-delight. The be-glad-and-rejoice-forever is the eschatological form of the Beatitudes' final promise: rejoice and be glad, for great is your reward in heaven (Matt 5:12).
65.19: I will rejoice over Jerusalem and take delight in my people; the sound of weeping and crying will be heard in it no more. The divine rejoicing over Jerusalem is the Zephaniah 3:17 promise (the Lord your God is with you; he is mighty to save; he will take great delight in you; he will rejoice over you with singing) applied eschatologically. In the NT: Rev 21:4 is the direct fulfillment — he will wipe every tear from their eyes; there will be no more death, mourning, crying, or pain, for the old order has passed away. The no-more-sound-of-weeping is the new-creation condition where the mourning of the covenant community finally ends — the promise that frames the entire Beatitudes of Matt 5:3-12.
65.20: Never again will there be in it an infant who lives but a few days, or an old man who does not live out his years; the one who dies at a hundred will be thought a mere child; the one who fails to reach a hundred will be considered accursed. The extension of life in the new creation is the reversal of the curse of Gen 3:17-19: the shortening of life by sin's consequence is undone by YHWH's creative renewal. The hundred-year-old as a mere child signals the eschatological lifespan — not just long life but the full realization of what life was designed to be. In the NT: I am the resurrection and the life; whoever believes in me will live, even though they die (John 11:25); death is swallowed up in victory (1 Cor 15:54) — the eschatological extension of life becomes the eternal life of the resurrection.
65.21: They will build houses and dwell in them; they will plant vineyards and eat their fruit. The reversal of the curse of Deut 28:30 (you will build a house but not live in it; you will plant a vineyard but not enjoy its fruit) — the new creation is the uncursing of the covenant community's existence. The simple enjoyment of one's own labor — building and living, planting and eating — is the Eden-restoration that sin's curse interrupted.
